[]{#part0000.html}

:::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0000.html_HT-fnmp-1 .calibre1}

::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0000.html_page_i .calibre1}Experimental Thinking

Experiments are a central methodology in the social sciences. Scholars
from every discipline regularly turn to experiments. Practitioners rely
on experimental evidence in evaluating social programs, policies, and
institutions. This book is about how to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}think["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
about experiments. It argues that designing a good experiment is a
slow-moving process (given the host of considerations), which is counter
to the current fast-moving temptations available in the social sciences.
The book includes discussion of the place of experiments in the social
science process, the assumptions underlying different types of
experiments, the validity of experiments, the application of different
designs, how to arrive at experimental questions, the role of
replications in experimental research, and the steps involved in
designing and conducting
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiments. The goal is to ensure that social science research remains
driven by important substantive questions and fully exploits the
potential of experiments in a thoughtful manner.

James N. Druckman is the Payson S. Wild Professor of Political Science
at Northwestern University. He was elected to the American Academy of
Arts and Sciences and, with Donald Green, helped found the Experimental
Research section of the American Political Science
Association.[]{#part0000.html_page_ii .calibre1}
:::
::::

[]{#part0007.html}

::::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0007.html_FMT-prf-1 .calibre1}

::: front-matter-part
# []{#part0007.html_page_xiii .calibre1}Preface {#part0007.html_calibre_pb_0 .fmt1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
Experiments are a central methodology in the social sciences. Scholars
from every discipline regularly turn to them. Practitioners rely on
experimental evidence in evaluating social programs, policies,
institutions, and information provision. The last decade has seen a
fundamental shift in experimental social science due not only to its
emergence as a primary methodology in many disciplines but also to
technological advances and evolving sociological norms (e.g., open
science). This book is about how to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}think["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
about experiments in light of these changes. It argues that designing a
good experiment is a slow-moving process (given the host of
considerations) that is counter to the current fast-moving temptations
available in the social sciences. The book includes discussion of the
place of experiments in the social science process; the assumptions
underlying different types of experiments; the validity of experiments;
the application of different designs (such as audit field experiments
and conjoint survey experiments); how to arrive at experimental
questions; the role of replications in experimental research; and the
steps involved in designing and conducting
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiments. The goal is to ensure that social science research remains
driven by important substantive questions and fully exploits the
potential of experiments in a thoughtful
manner.[]{#part0007.html_page_xiv .calibre1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0008.html}

:::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0008.html_FMT-ack-1 .calibre1}

::: front-matter-part
# []{#part0008.html_page_xv .calibre1}Acknowledgments {#part0008.html_calibre_pb_0 .fmt1}
:::

In some ways, I have been writing this book for much of my life. At an
early age, my mom, Marj Druckman, taught me how to systematically
address problems and think about them from multiple angles. Meanwhile,
my dad, Dan Druckman, exposed me to the social sciences and taught me
how to think about experiments. He even invited me to work with him on
my first experiment. I thank them both and dedicate this book to them. I
also thank the many students, teachers, and colleagues who have shaped
my thinking on experiments. Dozens of students have challenged me to
think of experiments in new ways; they also have shown me how to
creatively use experiments to address a wide range of questions. Several
current and former students assisted in the writing of this book
including Robin Bayes, Adam Howat, Suji Kang, Maya Novak-Herzog, Richard
Shafranek, Andrew Thompson, and Anna Wang. Jake Rothschild insightfully
commented on the book for which I am extremely appreciative.

I also thank my early mentors [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
particularly Ken Janda, Skip Lupia, and the late Mat McCubbins
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for teaching me about social science
methods and experiments. Mat read the first chapter and, in his typical
fashion, asked,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}What[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
your point?,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} thereby forcing me to make
it clearer. I will forever miss such direct but crucial feedback. I am
indebted to several colleagues who have talked with me about experiments
and/or encouraged me to flesh out my thoughts over the years. A partial
list includes Adam Berinsky, Toby Bolsen, Tabitha Bonilla, Cheryl
Boudreau, John Bullock, Ethan Busby, Dennis Chong, James Chu, Fay Lomax
Cook, Charles Crabtree, Catherine Eckel, David Figlio, Eli Finkel, D. J.
Flynn, Jeremy Freese, Laurel Harbridge-Yong, Larry Hedges, Lenka
Hrbková, []{#part0008.html_page_xvi .calibre1}Shanto Iyengar, Cindy Kam,
John Kane, Martin Kifer, Samara Klar, Jim Kuklinski, Thomas Leeper, Neil
Malhotra, Leslie McCall, Rose McDermott, Mary McGrath, Luke Miratrix,
Dan Molden, the late Becky Morton, Kevin Mullinix, Diana Mutz, Tom
Nelson, Dan O[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Keefe, Mike Parkin, Erik
Peterson, Dave Rand, Jenn Richeson, Josh Robison, John Barry Ryan,
Monica Schneider, Libby Sharrow, the late Lee Sigelman, Paul Sniderman,
Nick Stagnaro, Ben Tappin, Alex Theodoridis, Sophie Trawalter, Lynn
Vavreck, Jan Voelkel, Robb Willer, and Teppei Yamamoto. I am
particularly indebted to colleagues who read and commented on the book.
Adam Levine sent along nearly ten pages of profound comments. This led
to substantial revisions, which Yanna Krupnikov, Matt Levendusky, and
Rune Slothuus were kind enough to read [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
offering extreme insight and encouragement. This is nothing new as these
colleagues have been vital sounding boards for years. The same is true
of Don Green, who played a crucial role in supporting this endeavor.

I thank Robert Dreesen from Cambridge University Press, who guided me at
every stage, and the anonymous reviewers whose comments pressed me to
think more deeply about many of the topics covered in the book. I am
especially appreciative of Yoshi Ono, who arranged for me to give
several presentations on experimentation in Japan during the summer of
2019. He was a superb host and is a great colleague. I also thank the
participants who attended a conference on advances in experimental
political science at Northwestern University in May 2019. Their
presentations and conversations (and the volume coedited by myself and
Don Green, to which they contributed) significantly shaped my thinking.
I thank the National Science Foundation (SES-1822286), the Ford Motor
Company Center for Global Citizenship at the Kellogg School of
Management at Northwestern University (directed by David Austen-Smith),
the Institute for Policy Research at Northwestern University, and the
Department of Political Science at Northwestern University for
supporting the conference. All of these experiences formed the basis for
this book. Finally, my family, Nikki, Jake, and Sam, has been a constant
source of support in all the best ways.
::::

[]{#part0009_split_000.html}

::: {#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-1 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0009_split_000.html_page_1 .calibre1}1]{.label1} Why a Primer on Social Science Experiments? {#part0009_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: {#part0009_split_000.html_A-sec-01 .sec}
[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-337
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-931
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-563
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-919 .calibre1}In the
1909 American Political Science
Association[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s presidential address, A.
Lawrence Lowell stated, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}We are limited
by the impossibility of experiment. Politics is an observational, not an
experimental, science["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Lowell
[1910](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-351){#part0009_split_000.html_REFe-r-351-back
.calibre1}: 7). One hundred and ten years later, the
association[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s president, Rogers Smith
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0009_split_000.html_REFe-r-479-back
.calibre1}: 15), raised the question of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whether an excessive emphasis on
experiments will unduly constrict the questions political scientists
ask.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Clearly, much has changed in
political
science.[^1^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-1){#part0009_split_000.html_FN-fn-1-back
.calibre1} The same can be said about many social science disciplines
where experiments have evolved from a nonexistent method to an accepted
method to a primary method. Even psychology
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} where experiments have forever been a
central approach [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} has experienced
substantial changes in the last decade due to shifts in the social
sciences. Specifically, massive technological advances have facilitated
data access and analysis, and social scientists from all disciplines
have called for more ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}open
science["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} practices that involve
transparency and replication. There is a concern, however, that
[]{#part0009_split_000.html_page_2 .calibre1}these changes may cause
experimentalists to become ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}methods
driven,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} neither asking appropriate
questions nor maximizing the potential of the method (e.g., Thelen and
Mahoney
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-496){#part0009_split_000.html_REFe-r-496-back
.calibre1}: 19). These apprehensions accentuate the need for careful
discussion of the experimental method. That is the goal of this book:
The hope is to provide readers with a way to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}think["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
about experiments, both as []{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-564
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-920 .calibre1}users
and as consumers.

[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDXloc-tgt-98 .calibre1}From my perspective
as a political scientist, I discuss the evolution of experiments in
political science and use many examples from the discipline. That said,
the arguments I make and the suggestions I offer apply to any social
science application of an [experiment]{.italic}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which as will be discussed in detail
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} I define as a study where an
intervention (by a researcher or a natural event) provides the primary
mechanism by which one attempts to make a casual claim. What follows can
be read by those with no background and/or interest in political
science.

To be clear, this book is not a vigorous defense of experiments,
although it will become apparent that experiments have far-reaching
applications. Further, the book is neither a textbook on experimental
design and analyses [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} many such
treatments exist [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} nor an advanced
discussion of new developments, which is available in Druckman and
Green[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_000.html_REFe-r-148-back
.calibre1}) edited volume. Instead, the book is advisory and cautionary.
As social scientists forge ahead with experiments, it is crucial they do
so in the most productive and careful manner possible, remembering what
experiments are for; why and when they should be used; and how they
should be designed, implemented, and evaluated. In short, this book will
help social scientists think about experiments more productively.

My argument can be summarized as follows: In some sense, the social
sciences have become fast moving; computing technology and easily
accessible data sources have led to an explosion of experiments. This is
a dramatic change from earlier generations where the central challenges
for experimentalists involved gaining access to computing power and
data. Today, experiments seem often to be designed and implemented
quickly and not connected to the full scientific process. I argue that
the result is a problem. Specifically, experiments need to be thought of
as one part of a scientific process and not the first part. They need to
be used when appropriate and build on/have an interplay with questions,
observations, and theory. Moreover, conducting a quality experiment
requires thinking through a litany of decisions, ranging from how to
think about problems of casual inference to considering various
counterfactuals to how to interpret replications. [A good experiment is
slow moving (given the host of considerations), which is counter to the
current fast-moving []{#part0009_split_000.html_page_3
.calibre1}temptations available in the social sciences]{.italic}. This
book is about thinking through the parts that make an experiment slow
moving.[^2^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-2){#part0009_split_000.html_FN-fn-2-back
.calibre1}

I make this argument by discussing the following:

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   How to think about the place of experiments in the social scientific
    process [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, addressing the
    question of what role experiments play in the accumulation of
    knowledge (e.g., relative to theory building and other steps in the
    scientific method).

-   How to arrive at questions that experiments are useful to answer.

-   How to think about the assumptions underlying different types of
    experiments.

-   How to think about evaluating the realism and validity of
    experiments, as well as assessing experimental samples.

-   How to think about new experimental designs.

-   How to proceed after an experiment is completed, and particularly
    how to think about the replication of experiments.

-   How to think about the process of designing and conducting a
    ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    experiment; by this I do not mean the technical design details but
    rather all of the steps one should take to ensure experiments
    connect to theory and advance knowledge.
:::

My approach seeks to broaden conversations about experiments by placing
them in the larger research process where one must consider issues that
ostensibly have little direct connection to experimental design (e.g.,
questions of sampling and measurement) but are essential if one hopes to
design optimal experiments. At the same time, it focuses discussion by
highlighting the need to attend to precise causal inference assumptions
and counterfactual thinking. Further, I offer somewhat contrarian views
on experimental realism and validity [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
perspectives that also lead to some cautionary notes when it comes to
open science practices such as preregistration and replication. Some
highlights of the points I make include the following.

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   []{#part0009_split_000.html_page_4
    .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-361
    .calibre1}Experiments are useful only if there exists a
    substantively grounded question, a well-defined target population,
    carefully constructed measures, and clear points of comparisons.
    Many extant experiments fail to explicitly consider these issues
    ([Chapter
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-2-back-2
    .calibre1}).

-   All experiments [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} whether using
    random assignment or relying on experimental control
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} involve assumptions about causal
    inference that often receive scant consideration ([Chapter
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-2-back-3
    .calibre1}).

-   The goal of an experiment is to generalize a causal relationship. In
    many, but not all cases, the size of the effect from a single
    experiment is less important, and the sample used to document the
    effect is not crucial ([Chapter
    3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-3-back-2
    .calibre1}).

-   In most cases, assessing whether experimental treatments resemble
    the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
    world["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is misguided, as the focus
    should be on the theoretical construct of interest and ensuring
    successful delivery of the treatments ([Chapter
    3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-3-back-3
    .calibre1}).

-   Generalizing an experimental result is more complicated than
    evaluating the
    ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}representativeness["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    of the sample. In fact, the representativeness of the sample only
    matters when causal effects differ across relevant people (or when
    the goal of the experiment is to directly inform policy) ([Chapter
    3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-3-back-4
    .calibre1}).

-   Recent design innovations that use audit field experiments and
    conjoint survey experiments offer many opportunities, but these
    designs have limitations and are only useful under particular
    circumstances. Ultimately, an experimental design is only as good as
    the question being addressed and the hypotheses being tested
    ([Chapter
    4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-4-back-2
    .calibre1}).

-   The process of asking good questions for experiments develops from
    assessing the world and the field, socializing with a diversity of
    people, and building on prior experiments that did not go as planned
    ([Chapter
    5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-5-back-2
    .calibre1}).

-   Implementing a good experiment requires the documentation of every
    decision in detail. However, it does not require a formal
    registration of a pre-analysis plan. Such a plan, if done, should
    not constrain an experimentalist from exploratory data analyses or
    incorporating theoretical ideas that had not been initially
    considered ([Chapter
    5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-5-back-3
    .calibre1}).

-   After an experiment is done, repeating it for
    replication[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sake has limited value;
    however, replication can be used as a route to innovation (by
    extending prior designs) and aggregation so as to isolate the size
    of an effect ([Chapter
    5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-5-back-4
    .calibre1}).

-   Despite all the innovations in experimental social science, the
    steps needed to design a quality experiment remain the same and
    require []{#part0009_split_000.html_page_5 .calibre1}situating
    experiments in the entire scientific process. This starts with
    asking a relevant substantive question and, from there, involves a
    lengthy iterative process, but one that is doable and rewarding
    []{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-362 .calibre1}([Chapter
    6](#part0014_split_000.html_CN-bp-6){#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-6-back-2
    .calibre1}).
:::

Who would want to read this book? I hope the material is relevant to any
social scientist, including students who are just learning about social
science. Those who regularly conduct experiments may find that some
parts are familiar and other parts provide novel views. Those who do not
engage in experimentation, or even begrudge experiments, may learn about
the logic of experimentation, novel applications, and/or how to
interpret and generalize experiments. These have become requisite skills
for reading social science literature.

I proceed in this chapter with a discussion of the evolution of
experiments, illustrating this development through the field of
political science. I argue that the discipline currently finds itself in
a new era, parts of which apply to all of the social sciences. This new
era began around 2010 and reflects the confluence of experiments
achieving widespread acceptance in the discipline, technological
advances, and the open science movement (these latter two dynamics have
affected all of the social sciences). The era introduced many
opportunities but also new challenges. Ironically, the ease of
conducting experiments today has the potential to undermine
[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-338
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-932 .calibre1}their
quality.
:::::
::::::
::::::::

[]{#part0009_split_001.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0009_split_001.html_A-sec-1 .sec}
# []{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-9 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-91 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-287 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-363 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-939 .calibre1}The Evolution of Experiments {#part0009_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

In their foundational text on quasi-experiments, Campbell and Stanley
([1963](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-90){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-90-back
.calibre1}: 3) explain that we must

::: disp-quote
justify experimentation on more pessimistic grounds
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} not as a panacea, but rather as the
only available route to cumulative progress. We must instill in our
students the expectation of tedium and disappointment and the duty of
thorough persistence [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} We must expand
our students[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} vow of poverty to include
not only the willingness to accept poverty of finances, but also a
poverty of experimental results.
:::

This pessimistic portrayal reflects the prevailing reality of
experiments for much of social science history: Experimentalists had to
overcome the logistical challenges of and limited opportunities for data
collection. For example, in the first random assignment experiment
published in the [American Political Science Review]{.italic}
([APSR]{.italic}), Eldersveld
([1956](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-175){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-175-back
.calibre1}) relied on fifty students and four staff members to work for
about 400 hours []{#part0009_split_001.html_page_6
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-58
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-75
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-157
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-158
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-291 .calibre1}to study
500 subjects [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, it was far from
a straightforward process. Iyengar et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1982](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-292){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-292-back
.calibre1}) seminal agenda-setting experiment had all of twenty-eight
subjects, while Druckman[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2001](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-138){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-138-back
.calibre1}) study of framing had 264 subjects but took roughly five
months to collect the data. In addition to the shortage of readily
available experimental subjects (even student subject pools can be used
for only so many experiments), experimentalists also faced the
inevitable occurrence of null results. These results were rarely
published, which led Ioannidis
([2005](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-288){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-288-back
.calibre1}) to famously claim that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}most
published research findings are false.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

In the last few decades, experimentation has dramatically changed. Data
collection opportunities are plentiful thanks to crowdsourcing
platforms, internet panels, social media contacts, and elite samples via
e-mail. Computing advances allow for large-scale experiments, sometimes
on literally millions of participants (e.g., Bond et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-59){#part0009_split_001.html_REFe-r-59-back
.calibre1}). Moreover, scholars no longer dismiss null results as
inherently uninteresting due to the recognition that publishing only
statistically significant results can skew the research record. These
developments bring with them new opportunities but also a new type of
possible poverty. The ease of data collection and acceptance of
non-findings means scholars might be less incentivized to design and
implement quality experiments: There is much less at stake with each
experiment, given the relative ease of data collection and increasing
acceptance of null results. On the latter, it has become essential to
distinguish meaningful null results out of a carefully constructed and
implemented experiment from those resulting from a poorly designed
study. In short, the concerns are a poverty of poor designs,
inappropriate analyses, limited use of data, and/or flawed
interpretations. Even an infinite amount of data cannot compensate for a
thoughtlessly designed experiment. This makes it all the more important
to ensure that experimentalists design sound studies and properly
analyze, interpret, and present the data from particular samples. To
situate the relevance of the aforementioned concerns, I next turn to an
overview of how political science (as an example) arrived at its current
state when it []{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-10
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-92
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-288
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-364
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-940 .calibre1}comes to
experiments.
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0009_split_002.html}

:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: {#part0009_split_002.html_A-sec-1 .sec}
::::: {#part0009_split_002.html_B-sec-1 .sec}
## []{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-343 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-449 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-565 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-743 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1073 .calibre1}The Expansion of Experiments in Political Science[^3^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-3){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-3-back .calibre1} {#part0009_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .b}

As mentioned, the lessons that follow apply to any social science
discipline, but in this section I offer an example of how experiments
have []{#part0009_split_002.html_page_7 .calibre1}emerged in my home
discipline of political science. Similar trends, albeit at different
points, have occurred in other disciplines such as economics and
sociology. This differs from psychology where experiments have forever
been central. However, as I will discuss, technological changes and the
emergence of open science have altered experiments in all disciplines
including psychology.

[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-41
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-74
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-82
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-97
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-152
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-171
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-177
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-231
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-251
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-315 .calibre1}When it
comes to political science, the Lowell quote with which I started the
book makes clear that experiments were not present when the discipline
launched. With a few notable exceptions
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as
Gosnell[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1926](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-226){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-226-back
.calibre1}) study of voting mobilization
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} experiments remained, at best,
peripheral through most of the twentieth century. The 1950s and 1960s
saw some activity with a research program that used role-playing
experimental simulations to test how situational factors affect
decisions to go to war and international negotiations (e.g., Hermann and
Hermann
[1967](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-264){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-264-back
.calibre1}; Mahoney and Druckman
[1975](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-361){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-361-back
.calibre1}; Guetzkow and Valadez
[1981](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-241){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-241-back
.calibre1}). A bit later, a short-lived journal titled [The Experimental
Study of Politics]{.italic} appeared. The status of experiments began to
notably change, however, in the late 1980s and 1990s with experiments on
Congressional committee decision-making (Fiorina and Plott
[1978](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-188){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-188-back
.calibre1}), media effects (Iyengar and Kinder
[1987](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-291){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-291-back
.calibre1}), elections (McKelvey and Ordeshook
[1990](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-371){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-371-back
.calibre1}), and public opinion (Sniderman et al.
[1991](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-484){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-484-back
.calibre1}) (also see Kinder and Palfrey
[1993](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-312){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-312-back
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-11
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-537 .calibre1}This
slow emergence can be seen by charting the number of experimental
articles in the discipline[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s flagship
journal, the [American Political Science Review]{.italic}
([APSR]{.italic}), as documented in [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1-back-2
.calibre1}. There were no experimental articles from 1906 to 1956, and
thus the figure begins in the 1950s and continues through 2019,
reporting the number of experimental articles by
decade.[^4^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-4){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-4-back
.calibre1} (This is not a cumulative count of articles but rather the
specific number by decade.) The figure reveals the aforementioned bump
in the 1980s and 1990s and also shows the continuing increase in
subsequent decades to 31 in 2000[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2009
and 84 in
2010[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2019.[^5^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-5){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-5-back
.calibre1} The figure supports []{#part0009_split_002.html_page_8
.calibre1}the claim that experiments moved from being a
nonexistent/marginalized method to an accepted method, and then to a
primary method.

:::: {#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1 .fig}
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00017.jpeg){.graphic2}

::: caption
[Figure 1.1.]{.label2}

[American Political Science Review]{.italic} experimental articles by
decade
:::
::::

While any division into particular ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}eras
of experimentation["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} would contain an
arbitrary element, I label three periods. First is the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}preexperimental
period["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that lasted until roughly 2000.
As explained, the end of this period included the publication of some
influential experiments; however, applications remained concentrated in
a few substantive areas and experiments ostensibly were not a core part
of political science
curricula.[^6^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-6){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-6-back
.calibre1} For instance, in their highly impactful 1994 book on social
science methodology, King and his colleagues
([1994](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-314){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-314-back
.calibre1}: 125) provide virtually no discussion of experimentation,
stating only that experiments are helpful insofar as they
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}provide a useful model for understanding
certain aspects of non-experimental
[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-538
.calibre1}design.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-313
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-395
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-441
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-451
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-723 .calibre1}The
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experimental political science
1.0["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} era, starting around 2000, saw the
method become more mainstream/accepted as demarcated by two events.
First, in 2000, Gerber and Green
([2000](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-210){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-210-back
.calibre1}) published their field experiment on voter mobilization. This
study involved randomly assigning roughly 30,000 registered voters to
receive nonpartisan get-out-the-vote []{#part0009_split_002.html_page_9
.calibre1}messages via personal canvassing, direct mail, or telephone
calls. One of the more notable findings is that personal canvassing by
far has the largest impact on mobilizing voters. The paper offered
practical lessons for those interested in increasing turnout and spoke
to an ostensible paradox at the time concerning the decline in voting
turnout, linking it to the decrease in face-to-face mobilization. The
paper accentuated the power of experiments for academics and
practitioners. It also was only the third field experiment published in
the [APSR]{.italic}, and the first in nearly twenty years. The
confluence of the widely discussed results along with the reintroduction
of the field experimental method stimulated others to turn to field
experiments. It sparked burgeoning literature on voter mobilization
(e.g., Nickerson
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-411){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-411-back
.calibre1}; Green et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-229){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-229-back
.calibre1}) and vote choice (Wantchekon
[2003](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-510){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-510-back
.calibre1}), and more generally, ushered in the use of field experiments
in other subfields (e.g., Findley et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-186){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-186-back
.calibre1}; Hyde and Marinov
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-282){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-282-back
.calibre1}).[^7^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-7){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-7-back
.calibre1} It also cohered with the expansion of field experiments in
other disciplines (e.g., []{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-314
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-396
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-442
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-452
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-724 .calibre1}Bloom
[2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-56){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-56-back
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-292 .calibre1}Second, in 2001,
Time-Sharing Experiments for the Social Sciences (TESS) was established
with support from the National Science Foundation. TESS capitalizes on
economies of scale to enable scholars from across the social sciences,
on a competitive basis, to conduct survey experiments on
probability-based samples of the US population (see Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-398-back
.calibre1}). Since its founding, TESS has supported more than 600
experiments. Clearly, the first decade of the twenty-first century saw
the confirmation of experiments as a mainstream method.

[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-7
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-299 .calibre1}Another
change occurred about a decade into the twenty-first century: the
establishment of two major experimental institutions. This could be
called ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experimental political science
2.0.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} In 2009, Evidence in Governance and
Politics (EGAP) formed as a network for those engaged in field
experiments on governance, politics, and institutions. As it grew in
membership and capacity, EGAP also expanded its worldwide outreach
efforts to include instruction on experimental methods across the Global
South. Then, in 2010, the first meeting of the American Political
Science Association[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s experimental
research section took place, and a year later it
[]{#part0009_split_002.html_page_10 .calibre1}voted to launch the
[Journal of Experimental Political Science]{.italic} (the first issue of
which appeared []{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-8
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-12
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-300 .calibre1}in
2014).[^8^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-8){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-8-back
.calibre1}

These institutions reflected and further stimulated the use of
experiments, as is made clear in [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1-back-3
.calibre1} with eighty-four experiments published in [APSR]{.italic}
since 2010. Not only did the number of experiments substantially
increase, but the reach of these experiments expanded to new domains.
Examples include the study of elite responsiveness (Butler and Broockman
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-79){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-79-back
.calibre1}; Grose
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-233){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-233-back
.calibre1}; Costa
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-113){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-113-back
.calibre1}); social media communications (e.g., Settle
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-464){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-464-back
.calibre1}); governmental threats to use force (e.g., Tomz
[2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-500){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-500-back
.calibre1}; Kertzer and Brutger
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-310){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-310-back
.calibre1}); election monitoring (Ichino and Schündeln
[2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-283){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-283-back
.calibre1}; Hyde and Marinov
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-282){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-282-back-2
.calibre1}; Buzin et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-83){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-83-back
.calibre1}); foreign aid (Beath et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-32){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-32-back
.calibre1}); and governance and accountability (Dunning et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-168){#part0009_split_002.html_REFe-r-168-back
.calibre1}).[^9^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-9){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-9-back
.calibre1} It is telling that, since 2010, roughly 44 percent of the
experimental articles published in the [APSR]{.italic} can be classified
in the field of comparative politics (up from 19 percent during
2000[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2009 and 2 percent during
1956[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1999).[^10^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-10){#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-10-back
.calibre1}

In sum, the last decade has seen a dramatic growth of experimental
approaches across political science. It is clear that political
scientists think about and apply experiments in a very different manner
than a decade ago: They think of experimentation as a primary
methodology and apply it in novel domains. Understanding this new era of
experimentation (starting around 2010) requires more than recognition of
the growth of experiments, however. The basic nature of experimentation,
across the social sciences, has changed due to technological advances
and the open []{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-344
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-450
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-566
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-744
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1074 .calibre1}science
movement.
:::::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::

[]{#part0009_split_003.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0009_split_003.html_A-sec-1 .sec}
::: {#part0009_split_003.html_B-sec-2 .sec}
## []{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-137 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-213 .calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-973 .calibre1}Technological Change and Open Science {#part0009_split_003.html_calibre_pb_3 .b}

The initial emergence of experiments (in the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}preexperimental
era["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}) followed on the heels of several
technological advances. In the 1980s,
[]{#part0009_split_003.html_page_11 .calibre1}the advent of
computer-assisted telephone interviewing facilitated the implementation
of phone-based survey experiments (Sniderman and Grob
[1996](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-485){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-485-back
.calibre1}). The pace of technological change has accelerated even more
in recent years. The costs and logistical challenges of data collection
have dramatically dropped (e.g., Groves
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-236){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-236-back
.calibre1}), enabling researchers to access survey and behavioral data
at a notably larger scale (e.g., Bond et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-59){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-59-back-2
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-36
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-61
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-124
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-202
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-228
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-270
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-281 .calibre1}Consider
four dynamics. First, as intimated, data are now much cheaper and easier
to obtain thanks to the Internet and the emergence of crowdsourcing
platforms and commercial internet survey panels. These data can then be
shared due to the growing use of public data repositories, such as
Dataverse and Github. Second, social media offers researchers access to
behavioral data and the opportunity to intervene experimentally (e.g.,
Kramer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-321){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-321-back
.calibre1}; Guess
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-240){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-240-back
.calibre1}). Third, the advent of portable computers with
high-resolution screens has made it easy for researchers to deploy
surveys and lab-like treatments in field settings, which dramatically
lowers logistical costs. Fourth, advances in computing allow researchers
to analyze high-dimensional data, which is to say data with large
numbers of predictors or measurements.

Apart from technological advances, the social sciences have become
increasingly attuned to the challenges of accumulating knowledge, given
perverse incentives to exaggerate the size and statistical significance
of treatment effects or, conversely, to bury weak or unexpected
findings. The tendency for journals to publish splashy, statistically
significant findings is often termed
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}publication
bias["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Brown et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-65){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-65-back
.calibre1}). Evidence of this bias in many disciplines is not new, but
political scientists have only recently begun to document it (e.g.,
Gerber et al.
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-216){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-216-back
.calibre1}; Malhotra
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-362-back
.calibre1}). One response to publication bias has been a call for more
replications: emulating the extant
study[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s procedures but with new data
(e.g., OSC 2015; Coppock et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-112-back
.calibre1}). Scholars can undertake such replications in part because of
a push for researchers to make their procedures, stimuli, surveys, and
data publicly available. In political science, for example, most general
and experimental journals require data access upon publication (Lupia
and Elman
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-354){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-354-back
.calibre1}). The opportunities that come from data sharing and related
initiatives have invigorated a call for
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}open
science["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-412-back
.calibre1}; Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0009_split_003.html_REFe-r-96-back
.calibre1}) that includes standards for transparency, preregistration of
studies and analysis plans, and incentives for replication. In short,
fundamental technological [and]{.italic} sociological changes concerning
open science have transformed social science
[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-138
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-214
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-974
.calibre1}experimentation.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0009_split_004.html}

::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0009_split_004.html_A-sec-2 .sec}
# []{#part0009_split_004.html_page_12 .calibre1}Why a Primer? {#part0009_split_004.html_calibre_pb_4 .a}

The current state of experimentation can be characterized by the
explosion of new data sources; the use of new measurement techniques;
the introduction of previously underutilized designs; advancements in
statistical methods; increased discussion about robustness and
generalizability; and applications of experiments in more areas of study
(Druckman and Green
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_004.html_REFe-r-148-back-2
.calibre1}). Conducting and publishing experiments is much easier than
it was a few decades ago, and there are novel issues to consider (e.g.,
new sampling possibilities, design options, and open science
considerations). Consequently, now is the time for a primer on
experimental thinking [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a call to step
back and assess how to think about these opportunities. This approach
will ensure that experimentalists do not move so quickly that they lose
sight of (1) what experiments can contribute and (2) the place of
experiments in the larger social science enterprise. The ultimate goal
is to help experimentalists conduct high quality, substantively relevant
studies and avoid the aforementioned ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}new
poverty["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} of experiments.
:::

:::: {#part0009_split_004.html_A-sec-3 .sec}
::: {#part0009_split_004.html_calibre_pb_5 .calibre9}
:::
::::
::::::
:::::::

[]{#part0009_split_005.html}

:::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0009_split_005.html_A-sec-3 .sec}
# Book Outline {#part0009_split_005.html_calibre_pb_6 .a}

This book focuses on how to think about experiments, including the role
of experiments in the research process, the validity of experiments,
experimental designs, and the process of asking experimental questions
and conducting experimental replications. These topics are fundamental
to any experimental approach, and therefore sensible areas to cover in a
primer on developments in the social sciences. This means, however, that
I do not cover certain subjects, perhaps most notably ethics and
advanced statistical techniques. The exclusions are not meant to
minimize the importance of these (and other) areas. Rather, I opt for
essential foci in thinking about experiments that can be suitably
covered in a relatively short
book.[^11^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-11){#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-11-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-117
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-149
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-237
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-297
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-393
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-541
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-603
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-791
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-885
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-909
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-917 .calibre1}The book
contains five additional chapters, which are written in an interlinked
fashion to make the central point that, as stated, a good experiment is
slow moving (given the host of considerations that are covered in this
book), which is counter to the current fast-moving temptations available
in the social sciences. [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-2-back-4
.calibre1} starts by placing experiments in the scientific process;
experiments are only useful in the context
[]{#part0009_split_005.html_page_13 .calibre1}of well-motivated
questions, thoughtful theories, and falsifiable hypotheses. I then turn
to sampling and measurement, as careful attention to these topics,
despite being neglected often by experimentalists, is imperative. In the
remainder of [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-2-back-5
.calibre1}, I offer a detailed discussion of causal inference that I use
to motivate an inclusive definition of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiments.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
I view this as more than a pedantic exercise, as careful consideration
of approaches to causal inference reveals the often implicit assumptions
that underlie all experiments. I conclude the chapter by touching on the
different goals experiments may have and the basics of analysis. The
chapter serves as a reminder of the underlying logic of experimentation
and the type of mindset one should have when designing experiments. A
central point concerns the importance of counterfactual thinking, which
pushes experimentalists to think carefully about the precise comparisons
needed to test a causal claim.

[Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-3-back-5
.calibre1} focuses on how to think when evaluating experiments. This
includes a discussion of realism, particularly why mundane realism or
resemblance to the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
world["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} receives far too much attention,
as well as an overview of how to design experimental treatments. The
chapter then turns to validity issues, offering a new way to think about
external validity in assessing experiments. This includes a detailed
discussion of sampling and why the onus should be more on critics of an
experimental sample than on the experimentalist him/herself (i.e., to
justify a sample).

In [Chapter
4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-4-back-3
.calibre1}, I turn to experimental designs, focusing on three designs
that have gained prominence in many social science applications in the
last decade: audit field experiments, conjoint survey experiments, and
lab-in-the-field experiments. These designs also encompass each
conventional type of social science experiment (i.e., field, survey, and
lab) (Druckman et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-146){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-146-back
.calibre1}). The chapter reviews the basics of each design and provides
prominent examples. Importantly, it also discusses limitations and
challenges of the designs, or put another way, how to think about these
new designs. The chapter includes a brief overview of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}public policy
experiments["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}: It does so given the recent
rise in studies of political elites that ultimately connect to
policymaking and responsiveness. The chapter makes clear that the
substantive questions being explored should drive experimental design
choices and not vice []{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-118
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-150
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-238
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-298
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-394
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-542
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-604
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-792
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-886
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-910
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-918 .calibre1}versa.

[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-315
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-323 .calibre1}[Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-5-back-5
.calibre1} delves into the steps that occur prior to, during, and after
an experiment [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} including arriving at
questions to explore by using an experiment; documenting the steps in
the process of conducting an experiment; and considering whether to
replicate one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s findings after an
[]{#part0009_split_005.html_page_14 .calibre1}experiment. This
discussion touches on the themes of the aforementioned open science
movement, offering in many instances a cautionary perspective.

The final chapter ([Chapter
6](#part0014_split_000.html_CN-bp-6){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-6-back-3
.calibre1}) explains how to design
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiments. The primary point is that regardless of changes, the
fundamentals of conducing a sound experiment remain the same. I offer a
list of steps that should be taken for any design. The ultimate goal is
to place experiments [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and recent
developments in sampling, designs, and practice
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in the larger landscape of the
ever-changing []{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-316
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-324 .calibre1}social
sciences.
:::
::::

::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fng-1 .fn-group}
::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-1 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0009_split_000.html_FN-fn-1-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-237 .calibre1}While not my focus,
another contrast between Lowell and Smith concerns their perspectives on
race. Lowell served as the president of Harvard from 1909 to 1933,
during which time he attempted to ban Black students from living in
freshman halls (Sollors et al.
[1993](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-488){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-488-back
.calibre1}). In contrast, a fair amount of
Smith[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s work explores the incorporation
of minorities into political life, such as pointing out that the United
States has an ascriptive tradition that involves sexism, racism, and
nativism (Smith
[1993](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-478){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-478-back
.calibre1}). He states in his presidential address,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}we are not going to be able to
understand major political developments of the past, present, and future
if we do not explore more deeply the politics of identity formation,
using all methods that can help["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (12).
This comparison reveals the extent to which the discipline has changed
from one that largely ignored race for the first part of its history
(Blatt
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-54){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-54-back
.calibre1}) to one that is now recognizing the central role of studying
race and identity.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-2 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0009_split_000.html_FN-fn-2-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-267 .calibre1}In so doing, I hope
to make clear that, counter to Smith[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-479-back-2
.calibre1}) assessment, experiments when done carefully need not
constrict their reach. However, I simultaneously emphasize
Smith[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s point that experiments have a
place in the scientific process and the key to exploiting their power is
to understand that place, including their limitations. Put another way,
I follow Smith[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-479-back-3
.calibre1}: 16[)]{.label2} advice to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}find ways to place our particular
studies more explicitly in broader accounts of politics that can
credibly indicate their importance.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Smith
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-479-back-4
.calibre1}: 15) further states that the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}contributions of this experimental turn
are undeniable.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} My hope is that this
book will help make them even greater.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-3 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-3-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Parts
of this section and the next one come from Druckman and Green
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-148-back-3
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-4 .fn}
[[^4^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-4-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} For
the content analysis, I use a broad definition of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
that includes experiments that rely on random assignment; that pay
subjects based on their behaviors; and that exploit random or
near-random natural variations (i.e.,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}natural
experiments["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}). I offer a formal
definition of experiment in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0009_split_005.html_CN-bp-2-back-6
.calibre1}. I extend the timeline from Druckman et al.
([2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-255){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-255-back
.calibre1}) (also see Rogowski
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-453){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-453-back
.calibre1}). The total number of yearly articles in the journal remained
fairly constant, and thus any changes in the number of experimental
articles do not reflect alterations in the total number of articles
published. Finally, as with Druckman et al.
([2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-255){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-255-back-2
.calibre1}), I exclude Gosnell
([1926](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-226){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-226-back-2
.calibre1}) since he does not employ random assignment or other control
mechanisms.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-5 .fn}
[[^5^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-5-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Druckman and Green
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-148-back-4
.calibre1}) report results from the same content analysis. However,
their coding ended as of May 2019 (and any articled posted online at
that point), whereas my analysis includes all published articles in 2019
(and thus includes nine papers that had not been posted at the time of
Druckman and Green[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s analysis).
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-6 .fn}
[[^6^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-6-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} That
said, I agree that one could reasonably argue that the
1980s[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1990s should be differentiated
from the pre-1980s, given the growth of experiments in that time period
(see [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0009_split_005.html_FIG-fig-1-back-4
.calibre1}). Alternatively, one could merge the
1980s[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1990s with
2000[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2009, although, as explained
shortly, two major events (i.e., Gerber and Green
([2000](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-210){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-210-back-2
.calibre1}) and the start of the Time-Sharing Experiments for the Social
Sciences program) signal a qualitative shift in the availability and the
use of experiments around 2000.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-7 .fn}
[[^7^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-7-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Since
2000, roughly thirty field experiments have been published in the
[APSR]{.italic} and the [Annual Review of Political Science]{.italic}
has published several reviews of field experiments on a range of topics,
including collective action (de Rooij et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-125){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-125-back
.calibre1}), developmental economics (Humphreys and Weinstein
[2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-277){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-277-back
.calibre1}), political institutions (Grose
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-233){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-233-back-2
.calibre1}), and international relations (Hyde
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-281){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-281-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-8 .fn}
[[^8^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-8-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-67
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-91
.calibre1}[]{#part0009_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-93 .calibre1}Examples
of other institutional developments in political science include the
launching of subject pools in more than a dozen departments (Druckman et
al.
[2018a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-149){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-149-back
.calibre1}: 624) and the start of a Routledge book series focused on
experimental political science. These institutional innovations were
accompanied by some notable publications. This list includes the
explosion of experimental articles using
Amazon[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Mechanical Turk to furnish
research participants (Berinsky et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-41){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-41-back
.calibre1}; Mullinix et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-393-back
.calibre1}) and, in 2011, the [Cambridge Handbook of Experimental
Political Science]{.italic} (Druckman et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-146){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-146-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-9 .fn}
[[^9^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-9-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
last decade also has seen new methods, with experiments increasingly
using novel types of designs (e.g., conjoint survey experimensts,
audits) and samples (e.g., crowdsourcing platforms, social media) (see
Druckman and Green
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-148-back-5
.calibre1} for details). I discuss these developments in later chapters.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-10 .fn}
[[^10^](#part0009_split_002.html_FN-fn-10-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
These percentages come from a content analysis of the articles displayed
in [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0009_split_005.html_FIG-fig-1-back-5
.calibre1}.
:::

::: {#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-11 .fn}
[[^11^](#part0009_split_005.html_FN-fn-11-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Druckman and Green[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-148){#part0009_split_005.html_REFe-r-148-back-6
.calibre1}) edited volume provides a comprehensive treatment of the full
range of experimental topics. Also, on ethics, see Desposato (2016).
:::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_000.html}

::: {#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0010_split_000.html_page_15 .calibre1}2]{.label1} The Scientific Process and How to Think about Experiments {#part0010_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}
:::

:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0010_split_000.html_A-sec-02 .sec}
This chapter serves as a precursor. Most treatments of experiments begin
with a definition; yet, I start with prior considerations including the
place of experimentation in the scientific process, as well as
discussions of sampling and measurement. While these latter topics are
often covered in research design or statistics textbooks, many
experimentalists ostensibly ignore them in practice. This leads to lower
quality experiments and less progressive research agendas. I highlight
essential points that need explicit attention prior to conceiving of an
experiment. I then offer a discussion of causation, which leads me to an
inclusive definition of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
I differentiate types of experiments and highlight variations in
experimental goals. I conclude the chapter with a brief discussion of
analyses as it pertains to design issues. My goal is to highlight a host
of often overlooked considerations and assumptions that underlie any
experiment. I also aim to situate experiments in the larger social
science research process [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} by this, I
mean the role experiments play in the accumulation of knowledge (e.g.,
relative to theory building and other steps in the scientific method).
:::
::::
::::::

[]{#part0010_split_001.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0010_split_001.html_A-sec-4 .sec}
# []{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-907 .calibre1}The Scientific Process, Sampling, and Measurement {#part0010_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-903 .calibre1}Science involves a
process that produces systematized knowledge. For most, this involves
the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}scientific
method.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} The basic method involves five
steps: (1) ask a question; (2) develop a theory to answer the question;
(3) derive testable hypotheses; (4) collect data to test those
hypotheses; and (5) analyze the data. Other components involve external
review, []{#part0010_split_001.html_page_16 .calibre1}replication, and
ensuring that hypotheses are falsifiable (i.e., can be proven incorrect)
(Popper
[1959](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-440){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-440-back
.calibre1}, []{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-904
.calibre1}[1962](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-441){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-441-back
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-179
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-391
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-487
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-901 .calibre1}Let us
consider an example, one I will invoke several times. It starts with a
substantive research question: ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Does
media coverage of campaign finance reform policies affect opinions about
those policies?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} One might theorize that
since people often depend on the mass media for political information,
especially on issues that are more removed from their everyday lives,
then media framing of the issue will shape
individuals[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} opinions. It might be that
media coverage focusing on the impact of special interests in
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}buying
campaigns["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} will lead people to support
policies limiting such interests (i.e., more restrictive campaign
finance laws). The hypothesis is:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Compared to those not exposed to media
coverage, those exposed to coverage on how campaign finance laws will
limit the influence of special interests will become more supportive of
such laws, all else constant.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} The test
might involve surveying people and asking them whether they were exposed
to relevant media stories and about their opposition or support for
campaign finance laws. One would then analyze the survey to assess
whether those exposed exhibit greater support than those not exposed. If
there exists a significant difference, it would constitute evidence
consistent with the hypothesis; if not, the hypothesis can be rejected
and one would have to dismiss or modify the theory. In that sense, one
can never ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}prove a
theory["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}: Instead, one continues to test a
hypothesis that is assumed to be accurate until proven incorrect or
falsified.

To be clear, my characterization of the scientific method is both
debated and idealized; science in practice typically is messier (e.g.,
Sokal and Bricmont
[1997](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-487){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-487-back
.calibre1}). Failure to find evidence for a hypothesis need not lead one
to abandon a theory entirely but instead can generate refinements and
auxiliary hypotheses (e.g., Kuhn
[1962](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-328){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-328-back
.calibre1}; Lakatos
[1970](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-331){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-331-back
.calibre1}). Moreover, falsification [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
that is, researchers making testable claims that future observations
might reveal to be false (i.e., refutation) (e.g., Popper
[1959](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-440){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-440-back-2
.calibre1},
[1962](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-441){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-441-back-2
.calibre1},
[1974](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-442){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-442-back
.calibre1}; Miller
[1985](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-380){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-380-back
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} has evoked crucial debates
concerning its application in science (for general discussion, see
Miller
[1994](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-381){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-381-back
.calibre1}; Oreskes
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-420){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-420-back
.calibre1}:
15[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}68).[^1^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-12){#part0010_split_001.html_FN-fn-12-back
.calibre1} Nonetheless, I will appeal to falsification at several points
[]{#part0010_split_001.html_page_17 .calibre1}because it serves as a
basic albeit idyllic standard to assess scientific progress, especially
with respect to experiments (e.g., Cook and Campbell
[1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-106-back
.calibre1}:
25).[^2^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-13){#part0010_split_001.html_FN-fn-13-back
.calibre1}

Put another way, the scientific method and the idea of falsification
serve as useful heuristics when it comes to thinking about social
science experiments and their place in research programs. For political
science, the questions asked come from subfields such as American
politics, comparative politics, international relations, and political
philosophy. As a discipline defined by context rather than methodology
(Druckman and Lupia
[2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-158){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-158-back
.calibre1}), theories come in a host of guises ranging from formal
theory to psychological models to historical narratives and more. The
same can be said of other social sciences, to varying extents (e.g.,
even in economics, some rely on analytic narratives or behavioral
theories). Further, there exists no agreement on what constitutes a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good
theory["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
deductive validity, parsimony, psychological realism, etc. Nonetheless,
a primary goal, though by no means the only one, is to arrive at
generalizable causal (falsifiable) statements relevant to the social,
economic, and/or political world (King
[1991](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-313){#part0010_split_001.html_REFe-r-313-back
.calibre1}: 1049).

The focus of this primer concerns how to test such statements (i.e., the
fourth step in the idealized scientific method). There exists a
multitude of approaches for conducting such tests including case
studies, ethnography, focus groups, surveys, experiments, and so forth.
Regardless of the method employed, questions of sampling, measurement,
and analysis arise. While the discussion that follows may be review for
many readers, the crucial point is that, even so, many researchers
ostensibly do [not]{.italic} sufficiently attend to these issues. This
is especially true when it comes to designing and conducting
[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-180
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-392
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-488
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-902
.calibre1}experiments.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0010_split_002.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0010_split_002.html_A-sec-4 .sec}
::: {#part0010_split_002.html_B-sec-3 .sec}
## Sampling {#part0010_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .b}

[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-329
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-887 .calibre1}Most
propositions involve assertions about some type of group
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in the prior example, this might be
all Americans, or all media consumers.
[]{#part0010_split_002.html_page_18 .calibre1}Alternatively, one could
study countries with the intent to understand the conditions that lead
to war or trade agreements, or elected officials to identify when they
implement policies that align with public opinion, or international
negotiators with the goal to discover when they display greater
flexibility.

The first step involves identifying the population of interest
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, to whom does the hypothesis
apply? Identifying the population is a crucial, often missed step in
experiments (Westreich et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-511){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-511-back
.calibre1}: 439). As noted, the aforementioned hypothesis regarding
media exposure could concern all members of the US population, only
television viewers, or even only viewers of conservative media. Which
population one studies can dramatically change the conclusions one
reaches and should be determined by theory (i.e., step 2 in the
scientific process). It may be that US residents, on average, are moved
by a special interests campaign finance story; that regular television
viewers are less moved due to strong prior opinions developed from
watching different news (e.g., coverage that focuses on campaign finance
as a free speech issue); and that conservative viewers are not moved at
all, given a predilection to oppose government regulation. Researchers
need to not only specify the population prior to data collection, but
should also consider it at the theorizing stage
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and be explicit in doing so.

[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-210 .calibre1}If one collects
data from all members of the population, it constitutes a census; unless
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s population consists of a small
number of relatively accessible units (e.g., a class, a small set of
countries), obtaining a census quickly becomes infeasible given the
time, cost, and the reality of nonresponse. Instead, then, one draws a
sample [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and as Piazza
([2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-436){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-436-back
.calibre1}: 139) states, the idea of
[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-220 .calibre1}sampling
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}is really quite
remarkable.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} In essence, to make a
statement about the entire []{#part0010_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-255
.calibre1}population, a researcher need only collect data from a small
subset, a sample of the population. In the ideal, one collects a
probability sample where every unit (e.g., individual, country, elected
official, negotiator) in the population has a known and nonzero chance
of being in the sample. When this is done, in all likelihood, the sample
will reflect the population and even a small sample allows for
generalizable (causal) statements about the population.

For example, assume the population for the media example is all US
residents and the researcher obtains a random sample; this would require
not only identifying and locating those chosen at random but also
ensuring that they report their exposure to campaign finance media
coverage (that focuses on special interests) and their level of support
for campaign finance laws. These data may show, for example, that those
exposed are []{#part0010_split_002.html_page_19 .calibre1}10 percent
more likely to be supportive of campaign finance laws (compared to those
not exposed). That 10 percent effect constitutes a statistic one can use
to infer the population parameter (i.e., the actual effect of special
interests media exposure among all US residents). Doing so involves
basic statistics [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, engaging in
hypothesis testing where one computes the probability of no actual
relationship (i.e., the null hypothesis) in the population, given the
observed relationship in the sample. If that probability is low, which
often is taken to mean less than .05, one can reject the null hypothesis
of no relationship and conclude that exposure to media frames of special
interests correlates with the audience being more supportive of campaign
finance
laws.[^3^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-14){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-14-back
.calibre1} Importantly, one can arrive at a conclusion about these types
of relationships with fairly small samples (e.g., 120 individuals
surveyed). Even finding a moderate relationship in a relatively small
sample would allow one to conclude that the relationship likely exists
in the population (with some level of
confidence).[^4^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-15){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-15-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-777
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-891
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1079 .calibre1}In
practice, one can rarely, if ever, obtain a perfect probability sample,
given the challenge of identifying units and ensuring response
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for these reasons, a large literature
exists on sampling (e.g., Groves et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-237){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-237-back
.calibre1}; Beimer
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-45){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-45-back
.calibre1}; Blair and Blair
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-49){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-49-back
.calibre1}). One topic, to which I will later return, involves how to
use distinct weighting schemes with samples so that they better reflect
the population. For now, the key points for experimentalists are: (1)
explicitly specify the population; (2) consider how the sample reflects
