the population; and (3) remember the goal is to make an inference to the
population.[^5^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-16){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-16-back
.calibre1} A crucial question concerns whether a feature of the sample
leads to a distinct relationship from what one would find in the
population. For example, does a sample characteristic mean one would be
more or less likely to find a relationship between
[]{#part0010_split_002.html_page_20 .calibre1}special interest media
coverage and support for campaign finance law in the sample than in the
population? As I will discuss, in some cases, the nature of the sample
matters little for experimental (causal) inference, whereas in others it
fundamentally shapes the conclusions one can
draw.[^6^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-17){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-17-back
.calibre1} To see why the former case often holds
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} consider a scenario where one finds
special interests media coverage affects support for campaign finance
laws among a sample of students. That same causal relationship would
hold in the larger population of nonstudents unless some student
characteristic such as age means students react differently than
nonstudents to the []{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-778
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-892
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1080
.calibre1}coverage.

[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-889 .calibre1}Discussions of
sampling invariably focus on the units in the relevant study
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, the people or countries. This
emphasis may reflect the central role of sample surveys, often with
descriptive aspirations, in much of the social sciences. Here, ensuring
representative samples is crucial to making statements about
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}all
citizens["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}all
voters["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}all members of a given
group["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (e.g., Robison et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-452){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-452-back
.calibre1}). Yet, knowingly or not, scholars also sample the context,
topic, and measures (e.g., treatments/outcomes) (Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-466-back
.calibre1}: 23,
69[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}72).[^7^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-18){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-18-back
.calibre1} For example, one could collect data on the impact of media
exposure on campaign finance opinions during a time of national debate
on the issue (e.g., in 2002 when the McCain-Feingold campaign finance
law was passed, or 2009[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2010 when the
Supreme Court ruled on portions of the law), or during a time of little
relevant discussion. In the former context, one may not find an effect
because of widespread availability of countervailing messages, while in
the latter one may find a strong effect since this exposure may be the
only instance in which individuals receive relevant information. One
must always consider whether a finding generalizes to other contexts,
including different times, distinct locations (e.g., states,
[]{#part0010_split_002.html_page_21 .calibre1}countries), and varying
data collection situations (e.g., laboratory, survey, field) (Druckman
and []{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-890 .calibre1}Lupia
[2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-158){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-158-back-2
.calibre1}).[^8^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-19){#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-19-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-15
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-263
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-553
.calibre1}Analogously, for many studies, one chooses a topic, such as
campaign finance, with the goal of drawing inferences about
relationships across topics [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for
instance, about a range of public policy issues beyond the laws of
election funding. (Alternatively, one might wish to generalize across
negotiation topics, voting systems, conflict situations, etc.) The topic
choice can have notable consequences; indeed, Druckman and Leeper
([2012a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-153){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-153-back
.calibre1}) point out that many experiments on public opinion suggest
instability because they typically chose topics on which individuals do
not have strong standing beliefs (e.g., campaign finance, urban sprawl,
fictitious political candidates). Such issues contrast with the topics
that appear in many national polls (e.g., the economy, national defense,
partisan candidates with long histories) where opinions appear more
stable. Finally, a given study chooses particular constructs, such as
measures of media exposure and attitudes about campaign finance.
Different measures may lead to divergent findings. For example,
Ansolabehere et al.
([2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-14){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-14-back
.calibre1}) argue that including multiple measures for a given construct
(e.g., three questions about campaign finance) will lead to more
reliable findings.

In sum, sampling involves choosing not just units/individuals but also
contexts, topics, and measures. Most ignore the latter three for two
reasons. First, the populations of contexts, topics, and measures are
ill-defined (Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-466-back-2
.calibre1}: 23). Second, most studies have very small sample sizes on
these dimensions since data come from a single context at a given time
on a single (or few) topics with select measures. Thus, it may seem
infeasible to think about generalizing as it involves inferring to an
unknown population from a tiny sample. The practical reality that one
cannot run the same study with hundreds of distinct measures or in
hundreds of contexts does not mean researchers should ignore those
dimensions. Instead, they should thoughtfully consider their choices and
how they fit into the extant research program. In so doing, they can
also work to develop coordinated sets of experiments (Blair and
McClendon
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-52){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-52-back
.calibre1}) and rely on design principles from small-N research (e.g.,
Geering
[2001](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-205){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-205-back
.calibre1}: 206[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}225; Seawright
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-462){#part0010_split_002.html_REFe-r-462-back
.calibre1}: 76[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}106). Experimentalists
typically aim to make generalizable causal statements and in so doing,
they must reflect on how they chose their units, contexts, topics, and
measures. This will stimulate research programs that introduce
variations in context, topics, []{#part0010_split_002.html_page_22
.calibre1}and measures, while also still attending to common concerns
about having representative []{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-330
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-888
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-16
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-264
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-554 .calibre1}samples.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0010_split_003.html}

::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: {#part0010_split_003.html_A-sec-4 .sec}
:::::: {#part0010_split_003.html_B-sec-4 .sec}
## Measurement {#part0010_split_003.html_calibre_pb_3 .b}

[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-111
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-169
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-319
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-381
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-605
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-707
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1049 .calibre1}The
other crucial, often neglected, dimension in the design of an experiment
(or any type of study) is measurement. As I will discuss, most
experiments seek to measure the causal effect of a treatment (i.e., the
independent variable) on an outcome (i.e., the dependent variable).
Ensuring the validity of a treatment receives considerable attention and
I return to this challenge shortly. The measurement of outcomes and
variables that may condition an effect (e.g., partisanship might
condition the impact of campaign finance coverage on support for reform)
tend to get less attention.

In most cases, one cares about an abstract concept such as opposition or
support for a public policy. There is then an inductive leap from that
construct to the measure of that construct (e.g., the precise question
asking respondents about their support for campaign finance laws on a
1[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}7 scale ranging from strongly oppose
to strongly support). For many experiments, the measurement approach
involves using a survey that entails a systematic, standardized way to
collect information via questions (Wright and Mardsen
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-518){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-518-back
.calibre1}). This requires understanding how best to ask questions and
characterize an attitude, preference, emotion, or behavior with a
quantitative indicator (i.e., a number). Fortunately, there exist large
literatures on theories of survey response; how to write the most valid
and reliable questions; when to use open- versus closed-ended questions;
how many points to use on a scale; whether to label a scale; when and
how to ask sensitive questions, etc. (e.g., Krosnick and Presser 2010;
Vannette and Krosnick 2018). Generally, for any measure, be it on a
survey or not, researchers need to carefully consider validity and
accuracy.

Validity concerns the extent to which the measure/quantification
reflects the abstracted concept. For example, if a question asks
respondents to report whether they think candidates spend a lot of money
it would not be a particularly valid way to capture opposition or
support for a law limiting campaign spending. A measure that aims to
gauge political knowledge but asks about sports trivia also would be
problematic. These have poor ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}face
validity["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as they do not intuitively
correspond to the concept. Also relevant is content validity, which
refers to whether the measure covers the relevant aspects of the
concept; for instance, does a []{#part0010_split_003.html_page_23
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-5
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-167
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-245
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-597
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-837 .calibre1}measure
of political knowledge capture all dimensions of knowledge, including
that about institutions, processes, people, domestic politics, and
foreign affairs (Delli Carpini and Keeter
[1996](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-128){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-128-back
.calibre1}: 68)? Or, for campaign finance policy, does the measure gauge
attitudes about limits on both individual and organizational giving? In
addition to dimensionality, a measure should have high construct
validity in operationalizing the basic concept on a given dimension
(Messick
[1998](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-374){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-374-back
.calibre1}); for example, do political knowledge items about
institutions actually measure institutional knowledge? Asking if someone
has heard of the Supreme Court would have low construct validity as a
measure of knowledge about what the Supreme Court does. Operationally, a
measure with high construct validity also has convergent validity,
meaning it correlates with measures of analogous concepts (e.g.,
political knowledge and political interest), and discriminant validity,
implying no correlation to unrelated constructs (e.g., political
knowledge and food preferences) (Campbell and Fiske
[1959](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-89){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-89-back
.calibre1}). Finally, criterion validity means the measure predicts,
either concurrently or in the future, outcomes to which it theoretically
relates (e.g., more knowledge increases the likelihood of voting or
holding policy opinions that correlate with ideology; supporting
campaign finance reform means supporting candidates who do so, all else
constant). In [Table
2.1](#part0010_split_003.html_TN-tblw-1){#part0010_split_003.html_TN-tblw-1-back-2
.calibre1}, I offer a summary of these various types of measurement
validity.

::::: {#part0010_split_003.html_TN-tblw-1 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 2.1.]{.label2} [Measurement validity concepts]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00018.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

Measures not only need to be valid but also accurate, meaning reliable
and unbiased. Reliability entails arriving at the same value for the
same unit (e.g., individual) if the measure is taken repeatedly (and the
underlying construct remains unchanged). For example, presuming an
individual does not learn more about politics between two points in
time, a political knowledge measure exhibits reliability if that
individual exhibits the same measured level of knowledge at each point.
Failure to do so introduces measurement error that can skew results.
Fortunately, techniques to enhance reliability
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as using fully labeled scales and
the average from multiple measures for the same construct
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} increase reliability (e.g.,
Ansolabehere et al.
[2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-14){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-14-back-2
.calibre1}). A more perplexing challenge comes from measurement bias
that occurs when the measure systematically under- or overstates the
true value of the construct. Bias often occurs when it comes to items
that query sensitive behaviors (e.g., drug usage) or normatively
desirable actions (e.g., voting turnout). For example, the American
National Election Study regularly reports that approximately 75 percent
of the electorate votes in presidential elections, while actual turnout
rates are closer to 60 percent. The misestimation reflects measurement
bias. Approaches to reduce such bias exist (e.g., Tourangeau and
[]{#part0010_split_003.html_page_24 .calibre1}Yan
[2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-501){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-501-back
.calibre1}; Rosenfeld et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-454){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-454-back
.calibre1}), some of which rely on experimental methods such as list
experiments (e.g., Blair et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-51){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-51-back
.calibre1}).

The measurement issues discussed thus far do not solely apply to
[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-63
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-67
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-695
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-929
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1023
.calibre1}surveys. The increasing availability of behavioral data have
led experimentalists to use outcome measures such as validated voting,
social media searches, or campaign donations (e.g., Groves
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-236){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-236-back-2
.calibre1}; Peterson et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-433){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-433-back
.calibre1}). The same considerations apply; for example, do social media
posts validly reflect attitudes? Even administrative records that
ostensibly seem accurate can be ridden with errors; for example, Berent
et al.
([2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-38){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-38-back
.calibre1}) report that using administratively validated turnout figures
ends up being as []{#part0010_split_003.html_page_25
.calibre1}inaccurate as survey self-reports (even though the sources of
inaccuracy differ). Behavioral data also can skew
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sample in unintended ways
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for instance, using Twitter data to
capture political rhetoric in the United States would be highly
problematic since Twitter users tend to be ideologically extreme
relative to nonusers (e.g., Cohn and Quealy
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-101){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-101-back
.calibre1}). In short, with measurement, researchers need to carefully
consider issues of validity and accuracy. This practice often requires
piloting via focus groups and cognitive interviewing with individuals to
ensure they understand and view the measures as intended, or exploring
how administrative/behavioral data are collected (e.g., Beatty and
Willis
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-33){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-33-back
.calibre1}; Willis
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-515){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-515-back
.calibre1}).[^9^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-20){#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-20-back
.calibre1}

What does this challenge mean for experimentalists? The measurement
issues discussed certainly do not only apply to experiments. Yet, while
measurement concerns are commonplace in much observational research
(e.g., surveys), they usually receive less attention among
experimentalists. In many cases, experiments employ measures used in
prior work. As a general practice, that may not be problematic; however,
the stakes are quite high for experimentalists who typically go to great
lengths to design studies meant to document the causal factors that
explain a particular outcome. Failure to carefully consider the validity
and accuracy of the outcome could render the experiment useless.
Experimentalists should carefully specify the outcome of interest, how
it will be measured, and whether that approach has high validity and
accuracy. This practice might entail piloting, as discussed, and
educating oneself on measurement best practices in the given
[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-6
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-168
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-246
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-598
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-838
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-64
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-68
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-696
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-930
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1024
.calibre1}substantive area.

[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-17
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-647
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1075 .calibre1}To see
how such challenges can become complicated, consider Ansolabehere et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (1994) well-know and debated
experimental study showing that exposure to negative campaign
advertising depresses intention to vote by 2.5 percent relative to
seeing no
advertisement.[^10^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-21){#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-21-back
.calibre1} The experiment involved randomly exposing individuals to
seeing no political advertisement, an advertisement that was positive
for a candidate, or an []{#part0010_split_003.html_page_26
.calibre1}advertisement that was negative against the
candidate[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s opponent. It measured vote
intention by asking ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Looking forward to
the November election, do you intend to
vote?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (832). Alas, as mentioned, voter
turnout questions often contain considerable bias such that respondents
overstate the extent to which they vote, sometimes by more than 20
percent (e.g., Burden
[2000](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-73){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-73-back
.calibre1}). One solution involves normalizing the question to make
respondents feel less pressure to misreport an ostensibly normatively
preferred behavior, such as asking: ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}In
talking to people about elections, we often find that a lot of people
were not able to vote because they are not registered, are sick, or they
just don[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}t have time. How about you? Do
you plan to vote?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Such a question
generates less bias (e.g., Belli et al.
[2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-35){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-35-back
.calibre1}; DeBell et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-127){#part0010_split_003.html_REFe-r-127-back
.calibre1}). So, if one wants to build on
Ansolabehere[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s et al. study by
introducing other versions of negativity (e.g., adding incivility) or
conducting it at a different time, should one use the same outcome
measure as in the original experiment or a reworded less biased one? The
answer is not obvious; introducing a new measure means one can no longer
directly speak to the replicability of the Ansolabehere et al.
experiment since distinct results could stem from changes in the outcome
measure. This occurs if alterations in responses to the changed measure
correlate with reactions to the treatment (e.g., those who overstate
vote intention are more or less sensitive to the original treatment). In
most cases one would opt for what appears to be the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}best["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
measure, but that choice has implications for comparability across
experiments.[^11^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-22){#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-22-back
.calibre1} One needs to weigh these trade-offs and [make the case
explicitly]{.italic}, well before designing the precise experiment.
Experimentalists ought to attend to measurement: a point often neglected
[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-18
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-648
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1076
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-112
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-170
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-320
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-382
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-606
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-708
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1050 .calibre1}in
practice.[^12^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-23){#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-23-back
.calibre1}
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_004.html}

::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::: {#part0010_split_004.html_A-sec-4 .sec}
:::::::: {#part0010_split_004.html_B-sec-5 .sec}
## []{#part0010_split_004.html_page_27 .calibre1}Summary {#part0010_split_004.html_calibre_pb_4 .b}

In many ways, the discussion up to this point may read like a research
design textbook. Yet, these issues often evade precise discussion or
consideration in the design of experiments. Four takeaways include the
following:

::::::: {#part0010_split_004.html_NL-lst-1 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Experimentalists need to always put their work in the
larger context of the scientific process, which entails a substantial
amount of theoretical work before the design stage.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Counter to common practice, the population must be stated
explicitly.
:::

::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} Experimentalists need to recognize they are sampling not
only the units but also the context, topic, and measures.
:::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} Measurement warrants serious consideration and any
measure must be justified and defended in light of validity and accuracy
[]{#part0010_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-908 .calibre1}concerns.
:::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::
:::::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_005.html}

:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: {#part0010_split_005.html_A-sec-5 .sec}
# []{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-97 .calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-309 .calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-747 .calibre1}Causal Inference and Experiments {#part0010_split_005.html_calibre_pb_5 .a}

[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-25
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-89
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-415
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-655
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-703 .calibre1}The next
question, after resolving sampling and measurement issues, concerns
testing the causal hypothesis. The task of establishing that one
variable leads directly to changes in another is far from
straightforward and sits outside the realm of standard probability
theory (Pearl
[2000](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-428){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-428-back
.calibre1}: 134). Much of the social sciences, over the past two
decades, employs the Neyman-Rubin potential-outcome model (Neyman
[1923](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-410){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-410-back
.calibre1}; Rubin
[1974](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-457){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-457-back
.calibre1}; Imbens and Rubin
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-287){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-287-back
.calibre1}; Pearl and Mackenzie
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-429){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-429-back
.calibre1}: 269[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}280), the approach taken
here (for formalizations of the framework, see, e.g., Druckman et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-146){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-146-back-3
.calibre1}: chapter 2; Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-212-back
.calibre1}).

Recall the example of the special interests campaign finance story as a
cause of support for campaign finance law. Consider a study
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} with a representative sample and
strong measures [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that gauges exposure
and attitudes. Regardless of method (e.g., experimental or not), a first
step in establishing causation would be to reveal a correlation between
frame []{#part0010_split_005.html_page_28 .calibre1}exposure and reform
support; of course, such a correlation may be spurious since those who
support campaign finance laws may seek out stories consistent with that
attitude. A second step then involves establishing that exposure
preceded the formation of the attitude. Yet, time order does not address
the well-known lurking or confounding variable problem (i.e., a variable
that has an important effect on a relationship but is not included in
the analysis): for example, another variable such as Democratic
partisanship leads one to seek out such stories and be more supportive
of campaign finance laws. As is well known, association is not
equivalent to causation, even with an appropriate time sequence.

The ideal research design would be to take a given unit (e.g., person,
country) and assess the impact of a variable (e.g., exposure =
treatment; no exposure = control) at a single place and a single point
in time: [Outcome(treatment, unit) versus Outcome(control,
unit)]{.italic}. For example, compare the
individual[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
(unit[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s) campaign finance attitude
(outcome) when exposed to the story (treatment) against his or her
attitude when not exposed to the story (control). If the attitudes
differ, this difference constitutes a causal effect of the special
interests story. Alas, one cannot observe the same unit at the same time
and place but under different scenarios (i.e., exposure and not). Hence,
the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference: It
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}is impossible to observe the value of
\[the treatment outcome\] and \[the control outcome\] on the same unit
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the implicit threat of the
Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference is that causal inference is
impossible["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Holland
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-269-back
.calibre1}: 947).

[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-121
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-977
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1029 .calibre1}Holland
([1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-269-back-2
.calibre1}) outlines two solutions that, as will shortly become clear,
are largely experimental in nature. First, the scientific solution
entails observing a unit without treatment (e.g., no story) and
measuring its value of the dependent variable (e.g., policy attitude).
Then, at a later point in time, the same unit is exposed to the
treatment (e.g., the story) and its behavior (e.g., policy attitude) is
measured again. In using this technique, one must assume invariance:
that the unit and all other aspects of the situation do not change from
the time of the first (control) measurement to the time of treatment
application.[^13^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-24){#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-24-back
.calibre1} Put another way, one assumes [temporal stability]{.italic}
such that any prior exposure does not affect future exposure (and timing
sequence does not matter), and [causal transience]{.italic} such that
the measurement at one point in time does not affect measurement at a
later point in time (Holland
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-269-back-3
.calibre1}: 948). []{#part0010_split_005.html_page_29 .calibre1}These
conditions would allow one, for instance, to measure
people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s campaign finance attitudes at
one point in time, then expose them to the story and remeasure the
attitudes to see if the story mattered. The researcher assumes nothing
else happened over time to the individual or the context, and that the
prior measurement did not influence the second measurement. These tests,
then, typically involve within-unit or within-subject
[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-122
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-978
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1030
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-26
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-90
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-656
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-704
.calibre1}comparisons.

[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-21
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-108
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-144
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-240
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-283
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-284
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-302 .calibre1}Another
way to apply the scientific solution is to assume [unit
homogeneity]{.italic} [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that two
different units are equivalent in every (relevant) way (or that any
difference is controlled for) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and
expose only one of the units to the treatment. Thus,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}we may seek homogenous units across time
or across space["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (King et al.
[1994](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-314){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-314-back-2
.calibre1}: 93). For example, one may find identical individuals who
only differ in their exposure to the special interests campaign finance
story. Notably, a presumption of unit homogeneity envelopes the idea
that the units also did not self-select into treatment or control
conditions as that would make them
incomparable.[^14^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-25){#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-25-back
.calibre1}

The second solution to the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference is
the statistical approach that involves a focus on the average effects
across an entire sample, such that some units receive the treatment and
others the control. The crucial element entails random assignment of
units to one of these two conditions (Holland
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-269-back-4
.calibre1}: 948) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example, randomly
assigning units in the sample to receive or not receive the special
interests campaign finance story. By randomly assigning each unit, the
experimenter can confidently conclude that any differences between the
two groups, [on average]{.italic}, stem from exposure to the treatment.
This approach limits the causal interpretation to the treatment effect
or random sampling variability, where the latter can be quantified. Just
as with the scientific approach, the statistical approach entails
assumptions. In this case, one must assume [independence]{.italic}:
Assignment to the treatment or control is unaffected by any other
relevant variable that would impact the outcome of interest (e.g.,
partisanship does not determine exposure to the treatment story or
no-story control), including the outcome variable itself (e.g., campaign
finance attitude does not determine
[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-416 .calibre1}exposure).

[]{#part0010_split_005.html_page_30
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-38
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-252 .calibre1}The
benefits of random assignment result from an ability to assume on
average equivalency of the randomly assigned groups (e.g., same
percentage of women, same average income, same average ideology, etc.).
Thus, any average difference in the outcome after one group receives the
treatment likely reflects a direct effect of that stimulus, if the
relevant assumptions hold. It allows researchers to recover
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}sample average treatment
effect["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (SATE). Further, as I discuss
shortly, one can explore differences in subgroups, if there is not a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}constant
effect["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} across groups (e.g., different
partisans react differently to the treatment) (Holland
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-269-back-5
.calibre1}: 949); this would provide the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}conditional average treatment
effect["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (CATE). The statistical approach
contains so much power that Cook and Campbell
([1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-106-back-2
.calibre1}: 5) state, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Random assignment
is the great [ceteris
paribus]{.italic}[---]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}that is, other things
being equal[---]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}of causal
inference.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-86
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-274 .calibre1}Alas, two
other assumptions, beyond independence, underlie the statistical
approach (Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-212-back-2
.calibre1}: 39[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}44). First the [exclusion
restriction]{.italic} assumption means that outcomes vary as a function
of receiving the treatment [per se]{.italic} (e.g., and not due to
knowledge of being in a treatment group, or different
administrations/measures in the treatment versus control groups). Second
the [stable unit treatment value assumption]{.italic} (SUTVA) (or
noninterference assumption) means ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the
\[potential outcome\] observation on one unit should be unaffected by
the particular assignment of treatments to the other
units["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Cox
[1958](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-114){#part0010_split_005.html_REFe-r-114-back
.calibre1}, §2.4). For example, assignment to the campaign finance story
by one individual should not affect the policy preference of another
individual (e.g., which it could if they talked to one another about
it).[^15^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-26){#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-26-back
.calibre1}

In sum, the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference presents a serious
challenge for social scientists who hope to arrive at generalizable
causal inferences. There exist two approaches to making causal
inferences, although both require the satisfaction of assumptions. I
review those assumptions along with examples of violations, for each
approach, in [Table
2.2](#part0010_split_005.html_TN-tblw-2){#part0010_split_005.html_TN-tblw-2-back-2
.calibre1}. In thinking about experiments
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the topic to which I next turn
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one must always keep in mind the
fundamental causal inference problems and the assumptions one makes.

::::: {#part0010_split_005.html_TN-tblw-2 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-119 .calibre1}Table
2.2.]{.label2} [Assumptions underlying solutions to the fundamental
problem of causal inference]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0010_split_005.html_page_32
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-120
.calibre1}![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00019.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_006.html}

::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: {#part0010_split_006.html_A-sec-5 .sec}
:::::: {#part0010_split_006.html_B-sec-6 .sec}
## []{#part0010_split_006.html_page_31 .calibre1}Experiments {#part0010_split_006.html_calibre_pb_6 .b}

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-311
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-419
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-509
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-783
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-937
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-945
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-979 .calibre1}Perhaps
ironically, despite a shared focus on the potential outcomes framework,
scholars continue to disagree on what exactly constitutes a social
science
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
For example, some focus on random assignment (e.g., Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-212-back-3
.calibre1}: 8[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}17), while others attend
to researcher intervention and control (e.g., Morton and Williams
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-391){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-391-back
.calibre1}: 42). Here, I define experiments in terms of how one
addresses the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference: An experiment is
a study where an intervention provides the primary procedure by which a
researcher resolves the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference.

This inclusive definition contains two dimensions. First, an
[intervention]{.italic} refers to an event involving the researcher
influencing the subjects or a natural event that facilitates inference.
Second, there is a [procedure]{.italic} to address the Fundamental
Problem of Causal Inference: (1) the statistical solution that entails
satisfying the independence, excludability, and SUTVA assumptions; or
(2) the scientific solution that entails satisfying
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_33 .calibre1}the temporal stability and
causal transience assumptions, or the unit homogeneity
assumption.[^16^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-27){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-27-back
.calibre1} In [Table
2.3](#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3){#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3-back-2
.calibre1}, I present a depiction of variations in approach to
experimentation.[^17^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-28){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-28-back
.calibre1}

::::: {#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 2.3.]{.label2} [Experimental approaches]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00020.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-265
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-649
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-815 .calibre1}In many
disciplines, researcher intervention and random assignment constitute
the dominant approach (cell 1). Here, the researcher [randomly]{.italic}
assigns values of the independent variable, such as exposure to the
campaign finance story or not, and then measures the outcome, such as
campaign finance attitudes (e.g., Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-466-back-3
.calibre1}: 12; Morton and Williams
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-390){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-390-back
.calibre1}: 341, 2010: 47). For example, Druckman and Nelson
([2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-160){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-160-back
.calibre1}) randomly assigned respondents to read a news story depicting
campaign finance laws as limits on special interest influence or to read
no story. (Others randomly were selected to read a story that discussed
campaign finance as a free speech issue.) They then had respondents
complete a survey that measured their campaign finance attitudes,
finding that exposure to the special interests story increased support
for the laws. In other words, the average level of support in the story
group exceeded that in the control. When possible, this approach offers
a straightforward approach since the researcher simply compares the
average outcome values between the treatment and control. As discussed,
it requires that the independence, excludability, and SUTVA assumptions
are
satisfied.[^18^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-29){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-29-back
.calibre1} Moreover, other problems can arise, such as noncompliance,
where []{#part0010_split_006.html_page_34 .calibre1}subjects do not
receive a treatment to which they were assigned (i.e., they ignore the
story), and, attrition, where subjects who received a treatment never
respond to the outcome measure (i.e., those exposed decide not to
complete the survey) (Gerber and
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-266
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-650
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-816 .calibre1}Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-212-back-4
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-123
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-403
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-505
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-517
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-621
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-635
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-869
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-981
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1027
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1035 .calibre1}The
researcher intervention scientific solution (cell 2) brings with it the
challenge of either observing the same unit over time and thus
satisfying the assumptions that prior exposures and/or measurements do
not matter (i.e., temporal stability and causal transience assumptions),
or comparing non-randomly assigned units that are presumed to be
identical in every way but for treatment
assignment.[^19^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-30){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-30-back
.calibre1} The former method would include some within-subject
experiments where individuals experience multiple or all of the
treatments with their behaviors being compared across exposures (see
Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-212-back-5
.calibre1}: 273[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}276). For example, Mutz
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-397){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-397-back
.calibre1}) uses the approach to study the impact of uncivil discourse
on physiological arousal; she has each experimental participant watch
four different political debates that vary the civility of the exchange
and the camera angle focus (i.e., whether it was a close-up). That is,
every participant watches a video from each of four possible conditions.
Importantly, to address the possibility of temporal instability, she
counterbalanced (i.e., randomly ordered exposure to the video from each
condition) and hence could control for the order of condition
exposure.[^20^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-31){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-31-back
.calibre1} She finds that uncivil discourse significantly arouses
subjects relative to civil discourse, and close-ups do the same
(relative to angles further away). That the measures of arousal involved
skin conductance tests beyond
respondents[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} conscious control
facilitated this approach (for more on within-subject designs, see
Alferes
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-2){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-2-back
.calibre1}; Clifford et al. 2021; Mutz
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-399-back
.calibre1}).

Another and perhaps more common approach in the researcher intervention
scientific solution experiment (cell 2) involves addressing the unit
homogeneity assumption by using financial incentives. Many economic
experiments do this by employing induced value theory that makes
respondents comparable sans the independent variable
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_35 .calibre1}(Smith
[1976](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-480){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-480-back
.calibre1},
[1982](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-481){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-481-back
.calibre1}).[^21^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-32){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-32-back
.calibre1} Here, the experiment induces prespecified characteristics in
participants so that their ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}innate
characteristics become largely
irrelevant["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Friedman and Sunder
[1994](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-196){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-196-back
.calibre1}: 13) by offering an award medium (i.e., money) with: (1)
monotonicity: Subjects prefer more reward than less and do not become
satiated; (2) salience: Rewards for subjects depend on their actions in
the study as defined by a set of rules in a way that more reward is
received for a ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good
outcome["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}; (3) dominance: Changes in
subjects[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} utilities come from the reward
with other influences being negligible; and (4) privacy: All subjects
only learn about their own payoffs (Friedman and Sunder
[1994](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-196){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-196-back-2
.calibre1}: 12; []{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-124
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-404
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-506
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-518
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-622
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-636
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-870
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-982
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1028
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1036 .calibre1}Guala
[2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-238){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-238-back
.calibre1}:
232[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}236).[^22^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-33){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-33-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-463
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-789
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1059 .calibre1}The
approach assumes that payments contingent on
subjects[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} decisions make any two subjects
equivalent, except for alterations in the payment situation, or (1) some
other factor that the researcher causes to vary or (2) knows to
vary.[^23^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-34){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-34-back
.calibre1} An example of the researcher intentionally causing variation
comes from Habyarimana et. al. (2007), who explore public goods
provision in ethnically heterogeneous settings (in Uganda). They do so
by providing participants with money roughly equal to their per capita
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_36 .calibre1}daily income; participants
then choose to divide it among themselves and two other individuals who
varied in their ethnicities. They find that subjects do not allocate
more to members of their own ethnic group than to members of other
ethnic groups, except when the game is played without anonymity (i.e.,
their decisions were public). Here allocators give significantly more to
co-ethnics, revealing the power of social norms and social sanctions.
The validity of Habyarimana et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
causal results [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} regarding the power of
social sanctioning [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} rests on the
assumption that subjects are virtually identical to one another and
themselves each time they play the game because the payoff structure
stays constant but anonymity does
not.[^24^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-35){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-35-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-107
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-135 .calibre1}Examples
where the causal factor is one that the researcher
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}knows to
vary["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} come from studies that use economic
games to look at behavior across contexts. For instance, Henrich et al.
([2005](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-262){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-262-back
.calibre1}) compare individual behavior across fifteen different
societies in one-shot ultimatum games: where an individual receives an
endowment of money and offers an amount to a responder, who either
accepts the amount or rejects it, in which case neither player receives
money. They find notable variation in self-interest across societies
driven largely by degree of societal market integration (with
integration leading to more pro-social behavior/cooperation). Here, the
payment structure makes participants equivalent, allowing one to
attribute differences in behaviors to variations in the economic and
cultural settings (also see Cárdenas and Carpenter
[2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-93){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-93-back
.calibre1}; Eckel and []{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-464
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-790
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1060 .calibre1}Candelo
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-170){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-170-back
.calibre1}).[^25^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-36){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-36-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-477
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-489
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-503
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-513
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-813
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-821
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-829
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-983
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1003
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1067 .calibre1}The
other two cells in [Table
2.3](#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3){#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3-back-3
.calibre1} include studies where an intervention facilitates causal
inference, but it does not stem from actions taken by the researcher. In
the case of a ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}random lottery natural
experiment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (cell 3), a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
world["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} event generates the random
allocation of units (individuals) into treatment and control groups
(hence the statistical
solution).[^26^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-37){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-37-back
.calibre1} []{#part0010_split_006.html_page_37 .calibre1}One example
comes from Titiunik
([2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-498){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-498-back
.calibre1}), who relies on an intervention where some states (three in
her case), after reapportionment, randomly assign state senators to
serve either two-year or four-year terms. They thus, on average, are
equivalent to one another within a given state (i.e., the two-year
condition constituted the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}control["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and four-year condition the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}treatment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}).[^27^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-38){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-38-back
.calibre1} She finds that the shorter terms lead senators to campaign
more aggressively (via spending) and do less legislatively (e.g.,
introduce fewer bills). Thus, short terms do not improve legislative
performance. Another example is Ho and
Imai[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-266){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-266-back
.calibre1}) study that capitalizes on the random order in which
California ballots list candidates. They show that, in general
elections, minor party candidates gain .2 to .6 percentage points from
being listed first on the ballot, while in primaries, partisan
candidates gain 1[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}3 percentage points
(with minor candidates sometimes doubling their vote
share).[^28^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-39){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-39-back
.calibre1} The dynamic reflects the use of a cognitive shortcut where
voters opt for the first candidate that seems acceptable to avoid the
processing costs of analyzing them all. These types of random lottery
natural experiments allow researchers to employ the statistical solution
to the problem of causal inference, thanks to serendipitous random
interventions over which they do not have
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-478
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-490
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-504
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-514
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-814
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-822
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-830
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-984
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1004
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1068 .calibre1}direct
control.

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-461
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-519
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-613
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-641
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1039 .calibre1}The
final cell (cell 4) in the table likewise includes cases where the
researcher does not directly impact the units. In this case, the natural
intervention falls short of being strictly random, but nevertheless
works in such a way that allows the researcher to make a strong case for
unit homogeneity à la the scientific solution. Many refer to this method
as ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
randomization; however, the inference relies on unit homogeneity and
hence I refer to it as such. For instance, Meredith
([2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-373){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-373-back
.calibre1}) explores the impact of past voting eligibility (and possibly
actually voting) on habitual voting. He does so by identifying a natural
intervention of the eligibility to vote date. For example, people born
on November 7, 1982 were eligible to vote in the 2000 presidential
election, while people born on November 8,
[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_38 .calibre1}1982 were not. He then
compares the over-time voting behavior of those born just before the
cutoff to the behavior of those born just after. He finds that past
eligibility in presidential election years significantly increases the
likelihood that a person will vote well into the future. Moreover, in
2000, eligibility increased the probability of people registering as
Democrats rather than Independents. This constitutes a strong causal
demonstration of early political experiences on subsequent behaviors.
The key to the inference involves establishing that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}there are no other differences between
individuals born pre- and post-election-week that may affect their
subsequent participation["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Meredith
[2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-373){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-373-back-2
.calibre1}: 192[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}195). Meredith confirms
this unit homogeneity assumption by comparing voting behaviors of people
born around those dates in nonelection years.

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-6
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-83
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-140
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-151
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-303 .calibre1}Another
example is Hyde[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-280){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-280-back
.calibre1}) study of the impact of international election observers on
Election Day fraud in the 2003 Armenian presidential election (measured
by whether there was a reduction in the vote share of the incumbent who
was expected to commit fraud). Hyde compares polling stations with
observers against those without, arguing that they
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}were assigned to polling stations on
election day using a method that I did not supervise but that comes very
close to random assignment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (46). This
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
randomization approaches the statistical solution, but ultimately it
falls on Hyde to document unit homogeneity, again enveloping the
independence assumption. She (48) states, since
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the validity of this natural experiment
rests upon this point \[i.e.,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
random assignment and, in essence, comparability across groups\], I will
take some time to support it.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} She does
so by detailing the arbitrary selection of polling places to the
treatment, explaining the process by which observers traveled to polling
stations, and arguing that stations per se, given the demographics and
partisanship of the country, are unlikely to be predictable in terms of
voting patterns. She further points out the authorities did not
preannounce the monitoring locations, so candidates could not anticipate
locations and react
accordingly.[^29^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-40){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-40-back
.calibre1} In short, Hyde goes to considerable length to establish the
plausibility of unit homogeneity. While she appeals to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}close to
random["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} assignment (50), the inference
rests on her case for unit comparability à la the scientific solution of
causal inference. With this method, she finds that the observers have a
notable effect, []{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-462
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-520
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-614
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-642
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-1040
.calibre1}reducing fraud.

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_39
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-7
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-76
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-141
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-180
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-191
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-260
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-264
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-296
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-304 .calibre1}The unit
homogeneity natural experimental type stands out relative to other
discussions of experiments that either limit the definition to strict
random assignment (e.g., Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-212-back-6
.calibre1}: 15[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}16) or group random
assignment natural experiments with those that purportedly come
close.[^30^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-41){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-41-back
.calibre1} I use my classification, à la [Table
2.3](#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3){#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3-back-4
.calibre1}, for two reasons. First, in many of these designs, without
strict random assignment, the intervention does much of the work that
allows for causal inference, at least relative to statistical fixes.
Accounting for this leverage allows for an inclusive definition of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Second, when the assignment lacks clear randomness, the burden falls on
the researcher to establish unit homogeneity and in that sense it
entails taking a scientific
approach.[^31^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-42){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-42-back
.calibre1} As Dunning
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-166){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-166-back
.calibre1}: 28) notes in these cases,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the onus is
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} on the researcher to make a very
compelling case["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to treat it as an
experiment. He (2012: 239) details that such a case often requires
demonstrating that the comparison groups are balanced or homogenous; in
essence, unit
homogeneity.[^32^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-43){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-43-back
.calibre1} To be clear, though, I acknowledge that in some cases the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
randomization can be quite compelling [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
but, even there, from a technical perspective, one cannot fully rely on
the statistical solution and must instead appeal to homogeneity (e.g.,
Titiunik
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-499){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-499-back
.calibre1}).[^33^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-44){#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-44-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_006.html_page_40
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDXloc-tgt-233 .calibre1}As an
aside, I have avoided using the term
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}quasi-experiment.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Many define a quasi-experimental study as one with an intervention
without random assignment (Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-466-back-4
.calibre1}: 12). In that sense, the scientific solutions in my
characterization could be considered quasi-experiments. I largely avoid
the term, other than a brief discussion in [Chapter
4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0010_split_006.html_CN-bp-4-back-4
.calibre1}, since it seems to downgrade controlled studies where causal
inference can be strong; moreover, scholars do not consistently use the
term in the social sciences (Morton and Williams
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-391){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-391-back-2
.calibre1}: 25), sometimes using it to refer to studies where
statistical controls dwarf the intervention in terms of making a causal
inference (Dunning []{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-312
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-420
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-510
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-784
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-946
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-980
.calibre1}[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-166){#part0010_split_006.html_REFe-r-166-back-2
.calibre1}: 19).
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_007.html}

::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::: {#part0010_split_007.html_A-sec-5 .sec}
:::::::::::: {#part0010_split_007.html_B-sec-7 .sec}
## Summary {#part0010_split_007.html_calibre_pb_7 .b}

[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-107
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-515
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-947 .calibre1}What
makes an experiment unique relative to nonexperimental work, according
to my depiction, is that the intervention plays a more salient role in
making a [causal]{.italic} inference, relative to statistical
adjustments (e.g., matching) or intensive qualitative
exploration.[^34^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-45){#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-45-back
.calibre1} That said, in many cases, experiments require some
statistical inspection, particularly when unit homogeneity needs to be
established. Further, there exist
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}slippery["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
cases of studies where the importance of the intervention relative to
statistical adjustments remains unclear; this complication accentuates
the reality that the label
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
while useful, need not be definitive. This is the downside of my
definition, but the upside includes not only being inclusive of how
social scientists discuss experiments, but, more importantly,
highlighting four often neglected lessons that should guide experimental
design:

::::::::::: {#part0010_split_007.html_NL-lst-2 .list3}
::::::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Every experiment entails making untestable assumptions
that allow one to resolve the Fundamental Problem of Casual Inference.

:::::: {#part0010_split_007.html_NL-lst-3 .list4}
::: list-item
[[]{#part0010_split_007.html_page_41 .calibre1}a]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-103
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-197
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-429 .calibre1}For the
statistical, random assignment approach, this involves independence,
excludability, and
SUTVA.[^35^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-46){#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-46-back
.calibre1}
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} For the scientific approach, this involves temporal
stability and causal transience or unit homogeneity.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} The onus is on the researcher to justify the assumptions.
For this reason, many view random assignment as a stronger approach, as
the assumptions ostensibly can be met with more confidence. For
instance, random assignment virtually ensures the satisfaction of
independence, whereas unit homogeneity requires evidence that units in
distinct
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}conditions["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
do not differ in a way that could impact the causal
effect.[^36^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-47){#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-47-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::::
:::::::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} An experiment always involves an intervention that comes
from the researcher or a naturally occurring event; the intervention
either assigns values to units or introduces some type of control that
facilitates comparison within or across units.
:::

::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} Every experiment entails counterfactual thinking such
that a comparison is being made for one value of an outcome (treatment)
against another value (control). Experimentalists must carefully define
what values they will compare.
:::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} One hopes, when conducting an experiment, to generalize
the causal relationship being tested (e.g., generalize across samples,
contexts, treatments, and outcome measures).
:::
:::::::::::

The latter two points warrant discussion as they appear obvious in
theory, but researchers typically ignore them in practice. The
counterfactual thinking point pertains to all causal statements
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}\[e\]ffects of causes are always
relative to other causes (i.e., it takes two causes to define an
effect["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Holland
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_007.html_REFe-r-269-back-6
.calibre1}: 959; also see Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_007.html_REFe-r-466-back-5
.calibre1}: 5). This means any conjectured hypotheses must carefully
specify the comparisons, and this often involves difficult choices
(e.g., Sekhon and Titiunik
[2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-463){#part0010_split_007.html_REFe-r-463-back
.calibre1}; Sniderman
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-483){#part0010_split_007.html_REFe-r-483-back
.calibre1}: 261[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}262). For example, in
the campaign finance experiment, is it relevant to compare exposure to
the special interest framing to a situation with no message or a message
that focuses on campaign spending as a free speech issue? Researchers
need to []{#part0010_split_007.html_page_42 .calibre1}explicitly specify
the comparison point before doing an experiment (e.g., they should not
hypothesize that the special interests story will increase support
without saying relative to what).

Generalizing the causal relationship often (but not always) means trying
to generalize the existence of the relationship. This task differs from
descriptive inferences such as trying to characterize the demographic
and/or political features of a population. As I will discuss later, in
the case of causal generalization, the crucial questions concern whether
features of the sample, context, treatment, or outcome measures moderate
the causal effect. In Popperian terms, one aims to continually test the
causal proposition until rejecting it and then either abandoning it or
revising it; in the ideal, one pits alternative theories against one
another, but in the practice of the social sciences, one relies on
multiple tests and typically revises the theory accordingly (Cook and
[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-104
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-198
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-430
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-938
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-108
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-516
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-948
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-98
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-310
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-748 .calibre1}Campbell
[1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0010_split_007.html_REFe-r-106-back-3
.calibre1}: 25[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}32).
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_008.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0010_split_008.html_A-sec-6 .sec}
# Experimental Types and Goals {#part0010_split_008.html_calibre_pb_8 .a}

Beyond variations in experimental approaches to
[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-25 .calibre1}causal inference,
there also exists a diversity of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}types["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}goals.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
In terms of the former, most distinguish three types of experiments
based on where the []{#part0010_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-105
.calibre1}intervention occurs (Druckman et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-146){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-146-back-4
.calibre1}:
6[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}7).[^37^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-48){#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-48-back
.calibre1} First are []{#part0010_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-166
.calibre1}laboratory experiments, where the intervention (e.g.,
treatment, payment structure) occurs in a controlled setting: This could
involve participants coming to the
researcher[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s laboratory or the researcher
traveling to the participants to collect data in their town, such as at
a community center (often called lab-in-the-field studies). Second,
[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-285 .calibre1}survey experiments
occur when the []{#part0010_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-155
.calibre1}intervention comes as part of the survey; for example, an
experiment where some subjects receive a particular wording of a
question (e.g., ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Do you support
assistance to the poor?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}) while others
receive a different wording (e.g., ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Do
you support welfare?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}). The surveys could
be in-person, over the phone, or via the
web.[^38^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-49){#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-49-back
.calibre1} Third, participants in field experiments receive the
treatment in naturalistic settings, as part of their daily lives,
[]{#part0010_split_008.html_page_43 .calibre1}typically without
knowledge that it is an
intervention.[^39^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-50){#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-50-back
.calibre1} The natural experiments, highlighted in the second row of
[Table
2.3](#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3){#part0010_split_008.html_TN-tblw-3-back-5
.calibre1}, nearly always occur in field settings (e.g., Gerber and
Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-212-back-7
.calibre1}: 16), although in theory one could imagine a scenario where
one occurs in a laboratory setting (e.g., an unexpected disruption in a
controlled setting [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as a fire
alarm going off [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} might be studied in
terms of its effects on performance).

[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-443
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-453
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-617
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-865
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-911
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-993
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-1089 .calibre1}Roth
([1995](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-456){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-456-back
.calibre1}: 22) identifies three nonexclusive roles
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} regardless of their type
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that experiments can play. First, Roth
describes ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}searching for
facts,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} where the goal involves isolating
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the cause of some observed regularity,
by varying details of the way the experiments were conducted. Such
experiments are part of the dialogue that experimenters carry on with
one another.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} These types of experiments
often complement other research methods and perhaps constitute the modal
approach in much of the social sciences (other than perhaps economics)
where researchers test expectations derived from inductive
reasoning/logic and build research agendas on a given topic (e.g., how
media messages affect public opinion; how microfinance programs
influence well-being). A second role entails
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}speaking to
theorists,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} where the goal is
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}to test the predictions \[or the
assumptions\] of well-articulated formal theories
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Such experiments are intended to feed
back into the theoretical literature [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
i.e., they are part of a dialogue between experimenters and
theorists.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} These experiments tend to
test predictions from deductive formal models with precise expectations;
much of this work tests game theoretic predictions on topics such as
collective action, electoral systems, and coalition formation. The third
role involves ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whispering in the ears of
princes,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which facilitates
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the dialogue between experimenters and
policymakers [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} \[The\] experimental
environment is designed to resemble closely, in certain respects, the
naturally occurring environment that is the focus of interest for the
policy purposes at hand.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Examples of
experiments with this goal would be studies to test the effect of a
campaign[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s message on voting behavior or
attitudes, or a curriculum intervention in a school. As I will discuss
later, these types of experiments differ from others insofar as the
precise size of the intervention[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s impact
often matters more since those making policy (which includes government
officials or leaders of nongovernmental organizations) need to make
cost-benefit calculations about implementation; policy-oriented
[]{#part0010_split_008.html_page_44 .calibre1}studies also face unique
challenges regarding scaling up interventions for implementation (e.g.,
Al-Ubaydli et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-5){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-5-back
.calibre1},
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-6){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-6-back
.calibre1},
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-4){#part0010_split_008.html_REFe-r-4-back
.calibre1}).
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0010_split_009.html}

:::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: {#part0010_split_009.html_A-sec-6 .sec}
::::::: {#part0010_split_009.html_B-sec-8 .sec}
## Summary {#part0010_split_009.html_calibre_pb_9 .b}

The goal of the experiment fundamentally affects how one interprets the
results, as follows:

:::::: {#part0010_split_009.html_NL-lst-4 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} When ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}searching for
facts,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one hopes to identify and
generalize a causal effect, but that precise size of the causal effect
from a particular (single) study (e.g. how much does the special
interest story change campaign finance policy support relative to no
story [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} 2%, 10%, 20%?), often is less
important. The size may depend on context, timing, and
operationalizations that can be explored in subsequent studies. The size
of effects is best explored across experiments to see if distinct
circumstances vitiate or exacerbate (i.e., moderate) the size (I discuss
this in more detail in [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0010_split_009.html_CN-bp-5-back-6
.calibre1}).
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} When ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}testing
theories,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the precise size of the causal
effect also is usually not crucial. Of particular relevance is that the
experimental design closely matches the parameters of the theory.
:::

::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} When ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whispering in the
ears of princes,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the precise effect size
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, the magnitude of the
experimental treatment effect [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} often
matters, as those making policies make investment decisions based
[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-444
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-454
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-618
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-866
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-912
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-994
