.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1090 .calibre1}on the
impact.
:::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_010.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0010_split_010.html_A-sec-7 .sec}
# []{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-13 .calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-307 .calibre1}Experimental Analysis {#part0010_split_010.html_calibre_pb_10 .a}

The analysis of an experiment ideally involves simple comparisons of the
outcome measure(s) (e.g., average support for campaign finance laws,
amount of donations to co-ethnics), across relevant variations in the
treatment or independent variable (e.g., exposure to a special interests
story or not, anonymity of donations or not). If one finds differences
between the outcomes in the conditions such that the probability of
those occurring by chance are very low [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
that is, statistically significantly different
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it constitutes evidence consistent
with the causal hypothesis (such as a special interests message causing
a change in policy
opinion).[^40^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-51){#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-51-back
.calibre1} Analyses become more complex with even the partial violation
of one of the aforementioned assumptions, or due to noncompliance or
attrition. []{#part0010_split_010.html_page_45 .calibre1}Gerber and
[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-22
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-62
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-77
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-120
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-128
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-170
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-178
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-212
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-253
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-279 .calibre1}Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-212-back-8
.calibre1}) offer a superb discussion of these issues (also see Dunning
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-166){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-166-back-3
.calibre1}: 105[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}207). Here I offer a few
points on analyses relevant to design and sampling issues.

In many cases, an experimentalist wants to not just document a causal
relationship but also isolate the mechanism(s) behind a causal effect.
This involves mediation analysis, which refers to identifying the
pathways through which the treatment influences the outcome. For
example, the special interests campaign finance message may cause one to
worry more about special interests corrupting government that in turn
leads to increased support for strict campaign finance laws. The
increased
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}worry["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
about special interests serves as the mediator, or the psychological
process through which the message generates policy support. Alas,
identifying mediational processes with most experiments proves
exceptionally difficult [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a point that
has become widely recognized in the last decade. Gerber and Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-212-back-9
.calibre1}: 321) explain that it is
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}difficult
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to extract reliable inferences about
mediation from experimental data \[and\]
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} rare
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to encounter a convincing
demonstration in the social sciences.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} A
common approach entails regressing the outcome variable on a variable
indicating the treatment condition (e.g., from random assignment) along
with possible mediators (Baron and Kenny
[1986](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-29){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-29-back
.calibre1}). This method falls vulnerable, however, to omitted variable
bias or even reverse causation (e.g., participants rationalize their
opinions on campaign finance by elevating their worry about special
interests) (Bullock and Ha
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-72){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-72-back
.calibre1}).

There is a sizeable literature on statistical approaches (e.g., Imai et
al.
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-284){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-284-back
.calibre1},
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-285){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-285-back
.calibre1}; Imai and Yamamoto
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-286){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-286-back
.calibre1}; also see Glynn
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-220){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-220-back
.calibre1}, for an overview) and experimental design-based approaches
for those hoping to isolate mediation (e.g., Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-212-back-10
.calibre1}: 333[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}336; Bullock and Green
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-71){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-71-back
.calibre1}). Doing so means making explicit design choices so that
mediation becomes a primary purpose of the design. For guidance on such
designs, see Pirlott and MacKinnon
([2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-437){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-437-back
.calibre1}), and for general advice on when it is justifiable to proceed
with a mediation analysis, see VanderWeele
([2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-506){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-506-back
.calibre1}) and Glynn
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-220){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-220-back-2
.calibre1}). That said, researchers often neglect that while they may
not be able to establish mediation, they can often rule it out. One can
be confident that if a treatment does not have an effect on a potential
mediator variable (either directly or moderated, which I next discuss),
then that variable is an unlikely mediator. Put another way, rejecting
potential mediators entails much less than corroborating
mediators.[^41^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-52){#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-52-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_010.html_page_46
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-69
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-179 .calibre1}The
importance of identifying mediators depends on the nature of a given
project. For example, many scholars seek to explain the partisan
polarization on climate change in the United States
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Democrats believe in and hope to
address human-induced climate change whereas Republicans typically
express much more skepticism, even in the face of large amounts of
information. One explanation is that individuals engage in directional
motivated reasoning where they reject new information that contradicts
their standing beliefs: Republicans dismiss evidence for climate change
to protect what they already believe. Another explanation is that people
seek to hold accurate views and Republicans simply distrust scientists
and follow the advice of their party leaders who often question climate
change and related policies. Here the mediational process matters
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} when an experiment shows Republicans
do not alter their climate opinions when exposed to evidence for climate
change, is the mediational process one that involves motivated reasoning
or the assessment of the sources? Answering that question has
implications for effective messaging (e.g., try to change motivations or
find distinct sources of information) (Druckman and McGrath
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-159){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-159-back
.calibre1}), and thus, experimentalists have sought to explore mediation
by directly manipulating the potential mediators (i.e., motivations)
(e.g., Bayes et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-31){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-31-back
.calibre1}). Yet, in other situations, mediation matters less, such as
when it does not directly follow from a theory. For instance, the
mediational processes underlying effects in experiments on voter
mobilization may be intriguing, but the ultimate goal involves
pinpointing what messages stimulate turnout. In short, whether one
should invest in designing experiments to identify mediators depends on
the theory being tested (e.g., is the mediator crucial?) and the goal of
the experiment (e.g., is it to test psychological mechanisms or to show
the general impact of an intervention?).

[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-321
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-569
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-619
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-745
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-1019
.calibre1}Different from mediation is moderation. Moderation refers to a
lack of uniformity of treatment effects. For example, it might be that
Democrats, who tend to be less averse to government regulation, are
persuaded by the special interests campaign message and thus the message
affects (increases) their support for campaign finance laws. Yet,
Republicans with their proclivity to oppose regulation remain unmoved by
the message. In this case, partisanship moderates the treatment effects,
or, put []{#part0010_split_010.html_page_47 .calibre1}differently, there
exist heterogeneous treatment effects based on partisanship. Another
example is Malhotra and Popp[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-364){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-364-back
.calibre1}) study of reactions to messages about the likelihood of
future terrorist attacks in the United States. They report an effect
concentrated among Democrats who initially feared such an attack. Other
examples include when a pharmaceutical drug exhibits a stronger effect
on children than adults; political messages that have differential
effects based on respondents[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} gender or
race; and educational interventions that depend on
students[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} socioeconomic statuses.

Experimental analysts often look first at the effects of treatments in
the []{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-77
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-141
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-279
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-671
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-871
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-949
.calibre1}aggregate [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, the
sample average treatment effect (SATE) across the entire sample. Then,
in cases where the researcher expects that variation between a treatment
variable and an outcome variable may differ based on some third variable
(such as partisanship, age, race, or socioeconomic status), he or she
estimates the treatment effects by subgroup (i.e., conditional average
treatment effect (CATE) or the average treatment effect for a defined
subset of units) (Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-212-back-11
.calibre1}). The analysis appears ostensibly straightforward as it
involves partitioning the sample into subgroups or conducting
regressions where one regresses the outcome on the treatment interacted
with the relevant indicator/measure for the relevant subgroups. That
said, complications arise when the researcher lacks a theoretical basis
for looking for moderation, as some interaction is bound to be
significant if one looks at enough (without corrections for multiple
comparisons). Generally speaking, researchers might be in one of two
situations: They have a clear a priori expectation of a heterogeneous
effect based on a precise subgroup (e.g., partisanship) and they
directly test for it, or they do not, in which case they might explore
many potential subgroups. In the latter cases, advances in machine
learning offer opportunities to soundly identify heterogeneous effects
(see Green and Kern
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-228){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-228-back
.calibre1}; Egami and Imai
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-172){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-172-back
.calibre1}; Ratkovic and Tingley
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-450){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-450-back
.calibre1}; Ratokvic
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-449){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-449-back
.calibre1}).[^42^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-53){#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-53-back
.calibre1}

Two final points on moderation concern
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}statistical
power["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}blocking.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Power concerns the probability of failing to reject the null hypothesis
in the presence of a real effect (a Type II error)
[]{#part0010_split_010.html_page_48 .calibre1}(see Glennerster and
Takavarasha
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-219){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-219-back
.calibre1}: 241[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}297). For example, in
the population, the reality could be that the special interests story
affects opinions on campaign finance laws, yet in a given experiment
(putting the nature of the sample [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} if
it is representative [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and measurement
issues aside), there exists a chance that one will not find the
relationship. Statistical power captures the chance of this [not
happening]{.italic} or, put another way, it refers to the probability of
correctly rejecting a (false) null hypothesis. The example here would be
properly rejecting the hypothesis of no relationship between the special
interests message and campaign finance policy support. Statistical power
is desirable, and just how much power one has in a given experiment
depends on the sample size, anticipated effect size (e.g., does the
message have a small, medium, or large effect on policy support?), and
the statistical significance level one will employ (e.g., .01, .05, or
.10). Larger effect sizes and sample sizes increase statistical power,
while more stringent significance thresholds reduce it. Many computing
programs allow experimentalists to identify the needed sample size in
light of anticipated effect size and significance
level.[^43^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-54){#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-54-back
.calibre1}

In assessing the amount of power one needs, an initial question concerns
the importance of knowing a precise effect size versus knowing whether
one has evidence consistent with a theory, regardless of the size. As I
have mentioned, the effect size matters more for policy-oriented
experiments and in such cases, more power becomes essential to increase
precision (Glennerster and Takavarasha 2013: 262). Another consideration
concerns whether one anticipates heterogeneous treatment effects since
that requires distinct power calculations (e.g., Perugini et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-431){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-431-back
.calibre1}; Kenny and Judd
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-309){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-309-back
.calibre1}); once one considers CATEs, statistical power dwindles and
larger sample sizes become necessary (Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-212-back-12
.calibre1}: 312). The bottom line though is, regardless, one needs to
rely on prior work and pilot tests to anticipate the effect size for a
given study and adjust accordingly to ensure sufficient power (e.g.,
design an experiment with fewer conditions if a larger sample size is
needed to obtain sufficient power). Ideally, one would want power to
identify a small effect, but there exist invariable trade-offs between
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s confidence in the anticipated
effect size (e.g., it will be medium or large) against data collection
costs and design []{#part0010_split_010.html_page_49
.calibre1}complexity. I suggest starting a research program with caution
(i.e., anticipating a small effect) and building from
there.[^44^](#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-55){#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-55-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-131
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-197
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-239
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-268 .calibre1}In that
vein, researchers sometimes design experiments as what Sniderman
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-483){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-483-back-2
.calibre1}: 262) calls ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}null by
design,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} with the goal of showing that
treatments others may imagine mattering in fact do not. Sniderman offers
the example of Grimmer et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2015)
study that sought to show (and does show) that legislative constituents
respond as favorably to a legislator who only asks for a benefit for his
or her constituency as to one who actually delivers a benefit (i.e.,
counter to what one might intuitively think). In these situations,
researchers need high levels of statistical power to rule out that the
null results occurred due to low power.

Finally, blocking or stratified random assignment refers to a process
where the experimentalist partitions the sample into relevant subgroups,
such as Democrats or Republicans or men and women, prior to
randomization. Then, random assignment occurs separately within each
group (Geber and Green 2102: 71[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}80;
Glennerster and Takavarasha
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-219){#part0010_split_010.html_REFe-r-219-back-2
.calibre1}: 153[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}158). Blocking can
ensure that certain subgroups have a sufficient number of control and
treatment units for analyses of subgroup-specific effects
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example, it can ensure that enough
Republicans receive the special interests story or not so as to look at
that subgroup. Blocking becomes particularly useful when one has a
strong a priori expectation of a moderating effect and the
experiment[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sample
[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-78
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-142
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-280
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-672
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-872
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-950
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-322
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-570
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-620
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-746
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-1020
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-14
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-308 .calibre1}size is
small.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0010_split_011.html}

:::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: {#part0010_split_011.html_A-sec-7 .sec}
::::::: {#part0010_split_011.html_B-sec-9 .sec}
## Summary {#part0010_split_011.html_calibre_pb_11 .b}

:::::: {#part0010_split_011.html_NL-lst-5 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} When scholars care greatly about identifying casual
pathways or mediation, they need to refer to design approaches to
mediation [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} few social scientists have
conducted such experiments, but a growing literature offers guidance.
The point is that identifying mediation requires more than including
potential mediator measures as outcomes and correlating them with the
outcomes and treatments.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Moderation refers to differential effects among subgroups
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as I will later discuss, while these
subgroups typically refer to variations among the units, one could also
consider heterogeneity among settings, treatments, and outcome measures.
:::

::: list-item
[[]{#part0010_split_011.html_page_50 .calibre1}(3)]{.label2} The
availability of programming to search for heterogeneous effects may
deter scholars from theorizing in advance about moderators, but for
reasons of generalization that I will discuss later, theorizing about
moderators can be essential for the progression of knowledge.
:::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

[]{#part0010_split_012.html}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0010_split_012.html_A-sec-8 .sec}
# Conclusion {#part0010_split_012.html_calibre_pb_12 .a}

Experiments are far from a magical elixir that leads to generalizable
causal inferences. While most of the topics covered in this chapter
receive attention in research design texts, they remain under-discussed
in the practice of experimental social science. Indeed, the range of
studies that use the label
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
remains hugely heterogeneous, with little explicit discussion of what
ties them together. Moreover, experiments seem to rarely define their
target populations, and all too often experimental hypotheses fail to
state, much less motivate, the comparisons involved (e.g., which
experimental conditions are compared to which and why). Critiques of
experiments often neglect the goal of generalizing the existence of a
causal relationship, which differs from descriptively describing a
population.

I have attempted to remedy these issues by laying out how experiments
fit into the scientific process. I also offered a framework for thinking
about different approaches to experimentation and the assumptions
underlying the causal inferences to which experiments lead. I emphasized
the importance of counterfactual thinking in designing and presenting
experiments, and how design and presentation depend on
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goals. I conclude with a
reiteration of select points that an experimentalist should consider
well before data collection:

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   Identify the target population and justify the sampling approach
    (see [Chapter
    3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0010_split_012.html_CN-bp-3-back-6
    .calibre1} for further discussion on sampling).

-   Recognize how the context, topic, and measures compare to related
    studies and the implications for generalization.

-   Construct valid and accurate measures.

-   Consider the assumptions underlying causal inference, given the
    experimental design, and whether these assumptions are met.

-   Specify and theoretically justify the key comparison groups (i.e.,
    the key counterfactual).

-   State the goal of the experiment and consider implications for
    generalization (e.g., is the precise causal effect size important?).

-   If heterogeneous effects are expected, consider blocking and ensure
    the sample size is sufficiently large.
:::
::::
:::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fng-2 .fn-group}
::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-12 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0010_split_001.html_FN-fn-12-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
These include, for example, that scientific communities sometimes are
unable to differentiate whether a given observation falsifies a theory;
that it is not an accurate portrait of science in practice since many
refuse to abandon falsified theories even in the absence of auxiliary
hypotheses; that science is more of a community with norms than an
individual endeavor; and that the idea of relying on corroborated rather
than falsified theories is underdeveloped particularly since even a
corroborated theory cannot be assumed to be correct.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-13 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0010_split_001.html_FN-fn-13-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} I
also make arguments later in the book that may ostensibly challenge this
portrait of the scientific method and falsification. For example, with
regard to the former, I will discuss the invariable back and forth
between theory and data. With regard to the latter, I will suggest that
an experiment that refutes a hypothesis may do so for many reasons
(including methodological ones), an approach that coheres with distinct
philosophies of science. My goal though is not to deeply engage with
alternative perspectives but rather to use the scientific method and
falsification as baselines for discussion.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-14 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-14-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Some
debate what probability should be seen as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}significant["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(e.g., Benjamin et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-37){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-37-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-15 .fn}
[[^4^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-15-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
exact size of the sample needed depends on the size of the effect (i.e.,
whether the effect of one variable on the other
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} media exposure and policy support
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is small, moderate, or large), and the
confidence one would like to have in identifying the effect. However,
the size needed is orthogonal to the size of the population.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-16 .fn}
[[^5^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-16-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Doherty et al.
([2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-133){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-133-back
.calibre1}) serve as an exemplar of an experiment with an explicit
discussion of the population. They explore how the amount of winnings
from a state lottery affects attitudes toward estate taxes, government
redistribution, etc. (They find lottery-induced affluence decreases
support for estate taxes and, marginally, redistribution, but has no
effect on broader issues concerning government and the economy.) Their
target population is the US public and they go to considerable lengths
to show that their sample of lottery players generalizes to the target
population (445[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}446).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-17 .fn}
[[^6^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-17-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-254
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-256 .calibre1}That
said, in those latter cases, it could be that the goal is not a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}representative probability
sample["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} but rather a purposive sample
aimed at a specific population of interest (Klar and Leeper
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-316){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-316-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-18 .fn}
[[^7^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-18-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As I
later discuss, these four areas of generalization cohere with Shadish et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2002: 20) external validity
dimensions [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} they include units,
treatments, outcomes, and settings. For this discussion, though, I have
merged treatments and outcomes under the category of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}measures,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
used the term
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}contexts["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
instead of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}settings,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and added
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}topics.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Presumably, Shadish, et al.
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-466-back-6
.calibre1}) would incorporate
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}topics["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
into
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}treatments,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
but, for sampling discussions, it is worth differentiating topics. Also,
Cook and Campbell
([1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-106-back-4
.calibre1}) discuss generalizing over time. I incorporate this factor
under context (although, as Shadish et al.
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-466-back-7
.calibre1}: 20) note, timing could be seen as relevant to all types of
generalization).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-19 .fn}
[[^8^](#part0010_split_002.html_FN-fn-19-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} A
related issue for experiments is that typically participants have no
choice but to be exposed to the treatment, which may not be a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}typical["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
setting.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-20 .fn}
[[^9^](#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-20-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-104 .calibre1}To take one
example, scholars often use ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}feeling
thermometers["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to gauge what partisans
think of those from the other party. These measures ask individuals to
rate those from the other party on a scale running from 0 (very cold) to
100 (very warm). Scholars had presumed this captured what voters thought
of one another, but it turns out that the questions actually gauge
voters[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} feelings about elected officials
(elites) (Druckman and Levendusky
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-155){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-155-back
.calibre1}). This is a case where the measure did not capture what
scholars had assumed.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-21 .fn}
[[^10^](#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-21-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Considerable debate continues about the impact of negative advertising
(e.g. Lau et al.
[1999](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-335){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-335-back
.calibre1}; Lau et al.
[2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-336){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-336-back
.calibre1}; Krupnikov
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-324){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-324-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-22 .fn}
[[^11^](#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-22-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-599
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-601
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-1005 .calibre1}In this
case, it seems that the less biased measure uniformly increases accuracy
across all groups of individuals (Persson and Solevid
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-430){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-430-back
.calibre1}) and thus it seems safe to use that measure. However,
exploring that type of dynamic is exactly what an experimentalist needs
to do prior to choosing a measure. One additional consideration is
measurement mode effects [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example,
the original Ansolabehere et al. study was done in person and it seems
that online measurement of vote intention is less biased (Holbrook and
Krosnick
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-268){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-268-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-23 .fn}
[[^12^](#part0010_split_003.html_FN-fn-23-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
terms of surveys, a useful approach is total survey error (Biemer
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-45){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-45-back-2
.calibre1}). This refers to the difference between the population
parameter (e.g., mean, proportion, regression coefficient) and estimate
from the sample. It consists of sampling error
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, the inherent uncertainty from
sampling [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and non-sampling errors that
result from the design of the data collection (e.g., survey). These
non-sampling errors are important to consider when thinking about data
collection and include specification error (i.e., mismeasuring concepts
à la face validity); frame errors (e.g., excluding relevant parts of the
population from being eligible from the sample); nonresponse error;
measurement error; and processing error. Processing errors are often
underappreciated in an age of online survey software. Processing errors,
though, are common due to misprogramming
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as typos in questions or
assigning people to the wrong conditions/scenarios based on prior
responses (e.g., a male is asked about his female identity strength due
to a programing error). It is crucial that all aspects of an experiment
be piloted and proofed by several people who have not previously seen
the study, and that the researcher checks the flow of the
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-600
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-602
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-1006 .calibre1}survey.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-24 .fn}
[[^13^](#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-24-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
discussing underlying assumptions, I mostly follow Holland
([1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-269){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-269-back-7
.calibre1}), who notes these assumptions are not exhaustive.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-25 .fn}
[[^14^](#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-25-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Unit homogeneity seems to implicitly assume the exclusion restriction
and stable unit treatment value assumptions discussed shortly. These are
relevant because the assumption is that units are comparable apart from
exposure to the treatment, which is the same presumption, via a distinct
route, for the statistical solution discussed next.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-26 .fn}
[[^15^](#part0010_split_005.html_FN-fn-26-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-188 .calibre1}There also is an
assumption that there are no subjects who would receive the treatment if
assigned to the control group, and would not receive the treatment if
assigned to the treatment group.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-27 .fn}
[[^16^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-27-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-675
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-819
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-905 .calibre1}The
scientific approach is used in many physical sciences
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it is what physicists do with
electrons. Since electrons are all about the same and because they
eliminate disturbances, researchers can isolate the control and
treatment difference for each electron and for the population of
electrons. This also was a common approach in the early days of
psychology (e.g., the early psychophysical studies as well as
behaviorism studies).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-28 .fn}
[[^17^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-28-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Observational research is not captured in the table. That would involve
situations where an intervention is not the primary mechanism for
addressing the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-29 .fn}
[[^18^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-29-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Randomly assigned groups will, on average, be probabilistically
equivalent, particularly if there is a sufficiently large sample (sample
size concerns relate to statistical power, which I discuss shortly).
There always exists a chance that the groups will not be on average the
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-676
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-820
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-906 .calibre1}same.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-30 .fn}
[[^19^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-30-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Here, the researcher is not intervening and assigning values of the
independent variable as with random assignment but instead intervening
to control background factors and time trends.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-31 .fn}
[[^20^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-31-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-167 .calibre1}Mutz uses a Latin
square design, where orders are randomized but in a way such that every
condition follows every other as often as it precedes it, and each
condition appears exactly once in each of the four possible order
positions. I thank Diana Mutz for discussing the details of the study.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-32 .fn}
[[^21^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-32-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-46
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-89
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-106
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-133
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-241 .calibre1}That
said, many economic and political economy experiments also rely on the
statistical/random assignment solution. For example, in his discussion
of experimental economics, Guala
([2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-238){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-238-back-2
.calibre1}: 79) explains that in ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}many
cases, one does not have resources to control for all background
variations, because the required design would be too complicated, too
costly, incompatible with the other controls, or perhaps ethically
unacceptable, or simply because one does not know the full list of the
relevant background factors. In such cases (i.e., in most cases),
experiments rely on randomization["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also
see Guala
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-239){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-239-back
.calibre1}). Moreover, even where the scientific solution is crucial for
causal inference, randomization might be used in assigning ordering of
exposure or roles in the experiment. For example, the dictatorship
experiment entails allowing a subject to decide how much of a fixed sum
of money to keep for him- or herself and how much to give to another
subject. This experiment is used to test predictions about self-interest
(e.g., do subjects act entirely in their self-interest or do they split
the difference?), yet subjects are randomly assigned to the roles (e.g.,
of giving or receiving money). The causal inferences of interest in such
games often are about other factors affecting self-interest, as in the
examples discussed shortly, rather than the role.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-33 .fn}
[[^22^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-33-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
These conditions mean that the payment structures behaviors and is
distinct from paying participants just for taking part in the study, as
is often done in survey experiments and psychology-oriented lab
experiments.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-34 .fn}
[[^23^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-34-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Incentives are assumed to wash out all potentially relevant
non-comparabilities across units. In his seminal introduction of the
idea, Smith
([1976](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-480){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-480-back-2
.calibre1}: 275) states that control ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}is
the essence of experimental methodology, and in experimental exchange
studies it is important that one be able to state that, as between two
experiments, individual values (e.g., demand or supply) either do or do
not differ in a specified way. Such control can be achieved by using a
reward structure to induce prescribed monetary value on
actions.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} As mentioned, many studies
employ some mix of induced value control with randomization, such as
randomizing the sequence of exposures to control for learning effects
(Guala
[2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-238){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-238-back-3
.calibre1}: 78[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}80).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-35 .fn}
[[^24^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-35-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-31 .calibre1}The authors randomly
vary the order in which participants are matched with co-ethnics or
non-co-ethnics, but the key causal variable of non-anonymity is not
randomly assigned. Also, Habyarimana et al. present additional
experiments on the same topic (e.g., a puzzle game and a network game)
where random assignment provides the key to causal inference. I thank
Macartan Humphreys and Daniel Posner for discussing the details of the
study.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-36 .fn}
[[^25^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-36-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Burnham and Kurzban
([2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-75){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-75-back
.calibre1}) question the validity of these assumptions, suggesting the
study is a quasi-experiment.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-37 .fn}
[[^26^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-37-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-645 .calibre1}Dunning
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-166){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-166-back-4
.calibre1}: 16) states that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the data
used in natural experiments come from
[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}naturally[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
occurring phenomena [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} actually, in the
social sciences, from phenomena that are often the product of social and
political forces.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} My characterization
differs from his insofar as he groups random lottery natural experiments
with
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
random assignment natural experiments. I separate them since, in the
latter case, most of the work requires establishing unit homogeneity (as
I will []{#part0010_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-646 .calibre1}discuss
shortly).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-38 .fn}
[[^27^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-38-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} She
engages in several checks to ensure the states actually use
randomization.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-39 .fn}
[[^28^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-39-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-288 .calibre1}There is one
complication to the randomization insofar as it is a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}systematically randomized treatment
assignment.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} That is, the districts are
ordered and the first one has a random ballot order. The others have
specific orders that also are random but in particular ways contingent
on the randomization of the first district. The authors adjust for this
and a few other issues in their analyses. Additionally, Ho and Imai
([2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-266){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-266-back-2
.calibre1}) take steps to confirm randomization; they also address
potential threats such as strategic candidate campaigning and/or entry
as well as voters[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} consciously adjusting
their vote choices to correct for potential ballot order effects.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-40 .fn}
[[^29^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-40-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} She
also offers statistical evidence for the comparability of polling places
in the control and treatment groups
(56[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}57) and checks the results with
controls (57[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}60).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-41 .fn}
[[^30^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-41-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
his treatment of natural experiments, Dunning
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-166){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-166-back-5
.calibre1}) identifies three categories, including those that are truly
randomized or ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if
random,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} regression-discontinuity, and
instrumental-variables. Meredith
([2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-373){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-373-back-3
.calibre1}) is a regression discontinuity, while Hyde
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-280){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-280-back-2
.calibre1}) is an ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if
random,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which I argue should be thought
of distinctly from truly random. An instrumental-variables design occurs
when the intervention is correlated with the topic of interest, such as
using the Vietnam lottery to compare those who served in the military
from those who did not [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it is an
instrument since those whose lottery number suggested conscription did
not always end up serving (and service is the variable of interest in
these studies) (Angrist
[1990](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-11){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-11-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-42 .fn}
[[^31^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-42-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As
noted, though, satisfaction of the scientific assumption also must
ensure independence such that units do not self-select into experimental
groups (as well as excludability and SUTVA).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-43 .fn}
[[^32^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-43-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Sekhon and Titiunik
([2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-463){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-463-back-2
.calibre1}) raise another concern relevant to any type of natural
intervention; specifically, the researcher needs to take considerable
care to identify the proper treatment and control groups (i.e., it may
not always be so clear). They offer an example of clear random
assignment of gender quotas in elections. The original study focused on
the random assignment that took place in one year and compared, at a
later date, women candidates and election success from wards that had
had the quota to those that did not (to see if having quotas at one
point leads to more women representation once the quota is removed).
However, those are not necessarily the correct groups to compare, since
there had been randomized gender quotas at other times in various wards
that may have affected outcomes at the later date.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-44 .fn}
[[^33^](#part0010_split_006.html_FN-fn-44-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Titiunik
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-499){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-499-back-2
.calibre1}) uses a classification similar to mine, although she views
cases in my final cell (4) as observational data of a special type where
the intervention facilitates casual inference. She takes particular
issue with the presumption that the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}as-if["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
randomization assumption approximates what is needed for the statistical
solution (which requires equi-probable assignment). I agree with this
perspective, only differing in terminology and labeling. Also, in these
situations, balance tests between groups can be helpful, as can
auxiliary outcome ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}causal-process
observation["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Mahoney
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-360){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-360-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-45 .fn}
[[^34^](#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-45-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-173 .calibre1}Matching entails
identifying for every treated unit, a nontreated unit with similar
observable characteristics against whom the effect of the treatment can
be assessed. An example would be to take a sample of potential voters,
find a partner for everyone [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is,
near twins, [except]{.italic} the treated received a mobilizing message
and the control did not, and to explore if those exposed voted. In this
case, the bulk of the causal inference depends on the statistical
ability to identify matches. The approach seems less powerful than
experiments in identifying causal relationships (e.g., Arceneaux et al.
[2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-15){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-15-back
.calibre1}).

Also, my definition differs from some insofar as it is tied directly to
causal inference. For example, I would not characterize behavioral
decision-making games that demonstrate technical irrational
decision-making (e.g.,
[Quattrone](https://www.cambridge.org/core/search?filters%5BauthorTerms%5D=George%20A.%20Quattrone%26eventCode=SE-AU){.calibre1}
and Tversky 1988) as experiments, since they constitute descriptive
exercises rather than causal tests.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-46 .fn}
[[^35^](#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-46-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Campbell
([1969b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-88){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-88-back
.calibre1}) suggests the ideal is random assignment, but that is not
always feasible or morally justifiable.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-47 .fn}
[[^36^](#part0010_split_007.html_FN-fn-47-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Some argue that the assumptions of the scientific solution in practice
(e.g., using financial incentives) often are not met, arguing that
individuals cannot be considered comparable and/or that repeated play
creates a confound (Green and Tusicisny
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-230){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-230-back
.calibre1}; c.f., Morton and Williams
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-391){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-391-back-3
.calibre1}: 48).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-48 .fn}
[[^37^](#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-48-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} I
use the word
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}type["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
differently from the word
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}approach.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Approach,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
as described in [Table
2.3](#part0010_split_006.html_TN-tblw-3){#part0010_split_012.html_TN-tblw-3-back-6
.calibre1}, refers to the source of the intervention (researcher or
natural) and the way the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference is
addressed (statistical or scientific solution).
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Type["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
refers to the location of the intervention.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-49 .fn}
[[^38^](#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-49-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Many laboratory or field experiments use surveys to measure outcomes,
but those are not survey experiments, which require that the
intervention be part of the survey itself.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-50 .fn}
[[^39^](#part0010_split_008.html_FN-fn-50-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Gerber and Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-212-back-13
.calibre1}: 10[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}13) distinguish field
experiments further along four dimensions depending on the authenticity
of the treatments, participants, contexts, and outcome measures.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-51 .fn}
[[^40^](#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-51-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} See
Gerber and Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-212-back-14
.calibre1}: 95[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}130) on the role of
covariates in experimental analyses.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-52 .fn}
[[^41^](#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-52-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This is not quite as straightforward as it may sound. It is complicated
by the possibility that a null effect of a treatment on a potential
mediator could be hiding heterogeneity such that there is an effect, but
it is distinct for different subgroups. Thus, to rule out mediation, one
needs to also ensure there is not a moderated relationship between the
treatment and potential mediator. I thank John Bullock and Don Green for
discussion on this point.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-53 .fn}
[[^42^](#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-53-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-121
.calibre1}[]{#part0010_split_012.html_IDXloc-tgt-129 .calibre1}Gerber
and Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-212-back-15
.calibre1}: 311) explain that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}interpretation remains ambiguous
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Treatment-by-covariate interactions
may provide useful descriptive information about which types of
subgroups are most responsive to the treatment, but the theoretical
question of whether these interactions are causal \[i.e., the attribute
caused the differential reaction\] requires an experimental design that
randomly varies what are believed to be the relevant subject attributes
or contextual characteristics.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Of
course, varying many moderators such as individual attributes (e.g.,
partisanship) can be difficult if not impossible.
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-54 .fn}
[[^43^](#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-54-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} One
such program that provides many other properties as well is
DeclareDesign (Blair et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-50){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-50-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0010_split_012.html_FN-fn-55 .fn}
[[^44^](#part0010_split_010.html_FN-fn-55-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
While it is reasonable to consider insufficient power as an explanation
for nonsignificant results, one should not engage in retrospective power
analysis to explain away an insignificant result and then simply ignore
it (Hoening and Heisey
[2001](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-267){#part0010_split_012.html_REFe-r-267-back
.calibre1}).
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0011_split_000.html}

::: {#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0011_split_000.html_page_51 .calibre1}3]{.label1} Evaluating Experiments {#part0011_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}

## Realism, Validity, and Samples {.subtitle}
:::

:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0011_split_000.html_A-sec-03 .sec}
I began the book with a discussion of the relative explosion of
experiments in political science, and to varying extents, the other
social sciences. I left unanswered, though, the question of what exactly
drove resistance to the method for so many years (i.e., why experiments
were so rare prior to the 1990s[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2000s).
While it partially stemmed from the lack of technological opportunities,
as discussed, it also reflected fundamental concerns about realism,
external validity, and the nature of experimental samples. I turn to a
discussion of these topics in this chapter. I do not revisit past
concerns, but rather, I highlight ways to think about the topics that
continue to be misunderstood and
mischaracterized.[^1^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-56){#part0011_split_000.html_FN-fn-56-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_001.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0011_split_001.html_A-sec-9 .sec}
# []{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-325 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-333 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-629 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-749 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-823 .calibre1}Realism {#part0011_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

When it comes to assessing the contribution of a particular experiment,
there are at least two ways to do so (Aronson and Carlsmith
[1968](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-21){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-21-back
.calibre1}; Aronson et al.
[1998](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-22){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-22-back
.calibre1}). First, experimental realism refers to whether
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}an experiment is realistic, if the
situation is involving to the subjects, if they are forced to take it
seriously, \[and\] if it has impact on
them["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Aronson et al.
[1985](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-20){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-20-back
.calibre1}: 485); that is, whether experimental participants treat the
situation as real, as they would approach any situation in everyday life
(i.e., they are involved). Second, mundane realism concerns
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the extent to which events occurring in
the research setting are []{#part0011_split_001.html_page_52
.calibre1}likely to occur in the normal course of the
subjects[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} lives, that is, in the
[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
world['"]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Aronson et al.
[1985](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-20){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-20-back-2
.calibre1}:
485).[^2^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-57){#part0011_split_001.html_FN-fn-57-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-94
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-168
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-214
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-317 .calibre1}Much
debate about experiments revolves around mundane realism. When
participants do not match the population of interest or stimuli do not
resemble something one encounters regularly in daily life, many conclude
the study has limited value. If one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
goal is to ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whisper in the ears of
princes["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, to directly test the causal
effect of a precise intervention, such as a particular campaign message
or a curriculum [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} then low mundane
realism indeed constitutes a problem. Such policy-oriented experiments
aim to simulate a particular
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}reality.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Yet, many (and in some fields, most) experiments do not have such a goal
and in those cases, emphasis on mundane realism is misplaced (e.g., see
McDermott
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-368){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-368-back
.calibre1}; Morton and Williams
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-390){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-390-back-2
.calibre1}: 345); [of much greater importance is experimental
realism]{.italic}. Failure of participants to take the study and
treatments seriously compromises the basis of the causal inference,
which in turn renders the experimental results fairly meaningless (e.g.,
Dickhaut et al.
[1972](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-131){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-131-back
.calibre1}).[^3^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-58){#part0011_split_001.html_FN-fn-58-back
.calibre1} Moreover, scholars have yet to specify clear criteria for
assessing mundane realism, and, as Liyanarachchi
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-350){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-350-back
.calibre1}: 57) explains, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}any
superficial appearance of reality (e.g., a high level of mundane
realism) is of little comfort, because the issue is whether the
experiment [']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}captures the intended essence
of the theoretical variables['"]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(Kruglanski
[1975](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-323){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-323-back
.calibre1}: 106) (also see Berkowitz and Donnerstein
[1982](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-43){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-43-back
.calibre1}: 249; Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-398-back-2
.calibre1}: 133[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}135).

[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-591
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-985 .calibre1}When it
comes to theory-oriented experiments, efforts to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}match["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to
situations observed outside the study [per se]{.italic} become
problematic, as the goal involves generalizing the precise parameters
put forth in the given theory. Plott
([1991](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-438){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-438-back
.calibre1}: 906) explains that the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiment should be judged by the
lessons it teaches about the theory and not by its similarity with what
nature might have happened to have
created.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} The same can be said for
experiments with the goal of searching for facts; Mook
([1983](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-388){#part0011_split_001.html_REFe-r-388-back
.calibre1}: 385) []{#part0011_split_001.html_page_53 .calibre1}states
that we ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}may assume that in order to
[generalize]{.italic} to [']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
life,[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} setting should [resemble]{.italic}
the real-life one as much as possible [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
This assumption is false["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (italics in the
original). This conclusion follows because experiments aim to isolate
the impact of a causal variable, which means removing the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}noise["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} of
real life that then can be systematically built into subsequent studies.
Put another way, given that experiments aim to identify the effect of a
given variable, it becomes inevitable that they look different from the
multidimensional nature of ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
life.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} One often wants to establish what
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}can["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
happen; from a Popperian perspective, one cannot definitively prove what
does happen. In short, unless one has strongly applied goals to assess
an intervention implemented in real time, mundane realism should play
little to no role in assessing an experiment. Researchers should instead
focus on experimental realism as well as the design of valid and
accurate treatments, which I []{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-592
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-986 .calibre1}discuss
next[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-326
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-334
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-630
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-750
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-824 .calibre1}.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0011_split_002.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_002.html_A-sec-9 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_002.html_B-sec-10 .sec}
## []{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-507 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-633 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-637 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-827 .calibre1}Designing Treatments {#part0011_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .b}

The discussion of realism raises the question of how one should
construct experimental interventions that often serve as the causal
variable of interest. One hopes that the treatment alters values of the
independent variable (e.g., causes subjects to consider campaign finance
in terms of free speech) or induces certain beliefs among participants
(e.g., how much they will get paid). The mundane realism of the
treatment itself, unless one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goal is to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whisper in the ears of
princes,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is irrelevant. As Mutz
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-399-back-2
.calibre1}: 220) states, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}it is not
necessary that people in the real world frequently encounter \[the\]
treatment or experience [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} What is most
important about a treatment is that it systematically and substantially
changes the independent variable in the intended
direction["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-398-back-3
.calibre1}: 84[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}99).

Mutz
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-399-back-3
.calibre1}) offers the example of a researcher interested in the
influence of anxiety on political attitudes. An experimental approach
requires a manipulation that induces anxiety (such as a stressful task
or video clip). The key here is that some stimulus alters levels of
anxiety, not what that particular stimulus is. Mutz
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-399-back-4
.calibre1}) provides another example in which
[Reader[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Digest]{.italic} stories are
used to alter people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s level of social
trust (e.g., the story either described someone absconding with a wallet
found on the ground or returning it) (Mutz
[2005](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-396){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-396-back
.calibre1}).[^4^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-59){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-59-back
.calibre1} The intervention altered social trust; that it came from
[Reader[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Digest]{.italic} did not
matter.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_page_54
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-28
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-51
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-119
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-156
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-211
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-299
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-306 .calibre1}These
examples make clear that sound treatments do not depend on their mundane
realism but rather on whether the relevant independent variable changes.
When it comes to evaluating treatments, researchers should
[not]{.italic} trust themselves to validate them. For example, one
should not assume that a news article that seems to talk about special
interests and campaign finance will be read as such by experimental
participants; that a particular audio recording will stimulate anxiety;
or that a story will generate social trust. A crucial step taken in the
design of an experiment entails validating the intervention with a
sample that matches the experimental participants and/or the
participants themselves.

One approach to validation involves piloting the intervention
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one need not test the outcome
variables of interest but instead assess whether participants interpret
and react to the intervention as presumed (e.g., increased anxiety or
social trust). Piloting has the advantage of allowing one to evaluate
different approaches before implementing the actual experiment. Ideally,
one pilots on a sample drawn from the same population as the experiment.
If that is not possible, however, one should carefully think about
possible differences between the pilot sample and the experimental
sample.[^5^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-60){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-60-back
.calibre1} For example, many experiments use last names in treatments to
signify race, such as exposing some people to vignettes about Whites
(e.g., using the name
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Larsen["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans})
and others about Blacks (e.g., using the name
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Washington["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}).
The common practice is to identify names using objective birth data or
those used in other studies. Yet, as Crabtree and Chykina
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-115){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-115-back
.calibre1}: 21) note, the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}potential
problem here is that scholars often ignore the extent to which these
choices accurately map onto how individuals perceive
names.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} They show notable heterogeneity
in race of names across US counties (e.g., Washington is a common Black
name in some counties more than others). This finding exemplifies the
importance of piloting treatments on the target populations when
possible and, when not, carefully considering contextual confounds.
Ultimately, a good experiment offers corroboration for the intervention
that instills confidence that the independent variable is a valid and
accurate measure.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_page_55
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-13
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-154
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-162
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-172 .calibre1}In
addition to piloting, one can incorporate a manipulation check into the
experiment itself to empirically assess whether respondents receive and
perceive the treatment as intended. For example, this type of check
would involve post-treatment questions about how one interprets an
article or about their anxiety (see Mutz
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-399-back-5
.calibre1} for detailed discussion). Kane and Barabas
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-306){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-306-back
.calibre1}) differentiate subjective manipulation checks from factual
manipulation checks. The former involves asking respondents their
perceptions of the treatment; for instance, does a treatment meant to
change people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s beliefs about the
reinstatement of the draft actually change those beliefs (Horowitz and
Levendusky
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-271){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-271-back
.calibre1})? The latter entails testing for specific objective facts,
such as asking respondents what news source a vignette came from (e.g.,
when it was clearly labeled as coming from a source such as CNN).
Subjective checks ensure the treatment captures
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the latent variables of
interest["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Kane and Barabas
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-306){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-306-back-2
.calibre1}: 247). A factual information check can potentially not only
confirm treatment validity but also be used to test for attention, which
helps to establish experimental realism.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-27
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-571 .calibre1}In
practice, scholars should keep three lessons in mind. First, some
researchers use attention checks that are unrelated to the experimental
treatments, for example, asking a trick question such as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}In the following question, please check
only baseball even though it asks for
[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}all that
apply.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} What are your favorite sports?
Check all that apply.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} These questions
identify those not paying attention but provide no information about the
manipulation.[^6^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-61){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-61-back
.calibre1} Factual manipulation checks have the advantage of serving as
such attentional checks while also checking the manipulation itself.
Second, if one includes a manipulation check within the experiment,
rather than (or in addition to) relying on pilot tests, a prudent
approach is to include the manipulation check questions after the key
outcome measures to avoid interactions with the treatment itself
(Berinsky et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-42){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-42-back
.calibre1}; Hauser et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-254){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-254-back
.calibre1}).[^7^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-62){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-62-back
.calibre1} Third is a significant caveat to the prior point. While
manipulation checks within an experiment provide useful information, one
should not selectively remove respondents from conditions based on their
answers and then proceed with analyses among that subset. The removal of
respondents undermines the []{#part0011_split_002.html_page_56
.calibre1}solutions to causal inference, and doing so requires
nontrivial statistical adjustments (Angrist et al.
[1996](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-12){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-12-back
.calibre1}). Thus, when respondents fail a post-treatment manipulation
check it provides helpful information, but one should not remove those
who failed and then analyze the data as an
[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-28
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-572
.calibre1}experiment.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-243
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-300
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-311 .calibre1}This
point accentuates the usefulness of piloting to avoid discovering
problematic manipulations too late. Alternatively, Kane et al.
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-307){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-307-back
.calibre1}) suggest an approach [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
applicable to vignette experiments [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
that involves having all participants read a mock vignette similar in
structure to the experimental (treatment) vignette and then answer
factual questions about it. Subsequently, the experimenter randomly
assigns participants, regardless of their success on the factual
questions, to conditions. For example, participants read a story about
scientific publishing (e.g., concerning rules about public access to
federally funded research) and then answer questions about the details
of the story (e.g., how long did the article say publishers could wait
before releasing federally funded research to the public?). Next, the
experimenter randomly assigns participants to conditions; Kane et al.
offer the example of a student loan experiment where participants
receive information critical of loan forgiveness for college students (a
treatment condition) or no information (a control condition). Having the
prior mock vignette allows the researchers to identify inattentive
respondents prior to random assignment and then condition analyses on
(pretreatment) attention, likely isolating respondents who attend,
perceive, and understand the stimulus
itself.[^8^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-63){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-63-back
.calibre1} The advantage here is that the check resembles the
experimental manipulation and so those who pass the check likely process
the experimental treatment as intended. Indeed, Kane et al. find that
conditioning analyses on performance in the mock vignette leads to
stronger treatment affects. The inclusion of a pretreatment vignette
need not preclude the inclusion of a post-treatment manipulation check.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-577 .calibre1}Holding costs
constant, an ideal study would include extensive piloting, pretreatment
checks, and post-treatment manipulation checks. Despite the crucial role
of such checks, the trend, at least in political science, seems to be
against using manipulation checks; based on a content analysis of
political science journals (and comparing it with other content
analyses), Mutz
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-399){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-399-back-6
.calibre1}) suggests that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}current
scholars may be [less]{.italic} likely to employ manipulation checks
than earlier experimentalists, []{#part0011_split_002.html_page_57
.calibre1}even though there are more experiments in political science
journals than there were in the past["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(emphasis in the original). If true, it may reflect researchers rushing
to data collection without considering the elements needed for
[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-578
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-638 .calibre1}the
study.

[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-39
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-146 .calibre1}Alas,
even a well-piloted and checked treatment does not guarantee the
elimination of confounds. Dafoe et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-121){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-121-back
.calibre1}) point to the possibility of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}informational
equivalence["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} with treatments, such that a
given treatment operationalizes multiple concepts, only some of which
are of theoretical interest. For example, treatments that vary whether a
country is a democracy or not risk introducing perception not only of
governance systems but also of geography, culture, demographic
composition, and socioeconomic standing (also see Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_002.html_REFe-r-466-back-8
.calibre1}: 75). Another example comes from the aforementioned use of
names to signal race [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} even putting
aside the stated perceptual challenges, an individual may view a name
meant to signify a Black individual (e.g., Latoya Washington) as not
only revealing race but also class, and thus any impact of a treatment
using that name could reflect the effect of race or class. In the ideal,
an experimentalist varies only the key attribute across conditions to
avoid []{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-508
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-634
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-828
.calibre1}confounds.[^9^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-64){#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-64-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_003.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_003.html_A-sec-9 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_003.html_B-sec-11 .sec}
## []{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-23 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-567 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-593 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-727 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-987 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1011 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1053 .calibre1}Assessing Treatments {#part0011_split_003.html_calibre_pb_3 .b}

Since an experimental treatment constitutes a way to induce a value on
the independent variable of interest, measurement validity and accuracy,
as discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_003.html_CN-bp-2-back-7
.calibre1}, become relevant. The validity question revolves around
whether the manipulation alters levels of the theoretical construct
(i.e., measurement validity refers to the extent to which the measure
reflects the abstracted concept). Ensuring validity may well involve
deviating from what one may think is best for resembling the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}real
world["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
or as having high mundane realism (i.e., the likelihood that the exact
treatment will occur in the normal course of the
participants[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} lives). This is clear in
the [Reader[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Digest]{.italic} example
from Mutz
([2005](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-396){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-396-back-2
.calibre1}).

Another example from a theory testing experiment comes from Lupia and
McCubbins
([1998](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-355){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-355-back
.calibre1}). The authors develop a theory that absent particular
external forces (e.g., penalties for perjury), persuasion can occur
[]{#part0011_split_003.html_page_58 .calibre1}only if a receiver
perceives a speaker to share his or her interests and perceives him or
her to be knowledgeable. Other factors, such as actual common interests,
likeability, or reputation are not necessary for persuasion. They design
an experiment to test this, with the goal of manipulating perceptions of
common interests and knowledge. They did this by having receivers
