predict the outcome of a coin toss (i.e., heads or tails) such that he
or she made more money from correct predictions. The receiver received
advice from a speaker who either had common interests with the receiver
(e.g., made money when the receiver made a correct prediction) or
conflicting interests with the receiver (e.g., made money when the
receiver made an incorrect prediction). They also varied whether the
speaker had knowledge (e.g., observed or did not observe the coin toss
outcome). Lupia and McCubbins show that persuasion
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the receiver believed the
speaker[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s statement about the coin toss
outcome (i.e., followed the advice) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
occurs more with perceived knowledge and common interests. From the
perspective of mundane realism, this looks very poor as it (i.e.,
predicting coin flips) does not resemble political or social persuasion
scenarios. Yet, the treatment closely maps onto the theoretical concepts
identified. If the authors had instead varied whether the speaker was
likable or shared the receiver[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
partisanship, it would have lacked content validity, at the very least.
That is, likeability and/or partisanship may not cover the dimensions of
the construct of perceived common interests and knowledge. The authors
designed the experimental treatments to operationalize independent
variables that map onto theory and ensure experimental realism (i.e.,
the situation engages the participants and they take it seriously)
(Lupia and McCubbins
[1998](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-355){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-355-back-2
.calibre1}: 97[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}112; Bassi
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-30){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-30-back
.calibre1}). As explained in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_003.html_CN-bp-2-back-8
.calibre1}, in most circumstances, experimental realism takes precedence
over mundane realism.

[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-69
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-575
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-595
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1009
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1045 .calibre1}The
other dimension of measurement concerns accuracy. Recall from [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_003.html_CN-bp-2-back-9
.calibre1} that accuracy refers, in part, to being unbiased such that a
measure (or a treatment) does not systematically under- or overstate the
true value of the construct. Bias occurs when a treatment induces
participants to move too far in a given direction for reasons unrelated
to the treatment itself. Consider work on interpersonal contact. A large
literature explores whether intergroup contact can reduce prejudice; for
example, does interacting with those from a different racial or ethnic
group lead people to be more tolerant (e.g., Allport
[1954](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-3){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-3-back
.calibre1})? The results are mixed (Paluck et al. 2018). Building on
this idea, some explore whether having individuals
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}imagine["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
contact can reduce prejudice. The idea is that mentally simulating a
positive interaction can have an effect,
[]{#part0011_split_003.html_page_59 .calibre1}which, if true, would have
profound potential since it does not entail overcoming systemic
geographic and cultural forces that limit contact (e.g., Crisp and
Turner
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-119){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-119-back
.calibre1}; Crisp et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-118){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-118-back
.calibre1}). A common manipulation involves people thinking about
interacting with another person (either who shares their demographic
profile or not), offering details about where the imagined interaction
occurs, and encouraging participants to think carefully about it and
closing their eyes (e.g., Husnu and Crisp
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-278){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-278-back
.calibre1},
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-279){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-279-back
.calibre1}). This work often finds that imagining contact with an
out-group, relative to imagining something else or imagining contact
with a distinct group, can significantly reduce intergroup bias (e.g.,
create/lead to more positive attitudes toward the other group) (Miles
and Crisp
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-379){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-379-back
.calibre1}).

In assessing this treatment, one might worry about measurement bias or
accuracy: The manipulation might lead to systematic overstatement of the
positivity of the imagined contact relative to what would occur in
nonexperimental settings where the intervention may be
applied.[^10^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-65){#part0011_split_003.html_FN-fn-65-back
.calibre1} This could occur because experimental participants anticipate
that the experimenter has positive attitudes toward the out-group,
leading to a demand effect [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} where
participants want to please the experimenter. That is, participants
imagine an interaction not as they typically would in, say, an
educational setting (one domain where advocates suggest imagined contact
can influence attitudes) but rather in a way that coheres with what they
think the experimenter wants. There is social desirability bias in
imagining contacts that leads to overstating their positivity. Here, the
concern is not about measurement validity, but bias due to participants
trying to match the experimenter[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
desired views (Bigler and Hughes
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-46){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-46-back
.calibre1}: 132).

[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-405 .calibre1}A similar concern
arises in work that pays respondents financial incentives when asking
them about political facts. The theory here is that partisans often
misreport their factual beliefs so as to appear like
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good
partisans,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as reporting improved
unemployment or inflation rates when their party controls the
administration, regardless of objective reality. Some argue that an
intervention to stimulate respondents to be more accurate and/or
elaborative when thinking ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}reduce\[s\]
partisan divergence and elicit\[s\] responses more informative of
people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s true beliefs by offering
incentives to answer correctly["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Bullock
et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-70){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-70-back
.calibre1}: 526; also see Prior et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-443){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-443-back
.calibre1}). This work finds payment leads to less factually
[]{#part0011_split_003.html_page_60 .calibre1}incorrect reporting. The
question remains, however, whether the financial incentive offered to
the treatment group is a good proxy (measure) for elaboration (although
see Jamieson and Weller
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-294){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-294-back
.calibre1} on incentives and effort). An alternative possibility is that
respondents want to earn money and answer questions in the way they
think the study designers would like them to do so. That is, they may
believe the experimenter has certain beliefs, and that to earn the
money, they need to match those beliefs. In short, the treatment
generates an experimentally driven response reflecting a measurement
bias in the treatment. Respondents misstate their actual beliefs
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} appearing more factually correct than
they are [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to make money, since they
think the experimenters have false beliefs themselves, which means these
are not
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
true
beliefs.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[^11^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-66){#part0011_split_003.html_FN-fn-66-back
.calibre1}

My suggestions in the imagined contact and financial incentive
experiments constitute nothing more than untested assertions. It is
entirely possible that in both cases, there is no measurement bias in
the treatments (e.g., Mummolo and Peterson
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-394){#part0011_split_003.html_REFe-r-394-back
.calibre1}). Nonetheless, my point is that in assessing treatments, one
needs to recall the lessons of good measurement concerning validity and
accuracy, and evaluate the treatments along those
[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-406
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-70
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-576
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-596
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1010
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1046
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-24
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-568
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-594
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-728
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-988
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1012
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1054
.calibre1}dimensions.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_004.html}

:::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::: {#part0011_split_004.html_A-sec-9 .sec}
::::::::::: {#part0011_split_004.html_B-sec-12 .sec}
## Summary {#part0011_split_004.html_calibre_pb_4 .b}

:::::::::: {#part0011_split_004.html_NL-lst-6 .list3}
:::::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Many assess experiments based on their mundane realism
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, how much they resemble
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the real
world.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} If
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goal is to directly inform policy,
this standard is reasonable. Otherwise, mundane realism does not
constitute an important evaluative criterion. Much more important are
experimental realism and the construction of theoretically appropriate
treatments.

::::: {#part0011_split_004.html_NL-lst-7 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} Experimental realism requires ensuring that participants
take the study seriously (e.g., treat the situation as they would treat
any other situation in life).
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} Treatments should be constructed to operationalize the
relevant theoretical construct, which is orthogonal to the question of
mundane realism (unless the goal is to test a policy intervention).
:::
:::::
::::::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Pilot testing and/or manipulation checks are essential to
ensure experimental participants perceive the treatment(s) as intended.
:::

::: list-item
[[]{#part0011_split_004.html_page_61 .calibre1}(3)]{.label2} Consider
treatment confounds and whether they create problems for the causal
inference under study.
:::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} Assess treatments as a form of measurement
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} considering their measurement validity
and accuracy (or bias).
:::
::::::::::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::

[]{#part0011_split_005.html}

::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::: {#part0011_split_005.html_A-sec-10 .sec}
# Validity {#part0011_split_005.html_calibre_pb_5 .a}

When it comes to experiments, few topics garner as much discussion as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}validity,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
which, in this context, is distinct from measurement validity (as
discussed in the prior section and in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_005.html_CN-bp-2-back-10
.calibre1}). In their classic text, Shadish et al.
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-466-back-9
.calibre1}: 38) distinguish four types of validity, summarized in [Table
3.1](#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-4){#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-4-back-2
.calibre1}: (1) statistical conclusion validity concerns the confidence
one has in the covariation between the treatment and outcome; (2)
internal validity concerns the confidence one has in concluding a causal
relationship between the treatment and outcome; (3) construct validity
concerns the confidence one has in the inferences about the constructs
of interest (e.g., measurement validity, the setting reflects the focal
setting or population of interest); and (4) external validity concerns
the confidence one has that the
cause[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}effect relationship
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}holds over variation in persons,
settings, treatment variables, and measurement
variables.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[^12^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-67){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-67-back
.calibre1} Put another way, external validity refers to generalizability
(Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-398-back-4
.calibre1}: 133). With construct and external validity, another
dimension sometimes includes a finding holding over distinct time
periods, although time can also be enveloped in the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}different
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_62
.calibre1}settings["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} category of external
validity (Cook and Campbell
[1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-106-back-5
.calibre1}; Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-466-back-10
.calibre1}: 20,
70).[^13^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-68){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-68-back
.calibre1}

::::: {#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-4 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-163
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-369
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-499
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-701
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-951
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1017
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1047 .calibre1}Table
3.1.]{.label2} [Types of validity]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00021.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-122
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-130
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-175
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-265
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-295 .calibre1}When it
comes to statistical conclusion and internal validity, the key issues
revolve around meeting the assumptions of causal inference previously
discussed, in light of whichever approach one takes. Construct validity
partially, although not entirely, involves questions of measurement and
treatment design. In these senses, I have already touched on these
topics; moreover, extended discussion of these types of validity are
available in Shadish et al.
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-466-back-11
.calibre1}). Gerber and Green
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-212-back-16
.calibre1}) also offer superb guidance on ways to address statistical
conclusion and internal validity problems that arise from violating
assumptions underlying the random assignment statistical approach to
causal inference.

I focus here on external validity, as it receives a disproportionate
amount of attention in discussions of experiments. This attention
imbalance likely stems from three misperceptions. First, many assume
experiments possess high statistical conclusion and internal validity
due to the intervention; yet, as explained, assumptions still must be
met and experiments inherently do not guarantee high internal validity.
Second, many perceive a finite trade-off such that increased internal
validity means lower external validity, when in fact addressing one type
of validity does not invariably affect other types (see Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-466-back-12
.calibre1}: 96[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}102). Third, there exists
a widespread concern that experiments in the social sciences have low
external validity; as McDermott
([2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-369){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-369-back
.calibre1}: 34) states, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}political
scientists tend to focus, almost exclusively, on problems associated
with external validity["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-398-back-5
.calibre1}: 12). In what follows, I provide a constructive approach to
thinking about external validity [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} by
highlighting four key questions one should ask when generalizing
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to reduce the reflexive presumption
that an experiment has low external validity.

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-431 .calibre1}The first question
one must ask when assessing external validity is
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}what is being
generalized?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This ties back to my
earlier discussion about experiments as an approach for generalizing
casual relationships. It could be that one aims to generalize the
existence of an experimental treatment effect (be it via the randomized
intervention or controlled variation), [or]{.italic} the precise size of
a treatment
effect.[^14^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-69){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-69-back
.calibre1} The answer depends on the goal of
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_63
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-1
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-216
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-257
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-316 .calibre1}the
experiment. When the underlying goal involves
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whispering in the ears of
princes["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, speaking directly to policy
interventions [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} then the size of the
impact matters, since those making policy (e.g., government officials,
leaders of nongovernmental organizations) may use it to assess the costs
and benefits of policy programs. This introduces a host of challenges
coined as ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}threats to
scalability["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} by Al-Ubaydli et al.
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-5){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-5-back-2
.calibre1}). These authors (282) explain that when policy programs are
based on experimental findings, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the
program (treatment) effects diminish substantially in
size.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This occurs due to experimental
findings being false positives; the population from which the
experiment(s) drew the sample being amenable to larger effects (e.g.,
informed consent is obtained only from those likely to be affected,
superior compliance in experimental studies than in policy programs);
and loss of control once one leaves the experimental study setting
(e.g., the treatment administration is no longer overseen by the
researcher). The authors argue for more explicit consideration of
scalability problems, pushing researchers to flip

:::: disp-quote
the traditional model, calling on scholars to place themselves in the
shoes of the people whom they are trying to influence and produce more
policy based evidence. Our call is for policy research that starts by
imagining what a successful intervention would look like fully
implemented in the field, applied to the entire subject population,
sustained over a long period of time, and working as it is expected
because its mechanism is understood.

::: attrib
(Al-Ubaydli et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-6){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-6-back-2
.calibre1}: 1407)
:::
::::

When an experimentalist[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goal involves
searching for facts or testing theories, the precise effect size matters
much less. Here, one looks for evidence consistent with or contradictory
to a hypothesis or pattern. External validity questions concern whether
the causal relationship itself generalizes, regardless of size. For
social scientists, this is the case for many experiments. External
validity concerns then come down to assessing whether features of the
experimental sample, context, treatment, and measure preclude
generalizing the relationship beyond the
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-432 .calibre1}single
study.[^15^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-70){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-70-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_64
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-99
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-105
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-145
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-759
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-875
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-971
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1083 .calibre1}In
addition to assessing whether a given relationship generalizes beyond a
study, one needs to ask an often ignored question:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}to what is one
generalizing?["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} As emphasized in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_005.html_CN-bp-2-back-11
.calibre1}[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s discussion of sampling, one
must state to what population, settings, and causal variables one wants
to speak (e.g., Tipton
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-497){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-497-back
.calibre1} et
al.).[^16^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-71){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-71-back
.calibre1} With these statements in place, one needs to systematically
assess whether there exist features of the experiment that would
undermine the existence of a finding in the targets of generalization.
To see this in practice, consider sample generalization. For many
experiments, at least in the field of American political behavior, the
presumed population includes all US citizens (or permanent residents) of
voting age. An experiment may be done on a nonrepresentative convenience
sample and the issue then is whether a causal inference found in the
experiment would hold in the population. The only reason it would not
hold is if the causal relationship depends on individual attributes. Or
put another way, if no individual level characteristics moderate the
treatment effect, the same causal dynamic holds for all individuals in
the population and thus what one finds in any sample from the population
would be the same (putting aside measurement error, etc.).

To see this point in action, consider one of the most notable findings
in the social sciences about the impact of conformity
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} people follow descriptive norms by
adapting their attitudes when the majority of those around them hold
particular opinions (e.g., Sherif and Sherif
[1953](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-468){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-468-back
.calibre1}; Asch
[1956](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-23){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-23-back
.calibre1}; Dwyer et al. 2016; Davis et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-124){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-124-back
.calibre1}). Scholars invoke this finding to explain voting behavior
(e.g., Gerber et al.
[2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-211){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-211-back
.calibre1}; Sinclair
[2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-473){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-473-back
.calibre1}); the formation of policy beliefs (e.g., Kahan
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-301){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-301-back
.calibre1}); and conservation behaviors (Bayes et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-31){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-31-back-2
.calibre1}), inter alia. Yet, many of the direct causal (i.e.,
experimental) tests of conformity have relied on student samples,
raising the question of whether the dynamic generalizes to nonstudent
populations. It may not, because younger individuals have more malleable
attitudes, meaning they conform more easily (Bond and Smith
[1996](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-60){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-60-back
.calibre1}). Such a rationale is plausible [and necessary]{.italic} for
one to question whether the finding generalizes. That is, one must state
a reason why the experimental effect may not hold in the target
population; the onus falls on the critic
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} []{#part0011_split_005.html_page_65
.calibre1}as a Popperian perspective makes clear, nothing can be proven
and thus an argument and evidence should be accepted until shown
otherwise.

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-79
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-218
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-318 .calibre1}Yeager et
al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-519){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-519-back
.calibre1}) replicate the classic conformity experiment (randomly
assigning participants to a condition that stated a majority of
Americans support a public policy or a condition that stated a majority
oppose it) on a probability sample of
Americans.[^17^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-72){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-72-back
.calibre1} Yeager et al. replicate the conformity effect such that those
who learn a majority of Americans support a policy are more supportive
than those who are told a majority oppose it; this finding reveals the
generalizability of the conformity effect across samples. More
interestingly, they also report that the effect size in their
probability sample is significantly smaller than that found in the
student samples, and the smaller size comes from conformity having a
larger impact on respondents who resemble students in terms of age,
education, income, race, gender, and region. The Yeager et al. results
highlight: (1) the importance of distinguishing whether one wants to
generalize the existence of a causal effect or its size; (2) when
generalizing the existence (which I have suggested is the norm for much
of social science), one must identify sample features that would
undermine generalization to a specified target population; and (3)
generalizations of well-developed and previously tested causal
relationships often hold across samples (Yeager et al. replicate six out
of seven studies; also see Klein et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-317){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-317-back
.calibre1}; Mullinix et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-393-back-2
.calibre1}; []{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-100
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-106
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-146
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-760
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-876
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-972
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1084 .calibre1}Coppock
[2019b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-109){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-109-back
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-173
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-373
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-639
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-697
.calibre1}Assessing external validity thus entails specifying what one
is attempting to generalize and [to]{.italic} what one is attempting to
generalize. This means experimentalists need to carefully state their
targets of generalizations but also that critics who cite low external
validity need to specify what aspect of the study limits its
generalizability. Much of the discussion
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} including the example from Yeager et
al. [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} focuses on the generalizability of
the units or sample. A third point is that, as mentioned, external
validity means generalizing across (1) samples; (2) settings; (3)
treatments; and (4) outcome measures (Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-466-back-13
.calibre1}: 83; see Egami and Hartman
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-171){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-171-back
.calibre1} for a formalization that incorporates these four dimensions
into the potential outcomes
framework).[^18^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-73){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-73-back
.calibre1} This often missed []{#part0011_split_005.html_page_66
.calibre1}point reveals a double-edged sword for experimentalists. On
the one hand, scholars rarely explicitly consider how a study might
generalize to distinct settings, treatments, and outcome measures
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} with some notable exceptions being
explicit meta-experiments that look at contextual effects (e.g., Dunning
et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-168){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-168-back-2
.calibre1}; Blair and McClendon
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-52){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-52-back-2
.calibre1}).[^19^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-74){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-74-back
.calibre1} On the other hand, the same holds for most nonexperimental
empirical work.

While scholars sometimes obsess about the random selection from a
population of units and sometimes talk about settings of the experiment
(e.g., laboratory, field, survey), they rarely discuss the
representativeness of the larger context, treatments, and outcome
measures.[^20^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-75){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-75-back
.calibre1} As Mutz
([2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-398-back-6
.calibre1}: 131[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}132) emphasizes, the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}tendency to make assertions about the
generalizability of research results based solely on either the
[setting]{.italic} in which the study was done (i.e., laboratory versus
field) or the representativeness of the [subjects]{.italic} studied
reflects a lack of considered thought on \[the\] much broader and
ultimately more interesting subject [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
methodology alone provides little to no guidance when judging external
validity["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (emphasis in the original).
Mutz[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s point makes clear that assessments
of generalizability involve more than a mundane realism standard or the
sample, but also include the setting and the operationalizations, and
how well they match the target context, outcome measures, and
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-174
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-374
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-640
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-698
.calibre1}treatments.

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-199
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-547 .calibre1}One
telling study is Landy et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2020)
crowdsourcing initiative where more than a dozen research teams
independently created stimuli to test the same research questions (on
topics including moral judgment, negotiation, and implicit cognition).
For instance, the implicit cognition study built on initial research
measuring awareness (self-reports) of automatic prejudice with questions
like: ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Although I
don[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}t necessarily agree with them, I
sometimes have prejudiced feelings (like gut reactions or spontaneous
thoughts) that I don[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}t feel I can
prevent.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Another team operationalized
the construct with questions such as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Regardless of my explicit (i.e.
conscious) beliefs about social equality, I believe I possess automatic
(i.e., unconscious) negative associations towards members of stigmatized
social groups.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Yet, a distinct team took
the approach of first offering an introduction about automatic triggered
associations. Then they had respondents report their
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}first automatic reaction when you
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_67 .calibre1}think
about["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} various distinct groups (e.g.,
Blacks, Latin Americans, gay people) on a scale ranging from more
negative automatic associations to more positive automatic associations.
The study overall finds that across the five hypotheses tested there is
substantial variation in results based on the sets of materials (i.e.,
very different results from distinct research teams tasked with testing
the same hypothesis due to the use of variations in
operationalizations). This highlights that generalization across
operationalization is far from a foregone
conclusion.[^21^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-76){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-76-back
.calibre1}

This discussion makes clear that once one starts to consider tests over
all these dimensions (i.e., samples, settings, treatments, and outcomes
measures) ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}a strong case can be made that
external validity is enhanced more by many heterogeneous small
experiments than by one or two large
experiments["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Campbell and Cook 1979:
80). Experimentalists must consider all of these dimensions,
necessitating the need for multiple studies to capture
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-200
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-548 .calibre1}them
all.[^22^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-77){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-77-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-101
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-371 .calibre1}Fourth,
just as causal inferences require points of comparison, so should
statements of external validity; put another way, they should be
precise. If one assesses the generalization of a sample to a larger
population, then the relevant question is: Holding all other aspects of
the experiment constant, would the experiment have higher external
validity if carried out on a different sample (and what kind of
different sample)? As explained, a gain in external validity may occur
less often than presumed, but, regardless, holistic statements that an
experiment ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}has low external
validity["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} not only provide little
constructive guidance but also unknowingly employ an illusionary
idealistic study that randomly samples across units, settings,
treatments, and outcomes. A more useful approach entails evaluating each
dimension of external validity and considering how a different study may
have increased generalizability relative
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_68 .calibre1}to the extant study. Every
empirical study is confined in terms of generalization and assessments
need to be precise across dimensions of validity and points of
comparison.[^23^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-78){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-78-back
.calibre1} In [Table
3.2](#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-5){#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-5-back-2
.calibre1}, I offer a summary of the four key questions one should ask
when thinking of []{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-102
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-372
.calibre1}generalizing.

::::: {#part0011_split_005.html_TN-tblw-5 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 3.2.]{.label2} [Generalization questions]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00022.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-387 .calibre1}This brings me
back to falsification. Every empirical test of a causal proposition, if
done carefully, accumulates evidence and leads to knowledge progression
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} either by offering evidence consistent
with a hypothesis or not, in which case one refines the theory (or
starts
over).[^24^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-79){#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-79-back
.calibre1} No study should be dismissed due to vague statements about
external validity or mundane realism [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
every test helps and we need
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[many]{.italic} tests to determine
whether a causal proposition has or has not withstood
falsification["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Cook and Campbell
[1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-106-back-6
.calibre1}: 31; italics in the original). This accentuates a corollary
point that assessments of external validity ideally
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_page_69 .calibre1}occur over a range of
studies on a single topic (Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_005.html_REFe-r-398-back-7
.calibre1}: 135). The validity of any single study, regardless of the
nature of its participants, context, and operationalizations, should be
considered in light of the larger research agenda to which it hopes to
contribute.

In sum, discussions of external validity need to consider: (1) the goal
of the research, (2) the targets of generalizations and sources of
heterogeneity, (3) multiple dimensions of generalizability, (4) the
comparison points, and (5) the larger research agenda. External validity
has long been the bane of experimental studies, and this is for good
reason at times. Yet, when one critiques the external validity of an
experiment, the onus falls on that person to specify why the causal
relationship (or lack thereof) would not generalize to a target
population, context, treatments, and outcome measures. On the flip side,
experimentalists must take these considerations into account when they
attempt to generalize, which means clearly stating the goal of the
study, the target population/context/treatments/measures, and why the
causal relationship or size thereof would generalize. More precise and
sustained discussion about external validity will lead to improved
experiments and, ultimately, intellectual
[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-164
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-370
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-500
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-702
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-952
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1018
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1048
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-388
.calibre1}progress.
:::::::::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::

[]{#part0011_split_006.html}

:::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_006.html_A-sec-10 .sec}
::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_006.html_B-sec-13 .sec}
## Summary {#part0011_split_006.html_calibre_pb_6 .b}

:::::::::::: {#part0011_split_006.html_NL-lst-8 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Most take statistical conclusion and internal validity
for granted in experiments, but one must assess the causal assumptions
underlying claims from experiments, as enunciated in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_006.html_CN-bp-2-back-12
.calibre1}.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Experiments do not inherently have lower external
validity than other methods.
:::

:::::::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} The assessment of external validity requires answering
the following questions:

::::::: {#part0011_split_006.html_NL-lst-9 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} What is being generalized? In many cases, it may be the
existence of a causal relationship whereas, in applied studies, the size
of the relationship may be important.
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} To what is one generalizing? This requires specifying the
precise population of interest, as well as the target setting,
treatments, and outcome measures. With regard to the sample,
generalization depends partially on the existence of heterogeneous
effect sizes.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} Which dimensions of external validity are most
relevant/problematic? External validity entails generalizing across
samples, settings, treatments, and outcome measures (and timing).
[]{#part0011_split_006.html_page_70 .calibre1}Most experiments have no
variation in the latter three dimensions, necessitating the need for
multiple studies. A promising path involves systematically introducing
variation in the three dimensions (drawing on approaches in the case
study literature; see, for example, Gerring 2001).
:::

::: list-item
[d]{.label2} In assessing the generalizability/external validity of an
experiment, what is the comparison point? Scholars need to be precise
about the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}counterfactual["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
study to which one compares an experiment.
:::
:::::::
::::::::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} External validity should not be a reason to dismiss an
empirical result; all empirical evaluations of falsifiable hypotheses,
if soundly conducted, contribute evidence that accumulates over time.
:::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0011_split_007.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0011_split_007.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
# []{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-331 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-873 .calibre1}Experimental Samples {#part0011_split_007.html_calibre_pb_7 .a}

[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-435
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-665
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-679
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-781
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-961
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1031 .calibre1}In the
prior section, I discussed the some of the conditions that determine
whether a given experimental sample of units is problematic for
generalizing a causal inference. Here I explicate with more detail; I do
so because when it comes to critiques of the external validity of
experiments, the central culprit has long been the sample of units. This
stems from the prevalence of student samples used in experiments
throughout the social sciences and concomitant suspicion of such samples
(Sears
[1986](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-461){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-461-back
.calibre1}). Writing a little more than a decade ago, Kam et al.
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-305){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-305-back
.calibre1}: 421) state that a ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}simplistic
heuristic of [']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}a student sample lacks
external generalizability['"]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} exists.
Similarly, Gerber and Green
([2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-211){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-211-back-2
.calibre1}: 358) write, if ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}one seeks to
understand how the general public responds
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the external validity of lab studies
of undergraduates has inspired
skepticism["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see McDermott
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-368){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-368-back-2
.calibre1},
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-369){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-369-back-2
.calibre1} for
discussion).[^25^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-80){#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-80-back
.calibre1}

Much changed in the ensuing decade thanks to the rise of online data
sources that facilitate, in particular, the implementation of survey
experiments. These samples can be roughly categorized into one of three
types. First are probability internet panels where a company draws or
recruits a probability sample (i.e., every unit has a known and equal
chance of being sampled) of the population (e.g., a country), and
individuals then agree to []{#part0011_split_007.html_page_71
.calibre1}participate in periodic surveys for compensation. While
coverage and nonresponse remain challenges, these samples constitute the
gold standard; however, they come at a high financial cost. Second are
non-probability but purportedly representative internet panel samples.
In these cases, individuals opt in (e.g., via advertisements) to receive
compensation for taking surveys over time. The companies that oversee
these panels then, when hired by a researcher, use a version of quota
sampling to draw a set of respondents that matches a specified
population on key demographics [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as
having percentages of women and minorities that equal those found in the
US census. These samples vary in their ability to hit benchmarks, but
some have been demonstrated to do quite well (e.g., Vavreck and Rivers
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-508){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-508-back
.calibre1}).[^26^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-81){#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-81-back
.calibre1} These data vary in cost but almost always come cheaper than
probability samples.

[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-53
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-176 .calibre1}Third,
the most notable innovation comes from crowdsourcing labor market
platforms, with the best known being
Amazon[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Mechanical Turk (MTurk).
Researchers can directly use MTurk or analogous platforms to hire
individuals to complete tasks, including participating in survey
experiments for direct compensation. These platforms provide researchers
with cheap convenience samples that ostensibly offer more heterogeneity
than student samples. A substantial literature assesses the demographic
nature of these crowdsourcing platforms and whether experimental results
from them match those from other samples (Paolacci et al.
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-426){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-426-back
.calibre1}; Buhrmester et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-66){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-66-back
.calibre1}; Berinsky et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-41){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-41-back-2
.calibre1}; Krupnikov and Levine
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-325){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-325-back
.calibre1}; Huff and Tingley
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-275){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-275-back
.calibre1}; Mullinix et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-393-back-3
.calibre1}; Levay et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-344){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-344-back
.calibre1}; Coppock
[2019b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-109){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-109-back-2
.calibre1}). These samples also bring with them ethical concerns in
terms of survey participants not receiving a fair wage (e.g., Williamson
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-514){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-514-back
.calibre1}). Krupnikov et al.
([2021a](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-326){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-326-back
.calibre1}) offer an overview of this work, showing the rise in these
samples, as well as offering advice on how to best use student,
community, crowdsourcing, and other types of convenience samples.

[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-29
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-241
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-247
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-289
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-293
.calibre1}Technological innovation provides experimentalists with access
to other types of samples as well. For instance, while experiments have
long been done with elected officials [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
sometimes intentionally (e.g., Grose and Wood
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-235){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-235-back
.calibre1}) and sometimes naturally (e.g., Cirone and Van Coppenolle
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-98){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-98-back
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the ability to contact
them via e-mail has stimulated a relative explosion of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}elite["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiments (e.g., those who work in government; see Grose
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-233){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-233-back-3
.calibre1},
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-234){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-234-back
.calibre1}; Neblo et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-405){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-405-back
.calibre1}; Nathan and White
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-401){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-401-back
.calibre1}). Of particular note are audit experiments, which I discuss
in the [next
chapter](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0011_split_007.html_CN-bp-4-back-5
.calibre1} []{#part0011_split_007.html_page_72 .calibre1}(Costa
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-113){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-113-back-2
.calibre1}). The expansion of social media also provides
experimentalists with a data source, sometimes to recruit subjects
(e.g., Munger et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-395){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-395-back
.calibre1}), and other times to experimentally study social media
behavior (e.g., Bond et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-59){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-59-back-3
.calibre1}; Guess
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-240){#part0011_split_007.html_REFe-r-240-back-2
.calibre1}).

While all of these sampling opportunities (e.g., internet panels,
crowdsourcing platforms, social media samples) allay some of the
concerns about student samples, many still question: Do findings from a
given sample generalize (e.g., have external
validity)?[^27^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-82){#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-82-back
.calibre1} Ironically, as I explain next, the answer should be thought
of in terms of theory []{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-30
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-242
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-248
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-290
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-294
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-436
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-666
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-680
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-782
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-962
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1032 .calibre1}rather
than empirics.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0011_split_008.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_008.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_008.html_B-sec-14 .sec}
## Three Sampling Scenarios {#part0011_split_008.html_calibre_pb_8 .b}

[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-267
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-379
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-475
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-529
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-725
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-883
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-1021 .calibre1}In
essence, three scenarios exist [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as
formalized in Druckman and Kam
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-150){#part0011_split_008.html_REFe-r-150-back
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that could be the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}truth["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in
a given target population. First, it may be that the impact of the
treatment on the outcome variable (e.g., the impact of a persuasive
message on attitudes; the effect of electoral observers on fraud; the
influence of non-anonymity in allocation of funds to co-ethnics, etc.)
is homogenous, meaning the treatment has the same impact on all
individuals in the population. For instance, a persuasive message moves
policy attitudes by 10 percent among everyone, regardless of their
gender, income, race, partisanship, etc. The implication is that if one
tests the treatment only on young people, the treatment would push
attitudes by 10 percent, or if one tests it only on older people, one
would also find a 10 percent movement. The same is true if the sample
only consists of men or only included women; only low income or only
high income; only Democrats or only Republicans, etc. Since the
persuasive message treatment moves all segments of the population the
same amount, the nature of the sample becomes irrelevant. In short, with
such homogeneity, [any]{.italic} sample from the population will, with
the typical uncertainty, provide an unbiased estimate of the causal
treatment effect (and the same estimate will come from any sample).
There is no threat to external validity from any sample even when it
comes to the precise size of the effect.

A second sampling scenario is that the treatment effect in the
population is heterogeneous such that individual characteristics
moderate the []{#part0011_split_008.html_page_73 .calibre1}effect. For
example, a persuasive message about Medicare may differ across segments
of the population; it may be that the effect is larger among older
people and smaller among younger people. If one uses a sample that skews
toward older (younger) people then the treatment effect would be wrong
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, it would be overstated
(understated) since the effect is larger (smaller) among older (younger)
people. However, if the skewed sample has at least some mix of older and
younger people (i.e., some variance), then one can in essence control
for age by interacting it with the treatment variable. This is the
technique discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_008.html_CN-bp-2-back-13
.calibre1} for modeling heterogeneity by interacting the treatment with
the moderator (e.g., exposure to message X age). Doing so would result
in an unbiased estimate (with typical uncertainty) of the size of the
average treatment effect, even if the sample comes from a crowdsourcing
platform, community volunteers, or an opt-in panel. Druckman and Kam
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-150){#part0011_split_008.html_REFe-r-150-back-2
.calibre1}) use simulations to show that as long as there is just some
variance in the moderator (e.g., age), the estimate will be on target.

Here, an experimentalist controls for the sample bias that would skew a
treatment effect by interacting the source of the bias with the
treatment (with one caveat discussed shortly). Even if one uses a
perfectly representative sample but does not control for age via an
interaction, the treatment effect will be misleading, as it may look
moderate when in fact it is large for older people but small for younger
people; the heterogeneity would be masked. This would be akin to having
an omitted variable.

It follows that, in most cases, the external validity or
generalizability of a sample depends not on the composition of the
sample but rather on the strength of
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s theory underlying the causal effect
and then modeling the theory (i.e., the moderator) correctly. Scholars
spend much time empirically comparing demographic and political
benchmarks of samples when the issue ultimately concerns having a
stronger theory about moderators, and then constructing appropriate
samples when needed.

[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-669 .calibre1}The third sampling
scenario provides a caveat (e.g., the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}in
most cases["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} phrase in the prior
paragraph). A nonrepresentative (non-probability) sample becomes
problematic if it lacks variance in the moderator since, in that case,
one cannot model the heterogeneous effect. A student sample, for
example, would be problematic in the Medicare example, given it has no
variance on age to control for the sample
skew.[^28^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-83){#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-83-back
.calibre1} That said, even in []{#part0011_split_008.html_page_74
.calibre1}that case, the key is to develop a theory behind the causal
effect, state the population of interest, and choose an appropriate
sample. The example accentuates a common point of confusion
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} when it comes to estimating the
experimental causal effect, it is not essential that the sample be
representative of age [per se]{.italic} (i.e., the goal is to not
describe the demographics of the population), but rather that variance
on age
exists.[^29^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-84){#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-84-back
.calibre1} The demographics of experimental samples only matter when it
comes to moderators, given the goal to generalize the causal effect.
While this means the researcher needs to carefully develop the
[theory]{.italic} behind an experiment, it also suggests that those who
criticize the generalizability/external validity of a sample must do
more than point to its lack of representativeness of the population;
they must explain what moderator was ignored and why the given sample
was inadequate (i.e., lacked variance) to test for heterogeneous
effects.[^30^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-85){#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-85-back
.calibre1} A sample is only problematic for external validity if someone
theorizes a moderator and there exists almost no variance on that
moderator in the sample.

One could argue that much of social science theory is not suitably
developed to identify key moderators, given a common focus on general
tendencies among populations and data that lack sufficient power. My
response is twofold. First, if/when this is the case, it highlights the
importance of the inevitable conversation between theory and exploratory
data analyses to isolate moderators with the latter exploiting
methodological innovations I discuss in the next section. Second, at
best, it remains debatable where the burden lies
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} my position is that it lies on the
critic to explain why a particular sample may be problematic in terms of
moderator dynamics, as the alternative allows anyone to dismiss a sample
with virtually no []{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-670
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-268
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-380
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-476
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-530
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-726
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-884
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-1022
.calibre1}explanation.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_009.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_009.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_009.html_B-sec-15 .sec}
## []{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-469 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-877 .calibre1}Detecting Heterogeneity {#part0011_split_009.html_calibre_pb_9 .b}

Putting aside the theoretical emphasis of the prior section, one can ask
how often heterogeneous effects empirically exist in experiments on a
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_page_75
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-185
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-455
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-531
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-607
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-625
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-687
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1015
.calibre1}particular
topic.[^31^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-86){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-86-back
.calibre1} Mullnix et al.
([2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-393-back-4
.calibre1}) offer tentative evidence by comparing treatment effects
between convenience samples and probability samples for twenty-three
survey experiments, finding a remarkable rate of not just replication
but also matched effect sizes for eighteen of the
studies.[^32^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-87){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-87-back
.calibre1} This suggests no heterogeneity, or at least heterogeneity
that does not correlate with sample composition. Coppock et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-112-back-2
.calibre1}) provide a more direct test. They compare the sample average
treatment effects of twenty-seven experiments conducted on probability
samples against the same experiments on MTurk. They report vast
similarities that stem from homogenous effects in the
samples.[^33^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-88){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-88-back
.calibre1} Specifically, they explore six variables (age, education,
gender, ideology, partisanship, and race) and find none systematically
moderate the
effects.[^34^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-89){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-89-back
.calibre1} These experiments appear to fall into case 1: The nature of
the sample used is irrelevant to the estimation since the causal effects
are ostensibly constant in the population (also see Coppock
[2019b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-109){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-109-back-3
.calibre1}).

These striking results have implications not only for sample usage and
generalizability but also for understanding opinion formation (also see
Coppock et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-111){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-111-back
.calibre1}). Yet, as Coppock et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-112-back-3
.calibre1}: 12445) caution, one must not over-interpret the breadth of
homogeneity for four reasons. First, their studies only include survey
experiments, and thus the results do not generalize to studies conducted
in laboratory or field settings; and, as a general matter, survey
experiments are limited to brief interventions over short periods of
time (Sniderman
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-483){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-483-back-3
.calibre1}). Second, the study topics mostly include persuasion and
attitude formation (Coppock et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-112-back-4
.calibre1}: 12442), thereby offering no insight into heterogeneous
effects that may exist in other domains. For example, as discussed in
[Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_009.html_CN-bp-2-back-14
.calibre1}, Yeager et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-519){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-519-back-2
.calibre1}) report moderation across a set of psychology experiments
(e.g., on conformity, decision-making) by variables similar to those
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_page_76 .calibre1}examined by Coppock et al.
(e.g., age, education, income, race, gender, and region). Alternatively,
Green and Kern
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-228){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-228-back-2
.calibre1}) study heterogeneous reactions to a well-known
question-wording experiment where respondents exhibit significantly
greater support for ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}assistance to the
poor["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as opposed to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}welfare.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
The authors find stronger treatment effects among Republicans,
conservatives, younger people, and those with negative racial attitudes
towards []{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-456
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-532
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-608
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-626
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-688
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1016 .calibre1}Blacks.

[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-187
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-217 .calibre1}Third,
there may be unexamined moderators; indeed, other work on persuasion and
attitude formation suggests heterogeneity stems from political knowledge
(Druckman and Nelson
[2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-160){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-160-back-2
.calibre1}); processing style/engagement (Cacioppo et al.
[1983](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-84){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-84-back
.calibre1}); attitude strength (e.g., Taber and Lodge
[2006](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-494){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-494-back
.calibre1}); or other psychological constructs. As Coppock
([2019b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-109){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-109-back-4
.calibre1}: 624) aptly states, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}\[s\]ome
treatments of course have different effects for different subgroups
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Future disagreements about whether a
convenience sample can serve as a useful database from which to draw
general inferences should be adjudicated on the basis of rival theories
concerning treatment effect
heterogeneity.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This points again to
theory as a basis to develop expectations about heterogeneity and assess
the appropriateness of different samples. That said, I would be remiss
if not to acknowledge, as discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_009.html_CN-bp-2-back-15
.calibre1}, the availability of machine learning approaches to identify
moderators in a post hoc manner. These methods offer crucial
opportunities to systematically isolate sources of heterogeneity that
can stimulate theory development (see Ratkovic
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-449){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-449-back-2
.calibre1}) and, as mentioned, play a key role in the theory-empirical
analyses dialogue needed to develop clear hypotheses. This is the
approach taken by Green and Kern, whose findings offer guidance to
theorize about moderators and appropriate samples when it comes to
framing effects on
welfare/poverty.[^35^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-90){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-90-back
.calibre1} The usefulness of this approach for theory building, though,
does not remove the onus on those who critique particular samples for
lacking external validity from identifying reasons why they expect the
sample to be problematic.

[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-175
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-699 .calibre1}A final
point on heterogeneity comes back to a theme of this chapter
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the need to consider not just sample
validity but also variation in contexts,
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_page_77 .calibre1}treatments, and outcome
measures. Coppock et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-112-back-5
.calibre1}: 12445) explain, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}this
discussion of generalizability has been focused exclusively on who the
subjects (or units) of the experiments are and how their responses to
treatment may or may not vary [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} \[there
are\] four dimensions of external validity: units, treatments, outcomes,
and setting. In our study, we hold treatments, outcomes, and setting
constant by design.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-16
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-65
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-174
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-182
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-307
.calibre1}Identifying heterogeneity on these other dimensions likely
requires multi-study efforts. For example, Van Bavel et al.
([2016a](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-504){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-504-back
.calibre1}) show that across 100 experiments, contextual sensitivity
negatively correlates with the success of a replication attempt
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} context-sensitive studies were less
likely to replicate when conducted at a later time in a distinct
setting. In this case, the authors (6455) define context based on
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}time (e.g., pre- vs. post-Recession),
culture (e.g., individualistic vs. collectivistic culture), location
(e.g., rural vs. urban setting), or population (e.g., a racially diverse
population vs. a predominantly White
population)["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see Van Bavel et al.
[2016b](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-505){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-505-back
.calibre1}). The next step entails identifying what precisely about the
contexts moderates the success of
replication.[^36^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-91){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-91-back
.calibre1}

Blair and McClendon
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-52){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-52-back-3
.calibre1}) offer a comprehensive framework for designing research
programs [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that can be conducted
simultaneously or sequentially [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to
identify heterogeneity across contexts. This process often involves
overcoming the challenge of coordinating teams of scholars conducting
similar studies, but the potential knowledge gains are substantial. An
example of such an effort comes from the Metaketa Initiative
([https://egap.org/metaketa](https://egap.org/metaketa){.calibre1}). The
project facilitates collaborations of teams of researchers working on
related questions and using comparable interventions and measures across
contexts. Researchers conduct their individual studies in analogous
manners with a goal of formally synthesizing the findings from all
settings (e.g., six countries), which allows for the isolation of
contextual heterogeneity (e.g., Dunning et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-168){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-168-back-3
.calibre1}). The project explores thus far political accountability,
natural resource governance, community policing, and
women[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s non-electoral political
participation. Of course, coordinating institutions do not exist in many
research areas [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} but even without such
efforts, individual scholars should remain attuned to the potential for
heterogeneity from the sample, context,
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_page_78 .calibre1}treatments, and outcome
measures. As Goroff et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-225){#part0011_split_009.html_REFe-r-225-back
.calibre1}, 1) state, it ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}is time to
address this context sensitivity problem in social science research.
While we do not yet know [how]{.italic} to solve it, we believe social
scientists can make great progress by working together to build an
inference engine["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (italics in the
original).[^37^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-92){#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-92-back
.calibre1}

None of these points minimize the importance of Coppock et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s homogeneity findings. Those results
provide a stimulant, if not a call, for more systematic theoretical
exploration into heterogeneity of all types and consideration of
implications for sample selection and external validity. The central
lesson remains that the external validity of samples comes down to
theory more than empirics and constitutes just one of many dimensions of
[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-176
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-186
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-700
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-470
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-878
.calibre1}generalizability.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_010.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_010.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_010.html_B-sec-16 .sec}
## []{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-217 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-275 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-761 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-893 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-953 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-1081 .calibre1}Weighting {#part0011_split_010.html_calibre_pb_10 .b}

The discussion of sampling thus far has ignored the question of whether
one should weight experimental sample data. Weighting requires that one
obtain descriptive data of the target population, typically demographics
(Bethlehem and Callegaro
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-44){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-44-back
.calibre1}). For example, when the population includes all Americans,
one can use the US Census or American Community Survey for demographic
population figures. One then computes weights that account for each
respondent[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s probability of being
included in the sample. For example, if the population consists of 50
percent men but the sample contains only 40 percent men, then male
sample respondents will be weighted to count more in computations from
the sample (and women will be counted
less).[^38^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-93){#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-93-back
.calibre1} Survey researchers commonly use weights, even with many
probability samples, to ensure the accuracy of observational inferences
(e.g., the percentage of men who hold a particular attitude).

A downside of weighting is that it reduces statistical power. The
relevant N becomes the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}effective sample
size["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}: that is, the sample size one would
have been left with if he/she created a representative sample
[]{#part0011_split_010.html_page_79 .calibre1}with the given data (sans
weighting). This invariably smaller N leads to larger standard errors
and hence a loss of power (Piazza
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-436){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-436-back-2
.calibre1}: 97). The loss in power may partially explain why
experimentalists have neglected the topic (Franco et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-193){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-193-back
.calibre1}: 161).

[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-26
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-138
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-186
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-301 .calibre1}How
should experimentalists think about weighting? Recall the goal is to
generalize a causal inference rather than a description of the
population. Thus, the underrepresentation of a given group (e.g., men)
may be entirely irrelevant unless that group affects/moderates the
casual inference. Weighting becomes useful only if heterogeneous
treatment effects exist and the source of that heterogeneity (the
moderator) would be weighted (i.e., the group is misrepresented in
sample).[^39^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-94){#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-94-back
.calibre1} For example, weighting by sex only affects the results if men
react differently to the treatment than women. Miratrix et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-383){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-383-back
.calibre1}) investigate the impact of sample weighting with seven survey
experiments on non-probability samples. They (275) find that the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}\[s\]ample average treatment effect
estimates did not appear to differ substantially from their weighted
counterparts, and they avoided the substantial loss of statistical
power.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} In their data, heterogeneous
effects do not create problems for using non-probability samples sans
weights (286[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}287). This does not mean,
however, that researchers should always avoid using sample weights. They
can be essential in cases where researchers predict heterogeneity or
plan to engage in exploratory work to identify moderators. One can
compare weighted and unweighted samples and any difference may suggest a
moderator that a researcher can then isolate (see Miratrix et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-383){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-383-back-2
.calibre1}:
290).[^40^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-95){#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-95-back
.calibre1} That said, experimentalists only need to weight if there
exists a clear expectation of heterogeneity based on an underrepresented
group. Weighting then becomes a challenge (at times) since it requires
relevant measures of the target population (see
[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-218
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-276
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-762
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-894
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-954
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-1082
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-332
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-874 .calibre1}Hartman
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-253){#part0011_split_010.html_REFe-r-253-back
.calibre1}).
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_011.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0011_split_011.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
::: {#part0011_split_011.html_B-sec-17 .sec}
## []{#part0011_split_011.html_page_80 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-281 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-473 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-753 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-779 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-881 .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-969 .calibre1}The Need for Probability Samples {#part0011_split_011.html_calibre_pb_11 .b}

I have offered a defense against those who dismiss experimental work
based on sample composition. First, such critiques misconstrue how one
should view experiments with regard to intellectual progress
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} from a Popperian perspective, any test
can provide useful information. Consequently, outright dismissal of a
study because of its sample contradicts how many research agendas
proceed. Second, experiments aim to generalize causal inferences and
thus attending to demographic or other benchmarks of a given sample
misrepresents or misconstrues the endeavor. The focus should be on
theorizing, particularly about heterogeneous effects, rather than
critiquing the nature of a given sample. Moreover, much of the evidence
to date suggests homogeneity in at least some domains of experimental
social science. A partial caveat here concerns applied experiments where
isolating precise effects sizes is essential (and the theory is not
sufficiently developed to identify sources of heterogeneity). Third, the
sample only constitutes one dimension of external validity
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one that facilitates criticism of
experiments since any single study, regardless of method, typically
fails to generalize across the other dimensions.

In short, skeptics need to do better than point to samples that do not
represent a given population; yet, experimentalists also need to be
better in clarifying their target population and theorizing about what
they are testing. These two points highlight an irony such that the rise
of cheap convenience samples has generated an unproductive cycle:
Experimentalists often collect data before developing theory and
considering design issues (as I discuss in the [next
chapter](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0011_split_011.html_CN-bp-4-back-6
.calibre1}) and then critics dismiss these studies, but for the wrong
