reasons (e.g., because of the data source rather than the design
considerations). An experimental sample should cause concern with the
expectation of underrepresented moderators; otherwise, other topics
should receive more attention (e.g., measurement, contextual
generalizability).

Alas, this does not mean that experimentalists should abandon
probability samples, or, in the case of laboratory and field
experiments, more representative samples when possible (see, e.g.,
Lavrakas et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-337){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-337-back
.calibre1}). As mentioned, work to date explores only a few topics and
limited moderators, and in fact, rarely, if ever, looks at the
possibility of intersectional moderators (e.g., minority Democrats).
Probability samples offer opportunities to investigate heterogeneous
effects with more confidence, particularly if the samples can be large
(see Coppock et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-112-back-6
.calibre1}: 12445). []{#part0011_split_011.html_page_81
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-5
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-118
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-195
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-204
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-293 .calibre1}This
exploration can be done to isolate a priori heterogeneous predictions or
post hoc to build theory. Notably, too, when it comes to nonexperimental
work, the evidence makes clear that probability samples offer superior
accuracy on validated survey measures (e.g., sex, age, race, ethnicity)
compared to all other samples, regardless of weighting approaches
(MacInnis et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-359){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-359-back
.calibre1}).[^41^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-96){#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-96-back
.calibre1} Further, to arrive at the conclusion that non-probability
representative or convenience samples ostensibly offer a strong
inferential base, past work has engaged in comparisons with similar
studies on probability samples. Put another way, probability samples
serve as an irreplaceable baseline. Finally, applied research may
require probability samples to ensure precise causal inferences exist.
It is for these reasons that the Time-Sharing Experiments in Social
Sciences (TESS) program has been a valuable resource
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as discussed in [Chapter
1](#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-1){#part0011_split_011.html_CN-bp-1-back-2
.calibre1}, this program has allowed researchers, on a competitive
basis, to field more than 600 survey experiments on probability samples
of the US population
([http://tessexperiments.org](http://tessexperiments.org){.calibre1}).
TESS served as the basis of the data for the Mullinix et al. and Coppock
et al. comparisons. In the end, evaluating any sample depends on the
goal of the research, nature of the population, other dimensions of
external validity, and potential sources of
heterogeneity.[^42^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-97){#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-97-back
.calibre1}

One final note concerns the grossly neglected topic of panel
conditioning effects in experiments. Panel conditioning occurs when
participation in a prior study affects responses in subsequent studies.
The bulk of survey experiments now collect data from internet panels,
including some probability sample ones, where participants partake in
multiple surveys over time. While clear evidence of panel conditioning
in longitudinal surveys exists [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for
example, respondents in the General Social Survey become more likely to
report their personal income after the first survey (e.g.,
Halpern-Manners and Warren
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-248){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-248-back
.calibre1}; Halpern-Manners et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-249){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-249-back
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} scant research explores
the impact on experimental studies. This area is in need of inquiry,
especially given concerns about
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}professional
respondents["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} who participate in hundreds
of studies (e.g., Hillygus et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-265){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-265-back
.calibre1}, Stewart et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-491){#part0011_split_011.html_REFe-r-491-back
.calibre1}).[^43^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-98){#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-98-back
.calibre1} The professional class of respondents
[]{#part0011_split_011.html_page_82 .calibre1}themselves may condition
experimental effects, making panel conditions a more salient threat to
causal inference than the demographics of the
[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-282
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-474
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-754
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-780
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-882
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-970
.calibre1}sample.[^44^](#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-99){#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-99-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0011_split_012.html}

:::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_012.html_A-sec-11 .sec}
::::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_012.html_B-sec-18 .sec}
## Summary {#part0011_split_012.html_calibre_pb_12 .b}

:::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_012.html_NL-lst-10 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Experimental samples have been the source of much
critique, stemming historically from the prevalence of student samples.
The rise of internet panels [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
particularly, opt-in non-probability and convenience labor market panels
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} has provided experimentalists with new
opportunities for more heterogeneous samples when it comes to survey
experiments.
:::

::::::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} The main concern about samples, even internet panels, is
whether they generalize.

:::::: {#part0011_split_012.html_NL-lst-11 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} It is crucial to recall that the goal of an experiment is
to generalize a causal inference, rather than descriptive aspects of a
population.
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} As such, generalizing depends on whether the causal effect,
in the population, is homogenous; heterogeneous where the sample
includes variance on the moderator; or heterogeneous where the sample
includes virtually no variance on the moderator. Only the last case
would produce a biased treatment estimate, as long as the heterogeneous
effect is modeled. Consequently, even ostensibly wildly unrepresentative
samples can provide accurate estimates.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} Evaluating samples is more of a theoretical than empirical
process since it involves identifying moderators rather than comparing
population benchmarks.
:::
::::::
:::::::

::::::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} A growing empirical literature suggests that many
experimental effects are homogenous; however, this work is still in its
infancy.

:::::: {#part0011_split_012.html_NL-lst-12 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} Here, more heterogeneous samples
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} ideally probability samples
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} can be useful for post hoc
identification of moderators that can help stimulate theory building.
Such samples also serve as an important benchmark, and, in applied
domains, ensure more precise effect size estimates.
:::

::: list-item
[[]{#part0011_split_012.html_page_83 .calibre1}b]{.label2} Weighting
experimental samples may only be necessary when one expects a moderator
that correlates with what one would weight (e.g., groups
underrepresented in the sample).
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} Heterogeneity based on contexts, treatments, and outcome
measures remains underexplored; researchers should carefully consider
these dimensions when they generalize.
:::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0011_split_013.html}

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: front-matter-part
::::: {#part0011_split_013.html_A-sec-12 .sec}
# Conclusion {#part0011_split_013.html_calibre_pb_13 .a}

[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-283
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-375
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-389
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-589
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-631
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-751
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-825 .calibre1}I
conclude this chapter with an e-mail communication I received that
raised concerns about an experimental finding:

::: disp-quote
the smaller the effect size, the easier it is to think that there might
be no effect at all in a more realistic setting. To put it another way:
skeptics will deny that small estimates like these prove the existence
of a treatment effect in settings that we actually care about
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} we[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}re
probably all leaning too heavily on
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}existence
proof["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} justifications of our survey
experiments.
:::

The author of the e-mail wrote with good intention of in fact defending
an experiment and perhaps characterizing common critiques (while not
necessarily agreeing them them). Nevertheless, this message exemplifies
common problematic discussions of experiments. First, reference to the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}realistic
settings["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}settings that we actually care
about["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} misconstrues the purpose of
experiments. Matching mundane realism, which is what I imagine the
author of the e-mail has in mind, is not, with the exception of clearly
applied studies, an important evaluative dimension. Theoretical match is
what matters. Moreover, failure to be more direct about what that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}realistic
setting["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} entails makes the critique
problematic insofar as one can dismiss any study on a lack of realism.
For example, one could always say even studies using behavioral data may
not use behavioral data that correctly represent
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}typical
behavior.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

Second, all empirical tests evaluate a hypothesis and if the data cohere
with that hypothesis then that is positive proof; if not, then one needs
to reassess, perhaps considering the possibility of heterogeneous
effects, some other alteration to the theory, or rejecting the theory
entirely (or, as I later discuss, identifying problems with the
design/data collection). While some argue for stricter standards of what
the profession should view as a meaningful statistical relationship
(e.g., Benjamin et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-37){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-37-back-2
.calibre1}), that is somewhat of a distinct issue. Put another way, my
understanding of Popperian falsification is that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}existence
proofs["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} are virtually all one ever has,
since one can never definitively accept a hypothesis. This is not
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_page_84 .calibre1}to minimize the importance
of identifying the conditions under which a given effect holds (as I
will discuss in [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-5-back-7
.calibre1}), but ultimately, no empirical finding is conclusive. Third,
effect sizes from a given study or a few studies should generally be
read cautiously regardless. Given that a single study never includes
representative probability samples of all the dimensions of external
validity, the effect size from a single experiment rarely can be
generalized. As I will discuss in [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-5-back-8
.calibre1}, this explains why meta-analyses that aggregate similar
studies across settings, samples, operationalizations, measurement, and
time can be valuable.

I would, in fact, go so far as to argue that much more important than
discussions of external validity are considerations of designing
treatments that match theoretical constructs of interest and designing
studies that facilitate proper comparisons across meaningful
counterfactuals. As I will discuss in [Chapters
4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-4-back-7
.calibre1} and
[6](#part0014_split_000.html_CN-bp-6){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-6-back-4
.calibre1}, these tasks are [much]{.italic} more difficult than most
realize. The main takeaways from this chapter in terms of what scholars
should consider when evaluating experiments are as follows:

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   If one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goal is to test a policy
    intervention, mundane realism [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    resemblance to the intervention of interest
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} matters. In other cases, the focus
    should be on constructing treatments that resemble the theoretical
    constructs of interest and ensuring participants take the study
    seriously (i.e., experimental realism). In these cases, mundane
    realism matters little.

-   Validating a construct, as well as minimizing treatment confounds,
    requires empirical work via pilot testing and/or manipulation
    checks.

-   The assessment of external validity or generalizability requires
    clarifying what is being generalized (e.g., the existence of a
    causal relationship); to what one hopes to generalize; the nature of
    the sample, setting, treatments, and outcome measures; and the point
    of comparison (e.g., relative to what
    ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}other["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    hypothetical study?).

-   The assessment of whether one can generalize from a given sample
    requires much less concern about the
    ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}representativeness["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    of the sample, and much more concern about the existence of
    heterogeneous effects. Critique of a study based on its sample
    requires an accompanying specification of the variable on which the
    sample lacks variation to test for the posited moderator.

-   Representative (ideally probability) samples that facilitate
    searching for heterogeneity may offer more precision when effect
    sizes are essential (in applied studies), and also serve as a useful
    baseline. Thus, such samples play a role but are far from necessary
    for []{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-284
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-376
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-390
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-590
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-632
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-752
    .calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-826 .calibre1}most
    experiments.
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fng-3 .fn-group}
::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-56 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0011_split_000.html_FN-fn-56-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Parts of this chapter come from Druckman and Kam
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-150){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-150-back-3
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-57 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0011_split_001.html_FN-fn-57-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-227 .calibre1}A third evaluative
criterion is psychological realism, which refers to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the extent to which the psychological
processes that occur in an experiment are the same as psychological
processes that occur in everyday life["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(Aronson et al.
[1998](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-22){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-22-back-2
.calibre1}: 132). The relevance of psychological realism depends on
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s philosophy of science (c.f.,
Friedman
[1953](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-198){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-198-back
.calibre1}; Simon
[1963](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-470){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-470-back
.calibre1},
[1979](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-471){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-471-back
.calibre1}: 475[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}476; also see MacDonald
[2003](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-358){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-358-back
.calibre1}). I do not discuss it further as it has received relatively
little attention (compared to experimental and mundane realism) in work
on social science experiments.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-58 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0011_split_001.html_FN-fn-58-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} By
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}seriously,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
I mean analogous to how individuals treat the same stimuli in the
settings to which one hopes to generalize (and not necessarily
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}serious["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
in a technical sense). Experimental realism may be of less importance in
the case of policy oriented experiments, since it may reveal that in
fact individuals do not take the stimuli being tested seriously, which
itself would be a relevant finding, given the goal.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-59 .fn}
[[^4^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-59-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} This
study explored the impact of social trust on online purchasing behavior.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-60 .fn}
[[^5^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-60-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-145 .calibre1}When it comes to
applying induced value theory as part of the intervention in
experiments, a common manipulation check is for researchers to have
participants partake in practice sessions (e.g., Plott and Pogorelskiy
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-439){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-439-back
.calibre1}). Subjects are not included in the main experiment if they
fail to act in accordance with the requisites of induced value theory,
given the rewards offered (e.g., Cooper et al.
[1993](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-107){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-107-back
.calibre1}: 1309). Indeed, if they fail to do so, it is a manipulation
failure. This testing is done prior to any experimental treatments and
so, in a sense, is a pilot.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-61 .fn}
[[^6^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-61-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} A
robust literature on attention checks assesses the prevalence of
inattentiveness (Maniaci and Rogge
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-365){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-365-back
.calibre1}) and its consequences (Oppenheimer et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-419){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-419-back
.calibre1}; Hauser and Schwarz 2015).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-62 .fn}
[[^7^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-62-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Kane
and Barabas
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-306){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-306-back-3
.calibre1}) offer evidence that placing manipulation checks before or
after the main outcome measure makes little difference; however, placing
them after eliminates the possibility that the manipulation check itself
affects responses.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-63 .fn}
[[^8^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-63-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} This
approach also avoids the use of seemingly tangential odd questions,
often used for attention checks, that could confuse respondents.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-64 .fn}
[[^9^](#part0011_split_002.html_FN-fn-64-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} That
said, it may be that the relevant construct is multidimensional (e.g.,
the researcher wants to study the impact of democracies not just as
institutional systems but also all the associated features). If not, the
researcher should design the treatment to keep the confounding factors
constant (e.g., clarifying the demographic composition and socioeconomic
standings of the democracies and non-democracies) or utilize one of the
designs put forth by Dafoe et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-121){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-121-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-65 .fn}
[[^10^](#part0011_split_003.html_FN-fn-65-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} One
could also question construct validity if the construct was actual
contact; however, that may be a mistake since those who study imagined
contact make clear that the construct is not actual contact but the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}concept of contact, mentally articulated
in the form of an imagined interaction["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(Miles and Crisp
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-379){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-379-back-2
.calibre1}: 3).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-66 .fn}
[[^11^](#part0011_split_003.html_FN-fn-66-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Bisgaard
([2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-48){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-48-back
.calibre1}) suggests that measuring factual knowledge itself may have
insufficient content validity since other relevant dimensions include
attributions of responsibility for the facts (e.g., economic situation).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-67 .fn}
[[^12^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-67-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This concept is distinct from ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}ecological
validity,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which is akin to mundane
realism.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-68 .fn}
[[^13^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-68-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} On
timing, one particular salient consideration is pretreatment effects
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, whether experimental
respondents had been exposed to information analogous to the treatments
prior to the experiment (Gaines et al.
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-202){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-202-back
.calibre1}; Druckman and Leeper
[2012b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-154){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-154-back
.calibre1}; Slothuus
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-474){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-474-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-69 .fn}
[[^14^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-69-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Egami and Hartman
([2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-171){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-171-back-2
.calibre1}) draw a similar distinction in their formal framework where
they put forth testing procedures for exploring
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}effect-generalization
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} generalizing the magnitude of causal
effects \[and\] sign-generalization [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
assessing whether the direction of causal effects is
generalizable["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (1).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-70 .fn}
[[^15^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-70-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-80 .calibre1}This is not to say
effect sizes are completely irrelevant without a policy goal. As
discussed, expected effect sizes play a role in identifying the
appropriate sample size with regard to statistical power. In [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-5-back-9
.calibre1}, I also explain that while a single effect size from a given
study should be taken with great caution, the aggregation of effects
across studies plays a vital role in the accumulation of knowledge
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} my hesitancy here concerns
generalizing an effect size from a given study.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-71 .fn}
[[^16^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-71-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Westreich et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-511){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-511-back-2
.calibre1}: 439) explain that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}in few, if
any, studies [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} randomized trials or
otherwise [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} do authors report the target
population for their causal effect.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} They
introduce the concept of ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}target
validity,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which refers to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the total difference between the true
causal effect in the target population and the estimated causal effect
in the study sample["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (438). This metric
is useful insofar as it accentuates the importance of clearly defining
the population.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-72 .fn}
[[^17^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-72-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They also explore six other classic psychological experiments that had
been conducted mostly with student samples.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-73 .fn}
[[^18^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-73-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
These four dimensions appear to differ from what I emphasized in my
discussion of sampling in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0011_split_013.html_CN-bp-2-back-16
.calibre1}, where I pointed to sampling of units, contexts, topics, and
measures. However,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}settings["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
is synonymous with
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}context.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Measures can be broken out into treatments and outcome measures, and
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}topics["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
are enveloped in treatments. I opted for the distinct characterization
earlier since those are the elements substantively most relevant when
thinking about sampling (i.e., units, contexts, topics, measures), but
here I stay consistent with Shadish[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-466-back-14
.calibre1}) classic treatment.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-74 .fn}
[[^19^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-74-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} For
an example of generalizing over time, see Twenge et al.
([2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-502){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-502-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-75 .fn}
[[^20^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-75-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Shadish et al.
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-466-back-15
.calibre1}: 23[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}24) state,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}\[r\]andom selection occurs even more
rarely with treatments, outcomes, and settings \[and timing\] than with
people [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} sampling methods of any kind
are insufficient.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-76 .fn}
[[^21^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-76-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Interestingly, they also find that scholars were able to successfully
forecast which of the hypotheses being tested were more likely to have
consistent results.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-77 .fn}
[[^22^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-77-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-23 .calibre1}One also needs to be
precise about the causal inference being generalized. For example, some
critique experiments that show that exposure to a given media story
changes attitudes on the issue covered in the story. They argue that in
other settings where people can choose the media to which they are
exposed, the exposure effects diminish or disappear (e.g., Barabas and
Jerit
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-28){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-28-back
.calibre1}; Arceneaux and Johnson
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-16){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-16-back
.calibre1}). The problem here is that the initial media studies were
trying to generalize a relationship that assumed exposure (Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-398-back-8
.calibre1}:150[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}151), whereas the
critique involves a causal relationship between media choice
environments and attitudes. This is valuable and reveals a contextual
limitation of the initial studies, but it does not undermine the value
of those studies since the casual relationships being studied are
distinct.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-78 .fn}
[[^23^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-78-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-20
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-88
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-205 .calibre1}This is
similar to the idea of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}parallelism["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
in experimental economics [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}it should be [presumed]{.italic} that
results carry over to the world outside the laboratory. An honest
skeptic then has the burden of stating what is different about the
outside world that might change results observed in the laboratory.
Usually new experiments can be designed and conducted to test the
skeptic[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
statement["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Friedman and Sunder
[1994](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-196){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-196-back-3
.calibre1}: 16; italics in the original).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-79 .fn}
[[^24^](#part0011_split_005.html_FN-fn-79-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Causal hypotheses are never confirmed and evidence accumulates via
multiple tests, even if all of these tests have limitations. Campbell
([1969a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-87){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-87-back
.calibre1}: 361) states, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}had we achieved
one, there would be no need to apologize for a successful psychology of
college sophomores, or even of Northwestern University coeds, or of
Wistar staring white rats.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-80 .fn}
[[^25^](#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-80-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-169 .calibre1}An intriguing issue
that has received relatively less attention concerns the comparability
of student samples from different institutions. Lupton
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-356){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-356-back
.calibre1}) studies this issue, finding demographic and attitudinal
differences across student samples from different schools but largely
consistent treatment effects. She also finds variation in participant
dropout rates depending on whether the school has a dedicated research
pool.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-81 .fn}
[[^26^](#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-81-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} One
challenge for many of these samples, strictly speaking, is they often
have poor joint distribution issues, such as lacking a sizeable
percentage of low-income minorities.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-82 .fn}
[[^27^](#part0011_split_007.html_FN-fn-82-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
trend in using these new sampling opportunities can be seen by counting
the number of experimental articles in the [APSR]{.italic} (as displayed
in [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0011_split_013.html_FIG-fig-1-back-6
.calibre1}) that employ such
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}nontraditional
samples["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (i.e., nonstudent convenience
samples, social media, or elites). The use of these samples jumped by 6
percentage points in articles from 2010 to 2019, relative to 2000 to
2009 (52 to 58 percent).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-83 .fn}
[[^28^](#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-83-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This also is the case if one anticipates differential treatment
reactions across cultures but collects data from only one culture. This
is a particular concern given that many studies only
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-48 .calibre1}collect data from
Western, educated, industrialized, rich, and democratic (WEIRD)
societies (Henrich et al.
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-263){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-263-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-84 .fn}
[[^29^](#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-84-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As
Coppock et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-112-back-7
.calibre1}: 12) state, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}simply noting
that convenience and probability samples differ in terms of their
background characteristics is not sufficient for dismissing the results
of experiments conducted on convenience
samples.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-85 .fn}
[[^30^](#part0011_split_008.html_FN-fn-85-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Druckman and Kam
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-150){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-150-back-4
.calibre1}: 50) state that an
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}implication is that the burden, to some
extent, falls on an experiment[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s critic
to identify the moderating factor and demonstrate it lacks variance in
an experiment[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
sample.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-86 .fn}
[[^31^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-86-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Another empirical question concerns whether different types of commonly
used samples contain sufficient variance on most variables relevant to
social inquiry. Druckman and Kam
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-150){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-150-back-5
.calibre1}) investigate this question with regard to college student
samples and find that in fact, other than age, student samples do very
well, not only in terms of variance but even averages.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-87 .fn}
[[^32^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-87-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
That said, they find that survey experiments embedded in election exit
polls tend to have distinct dynamics, perhaps suggesting something about
that context or sample composition.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-88 .fn}
[[^33^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-88-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} An
alternative possibility that they rule out is that the apparent effect
homogeneity stems from similar heterogeneous treatment effects within
samples that aggregate similarly (i.e., heterogeneous effects that do
not correlate with sample composition).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-89 .fn}
[[^34^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-89-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Put
another way, the conditional average treatment effects are tightly
clustered around the overall average treatment effect in each study
version.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-90 .fn}
[[^35^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-90-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-164 .calibre1}These approaches
also can offer crucial applied insights; for example, Künzela et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-329){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-329-back
.calibre1}) identify a subgroup (e.g., people who voted infrequently in
recent elections) on whom voter mobilization mailers may have a
backlash; this finding is important for those looking to mobilize
voters. As the authors (4163) explain,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}if the number of mailers is limited, one
should target potential voters who voted three times during the past
five elections, since this group has the highest ATE \[average treatment
effect\] and it is a very big group of potential
voters.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-91 .fn}
[[^36^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-91-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-457
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-533
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-1001 .calibre1}An
example of a timing effect comes from the aforementioned Green and Kern
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-228){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-228-back-3
.calibre1}: 505) study; they report stronger treatment effects in their
aid-for-poor versus welfare experiment during the years of the Clinton
administration when welfare policy was hotly debated. Also, some
crowdsourcing data seem sensitive to whether the study is done during
the week or on the weekend (and whether it is done during the day or in
the evening) (Arechar et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-17){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-17-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-92 .fn}
[[^37^](#part0011_split_009.html_FN-fn-92-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
authors propose some intriguing meta-institutions that would extract
relevant contextual information from past and ongoing
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-458
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-534
.calibre1}[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-1002
.calibre1}studies.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-93 .fn}
[[^38^](#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-93-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
process often involves iteratively adjusting for multiple demographics.
The idea is to assign an adjustment weight to each respondent such that
those from underrepresented groups receive weights greater than 1 and
those from overrepresented groups receive weights less than 1. There are
a host of ways to specifically weight (DeBell
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-126){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-126-back
.calibre1}: 520), and caps may be placed on how much a given observation
can be weighted so that one respondent does not overly determine the
outcomes.
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-94 .fn}
[[^39^](#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-94-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Miratrix et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-383){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-383-back-3
.calibre1}: 289) explain that the population average treatment effect
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}can only be different from the \[sample
average treatment effect\] when two things hold: (1) there is meaningful
variation in the treatment impact, and (2) that variation is correlated
with the weights.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-95 .fn}
[[^40^](#part0011_split_010.html_FN-fn-95-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0011_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-47 .calibre1}I have not touched
on weighting with convenience samples such as online labor markets. Here
weighting is difficult since the sampling process is unknown (Miratrix
et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-383){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-383-back-4
.calibre1}: 276). Another challenge is that the key moderators may be
non-demographic variables (e.g., partisanship, racial attitudes). It is
difficult to weight on such factors since there are not clear population
data available. One approach, however, is to use established probability
surveys such as the General Social Survey or American National Election
Study to obtain population estimates of different dispositional
variables (e.g., Goldberg et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-222){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-222-back
.calibre1}: 21).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-96 .fn}
[[^41^](#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-96-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Probability samples also suggest a more moderate political population
(in terms of attitudes toward policy issues) than non-probability
samples (Bilgen et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-47){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-47-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-97 .fn}
[[^42^](#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-97-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
many cases the resources needed for a probability sample could be better
spent on other aspects of the study (e.g., Shadish et al.
[2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-466){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-466-back-16
.calibre1}: 386).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-98 .fn}
[[^43^](#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-98-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This is a particularly acute concern when it comes to crowdsourcing
platforms like MTurk where there are additional issues about inauthentic
responses from bots (Chmielewski and Kucker
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-94){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-94-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0011_split_013.html_FN-fn-99 .fn}
[[^44^](#part0011_split_011.html_FN-fn-99-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Professional respondents may anticipate experimental hypotheses,
creating demand effects that occur when participants change their
behavior in response to their perception of the
experiment[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s objective (Glennerster and
Takavarasha
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-219){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-219-back-3
.calibre1}: 317). Mummolo and Peterson
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-394){#part0011_split_013.html_REFe-r-394-back-2
.calibre1}), however, show that more generally, demand effects seem
unlikely, at least in political science survey experiments.
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_000.html}

::: {#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0012_split_000.html_page_85 .calibre1}4]{.label1} Innovations in Experimental Designs {#part0012_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}

## Opportunities and Limitations {.subtitle}
:::

:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0012_split_000.html_A-sec-04 .sec}
[]{#part0012_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-229 .calibre1}In this chapter, I
turn to designing experiments. I emphasize that experimental designs
need to be driven by the substantive questions being asked. While
textbooks on design elements elucidate crucial design options (e.g.,
Schneider
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-459){#part0012_split_000.html_REFe-r-459-back
.calibre1}), the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}best["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
designs are tailored to a question and context. Experimentalists should
never choose a design approach before thinking through the question
being addressed, and should always be cognizant of what they want the
design to accomplish. The concern that design choices drive the
questions being asked likely led Smith
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0012_split_000.html_REFe-r-479-back-5
.calibre1}: 15), as quoted at the start of the book, to worry that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}experiments will unduly constrict the
questions political scientists ask.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Experiments are not the right approach for many questions and, even when
they are, there are not off-the-shelf designs.

I focus on three designs that have gained prominence: audit field
experiments, conjoint survey experiments, and lab-in-the-field
experiments.[^1^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-100){#part0012_split_000.html_FN-fn-100-back
.calibre1} These constitute examples of field, survey, and lab
experiments, respectively (i.e., the main
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}types["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} of
experiments). I provide readers with overviews of each design to
facilitate use of the given approach. I also discuss limitations in
order to accentuate what each design can and cannot do. I do not mean to
sully the substantial advances offered by these methods, but rather to
remind readers that an approach works only
[]{#part0012_split_000.html_page_86 .calibre1}when it fits the question
being asked, and any approach faces constraints in terms of the
questions it can address. I conclude the chapter with a discussion of
how different experimental designs can be used in the bourgeoning area
of public []{#part0012_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-230 .calibre1}policy
evaluations.
:::
::::
::::::

[]{#part0012_split_001.html}

::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: {#part0012_split_001.html_A-sec-13 .sec}
# []{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-51 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-219 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-249 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-801 .calibre1}Audit Field Experiments {#part0012_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-31
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-171
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-189
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-243
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-989
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1087 .calibre1}Field
experiments entail the administration of the treatment in a naturalistic
setting, often unobtrusively. These studies allow for the assessment of
effects in naturalistic contexts, which is essential for applied studies
aimed to ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}whisper in the ears of
princes.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} They also can be helpful when
the experimental goal involves theory building: Field experiments allow
researchers to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}control["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
for some contextual confounds that may have been ignored or are too
difficult to account for in a survey or laboratory experiment.

Social scientists have utilized field experiments in countless settings
to study a diverse range of phenomena such as voter mobilization,
election monitoring, micro-finance programs, aid programs, educational
interventions, policy interventions, the impact of interpersonal
contact, etc. (e.g., Bloom
[2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-56){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-56-back-2
.calibre1}; Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-212-back-17
.calibre1}; John
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-300){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-300-back
.calibre1}). As I discuss further in [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0012_split_001.html_CN-bp-5-back-10
.calibre1}, there now even exists an organization that matches
researchers with organizations to collaboratively implement studies,
including field experiments (i.e., research4impact). There is also a
well-developed statistical literature on how to address inherent
challenges in field experiments, including compliance (do those in the
treatment group receive the treatment?); attrition (can those in the
study be followed for measurement?); and spillover (will those in the
treatment talk to those in the control?) (Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-212-back-18
.calibre1}).

The particular type of field experiment on which I focus is called an
audit or correspondence study. I do so, in part, because of the rapid
growth in application, but also because the issues I address with this
precise design highlight more general design principles. Audit studies
aim to document discrimination, defined as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}unequal treatment of persons or groups
on the basis of their race or ethnicity["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
or some other attribute (Pager and Shepherd
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-424){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-424-back
.calibre1}: 182). Discrimination differs from prejudice, racism, or
stereotypes as it constitutes a behavioral outcome. It can stem from
differential treatment [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} based on tastes
or aggregate statistical perceptions [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
or biased institutional rules. Social scientists have a keen interest in
documenting the presence of discrimination, given the fundamental value
of equality and the ideal that democratic government
[]{#part0012_split_001.html_page_87 .calibre1}officials treat
citizens[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} input equally (Dahl
[1956](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-122){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-122-back
.calibre1},
[1971](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-123){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-123-back
.calibre1}). Moreover, a host of nondiscrimination legal protections
based on particular attributes exist (e.g., laws against housing and
employment []{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-32
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-172
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-190
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-244
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-990
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1088
.calibre1}discrimination).

[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-35
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-417
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-757 .calibre1}Audit
studies entail researchers sending out fictitious or real but controlled
applications/messages that are identical except for randomly varied
dimensions of interest, such as the
applicant[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s/messenger[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
race, religion, age, gender, disability, etc. [Figure
4.1](#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-2){#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-2-back-2
.calibre1} displays the basic process. One first identifies the
population of interest [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in many cases
in sociology and economics, this consists of a group of employers. One
then acquires the sample, which in some cases can be a census if the
researcher obtains contact information for all units (e.g., all
employers). Next, the researcher devises, in this running example, two
resumes that are identical other than that one comes from a White
applicant and the other from a Black applicant (more on how to vary race
shortly). The researcher then sends a job application, randomly
determined, to each employer and waits for a response, such as an
invitation to interview for the job. If the response rates significantly
differ (e.g., higher response rate to White applicants), this disparity
constitutes evidence of racial discrimination in the job market, since
race constitutes the only differentiating dimension across resumes. In
short, the design relies on the statistical solution
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} via random assignment
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to the Fundamental Problem of Causal
Inference to conclude that race causes
discrimination.[^2^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-101){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-101-back
.calibre1} These studies
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}audit["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
the market []{#part0012_split_001.html_page_88 .calibre1}place for
discrimination, which can, in turn, lead to legal protections to
minimize
bias.[^3^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-102){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-102-back
.calibre1}

:::: {#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-2 .fig}
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00023.jpeg){.graphic2}

::: caption
[Figure 4.1.]{.label2}

[Audit study logic]{.label2}
:::
::::

[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-39
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-57
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-79
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-523
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-709
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-803 .calibre1}Job
audit market studies have a long history, stemming back to the efforts
of the British Race Relations Board to identify discrimination in
housing and employment in the 1960s, and fair housing audits in the
United States in the 1970s (Gaddis
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-201){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-201-back
.calibre1}). One of the more influential audit studies is Pager
([2003](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-423){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-423-back
.calibre1}). She explores the consequences of previous incarceration on
employment prospects for White and Black job seekers in Milwaukee. The
addition of incarceration as a factor alongside race allows one to
disentangle the two variables; this step is necessary because a
disproportionate percentage of the Black population is incarcerated,
confounding the two
factors.[^4^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-103){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-103-back
.calibre1} Pager assigned each employer to randomly receive (in-person)
entry level applications from two similar White job applicants where one
had a criminal record and the other did not, or from two similar Black
job applicants where one had a criminal record and the other did
not.[^5^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-104){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-104-back
.calibre1} The White pair applied for 150 jobs while the Black pair
applied for 200 jobs. The callback rates for different applicants (e.g.,
receiving an interview) appear in [Figure
4.2](#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-3){#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-3-back-2
.calibre1} (taken from her paper) where the black bars come from those
with a criminal record and the striped bars come from those with no
criminal record. Pager finds clear evidence
[]{#part0012_split_001.html_page_89 .calibre1}of racial bias
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in fact, even Whites with a criminal
record receive slightly more callbacks than Blacks without a criminal
record. The results also reveal notable discrimination against those
with a criminal record such that it decreased the likelihood from 34
percent to 17 percent for White applicants and from 14 percent to 5
percent for Black applicants. This striking evidence of discrimination
played a role in the Ban the Box effort to force companies to eliminate
questions on job applications about past felony
[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-36
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-418
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-758
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-40
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-58
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-80
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-524
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-710
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-804 .calibre1}records.

:::: {#part0012_split_001.html_FIG-fig-3 .fig}
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00024.jpeg){.graphic2}

::: caption
[Figure 4.2.]{.label2}

[Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s results]{.label2}
:::
::::

[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-236
.calibre1}Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s study is one of many
explorations into job market discrimination. Quillian et al.
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-448){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-448-back
.calibre1}) synthesize twenty-eight hiring discrimination studies,
reporting that, on average, Whites receive 36 percent more callbacks
than Blacks, and 24 percent more callbacks than Latinos. Moreover, the
authors find no change in the level of discrimination against Blacks
over the first part of the twenty-first century, and only modest
evidence of a decline in discrimination against Latinos (also see
Neumark
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-409){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-409-back
.calibre1} for a
review).[^6^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-105){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-105-back
.calibre1} Other studies explore discrimination based on gender, age,
disability, immigration status, mental health, military service,
parental status, physical appearance, religious affiliation, sexual
orientation, social class, and unemployment status, inter alia (Gaddis
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-201){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-201-back-2
.calibre1}). Audits expand beyond the job market to look at topics such
as housing inquiry responses, response to roommate requests,
doctor[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s appointment scheduling,
responsiveness from professors, and the price paid for bargained goods
(Gaddis
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-201){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-201-back-3
.calibre1}).

The bulk of more recent studies no longer use
Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s in-person approach (i.e., her job
applicants went in-person to the employers, acting as confederates), and
instead apply or send inquiries via phone, mail, online systems, and
especially e-mail. Many refer to these approaches as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}correspondence["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
studies (echoing the terminology of Internal Revenue Service tax audits
and correspondence audits). Most recent studies also do not use the
paired approach à la Pager but simply randomly assign a single applicant
to each target (i.e., non-paired) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} this
is done since in correspondence studies, all aspects but the attribute
of interest are kept completely constant (i.e., literally identical
resumes) rather than ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}largely
similar["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as in
Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s paired in-person case.

[]{#part0012_split_001.html_page_90
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-90
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-314
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-81
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-85
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-555
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-811
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-859 .calibre1}A
prominent political science application involves auditing legislative
responsiveness. In their foundational study, Butler and Broockman
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-79){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-79-back-2
.calibre1}) sent (fictitious) e-mail requests to state legislators in
forty-four states requesting information about how to register to vote
in upcoming primary elections. Each legislator received a request that
randomly varied the race and partisanship of the sender. The authors,
following the common approach, signaled race with the name Jake Mueller
for White and DeShawn Jackson for Black, pointing to the objective
correlations of those names with the given races (e.g., from census
data).[^7^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-106){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-106-back
.calibre1} They report that when emails do not signal partisanship,
legislators, overall, are roughly 5 percent less likely to respond to
the request from the Black sender
(DeShawn).[^8^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-107){#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-107-back
.calibre1} In contrast, minority Democratic legislators are dramatically
more likely (by 16.5 percent) to respond to minority inquiries. This
paper stimulated an enormous literature of more than forty studies that
consistently report racial bias in responsiveness (e.g., Costa
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-113){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-113-back-3
.calibre1}). Political scientists also have used the audit design to
study the responsiveness of local electoral officials (White et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-513){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-513-back
.calibre1}), bureaucrats (Einstein and Glick
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-174){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-174-back
.calibre1}), welfare officers (Hemker and Rink
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-261){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-261-back
.calibre1}), school principals (Pfaff et al. 2021), and more (for
reviews see Butler and Crabtree
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-81){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-81-back
.calibre1}; Nathan and White
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-401){#part0012_split_001.html_REFe-r-401-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_002.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0012_split_002.html_A-sec-13 .sec}
::: {#part0012_split_002.html_B-sec-19 .sec}
## []{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-41 .calibre1}Audit Limitations {#part0012_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .b}

Audit/correspondence studies afford much insight by providing estimates
of discrimination. They also follow a relatively straightforward design
logic. Even so, there exist fundamental limitations of the approach that
researchers need to keep in mind. I touch on three here.

[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-135 .calibre1}The first issue
concerns points of comparison [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as I
emphasized in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_002.html_CN-bp-2-back-17
.calibre1}, experimentalists need to take care when identifying the
counterfactual of interest. Consider Butler and
Broockman[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s study where their primary
goal concerns whether legislators, particularly White legislators,
discriminate against constituents based on their race. They find that
they do since, for example, minority Democratic legislators respond to
minorities 16.5 percent more often than to nonminorities, and White
Democrats respond 6.8 percent less to minorities (than to
nonminorities). Here the []{#part0012_split_002.html_page_91
.calibre1}comparison is between responses to the Black inquiry versus
responses to the White inquiry.

However, drawing further inferences becomes complicated. The authors
state that one of their motivations involves exploring descriptive
representation; in light of their results, they state,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}one of the arguments often advanced for
increasing the number of minority legislators through mechanisms such as
majority-minority legislative districts is that elected officials better
represent \[those with\] whom they share characteristics
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} our results provide direct support
for the broader argument that how effectively minorities are represented
does depend on the race of their representatives, regardless of their
party["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(472[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}473). The experimental results
support the first point in this quote. But the second point about
effective representation depending on race is less clear because one
needs to think through what it means to be
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}effectively
represented.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} To see why, consider that
the overall response rates show that White Democrats respond to 54
percent of Black requests while minority Democrats respond to 46 percent
of Black
requests.[^9^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-108){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-108-back
.calibre1} If one uses the rate of response rather than response across
experimental conditions, then White legislators may be more effective
versus minority
legislators.[^10^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-109){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-109-back
.calibre1}

This raises the question of what constitutes the appropriate comparison:
relative response across experimental conditions or total response
rates? The former suggests greater descriptive representation among
minority legislators since they respond relatively more, but the latter
suggests greater absolute responsiveness by White
legislators.[^11^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-110){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-110-back
.calibre1} Should descriptive representation be based on overall
response or differential response? This requires engagement with
normative theory. Put another way, the
researchers[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} aims were met here and they
used a reasonable point of comparison (and carefully drew their
conclusions), but in drawing larger inferences, one needs to consider
alternative []{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-82
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-86
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-136
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-556
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-812
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-860 .calibre1}points.

[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-59
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-195
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-525
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-711 .calibre1}A
similar point can be made about Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
study: She compares an applicant with a criminal record against one
without a criminal
record.[^12^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-111){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-111-back
.calibre1} []{#part0012_split_002.html_page_92 .calibre1}As with Butler
and Broockman, her chosen comparison makes sense, given the main goal of
the study. Yet, again, once one tries to draw further inferences, it
becomes tricky to identify the appropriate counterfactual comparisons.
For example, should the comparison be an ex-felon against someone with
an ambiguous background? This question is relevant as some evidence
suggests that the aforementioned Ban the Box movement has ironically led
to an increase in racial discrimination (Doleac and Hansen 2020):
Without an explicit statement of not having a criminal record, employers
discriminate by assuming Black applicants are more likely to have a
criminal []{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-60
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-196
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-526
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-712 .calibre1}record.

While concerns about the relevant counterfactual apply to any
experimental design, it can be particularly troublesome with audits
since a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}pure["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
control group does not exist [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} everyone
must receive a treatment inquiry of some sort to measure the response to
a request. Consequently, experimentalists need to carefully attend to
what comparisons support what conclusions. As is made clear by the
discussion of two of the most influential audit studies in the social
sciences, this is far from straightforward.

[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-37
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-49
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-129
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-147
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-165
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-437
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-573
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-735
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-807
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1013
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1041 .calibre1}A
second issue concerns treatment confounds/construct validity. Most
audits require signaling an attribute, such as race, to identify
discrimination based on that attribute. With race, most rely on names,
as in the Butler and Broockman study, often turning to objective data on
the frequency of name usage by racial groups. Yet, one could question
whether respondents [perceive]{.italic} the race accurately. Many worry
particularly about confounding race and class, given the realities of
the correlation between the two in the United States (Butler and Homola,
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-82){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-82-back
.calibre1}; Gaddis
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-200){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-200-back
.calibre1}). Researchers face particular challenges with audit
experiments because one cannot include manipulation checks, such as
asking respondents for their perceptions of the requestor. This
limitation then introduces a possible confound (e.g., treatments
differentially signal class) (Fryer and Levitt
[2004](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-199){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-199-back
.calibre1}).[^13^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-112){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-112-back
.calibre1} The problem becomes even more acute with perceived variance
in unobservable differences; for example, a legislator might assume the
average college educated White constituent votes at a similar rate as an
average college educated Black constituent, but may suspect greater
variance in voting among the latter group that, in turn, conditions
response (Neumark
[2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-408){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-408-back
.calibre1}). Solutions such as extensive piloting exist, but that
requires having a large enough sample pool to use.
[]{#part0012_split_002.html_page_93 .calibre1}As mentioned in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_002.html_CN-bp-3-back-7
.calibre1}, there may even be geographic variation in name perception
(Crabtree and Chykina 2018), and thus piloting might require using
heterogeneous parts of the sample and then implementing the study using
a host of names (Butler and Crabtree
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-81){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-81-back-2
.calibre1}). These same concerns apply to other attributes beyond names,
with the general point being that the inability to include manipulation
checks and the challenge of large-scale piloting constrain audit
[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-38
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-50
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-130
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-148
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-166
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-438
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-574
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-736
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-808
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1014
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-1042
.calibre1}designs.[^14^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-113){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-113-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-33
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-87
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-191
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-253
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-427
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-609
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-731
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-793
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-805
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-839 .calibre1}Third,
audit designs typically aim to document the existence of racial or other
types of societal biases. As such, their contributions remain
undeniable. Yet, if not a primary goal, the designs rarely provide much
insight into mechanisms. Consider a study that reveals racial
discrimination. That data alone cannot reveal whether the discrimination
reflects cultural racism, biological racism, racial stereotypes, or some
other mechanism (Jardina and Piston
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-296){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-296-back
.calibre1}). In the case of the Butler and Broockman study, the
mechanism could be any of those levers, or it could reflect political
stereotypes about turnout or donor
behavior.[^15^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-114){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-114-back
.calibre1} That said, some recent innovative studies provide examples of
ways to address mechanisms. One approach is to complement an audit study
with another type of experiment. For example, Quadlin
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-444){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-444-back
.calibre1}) uses an audit study to show that high-achieving (via grade
point average) women face discrimination in the job market. She then
implements a survey experiment to identify gendered standards as the
mechanism (i.e., employers privilege likeability in assessing women as
opposed to competence and commitment in evaluating men). Alternatively,
one can design a correspondence study that manipulates mechanisms within
the treatment messages. For example, Pfaff et al. (2021) document
discrimination by public school principals toward potential Muslim and
atheist students. They further show that that discrimination increases
when the requestor expresses more intense beliefs. Pfaff et al. thus
identify a possible mechanism via their manipulation: The perceived
costs/difficulty of accommodating those minority religions leads to
discrimination. Pfaff et al. were able to do this because they had an
enormous sample of more than 45,000 principals that
[]{#part0012_split_002.html_page_94 .calibre1}facilitated the inclusion
of more than a dozen conditions [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} an
option often not available (and even then, they only could study one
possible mechanism). Nonetheless, the idea behind the design sets an
example of what research can []{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-34
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-88
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-192
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-254
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-428
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-610
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-732
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-794
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-806
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-840 .calibre1}do.

[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-45
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-863 .calibre1}Each of
these limitations accentuates the importance of aligning an appropriate
design with the substantive question being asked; one must carefully
consider what the comparisons reveal, what the treatments suggest, and
what underlies a causal
effect.[^16^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-115){#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-115-back
.calibre1} One also needs to remain cognizant of the major questions
driving the research in the first place. For instance, audit studies in
political science provide insight into representation by identifying
bias in a type of responsiveness. Yet, the approach cannot speak to many
other questions about democratic representation. While some have used
audits to study if legislators change their roll call voting behavior in
response to constituents[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} communications
(e.g., Bergan and Cole
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-39){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-39-back
.calibre1}), audits offer little insight into many factors that drive
substantive representation such as overtime lobbying efforts, pressure
from party leaders, and the competitive stream of communications public
officials receive from citizens, groups, and the media. This type of
competition defines democracy but is largely outside the purview of
audits. The approach also has so far offered scant insight into another
major dimension of democracy: participation. For example, one might ask
whether responses or nonresponses to inquiries affect efficacy and
engagement among requestors. This would be a viable extension of audit
experiments (e.g., exploring the reactions of real-world auditors): How
much do such interactions affect participation and engagement? Further,
as Butler
([2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-78){#part0012_split_002.html_REFe-r-78-back
.calibre1}: 127) points out, the bias in the political responsiveness
the audits reveal raises another question of how politicians who seem to
discriminate arrive in office in the first place
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} we need to understand
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}what determines who
serves.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Audits contribute to what we
know about one dimension of democratic representation. However, they
cannot themselves address many other questions about representation.
Scholars need to always situate the design in the context of relevant
questions, and []{#part0012_split_002.html_page_95 .calibre1}recognize
the potential and limitations. Do not conduct an audit just to do so
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} think about the larger question being
asked and what new knowledge can be gained, particularly given that the
subject pools for many audits are overtaxed (i.e., there have been many
experiments []{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-46
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-864 .calibre1}on
elected officials).

The importance of ensuring a given design connects with the question
being asked applies to any type of experiment. Moreover, the limitations
highlighted, while discussed with regard to audits, constitute widely
applicable considerations. As emphasized in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_002.html_CN-bp-2-back-18
.calibre1}, experimentalists often fail to carefully justify their
points of comparison and that can undermine any design, particularly
when a pure control/no treatment condition is absent. Also, as discussed
in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_002.html_CN-bp-2-back-19
.calibre1}, measurement and treatment design can be challenging and need
particular attention with the absence of manipulation checks. Finally,
testing for mechanisms entails distinct designs that should be pursued
when such processes are vital for
advancing[]{#part0012_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-42 .calibre1}
knowledge.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0012_split_003.html}

::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::: {#part0012_split_003.html_A-sec-13 .sec}
:::::::::::: {#part0012_split_003.html_B-sec-20 .sec}
## Summary {#part0012_split_003.html_calibre_pb_3 .b}

[]{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-47 .calibre1}Field experiments
allow for strong causal claims in naturalistic settings. Most
discussions of the limitations of field experiments revolve around
internal validity challenges that arise due to noncompliance, attrition,
or SUTVA violations. Yet, one must also consider the extent to which the
given design fits the theory being tested; doing otherwise runs the risk
of arriving at a set of empirical relationships with little
understanding of the underlying processes. The opportunities and
challenges can be seen with audit field experiments.

::::::::::: {#part0012_split_003.html_NL-lst-13 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Audit field experiments offer researchers a unique
opportunity to study discrimination. The design entails sending
ostensibly realistic requests to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}receivers["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and randomly varying an element(s) of the request. Researchers can then
identify discrimination based on different elements.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Audits have been used for decades to study job market
discrimination, and more recently, to study government responsiveness.
Scholars commonly worry about the ethical and logistical challenges of
audits, as well as the potential for
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}spoiled["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
overused subject pools (see [footnote
16](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-115){#part0012_split_003.html_FN-fn-115-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

:::::::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} Yet, there also are basic design challenges.

::::::: {#part0012_split_003.html_NL-lst-14 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} Audits do not have a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}pure["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
control condition, meaning researchers must carefully consider the
points of comparison.
:::

::: list-item
[[]{#part0012_split_003.html_page_96 .calibre1}b]{.label2} One cannot
include manipulation checks in audits, and thus, ensuring construct
validity requires extensive piloting.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} It is difficult to isolate mechanisms in audit designs, and
