even when such approaches can be taken, it requires large samples.
:::

::: list-item
[d]{.label2} Evidence from audit experiments needs to be placed in the
context of the questions that drive the research in the first place
(e.g., questions of job market inequities, unequal political
representation, []{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-48
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-52
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-220
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-250
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-802 .calibre1}etc.).
:::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_004.html}

:::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: {#part0012_split_004.html_A-sec-14 .sec}
# []{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-155 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-221 .calibre1}Conjoint Survey Experiments {#part0012_split_004.html_calibre_pb_4 .a}

The []{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-127
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-139
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-303
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-411
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-581
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-627
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-713
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-809
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-933
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-963
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-975
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1063 .calibre1}initial
rise of survey experiments, in the
1980s[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1990s, stemmed partially from
technological advances. Computer-assistant telephone interviewing
facilitated the implementation of experiments within surveys where one
could include a host of conditions (since the interviewer could rely on
the computer to generate the conditions rather than tracking paper
records) (Sniderman and Grob
[1996](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-485){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-485-back-2
.calibre1}). One of the more prominent designs involves vignettes where
one varies different aspects of a narrative (Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-398-back-9
.calibre1}). Take, for example, Freese and
Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2004](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-194){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-194-back
.calibre1}) design that varies race (Black/White/not specified); past
life events (laid off/fired/prison); and current circumstance (steady
job/unsteady job) to gauge the impact of each factor on opinions about
government assistance (Mutz
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-398){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-398-back-10
.calibre1}: 55[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}56). The vignette reads
(with experimental variations in italics):

::: disp-quote
Michael is a twenty-six year old \[[Black/White/no race
specified]{.italic}\] male with a high school degree. About two years
ago, Michael was \[[laid off from work/fired from his job/sent to prison
for a felony conviction]{.italic}\]. Prior to \[[getting laid off/being
fired/going to prison]{.italic}\], Michael \[[had held down a steady job
for a few years/had trouble holding down a job for more than a few
months]{.italic}\]. Since he \[[lost his job/was released]{.italic}\],
Michael has been actively seeking employment, but has had great
difficulty landing a job.
:::

This design already includes eighteen conditions, thereby requiring a
large sample for sufficient power, and may not include all relevant
criteria people use when assessing public assistance deservingness.
Conjoint survey experiments offer a solution for some multidimensional
choice situations. These designs go back to the 1960s and 1970s (e.g.,
Luce and Tukey
[1964](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-352){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-352-back
.calibre1}; Green and Srinivasan
[1978](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-231){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-231-back
.calibre1}) and have been widely applied in marketing and economics.
They have become even more popular throughout the social sciences in the
latter part of the 2010s.

[]{#part0012_split_004.html_page_97 .calibre1}A conjoint design asks
respondents to choose from or rate (e.g., on a seven-point scale)
hypothetical profiles described with multiple attributes to estimate the
relative impact of each attribute on choice. For example, a respondent
may be shown [Table
4.1](#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-6){#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-6-back-2
.calibre1} and asked to choose which washing machine he/she would buy.
Then, the respondent receives another version, such as [Table
4.2](#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-7){#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-7-back-2
.calibre1}, and is asked again to make a choice, and so on, up to five,
six, or more
times.[^17^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-116){#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-116-back
.calibre1} The repetition vastly enlarges the data collected, thereby
allowing the experimenter to introduce a larger number of factors
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for instance, here there are six
attributes (e.g., capacity, load), and each one may have multiple
values/levels (e.g., price may include more than what is in these
figures, such as including \$400, \$598, \$648, \$700,
[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-128
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-140
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-304
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-412
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-582
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-628
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-714
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-810
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-934
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-964
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-976
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1064 .calibre1}and
\$755).[^18^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-117){#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-117-back
.calibre1}

::::: {#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-6 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 4.1.]{.label2} [Washing machine profile 1]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00025.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

::::: {#part0012_split_004.html_TN-tblw-7 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 4.2.]{.label2} [Washing machine profile 2]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00026.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0012_split_004.html_page_98
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-53
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-55
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-159
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-465
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-479
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-491
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-835
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-921
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1055 .calibre1}With
this setup, it is relatively straightforward to use regression to obtain
the average marginal component effect (AMCE), which is the effect of a
particular attribute[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s value against
another of its values (e.g., \$400 versus \$598), holding the other
attributes constant at their average values in the design (Hainmueller
et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-246){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-246-back
.calibre1}). Thus, one needs to carefully think of the relevant
baselines for each variable/attribute when presenting the results: A
given effect is relative to the specified baseline (e.g., \$598 versus
\$400) and confined to the scenario in the experiment, since other
variables stay constant at their averages in the design. One can also
identify heterogeneous AMCEs where individual attributes of the
respondents serve as moderators (Leeper et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-340){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-340-back
.calibre1}).[^19^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-118){#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-118-back
.calibre1}

That conjoint survey experiments allow for the study of many factors at
once makes them enticing. Sniderman
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-483){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-483-back-4
.calibre1}: 265) calls their broad rise in the social sciences
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}arguably the most promising design
innovation in survey experiments developed over the past
decade.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Bansak et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-27){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-27-back
.calibre1}) identify more than 120 political science applications from
roughly 2015 to 2019. The topics include voting and public opinion
(Peterson
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-432){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-432-back
.calibre1}); immigration (Hainmueller et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-244){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-244-back
.calibre1}); climate change (Bechtel et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-34){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-34-back
.calibre1}); housing (Hankinson
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-251){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-251-back
.calibre1}); roommate choice and partisanship (Shafranek
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-467){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-467-back
.calibre1}); and more. Consider Hainmueller and
Hopkins[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
([2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-245){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-245-back
.calibre1}) study on public views of what types of immigrants should be
admitted to the United States. The authors argue that, unlike prior
work, they can look at more than a few immigrant characteristics at
once. They include nine attributes (and fifty levels overall within
those attributes), asking respondents to choose which of two immigrants
they would give priority for entrance, and to rate each immigrant on a
seven-point
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}admit["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
scale. [Figure
4.3](#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-4){#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-4-back-2
.calibre1} displays one example profile from their study, while [Figure
4.4](#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-5){#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-5-back-2
.calibre1} presents their results on four of the attributes. It shows
that Americans prefer well-educated immigrants who speak fluent English
and do not come from
Iraq.[^20^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-119){#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-119-back
.calibre1} The aforementioned relative nature of conjoint results means
there is a preference against Iraqi immigrants relative to immigrants
from India (i.e., the baseline), and fluent, educated immigrants
relative to those explicitly with no formal education and
non-[]{#part0012_split_004.html_page_99
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_page_100 .calibre1}fluent English
(i.e., the comparisons are not relative to ambiguously described
immigrants).

:::: {#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-4 .fig}
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00027.jpeg){.graphic2}

::: caption
[Figure 4.3.]{.label2}

[Immigrant profile]{.label2}
:::
::::

:::: {#part0012_split_004.html_FIG-fig-5 .fig}
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00028.jpeg){.graphic2}

::: caption
[Figure 4.4.]{.label2}

[Immigrant conjoint results]{.label2}
:::
::::

[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-312 .calibre1}As prevalent as
applications to immigration are studies of voting. For example, Bansak
et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-27){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-27-back-2
.calibre1}) explore candidate preference in the 2020 Democratic primary
(i.e., preference to run against Trump), looking at age, gender, sexual
orientation, race/ethnicity, previous occupation, military service
experience, political experience, health-care policy position,
immigration policy position, and climate change policy position.
Hainmueller et al.
([2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-246){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-246-back-2
.calibre1}: 4) look at hypothetical presidential voting based on
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}factors that emerged in recent
campaigns,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} including age, religion,
college education, profession, income, race/ethnicity, military service,
and gender. Peterson
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-432){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-432-back-2
.calibre1}: 1194) studies congressional voting attributes, drawn from
prior studies and voter guides, such as party, education, gender, family
status, race, age, profession, military service, abortion policy
position, and government spending
[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-54
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-56
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-160
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-480
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-836
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-922
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1056 .calibre1}policy
position.[^21^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-120){#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-120-back
.calibre1}

The power of these designs lies in their ability to test a host of
factors in a fairly straightforward fashion. Moreover, it seems
advantageous that survey respondents appear not to engage in satisficing
behavior, such that they can handle more than ten variables/attributes,
and more than fifteen, possibly up to thirty tasks (Bansak et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-27){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-27-back-3
.calibre1}); they also seem to adjust
[]{#part0012_split_004.html_page_101 .calibre1}their information
processing efficiently as the amount of information increases (Jenke et
al.
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-298){#part0012_split_004.html_REFe-r-298-back
.calibre1}).
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_005.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0012_split_005.html_A-sec-14 .sec}
::: {#part0012_split_005.html_B-sec-21 .sec}
## Conjoint Limitations {#part0012_split_005.html_calibre_pb_5 .b}

[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-71
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-151
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-467
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-495
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-585
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1091 .calibre1}While
some have raised cautionary notes about data analysis (e.g., Leeper et
al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-340){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-340-back-2
.calibre1}), the positive aspects of conjoint experiments have swamped
consideration of limitations. Yet, at least three concerns exist that
aggregate into a danger of letting the method drive the question. First,
one must carefully identify the relevant available considerations in the
information environment. When it comes to market products, it is
relatively straightforward since the information often appears on the
products. This also proved easy in one of the most cited conjoint survey
experiments [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the Hainmueller,
Hangartner, and Yamamoto
([2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-244){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-244-back-2
.calibre1}) study that explores Swiss attitudes toward immigrants. They
supplied information about gender, country of origin, age, years since
arrival, education, integration status, and German language proficiency.
They find that a paired conjoint choice design matches actual data from
referenda that asked citizens to vote on whether individuals should be
allowed to naturalize (e.g., country of origin matters quite a bit with
penalties for those from Turkey or Yugoslavia). An often-neglected point
about these results, however, is that the provided information perfectly
matches what voters received in the referenda. In many applications such
a match is far from straightforward. Consider the aforementioned US
voting conjoint studies that differ from one another in the provision of
issue positions (e.g., health care, immigration, climate change, or
abortion and government spending) and personal characteristics (e.g.,
religion, sexual orientation). Which factors should they offer? Which
factors do voters typically encounter? One needs to be explicit about
the environment being studied and motivate the inclusion/exclusion of
different factors. That ensures sufficient construct validity of the
treatment.

Second, aside from the availability of information, one needs to
consider how people attend to information. Many studies show that people
engage in biased searches, making it unrealistic to assume that everyone
attends to the same information in the same manner (e.g., Hart et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-252){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-252-back
.calibre1}; Iyengar and Hahn
[2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-290){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-290-back
.calibre1}; Druckman et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-142){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-142-back
.calibre1}; Luo and Zhao
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-353){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-353-back
.calibre1}). Again, the Swiss example stands out, given that each
respondent received the aforementioned information (e.g., gender,
country of origin, etc.) and just that information. That worked, since
in the referenda being emulated, voters directly received the same
information (although it []{#part0012_split_005.html_page_102
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-549
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-831
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-861
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-895 .calibre1}remains
unclear that they attended to it in the same way as the experimental
respondents did). Yet, during many campaigns or similar situations,
ignoring what individuals choose to attend to seems problematic. Lau and
Redlawsk
([2001](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-333){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-333-back
.calibre1},
[2006](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-334){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-334-back
.calibre1}) made this point long ago; specifically, they argued that if
one wants to study political campaigns or analogous environments, a
static approach that exposes respondents to an information board akin to
a conjoint table serves as a poor analog. A much better approach
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and the one they developed
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} has respondents navigating and
choosing information from a large amount of streaming information (i.e.,
a dynamic environment). I do not mean to suggest that studies must
incorporate attention-seeking behavior into their designs; rather,
experimentalists using conjoint designs need to consider likely
availability and attention when developing stimuli to match the
situation of
interest.[^22^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-121){#part0012_split_005.html_FN-fn-121-back
.calibre1}

Third, in many cases, individuals lack the motivation and/or opportunity
to process multiple dimensions as they are asked to do in conjoints
(e.g., Fazio
[1995](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-183){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-183-back
.calibre1}). Ironically, discussions of conjoint experiments often focus
on whether respondents engage in satisficing behavior if asked too much
(e.g., Hainmueller et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-244){#part0012_split_005.html_REFe-r-244-back-3
.calibre1}: 2400). Yet, it may be that satisficing captures how people
make decisions. Here again, the Swiss study is exceptional as it
involved a personally high salience choice (i.e., about who would live
in the country) that likely stimulated motivation. The question going
forward should be whether the situation under study generates the
assessment of all the dimensions offered in the conjoint
design.[^23^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-122){#part0012_split_005.html_FN-fn-122-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0012_split_005.html_page_103 .calibre1}The limitations I have
highlighted point to design considerations that apply to any survey
experiment. Experimentalists need to construct treatments that
operationalize the theoretical concepts of interest (see discussion of
designing treatments in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_005.html_CN-bp-3-back-8
.calibre1}). Survey experiments, in most cases, also presume a captive
audience who receives the information being tested. That is not
problematic on its face, but experimentalists need to take care in
specifying the context to which they want to
[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-550
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-832
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-862
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-896
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-72
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-152
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-466
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-468
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-492
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-496
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-586
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-1092
.calibre1}generalize.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0012_split_006.html}

::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::: {#part0012_split_006.html_A-sec-14 .sec}
:::::::::::: {#part0012_split_006.html_B-sec-22 .sec}
## Summary {#part0012_split_006.html_calibre_pb_6 .b}

[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-65
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-153
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-157
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-583 .calibre1}Few
designs have so quickly captured the interest of social scientists as
conjoint survey experiments. The attraction comes from the ability to
introduce many variables and retain sufficient power to make inferences.
Yet, the validity of these designs remains unclear, given that the main
case of verification comes from a Swiss referenda study that contains
qualities that differ from other applications in terms of the
availability of information and the salience of the
choice.[^24^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-123){#part0012_split_006.html_FN-fn-123-back
.calibre1} In fields where conjoints have a longer history, scholars
have recognized concerns about introducing too many variables without
theoretical motivation. For example, Ben-Akiva et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-36){#part0012_split_006.html_REFe-r-36-back
.calibre1}: 1) state, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}The promise of
stated preference experiments is that they can provide deeper and
broader data on the structure of consumer preferences than is obtainable
from revealed market observations, with experimental control of the
choice environment that circumvents the feedback found in real market
equilibria. The risk is that they give pictures of consumers that do not
predict real market behavior.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} McFadden
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-370){#part0012_split_006.html_REFe-r-370-back
.calibre1}: 161[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}162) identifies a set of
conditions under which conjoint designs work best and these include
ensuring the alternatives are ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}fully
described["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} but not
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}overly
described.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} In sum, one should arrive at
a conjoint design when the question being asked and theory offered
suggest a decision-making environment where individuals have the given
information available, access it, and process it in a multidimensional
fashion. Testing extra factors simply because the design allows for it
impedes theoretical development [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as it
makes for a stimulus that has poor construct validity. This is the case
for any survey experimental design, regardless of whether it is a
conjoint.

::::::::::: {#part0012_split_006.html_NL-lst-15 .list3}
::: list-item
[[]{#part0012_split_006.html_page_104 .calibre1}(1)]{.label2} The
conjoint survey design offers experimentalists the opportunity to
incorporate a large number of factors by asking respondents to
repeatedly rate hypothetical profiles with multiple attributes.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Inferences from the design need to be interpreted in
terms of the (all else constant) relative impact of a change in a given
value against the designated baseline. This approach makes choosing the
baseline important, since in essence it serves as the control
comparison.
:::

::::::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} When using the design, experimentalists should ensure the
situation about which they are theorizing:

:::::: {#part0012_split_006.html_NL-lst-16 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} includes information analogous to that in the experiment;
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} prompts individuals to access information in a manner that
echoes how they do so in the experiment; and
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} stimulates individuals to process information in a way
similar to how they do so in the experiment.
:::
::::::
:::::::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} Many decisions entail individuals accessing little
information and processing it in a heuristic manner, and in such cases,
it remains unclear that a conjoint design is
[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-66
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-154
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-158
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-584
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-156
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-222
.calibre1}appropriate.
:::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::
:::::::::::::
::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_007.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0012_split_007.html_A-sec-15 .sec}
# []{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-545 .calibre1}Lab-in-the-Field Experiments {#part0012_split_007.html_calibre_pb_7 .a}

[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-114 .calibre1}Laboratory
experiments offer researchers incomparable control that facilitates
addressing the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference (e.g., using the
scientific solution when applicable), and can bolster experimental
realism (e.g., attention can be monitored). However, experiments in the
lab also typically face substantial logistical challenges, such as
coordinating with participants, monitoring treatment exposure,
establishing the desired context, etc. Moreover, the nature of
traditional laboratory experiments means reliance on convenience samples
that can come to the lab. For this reason, researchers have sought to
import the advantages of these designs to other settings by
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}bringing the
lab["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to a given population. This practice
is known as a lab-in-the-field experiment:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}one conducted in a naturalistic
environment targeting the theoretically relevant population but using a
standardized, validated lab paradigm["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(Gneezy and Imas
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-221){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-221-back
.calibre1}: 440).

[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-183
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-539
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-737
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-879
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-967 .calibre1}While
social scientists have increasingly used lab-in-the-field experiments
(e.g., Enos and Gidron
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-178){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-178-back
.calibre1}), their usage comes nowhere close to audits and conjoints.
This scarcity likely reflects the aforementioned challenges to
implementing laboratory experiments combined with even more hurdles when
one moves to the field. Such difficulties include ensuring sufficient
variance in the sample being studied (as discussed
[]{#part0012_split_007.html_page_105 .calibre1}shortly); extensive
piloting with convenience and targeted samples; recruiting a
sufficiently large sample in the given location; accounting for safety
and ethical concerns; forming and training a team to travel to the
location for implementation; acquiring the needed budget; investing the
time needed for data collection, which sometimes means running sessions
with individuals or very small groups; confirming the literacy of the
sample when necessary; and obtaining relevant permissions and involving
the community when necessary (Eckel and
[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-184
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-540
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-738
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-880
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-968 .calibre1}Candelo
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-170){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-170-back-2
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-177
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-181
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-277
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-439
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-471
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-535
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-611
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-775
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1043 .calibre1}These
hurdles mean investing in a lab-in-the-field study should not be taken
lightly [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it is not worth testing a
proposition outside of a nonmobile (e.g., campus) laboratory just to
introduce heterogeneity for the sake of doing so. As detailed in
[Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_007.html_CN-bp-3-back-9
.calibre1}, when estimating an experimental treatment effect, the nature
of the sample of units matters only when (1) heterogeneous effects (that
correlate with how the sample is drawn) exist; and (2) either one cares
about a precise effect size or insufficient variance on the moderator
exists.[^25^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-124){#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-124-back
.calibre1} Put another way, one reason to use the method is to exploit
the control of the lab while also ensuring sample variance that would
not otherwise be available. A second reason concerns contextual
variation. Experiments conducted in a single location can only go so far
in varying situational factors. Lab-in-the-field studies can overcome
this limitation by implementing studies in distinct locations that
differ in key ways. Here, one exploits context, rather than unit,
variability. Next I provide an example of each
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}type["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} of
lab-in-the-field study: sampled focused studies and context focused
studies.[^26^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-125){#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-125-back
.calibre1}

Kim
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-311){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-311-back
.calibre1}) explores the question of why Americans continue to believe
in the prospects of upward mobility despite growing income inequality
and evidence of static or downward mobility. She theorizes that exposure
to entertainment television programs that use a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}rags-to-riches["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
narrative (e.g., America[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Got Talent,
Shark Tank, American Ninja Warrior, Toy Box) lead Americans to hold
mobility beliefs. She offers suggestive evidence from correlational
surveys, and then turns to an []{#part0012_split_007.html_page_106
.calibre1}experiment to pin down the causal effect. In so doing, she
recognizes the possibility of heterogeneous treatment effects, since
those with preexisting beliefs in the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}American
Dream["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} may be more susceptible to
priming. Further, Republicans who tend to endorse individualism may
exhibit stronger reactions. Kim therefore faces a design dilemma, since
she seeks the control of a laboratory experiment (where she can ensure
exposure to the stimuli [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} i.e.,
programs) but also needs a sample that varies in terms of prior beliefs
and partisanship. On the latter point, she points out that obtaining
such a sample in a liberal, metropolitan city (where she was located) is
challenging. She therefore turns to a lab-in-the-field
experiment.[^27^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-126){#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-126-back
.calibre1} She used a mobile media laboratory (with chairs, television
screens, and tablet computers) in three counties where the partisan
presidential vote in 2016 was split nearly 50 percent/50 percent. She
recruited participants from farmers markets, flea markets, and summer
festivals, and obtained a heterogeneous partisan mix of people who seem
to have faced some economic hardship but may have some standing beliefs
in mobility (i.e., the American Dream). She randomly assigned
respondents to watch a control broadcast (Cesar 911) or a treatment that
mixed in various segments of the aforementioned reality television
shows.[^28^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-127){#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-127-back
.calibre1}

Kim finds that exposure to the rags-to-riches reality television program
leads the audience to increase their beliefs in the prospect of upward
mobility (by about 6 percent). Moreover, she finds heterogeneity such
that, relative to Democrats, Republicans are much more strongly affected
by the programing. Further, exposure significantly affects those who
previously believed anyone who works hard can get ahead, while
backfiring on those who disagreed that anyone who works hard can get
ahead (i.e., those who were originally pessimistic about the American
Dream). Kim[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s study highlights the value
of a lab-in-the-field experiment, as she obtained a sample that allowed
her to isolate differential causal effects while ensuring valid delivery
of treatments. The results make clear that future work on economic
mobility and entertainment television needs to attend to sample
[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-182
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-278
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-440
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-472
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-536
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-612
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-776
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1044
.calibre1}variance.

[]{#part0012_split_007.html_page_107 .calibre1}A
[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-109
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-425
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-445
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-587
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-925
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1057
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1077 .calibre1}second
reason to use lab-in-the-field experiments is to exploit variation in
contextual exposure. Consider, for example, Gilligan et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2014) study of civil war and
social cohesion. The authors posit that, on the one hand, fatal violence
from civil wars could increase collective social action via a purging
mechanism (fewer social individuals leave the area) and a collective
coping mechanism (people band together due to a shared bad experience).
They note that, alternatively, violence could depress cohesion due to an
ensuing lack of trust and the destruction of civic associations. Testing
the causal impact of violence requires identifying otherwise analogous
people who differ only in their exposure to violence; that is, finding a
way to ensure homogeneity and the scientific solution to the problem of
causal inference. The authors turn to a lab-in-the-field study in Nepal
(in 2009[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2010). They (609) find a set of
matched communities that strongly resemble one another (e.g., in terms
of region, military control, timing of war exposure, ethnic and caste
composition, socioeconomic development, etc.), other than exposure to
low or high levels of violence.

They then went to these communities (setting up
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}labs["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in
the field) and asked participants to take part in a series of economic
games that measure
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}sociality.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
They included measures of altruism (i.e., the dictator game where
individuals give money to a partner); trust (i.e., where an individual
gives an amount of money to a partner that is tripled and the partner
decides how much to return, if any); and public goods contribution
(i.e., individuals can keep an allocated amount for themselves or share
it with others such that if everyone shares, everyone would be better
off). Higher values in each game indicate more pro-social outcomes. The
authors find substantially more pro-social outcomes in the
violence-affected communities relative to those with less violence. For
example, compared to those in nonviolent communities, the respondents in
violence-affected communities displayed about 13 percent more altruism
(i.e., sent about 13 percent more in the dictator games) and 35 percent
more trust (i.e., sent about 35 percent more in the trust game). These
findings constitute clear evidence in favor of the first mechanism that
violence increases collective social action, suggesting a way to build
after violent experiences.

From an experimental perspective, this study shows how one can exploit
the control of the laboratory to take advantage of natural variance that
occurs in the field. The researchers do not control the independent
variable (e.g., violence), but they still can make a casual inference
about its effect due to the controlled setting
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} à la the scientific solution to causal
inference. A fairly robust literature, particularly in economics,
employs []{#part0012_split_007.html_page_108
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-136
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-261 .calibre1}this
approach. The Henrich et al.
([2005](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-262){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-262-back-2
.calibre1}) study on self-interest across cultures, discussed in
[Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_007.html_CN-bp-2-back-20
.calibre1}, is a well-known example (for a detailed review, see Eckel
and Candelo
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-170){#part0012_split_007.html_REFe-r-170-back-3
.calibre1}). Researchers using this approach pay high costs for
implementation (i.e., gaining access to the communities, recruiting,
ensuring appropriate translations, etc.), but the payoffs can be
enormous in helping to understand societal forces that ostensibly belie
[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-110
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-178
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-426
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-446
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-588
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-926
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1058
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1078
.calibre1}experimentation.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0012_split_008.html}

:::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: {#part0012_split_008.html_A-sec-15 .sec}
::::::: {#part0012_split_008.html_B-sec-23 .sec}
## Summary {#part0012_split_008.html_calibre_pb_8 .b}

[]{#part0012_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-543 .calibre1}This section of
the experimental design chapter differs from the former two; in those
cases, I focused on the appropriate usage of popular designs, noting
limitations and accentuating the importance of question-and-theory
driven research. With lab-in-the-field designs, the
challenges/limitations become apparent quickly. Caution should be taken
in terms of when to make the sizeable investment in these designs
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} they can be useful to reach
populations that contain crucial variance, or to take advantage of
variance in societal conditions to test the effects of those
differences.[^29^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-128){#part0012_split_008.html_FN-fn-128-back
.calibre1} In these cases, lab-in-field experiments serve as a powerful
method.

:::::: {#part0012_split_008.html_NL-lst-17 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Lab-in-the-field experiments entail using a standardized
experimental laboratory approach in a field setting to target a
population. It allows for strong control over relevant populations.
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Lab-in-the-field experiments can be particularly useful
to exploit needed variance in samples or contexts.
:::

::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} The challenges inherent in conducting lab-in-the-field
experiments mean they should be used when the sample or contextual
variance is not otherwise available to test theoretically informed
hypotheses.
:::
::::::

By pointing to the implementation challenges, I do not mean to dissuade
researchers from using lab-in-the-field experiments. More generally,
there seems to be a decline in the use of laboratory experiments of any
kind (Rogowski
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-453){#part0012_split_008.html_REFe-r-453-back-2
.calibre1}). Yet, researchers would benefit from more appreciation of
the fact that any type of laboratory experiment provides control vital
to ensuring experimental realism, addressing the assumptions underlying
casual inference, and understanding the nature of contextual factors.
[]{#part0012_split_008.html_page_109 .calibre1}As detailed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_008.html_CN-bp-2-back-21
.calibre1}, these advantages probably dwarf mundane realism
considerations that likely play a role in the decline in laboratory
[]{#part0012_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-544
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-546
.calibre1}experiments.
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_009.html}

:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: {#part0012_split_009.html_A-sec-16 .sec}
# []{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-113 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-661 .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-741 .calibre1}Using Experiments to Study Policy {#part0012_split_009.html_calibre_pb_9 .a}

[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-87
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-93 .calibre1}One trend
that I have thus far ignored is the growth in demand for experiments
from outside organizations. While this has been the case in some
disciplines, such as economics, for some time (e.g., Glennerster and
Takavarasha
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-219){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-219-back-4
.calibre1}; Karlan and Appel
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-308){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-308-back
.calibre1}), it is a more recent trend in other disciplines. This often
involves nongovernmental organizations collaborating with social
scientists to assess the impact of interventions (e.g., Levine
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-347){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-347-back
.calibre1}). This work sometimes focuses on privately motivated programs
(e.g., educational programs), and other times involves public policy
evaluations. In this section, I offer a brief coda by discussing the
design of policy oriented experiments that often, but not always,
involve partnerships with organizations.

The idea of using experiments to study policy goes back at least to
Campbell[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1969b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-88){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-88-back-2
.calibre1}: 409) plea that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}nations
should be ready for an experimental approach to social reform, an
approach in which we try out new programs designed to cure specific
social problems, in which we learn whether or not these programs are
effective, and in which we retain, imitate, modify, or discard them on
the basis of apparent effectiveness on the multiple imperfect criteria
available.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Campbell
([1969b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-88){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-88-back-3
.calibre1}) recognizes that in many cases the ideal of random assignment
is neither politically feasible nor morally justifiable. That reality
led him to emphasize
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}quasi-experimental["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
approaches. As explained in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_009.html_CN-bp-2-back-22
.calibre1}, this means using the scientific solution to causal inference
by controlling for differences or assuming that non-randomly assigned
units are equivalent. One famous example involves comparing the impact
of Connecticut[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s crackdown on speeding in
1955, such that there were fewer fatalities after than before (this
assumes nothing else changed).

Generally, when it comes to policy experiments, one can differentiate
the solution taken to causal inference [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
statistical/random or scientific, which for this section I assume
involves imperfect control (i.e., quasi-experimental). The outcomes of
interest might be the impact of a policy, à la Campbell, or
policymakers[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} decisions. [Table
4.3](#part0012_split_009.html_TN-tblw-8){#part0012_split_009.html_TN-tblw-8-back-2
.calibre1} displays the possibilities. In this framework,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}policymaker["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
refers to governmental actors, [or]{.italic} nongovernmental actors that
seek to provide services or information (e.g., nongovernmental
organizations (NGOs)).

::::: {#part0012_split_009.html_TN-tblw-8 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 4.3.]{.label2} [Using experiments to study policy]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00029.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

[]{#part0012_split_009.html_page_110
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-11
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-73
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-134
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-181
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-272
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-286
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-94 .calibre1}In this
configuration, audit studies of elected officials, as discussed earlier
in this chapter, are an example for cell
2.[^30^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-129){#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-129-back
.calibre1} Alternatively, one can employ random assignment to study the
behavior of members of an NGO or analogous policy-relevant organization.
For example, Han
([2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-250){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-250-back
.calibre1}) worked with a professional organization to send randomly
varied versions of messages to doctors, encouraging them to sign a
petition in support of the Affordable Care Act (in 2011 when the act was
being challenged on various fronts). She finds, relative to a generic
message, a message emphasizing
recipients[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} goals (by referencing their
past actions), and, even more so, their personal goals (by referencing
specific past statements they made) increase the likelihood of signing
the petition. Han also explores how messages affect the likelihood of
group members recruiting others to take policy action and attending a
meeting. Overall, she shows that organizations can use relational
messaging (e.g., connecting to people[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
personal beliefs, past history) approaches to stimulate actions that
have consequences for public policy, in this case, health
care.[^31^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-130){#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-130-back
.calibre1} Another example is Campbell and Spilker
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-86-back
.calibre1}) who implemented a survey experiment with individuals working
for donor organizations (e.g., NGOs that offer humanitarian aid). In
violence scenarios, donors tend to reduce budgetary and development aid
and increase transitional and humanitarian aid. They do the reverse in
peaceful scenarios. These studies employ random assignment to isolate
factors that affect those who shape and make policy.

[]{#part0012_split_009.html_page_111
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-30
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-52
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-125
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-126
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-132
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-258
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-310 .calibre1}The other
three cells have received relatively less attention. Cell 4 includes
governmental responses to natural events such as disasters or more
slow-moving phenomena such as climate change. For example, local
governing bodies have implemented policies to respond to climate change
events at distinct points in time and in different ways. This work
reveals how institutional features of governments moderate policy
response once climate change conditions hit a salient point (e.g.,
Bulkeley and Broto
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-67){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-67-back
.calibre1}). Alternatively, government procurement approaches seem to
depend on external events such as variations in marketplace forces
(e.g., Bandiera et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-25){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-25-back
.calibre1}), and government spending can depend on near-random
variations in population size and institutional rules (Egger and
Koethenbuerger
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-173){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-173-back
.calibre1}).[^32^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-131){#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-131-back
.calibre1}

An example of studying nongovernmental organizational responses using
the scientific approach (cell 4) is
Crawfurd[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-117){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-117-back
.calibre1}) study of Latter-day Saints missionaries. Church officials
assign missionaries in a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}quasi-random["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
fashion using some information in a database but that is ultimately
based on a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}revelation.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Crawfurd focuses on assignments to high-income European countries or
low- and middle-income Asian, African, or Latin American countries. He
invokes a unit homogeneity assumption by presenting background
differences between those assigned, showing only minor differences
(e.g., regarding age, gender, language, prior countries visited,
standardized test scores). He then reports that those assigned to
African countries later report more interest in global development, and
more policy actions such as donating and volunteering with international
nonprofits.

Cell 1 refers to the situation where the government or another
policymaking body implements a policy in a literally random way. Social
scientists [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} other than well-known
Vietnam lottery studies (e.g., Erikson and Stoker
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-180){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-180-back
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} have not fully exploited
these events (Grose and Wood
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-235){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-235-back-2
.calibre1}). Examples include random assignment of the implementation of
federal election laws (Grose and Wood
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-235){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-235-back-3
.calibre1}); audits of lobbying disclosures (Wood and Grose 2020); and
random assignment of land distribution taken from indigenous peoples
(Hall et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-247){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-247-back
.calibre1}). As Grose
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-234){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-234-back-2
.calibre1}) emphasizes, these provide unparalleled opportunities to
assess policy effects and, even if not that frequent when it comes to
governments, deserve more attention from scholars.

[]{#part0012_split_009.html_page_112
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-3
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-75
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-125
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-305
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-401
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-511
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-521
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-623
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-663
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-817
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1033 .calibre1}This
type of random assignment policy experiment (cell 1) occurs more
frequently with NGOs or nonprofits who knowingly randomize to study
interventions. For example, Jayachandran et al.
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-297){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-297-back
.calibre1}) worked with a conservation nonprofit (Chimpanzee Sanctuary
and Wildlife Conservation) that randomly assigned payments for ecosystem
services to forest-owning households in Uganda if they conserved their
forest. They find that tree cover declined less in villages that
received the payment versus not. Another example is Blattman et al.
([2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-55){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-55-back
.calibre1}) who worked with the United Nations High Commission for
Refugees and an NGO, the Justice and Peace Commission, to study the
impact of an alternative dispute resolution educational workshop. They
randomly assigned to some communities (in Liberia) to receive the
workshop (i.e., 86 of 246 towns randomly received the workshop
intervention). The authors find that a year later, towns where workshops
took place reported a higher resolution of land dispute and less
violence. On the other hand, they also find an increase in the use of
illegal extrajudicial punishments and some nonviolent disputes. A
burgeoning literature takes similar approaches to look at the impact of
nongovernmental policy interventions (see Matanock
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-367){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-367-back
.calibre1}).

Finally, cell 3 is a path taken with some regularity by economists who
exploit variation in policy implementation to isolate its effects. Other
disciplines have ostensibly made less use of such designs. An example of
the design is the Moving to Opportunity experiment implemented by the US
Department of Housing and Urban Development in
1994[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1997. They chose five cities
(Baltimore, Boston, Chicago, Los Angeles, and New York) to study the
impact of housing on economic and health outcomes among more than 4,000
low-income families. They had hoped to employ a random assignment
experiment (cell 1) with randomly selected families staying in
low-income residencies, having the option of moving to a nicer apartment
in close proximity to their current neighborhoods (Section 8 treatment),
or moving to a neighborhood with a poverty rate less than 10 percent.
Since a nontrivial number of families assigned to the treatments
declined to move, the design falls into cell 3 (comparing
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}similar["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
families across conditions à la the scientific
approach).[^33^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-132){#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-132-back
.calibre1} Initial results, 4[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}7 years
after implementation, suggested few clear effects on employment or
earnings, and marginal effects on
families[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} mental health. However, later
results revealed that moving to higher income neighborhoods had
[]{#part0012_split_009.html_page_113 .calibre1}dramatic positive effects
on children who were young (less than thirteen years old) when moving
(e.g., higher income, more likely to attend college, better health on
some indicators) but negative, albeit not large, effects on older
children[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-4
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-76
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-126
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-306
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-402
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-522
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-624
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-664
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1034 .calibre1}
(Liebman et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-348){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-348-back
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-35
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-92
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-109
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-116
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-185
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-313
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-512
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-818 .calibre1}Gay
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-203){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-203-back
.calibre1}) takes advantage of the policy experiment to isolate
political effects, showing that moving negatively affected voter
turnout, with it being higher among non-movers, followed by the Section
8 movers, followed by the new neighborhood movers. This effect stems
from the new costs of political participation (e.g., finding
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s polling place) and decreased social
mobilization (e.g., socialization with
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s neighbors declined, which can
diminish participation). Gay[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s work is a
rare example of a political scientist using near random variation in
policy implementation. Other underexplored examples would be the impact
of state changes in minimum wage (e.g., does it change political
beliefs?) (e.g., Card and Krueger
[1994](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-92){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-92-back
.calibre1}) and the impact of the overtime rollout of the food stamp
program (e.g., does it affect political engagement? e.g., Hoynes et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-272){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-272-back
.calibre1},
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-273){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-273-back
.calibre1}).

Another design that falls into cell 3 involves looking at pre- and
post-intervention behaviors while also including a comparison group. For
example, León et al.
([2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-342){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-342-back
.calibre1}) study an NGO intervention in rural India that provided
information about family planning. They did not randomly assign the
intervention, instead looking at pre- and post-intervention behavior in
an area that received the intervention and a comparable albeit not
equivalent area that did not (e.g., assuming causal transience and unit
homogeneity). They find the intervention increased
women[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s beliefs about their power
regarding money and social relationships. This empowerment seemed to
help with meeting contraception needs. Another nongovernmental example
is Gilligan et al.
([2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-217){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-217-back
.calibre1}), who use an exogenous shock where one of three NGOs
implementing a reintegration package was not ready to start until a year
later, in post[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}civil war Burundi. They
find the program results in a 20[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}35
percent reduction in poverty but did not help political reintegration.

I do not offer a formal summary section. I reviewed these studies with
the hope of stimulating more experiments to explore policy and those
involved in policymaking. While such studies may not fully satisfy
Campbell[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1969b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-88){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-88-back-4
.calibre1}: 409) plea for ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}an
experimental approach to social reform,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
the reviewed work demonstrates enormous
potential.[^34^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-133){#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-133-back
.calibre1} As this []{#part0012_split_009.html_page_114
.calibre1}work[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-213 .calibre1}
proceeds, an aspirational goal is to consider the full policy feedback
process [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} from forces that drive
government officials or NGOs to implement a policy, to the effects of
that policy on constituents and then back to how those making policy
react to constituents. For instance, one could implement a dispute
resolution intervention, study its effects on constituents, and examine
constituents[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} subsequent impact on
policymakers, etc. (e.g., Mettler and Soss
[2004](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-375){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-375-back
.calibre1}). When do policies lead to backlash among constituents that
then causes those making policy to retreat, as opposed to generating
momentum in the direction of the initial policy? Is there push back
against too many dispute resolution programs in a developing country or
do the programs build momentum such that demand for them increases?
Another aspirational goal is to continue to address, as discussed in
[Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_009.html_CN-bp-3-back-10
.calibre1}, the inevitable challenge of scaling results to inform
policymakers who often face implementation challenges (Al-Ubaydli et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-5){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-5-back-3
.calibre1},
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-6){#part0012_split_009.html_REFe-r-6-back-3
.calibre1}, 2021)[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-114
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-662
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-742 .calibre1}.
::::::
:::::::
::::::::

[]{#part0012_split_010.html}

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0012_split_010.html_A-sec-17 .sec}
# Conclusion {#part0012_split_010.html_calibre_pb_10 .a}

With []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-225 .calibre1}experiments
now being a widely accepted methodology in most of the social sciences,
researchers often look to new designs for innovation. This can be
valuable when the designs allow for the identification of previously
undocumented causal relationships and/or theory development. Yet, the
end goal remains unchanged: to accumulate knowledge to address relevant
social science questions. It is crucial to not jump to designs for their
novelty, but only to turn to them when they offer an advantage over what
could otherwise have been
done.[^35^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-134){#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-134-back
.calibre1} There is no question the designs covered in this chapter
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} audit field experiments, conjoint
survey experiments, and lab-in-the-field experiments
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} have contributed in major ways to
multiple literatures. In that sense, the incorporation of these
approaches highlights just how expansive experiments can be. Yet,
scholars must move carefully to use these designs when appropriate, and
in so doing, recall the basic lessons covered in the prior
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_page_115 .calibre1}chapters.
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-9
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-14
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-42
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-44
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-101
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-137
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-139 .calibre1}For
example, in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0012_split_010.html_CN-bp-2-back-23
.calibre1}, I emphasized the importance of carefully considering points
of comparison in an experiment. Audit experiments complicate identifying
the relevant baselines, in part, because they lack a pure control
condition, as was made clear in the discussion of
Pager[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s and Butler and
Broockman[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s classic studies; which
baseline one uses can change what one concludes.

In [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_010.html_CN-bp-3-back-11
.calibre1}, I argued for increased consideration of context in
discussions of external validity. This applies particularly to
conjoints, given the possibility of incorporating unnecessary factors
and/or presenting respondents with information environments that do not
match those about which one theorizes. This point is not about mundane
realism; rather, the concern involves putting respondents in a context
where the available information and their motivations do not resemble
those that underlie psychological theories about processing. I also
spent substantial time discussing the conditions under which one should
worry about using particular respondent samples in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_010.html_CN-bp-3-back-12
.calibre1} [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the crucial question
revolves around the nature of heterogeneous treatment effects. When a
given sample may misestimate such effects, the lab-in-the-field approach
can be extremely useful; it also offers an approach when it comes to
contextual variation. Otherwise, even though lab-in-the-field
experiments [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} just like audits and
conjoints [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} offer a new intriguing
approach, they may be unnecessary for building knowledge (although
laboratory experiments more generally offer advantages that seem
underappreciated). The bottom line is that novel designs offer new
opportunities, but the fundamentals of what makes for a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiment do not change. (I offer more details on constructing a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiment in [Chapter
6](#part0014_split_000.html_CN-bp-6){#part0012_split_010.html_CN-bp-6-back-5
.calibre1}.) As Huber
([2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-274){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-274-back
.calibre1}: 2) explains, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}\[p\]erhaps
there[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s a sense that we have plenty of
theories and that the main challenge we face is to figure out which
variables actually have a causal effect. But this is wrong-headed
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the very nature of research on causal
identification requires a heightened rather diminished role for careful
theorizing.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[^36^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-135){#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-135-back
.calibre1} The usefulness of a causal test in the social sciences can
only be assessed in terms of its contributions to extant knowledge and
theory. The key points covered in this chapter are
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-226 .calibre1}as follows:

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   []{#part0012_split_010.html_page_116 .calibre1}The
    []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-12
    .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-43
    .calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-165
    .calibre1}expansion of experiments in the social sciences has led
    scholars to introduce novel designs; three notable examples include
    audit field experiments, conjoint survey experiments, and
    lab-in-the-field experiments. These offer new opportunities but also
    have limitations that recent work sometimes does not sufficiently
    acknowledge.

-   Audit experimental designs have a long history of producing
    knowledge about discrimination in the job market context. Political
    scientists have adapted the design to study bias in democratic
    responsiveness. In so doing, researchers should carefully attend to
    making appropriate comparisons across conditions, ensuring construct
    validity via piloting, and using supplemental methods to pinpoint
    mechanisms.

-   Conjoint survey experiments enable researchers to study a large
    number of factors; in so doing, researchers need to carefully
    theorize about the appropriate information environment, and
    respondents[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} attentional tendencies
    and information processing approaches. The concern is conjoints may
    oversaturate the environment and prompt attention to information
    that otherwise would not be accessed.

-   Lab-in-the-field experiments combine the control of the lab setting
    with access to populations of interest. They offer a valuable
    approach [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} due to the general
    advantages of laboratory experiments
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} when one suspects heterogeneous
    treatment effects that require seeking out a targeted sample or
    variable contextual effects that cause different reactions. In such
    scenarios, returns on the substantial implementation costs are high.

-   There are a host of experimental approaches to studying policy and
    contexts in which to study it that have not been fully exploited by
    social scientists.

-   As experimentalists turn to new designs, they must be careful to
    recognize their limitations and use the designs when appropriate. A
    design is useful only when researchers use it in a way that allows
    them to advance knowledge.
:::
::::
:::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fng-4 .fn-group}
::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-100 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0012_split_000.html_FN-fn-100-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
emergence of these designs can be seen from an analysis of the
experimental articles in the [APSR]{.italic}, as displayed in [Figure
1.1](#part0009_split_002.html_FIG-fig-1){#part0012_split_010.html_FIG-fig-1-back-7
.calibre1}. Each article was coded for whether it used a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}nontraditional
design,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} which included an audit field
experiment, conjoint survey experiment, lab-in-the-field experiment, or
a related novel design (or a ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}natural
experiment["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}). Before 2000, 4 percent used
one of these designs. This number jumped to 13 percent from 2000 to
2009, and 32 percent after 2009.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-101 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-101-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-85
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-275 .calibre1}It is
presumed the underlying assumptions are met, such as SUTVA (employers do
not talk to one another) and the exclusion restriction (employers are
not aware of the study or affected by other factors).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-102 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-102-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-143
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-269 .calibre1}The
approach limits social desirability bias in measurement that could arise
with survey approaches.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-103 .fn}
[[^4^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-103-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} It
also allows one to address the question of whether prior incarceration
has a causal role in lower employment rates (as it could be a spurious
relationship since incarcerated individuals may be more likely to have
behavioral problems, poor interpersonal skills, substance abuse issues,
etc.).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-104 .fn}
[[^5^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-104-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
applicants were similar in terms of physical appearance and
self-presentation, and were made to appear similar in terms of objective
characteristics (e.g., educational attainment and work experience).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-105 .fn}
[[^6^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-105-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Quillian et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-446){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-446-back
.calibre1}) analyze ninety-seven discrimination studies in nine
countries, finding significant discrimination against nonwhite natives
in all countries; discrimination against White immigrants is present but
low. Quillian et al.
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-447){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-447-back
.calibre1}) analyze twelve studies that include a job offer outcome
(beyond hiring). They find additional discrimination in hiring after the
callback: Majority applicants receive 52 percent more callbacks and 128
percent more job offers than comparable minority applicants.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-106 .fn}
[[^7^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-106-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They signaled party by having the requestor mention a particular
party[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s primary.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-107 .fn}
[[^8^](#part0012_split_001.html_FN-fn-107-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They also find legislators from both parties are significantly more
responsive to co-partisans (by about 4.5 percent).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-108 .fn}
[[^9^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-108-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Minority Democrats responded to about 29 percent of White requests.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-109 .fn}
[[^10^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-109-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
The authors group minority legislators (including Blacks, Latinos, Arab
Americans, etc.), and it may be that strictly Black legislators are more
responsive [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} but the larger point is
that one needs to think carefully about making inferences on
responsiveness.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-110 .fn}
[[^11^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-110-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
One possibility is that minority Democrats disproportionally represent
safe districts.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-111 .fn}
[[^12^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-111-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Pager
([2003](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-423){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-423-back-2
.calibre1}: 951) operationalizes the latter by having the non-offender
graduate from high school a year later and having a work record
thereafter, thereby accounting for an ostensible complete adult life
history.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-112 .fn}
[[^13^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-112-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-10 .calibre1}The earlier
in-person audits were particularly vulnerable to the criticism of the
impact of unobservables stemming from how confederates presented
themselves (Heckman
[1998](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-258){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-258-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-113 .fn}
[[^14^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-113-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-40
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-147
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-280
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-290 .calibre1}One can
try to control for confounds within the treatments (e.g., sending
signals of socioeconomic class, voting history), but this approach
requires identifying potential confounds. The concern is similar to the
informational equivalence issue discussed in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0012_split_010.html_CN-bp-3-back-13
.calibre1}.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-114 .fn}
[[^15^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-114-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This ambiguity relates to the difficulty of distinguishing taste-based
discrimination (e.g., racial animus) versus statistical discrimination
(e.g., presumptions that minorities, on average, are historically less
likely to vote). Of course, regardless of whether discrimination is
taste-based or statistical, the victims still lose out.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-115 .fn}
[[^16^](#part0012_split_002.html_FN-fn-115-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} I
have []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-84
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-273 .calibre1}not
touched on other audit design considerations, including concern about
overusing the available population, such that the respondents (e.g.
legislators) learn of the studies, which
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}spoils the
pool["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}; significant ethical issues, given
there is no consent or debriefing; approaches to studying how to vitiate
discrimination (see Butler and Crabtree
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-80){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-80-back
.calibre1}); the challenges of using the content of the responses as a
relevant outcome measure (see Coppock
[2019a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-108){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-108-back
.calibre1}); and logistical challenges (e.g., mail merges, personalizing
requests). For a discussion of many of these issues, see Gaddis
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-201){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-201-back-4
.calibre1}); Butler and Crabtree
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-81){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-81-back-3
.calibre1}); and Nathan and White
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-401){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-401-back-3
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-116 .fn}
[[^17^](#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-116-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
some cases, the design may be ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}single
profile,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} meaning there is just one
profile presented (e.g., one washing machine). The order of the
attributes is typically randomized. Bansak et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-27){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-27-back-4
.calibre1}) suggest randomizing across (rather than within) respondents
to prevent confusion and cognitive overload.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-117 .fn}
[[^18^](#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-117-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Another option is to offer respondents vignettes with all the variations
(rather than []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-266
.calibre1}tables).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-118 .fn}
[[^19^](#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-118-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As
with any interaction, the moderator[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
causal status must be interpreted with caution as it is not randomly
