assigned and, in this case, also is contingent on the excluded reference
category.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-119 .fn}
[[^20^](#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-119-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They also report a preference for skilled, experienced immigrants who
plan to work upon arrival and have made no previous unauthorized trips
to the United States (and are escaping persecution).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-120 .fn}
[[^21^](#part0012_split_004.html_FN-fn-120-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} He
shows that people rely less on partisanship as more detailed information
is provided.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-121 .fn}
[[^22^](#part0012_split_005.html_FN-fn-121-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As
mentioned, []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-207 .calibre1}Peterson
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-432){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-432-back-3
.calibre1}: 1194) explains that he drew information in his
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-3 .calibre1}conjoint from voter
information booklets, but how many respondents read those and processed
all the dimensions he included? Notably absent from most
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-308 .calibre1}conjoint studies of
voting are cues from friends and information on issues personally
salient to the respondent (although see Fowler
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-189){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-189-back
.calibre1} for discussion). Another example is
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-29 .calibre1}climate change
attitude studies that exclude partisanship (e.g., Bechtel and Scheve
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-34){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-34-back-2
.calibre1}), despite partisanship [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} at
least in the United States [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} being a key
driver of climate opinions (Bolsen et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-58){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-58-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-122 .fn}
[[^23^](#part0012_split_005.html_FN-fn-122-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
These assumptions concerning information availability, attention, and
processing are implicitly assumed with the analysis approach since it
identifies the relative impact of an attribute holding other factors at
their means. If those other factors are not attributes that would be
considered, then the impact of a given attribute may be misleading.
Another issue is that the randomization of levels within attributes can
significantly affect the results and thus should be carefully considered
to resemble the situation (population) of interest (e.g., Hainmueller et
al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-244){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-244-back-4
.calibre1}; Bansak et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-27){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-27-back-5
.calibre1}). Finally, as Leeper et al.
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-340){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-340-back-3
.calibre1}) explain, subgroup analyses can be tricky due to respondent
subgroups differing in their evaluations of the comparison points (e.g.,
effects are relative to a baseline such as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}no
education["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in an immigration conjoint,
and respondents may differ in their evaluations of the baseline).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-123 .fn}
[[^24^](#part0012_split_006.html_FN-fn-123-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
There are other validation studies in marketing (e.g., Montgomery and
Wittink
[1979](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-387){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-387-back
.calibre1}); however, the applicability of those to the other social
sciences remains unclear.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-124 .fn}
[[^25^](#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-124-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} An
alternative reason is that a sample from the population of interest
requires that one bring the lab to the field. For example, Nelsen
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-406){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-406-back
.calibre1}) designed an experiment to explore how distinct pedagogical
approaches in civic education affect political participation (across
different racial groups). He implemented the study by going to high
schools throughout the Chicago area, where he randomly assigned students
to read alternative excerpts. Here, bringing the lab to the field
constituted the easiest and perhaps only way to reach the relevant
population.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-125 .fn}
[[^26^](#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-125-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Eckel and Candelo
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-170){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-170-back-4
.calibre1}) make a similar distinction, calling them respectively
one-context and []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-55
.calibre1}cultural-comparison []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-198
.calibre1}lab-in-the-field []{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-242
.calibre1}experiments.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-126 .fn}
[[^27^](#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-126-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
She complements her lab-in-the-field experiment with a survey experiment
collected on an MTurk sample. The downside of the latter is that she
cannot ensure exposure, and that the sample leans in a liberal
direction.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-127 .fn}
[[^28^](#part0012_split_007.html_FN-fn-127-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
The use of multiple segments increases the construct validity of her
treatment since the treatment is not contingent on the idiosyncrasies of
one show. She also engaged in extensive piloting to ensure that the
shows included the aforementioned features of reality television. The
control show focused on life-quality features that do not depend on
visible financial gains.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-128 .fn}
[[^29^](#part0012_split_008.html_FN-fn-128-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-19
.calibre1}[]{#part0012_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-78 .calibre1}Eckel and
Candelo
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-170){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-170-back-5
.calibre1}: 98) explain that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}a
population that is outside the lab is not inherently more interesting
nor more relevant than one that is in the lab
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} We assert that the question leads the
researcher to implement this type of research because the attributes of
the population or context are critical for addressing the
problem.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-129 .fn}
[[^30^](#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-129-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Others use survey experiments with public officials (e.g., Druckman and
Valdes
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-164){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-164-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-130 .fn}
[[^31^](#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-130-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
For a broader treatment of experiments on
citizens[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} public policy (civic) oriented
behaviors (e.g., recycling, volunteer, organ donation), see John et al.
([2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-299){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-299-back
.calibre1}). Also, see Bloom
([2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-56){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-56-back-3
.calibre1}) on analytic approaches for policy oriented experiments.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-131 .fn}
[[^32^](#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-131-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Political scientists have used experiments to explore
citizens[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} reactions to natural events
much more than policy effects (e.g., Malhotra and Kuo
[2008](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-363){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-363-back
.calibre1}; Healy and Malhotra 2010).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-132 .fn}
[[^33^](#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-132-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Among Section 8 families, 61 percent chose to move and 47 percent of new
neighborhood families chose to move.
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-133 .fn}
[[^34^](#part0012_split_009.html_FN-fn-133-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Some areas that may come close to meeting
Campbell[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s vision include work in labor
and development economics (e.g., Banerjee and Duflo
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-26){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-26-back
.calibre1}), and education evaluations (e.g., Cook
[2002](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-105){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-105-back
.calibre1}; Shadish and Cook
[2009](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-465){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-465-back
.calibre1}). As more policy oriented experiments are implemented, one
consideration will be public concern about such interventions (e.g.,
Meyer et al.
[2019a](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-376){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-376-back
.calibre1}, b; Mislavsky et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-384){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-384-back
.calibre1},
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-385){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-385-back
.calibre1}; Heck et al. 2020).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-134 .fn}
[[^35^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-134-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Of
course, one cannot intuit a researcher[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
motivation for using a given design. However, the extent to which new
designs (e.g., conjoints) become applied with such frequency following
initial applications suggests an allure of prioritizing the design
regardless of the substantive question (another sign is the frequency
with which the names of new designs appear in titles of papers).
:::

::: {#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-135 .fn}
[[^36^](#part0012_split_010.html_FN-fn-135-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Huber
([2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-274){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-274-back-2
.calibre1}: 2) further states that some
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}take the strong position that social
science research that cannot solve the identification problem is not
worth doing, or at least is not worth publishing in leading journals. If
we move toward this position, we excessively narrow the range of
questions we ask, and thus unnecessarily limit our understanding of the
social processes we study.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This concern
echoes Smith[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0012_split_010.html_REFe-r-479-back-6
.calibre1}), as discussed at the start of the chapter.
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0013_split_000.html}

::: {#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0013_split_000.html_page_117 .calibre1}5]{.label1} What to Do before, during, and after an Experiment {#part0013_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}
:::

:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0013_split_000.html_A-sec-05 .sec}
[]{#part0013_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-681 .calibre1}Experiments
constitute one part of the scientific process. As explained in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-2-back-24
.calibre1}, that process entails a series of steps that leads to the
systematic production and organization of knowledge. The first step
involves asking a question while the last step entails data analysis.
Here I turn to practical issues involving events prior, during, and
after one follows these steps (and in the context of doing an
experiment). How do we arrive at questions in the first place? How do we
document the formal steps we take [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
including the theory, hypotheses, data collection details, and analyses?
What do we do when we are done; should we do it again and replicate the
study?

Addressing these questions align with the open science movement. Open
science initiatives accentuate the importance of making all aspects of
the experiment transparent, preregistering experiments in public
repositories, and replicating prior experiments (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_000.html_REFe-r-412-back-2
.calibre1}: 1422, 1425; Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0013_split_000.html_REFe-r-96-back-2
.calibre1}).[^1^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-136){#part0013_split_000.html_FN-fn-136-back
.calibre1} These are inarguably worthwhile
[]{#part0013_split_000.html_page_118 .calibre1}considerations (also see
Elman et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-176){#part0013_split_000.html_REFe-r-176-back
.calibre1}: chapters 6[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}13); however, as
will become clear, I offer a cautionary perspective on pursuing open
science principles too far. Social scientists need to constantly
evaluate whether their beliefs about what leads to good science actually
result in good science in practice. This is vital, as part of the
cultural authority of science comes from its collective, diverse nature
and practices (e.g., peer review, opportunities for criticism), and an
avoidance of a hidebound commitment to certain methodologies or
practices ([]{#part0013_split_000.html_IDClocr-tgt-682 .calibre1}Oreskes
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-420){#part0013_split_000.html_REFe-r-420-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::
::::
::::::

[]{#part0013_split_001.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0013_split_001.html_A-sec-18 .sec}
# []{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-353 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-677 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-799 .calibre1}How Do We Ask Questions? {#part0013_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-493 .calibre1}Experiments
provide knowledge only when they offer insight into important questions.
The scientific method starts with a question, but of course it also
matters how one arrives at the question in the first place. How that
happens goes to the heart of vigorous debates about the nature of
science itself, including varying epistemic cultures (Knorr-Cetina
[1999](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-319){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-319-back
.calibre1}) and the reality that
researchers[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} interests and values
underlie all scientific work (whether consciously or not) (National
Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2009). These broader
issues are beyond my purview [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} instead,
I offer a discussion of ways that social science experimentalists arrive
at their ideas and the questions they ask. My discussion will be
incomplete in the spirit of Oliver[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2004](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-416){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-416-back
.calibre1}) fascinating book [The Incomplete Guide to the Art of
Discovery]{.italic}, which begins with:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}no matter how well we prepare and plan,
no matter how carefully we make our decisions and guide our actions,
fate still seems always to play a central role in our careers and our
lives["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (ix).

Upon reading this quote, I reflected on how I arrived at some of my own
research foci [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} particularly regarding
partisan polarization among the public. I realized that it (roughly)
sequentially involved: serving as an editor on an early article on the
topic (Iyengar et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-293){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-293-back
.calibre1}); offering comments on a book manuscript that generated a
conversation (Levendusky
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-346){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-346-back
.calibre1}) (which led to the two-step communication flow experiment
described shortly); attending a meeting and ruminating on an offhanded
remark about measurement from a colleague (that led to a large scale
collaboration with that colleague and others); chatting with a student
who happened to sit next to me at a conference dinner (that led to
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_page_119 .calibre1}expanding the just
mentioned collaboration to address other questions); spending a summer
implementing failed efforts to pilot stimuli (to alter
partisan[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s animus toward the other
party); and taking advantage of an unexpected, albeit highly
unfortunate, event (the COVID-19 pandemic) to test a theory (see Geddes
[2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-204){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-204-back
.calibre1} on the role of world events in shaping research). That is a
lot of []{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-494
.calibre1}happenstance.

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-8
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-24
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-117 .calibre1}At the
most basic level, experimentalists constantly should be asking questions
about every event they observe. An experimental approach leads one to
realize the difficulty of documenting a causal relationship, and thus,
it becomes almost automatic to question and contemplate relationships in
the world [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and their counterfactuals.
Even though she is not discussing experimental work, Geddes
([2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-204){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-204-back-2
.calibre1}: 29) captures this dynamic in stating
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good research in the field is more often
motivated by curiosity about the world and intuition about
cause-and-effect relationships.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Beyond
this point, I provide a non-exhaustive, nonexclusive list of how to
arrive at questions, captured in a threefold categorization with the
acronym [ASK]{.italic}: [assessing, socializing, and kaput]{.italic}.

Assessing occurs as we witness the world around us: watching people,
reading the news, thinking about behavior, etc. Most social scientists
enter the field for a reason or a passion based on personal assessments
of the world and that can generate questions for study and
experimentation. Another aspect of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}assessing,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
beyond daily observations and personal interests, is more academic.
Specifically, scholars reflect on ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}big
questions,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} such as why and how people
form preferences and behave the way they do, and what that implies for
social, political, and economic systems. These
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}big["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
questions sometimes lead experimentalists to a particular study, but
even when not, scholars should step back and consider how their work
(regardless of how they arrived at the topic) speaks to these larger
issues. At a more mundane level, scholars read and assess the status of
extant academic literatures, finding gaps and thinking of theory and
experiments to fill them. In so doing, it is often useful to revisit
classic works on a topic, though, do not hesitate to question such work
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it is often useful to feel
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}indignation, annoyance, and
irritation["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} with what has been written
and these emotional reactions can generate new research (Geddes 203:
30).

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-407
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-653 .calibre1}More
pointedly, I recommend an approach in which researchers design new
experiments by building on prior designs. For example, many of the early
framing studies, including the campaign finance study discussed in the
earlier chapters, relied on the material used in
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-192 .calibre1}Nelson et
al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (1997) seminal framing experiment.
These works sought to expand on the []{#part0013_split_001.html_page_120
.calibre1}original experiment by replicating parts of it and then
extending it by, for example, varying the source of the frame (Druckman
[2001](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-138){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-138-back-2
.calibre1}), or the number of frames offered (Sniderman and Theriault
[2004](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-486){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-486-back
.calibre1}; Chong and Druckman
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-95){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-95-back
.calibre1}). The point of replication here was not to challenge or
question Nelson et al., but rather to expand on their highly credible
work to isolate boundary conditions. As I will discuss shortly, that
Nelson et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s willingness to share
their material shows how transparency can spark a robust
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-408
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-654
.calibre1}literature.[^2^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-137){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-137-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-927 .calibre1}A second way to
arrive at what questions to ask involves socializing, which can occur in
a host of guises. For example, professional conferences exist, in part,
to provide opportunities to discuss work with those who have similar and
different perspectives. This can stimulate questions and ideas;
sometimes this occurs via a formal setting such as a panel but often via
informal conversations. For me, as mentioned, an entire research agenda
began due to a colleague saying in passing
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} literally as she was walking out of
the room during a meeting break [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that
she had no idea what people thought about when asked to assess political
parties (although she actually had ideas that were different from what
prior research suggested). That comment sparked further conversations
and several studies to explore what people have in their head,
particularly when they express animus toward opposing partisans (e.g.,
Druckman et al. n.d.). This is only one of many such examples from my
career. The first professional paper I ever wrote reflected a similar
offhanded question that a professor of mine asked during a class break
(Druckman
[1996](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-137){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-137-back
.calibre1}), and the main research agenda for the first part of my
career came about due to ruminations at a dinner with my future
collaborator (e.g., Chong and Druckman
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-95){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-95-back-2
.calibre1}). The lesson is to spend time engaging with colleagues as
that often leads to questions that demand exploration and experimental
inquiry. This can occur in meetings, collegial conversations, or other
contexts.[^3^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-138){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-138-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-959 .calibre1}This type of
socialization also occurs in the classroom. Not only do students derive
ideas from professors, but professors also get ideas from students.
Students, graduate and undergraduate, often bring fresh
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_page_121 .calibre1}perspectives to topics
that can generate novel questions, and ultimately, experiments. This can
occur from advising students. Graduate students can also generate ideas
in collaborative group settings, such as a reoccurring lab meeting (see
Druckman et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-254){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-254-back-2
.calibre1}). Undergraduates often conduct research projects in small
classes, which can lead to collaborations with professors (Druckman
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-141){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-141-back
.calibre1}). For example, multiple experiments I conducted with students
stemmed from questions we discussed during class, such as how to measure
drug usage among student athletes (Druckman et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-141){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-141-back-2
.calibre1}) or how media affects vote choice (Druckman
[2004](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-139){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-139-back
.calibre1}). Further, preparing for class and observing
students[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} reactions during class
discussions can provide insight into useful research
questions.[^4^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-139){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-139-back
.calibre1} Undergraduate advising offers many opportunities. Students
can provide novel research ideas, and an advisor can help them situate
the question in an academic literature and design an experiment (when
that is the appropriate method). Of course, in all these cases, advisors
should be transparent about the goal of collaborating toward writing a
professional article. In my experience, this regularly proves profitable
for the student and for the pursuit of knowledge (and
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-960
.calibre1}publication).[^5^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-140){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-140-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-83
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-133
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-527
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-659
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-691
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-857
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1071
.calibre1}Questions also frequently emerge from socializing in
nonacademic settings. During dinner conversations, friends and family
may unknowingly raise novel questions not sufficiently addressed in the
academic literature. Complete strangers have even probed me with
questions that turned out to be useful. More directly, questions often
emerge through relationships with individuals or organizations with whom
a researcher may have a shared goal. Academic and practitioner (e.g.,
nonprofits, campaigns) relationships are certainly not new, but
researchers have []{#part0013_split_001.html_page_122
.calibre1}increasingly leveraged them to identify pressing questions
relevant to the academic[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s interest and
the organization[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s mission, which can
then be answered with an experiment. Such collaborations can occur
informally. For instance, Gerber et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
(2008) well-known field experiment on how social pressure mobilizes
voters came about through interactions with a campaign consultant who
sought a cost-effective way to increase turnout (see Green and Gerber
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-227){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-227-back
.calibre1}). Collaboration can involve more formal relationships
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as has been documented in various
fields (see, e.g., Karlan and Appel
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-308){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-308-back-2
.calibre1}). For instance, Kalla and Broockman
([2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-304){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-304-back
.calibre1}) partnered with a liberal political organization, CREDO
Action, with roughly 3.5 million members. They worked with the
organization to design an experiment where members attempted to arrange
meetings with members from the US Congress (from one party) who had not
already cosponsored a bill to ban a chemical. They randomly assigned
whether meeting inquiries came from active political donors or from
concerned
constituents.[^6^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-141){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-141-back
.calibre1} They report some of the most striking evidence to date that
donations affect access [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} senior
policymakers made themselves available to political donors between three
to four times more often than to concerned constituents. This experiment
highlights the gains that can come about from an
organizational-researcher partnership (also see the examples in [Chapter
4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0013_split_001.html_CN-bp-4-back-8
.calibre1}).

Fortunately, the organization research4impact
([www.r4impact.org](https://www.r4impact.org){.calibre1}) facilitates
this process by matching practitioners and social scientists (with a
focus on experimentation). They also offer workshops on how to pursue
research relationships; as they say on their website
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Connecting with a practitioner will
produce new questions, new research ideas, and possibly new formal
collaborations["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see Levine
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-347){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-347-back-2
.calibre1} on how to form organizational partnerships to run
experiments). Even when these relationships do not go as planned and/or
fail, they still lead to new ideas and lessons about what not to do next
time (see []{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-84
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-134
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-528
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-660
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-692
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-858
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1072
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-928 .calibre1}Karlan
and Appel
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-308){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-308-back-3
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-95
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-345
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-383
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-721
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-941 .calibre1}A third
way to generate questions is what I refer to as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}kaput,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
in essence, failure. In [Chapter
1](#part0009_split_000.html_CN-bp-1){#part0013_split_001.html_CN-bp-1-back-3
.calibre1}, I noted Campbell and
Stanley[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1963](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-90){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-90-back-2
.calibre1}: 3) statement that we ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}must
instill in our students the expectation of tedium and disappointment and
the duty of thorough persistence.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This
pessimistic portrayal reflects the prevailing reality that many
experimental efforts will have disappointing results and be initially
interpreted as []{#part0013_split_001.html_page_123 .calibre1}kaput.
Campbell and Stanley are right that persistence is absolutely essential.
Persistence includes learning from the failed results, which can in
fact, in the long run, lead to even better experiments (what Karlan and
Appel
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-308){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-308-back-4
.calibre1} call ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}learning when things go
wrong["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}). Indeed, the first thing one
should do when an experiment fails is to ask why it failed. It may be
due to a problem with the design. For example, I once implemented a
framing experiment focused on attitudes about urban growth initiatives.
I exposed people to the argument that the issue should not be left to
voters, but the frame had no effect whatsoever, and even backfired a
bit. The reflection that followed the null result led me to recognize
the importance of assessing the argument strength of different frames, a
topic that led me to consider a new theory and several distinct
experiments (e.g., Chong and Druckman
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-95){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-95-back-3
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-64
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-100
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-111 .calibre1}Aside
from theoretical missteps/inaccuracies, an experiment can fail for
countless implementation reasons, including distracted participants,
poorly constructed stimuli or measures, insufficient power,
noncompliance or attrition, and related issues. Each of these design
elements, as discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_001.html_CN-bp-2-back-25
.calibre1}, requires careful attention and consideration, should an
experiment not go as planned. An additional possibility concerns events
happening outside the experimental context. One glaring example comes
from an experiment I implemented regarding the way different arguments
affect Americans[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} support for nuclear
energy. The data collection took place in March 2011, which as it turned
out overlapped with the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster in Japan (due
to an earthquake and tsunami). This obviously undermined the data
collection, as any argument for nuclear energy became impotent in the
midst of the disaster. But the scenario begged the question of whether I
should include questions about the information environment to measure
how the context moderated treatment effects (see Druckman and Leeper
[2012b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-154){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-154-back-2
.calibre1}).

[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-399
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-957
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-965
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1065
.calibre1}Distinct from contextual sources of failures are unexpected
experimental participant reactions. In one case, I attempted to embed a
survey experiment in an Election Day exit poll in Minnesota. Suffice it
to say, Election Day in Minnesota means cold and snow. Given these
conditions, I thought it may help to offer a \$5 incentive to take the
survey. Much to my surprise, however, the incentive turned people away.
Apparently, the social capital is so high in Minnesota that the response
rate was higher when the student pollsters did not offer the \$5. I
stopped offering incentives, midday, once it became apparent that
everyone was declining the money and subsequently declining to
participate. The subjects seemed to feel they were contributing to the
public good by taking the survey and
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_page_124 .calibre1}helping students, but
when money was involved, they felt insulted by my assumption that they
would demand pay. In another example, I sought to use experimental
vignettes to assess whether athletic trainers display racial bias in
pain assessments of student-athletes. In the recruitment e-mail, we
referred to potential participants as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}trainers.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Dozens responded that they would not participate, feeling insulted that
we had not used their official titles of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}athletic
trainer["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}certified athletic
trainer.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} We apologized and adapted the
language accordingly (Druckman et al.
[2018c](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-163){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-163-back
.calibre1}). These examples accentuate the importance of anticipating
responses of participants in experiments, particularly when using
targeted populations, which is an increasingly common experimental
approach (e.g., Klar and Leeper
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-316){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-316-back-2
.calibre1}). It is important to talk about the study to members of the
groups before you start in order to anticipate the issues that may
arise. Pilots are not just useful for researchers to test manipulations,
but also to assess response rates and unexpected responses to the study
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-400
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-958
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-966
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-1066
.calibre1}materials.

Sometimes an experiment goes kaput due to the reality that the method
does not fit the question. For instance, a collaborator and I hoped to
causally assess whether animus toward the other party affects issue
opinions; we sought to do so by manipulating
participants[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} levels of contempt for the
other party (e.g., making Democrats dislike Republicans more) to see if
increased contempt led partisans to follow party cues more on issues. We
piloted nine treatments we thought could prime out-party animus and
every one []{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-206 .calibre1}failed
(perhaps due to a ceiling effect). We concluded an experiment would not
work for this test and instead kept searching for other possibilities,
one of which came about at the onset of the
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-50 .calibre1}COVID-19 pandemic.
There we used a prior survey measuring partisan animus to gauge COVID-19
attitudes, thereby allowing us to use panel data with an exogenous
measure (i.e., one taken prior to the emergence of COVID-19) that linked
out-party dislike to partisan cue-taking. The pilot failures prompted us
to ask what method could work and we took advantage of an opportunity
that naturally arose (Druckman et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-151){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-151-back
.calibre1}).

In sum, experimental failures can be dispiriting, to say the least, but
they are also inevitable. In the moment, disappointment, anxiety, and
frustration constitute the natural responses, and every failure might
not straightforwardly lead to another question that works out well. Yet,
in most cases, researchers can learn lessons that can advance theory,
and/or improve implementation. In Campbell and
Stanley[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s words, the key is to persist,
even when incredibly difficult, in the face of disappointment.
[]{#part0013_split_001.html_page_125 .calibre1}Success will come with
diligence and innovation. Or as Oliver
([2004](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-416){#part0013_split_001.html_REFe-r-416-back-2
.calibre1}: 34) states, the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}path of
science is strewn with failures of what once seemed great ideas and
promising new directions [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Science is
built upon past failures, as well as upon successes, and a select few of
those with the boldness and daring to depart from convention will always
lead the []{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-96
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-346
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-384
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-722
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-942
.calibre1}way.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[^7^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-142){#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-142-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0013_split_002.html}

::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: {#part0013_split_002.html_A-sec-18 .sec}
:::::: {#part0013_split_002.html_B-sec-24 .sec}
## Summary {#part0013_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .b}

[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-19
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-797 .calibre1}At first
glance, it may appear that arriving at a good research question should
not be difficult; yet, it requires a researcher to identify an
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}important
question["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} relevant to their field, and
which can be realistically addressed to add to an accumulating knowledge
base. It is thus not so easy. It necessitates maintaining a curiosity at
all times, as questions can emerge in the most unexpected settings
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in essence, many experiments and
findings come about due to a mix of luck, curiosity, and adherence to
systematic thinking (Dunbar and Fugelsang
[2005](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-165){#part0013_split_002.html_REFe-r-165-back
.calibre1}).[^8^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-143){#part0013_split_002.html_FN-fn-143-back
.calibre1}

The flip side of the role of luck is that one might often feel that
their diligence seems unrewarded. Diligence acts as a necessary but not
sufficient ingredient in leading to a satisfying experiment. At times,
experimentalists even have to let go and move on from an experiment, but
do not view this as wasted time (it is part of the inevitable process).
Those who maintain curiosity with at least a sliver of irreverence
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} they do not accept any answer as the
final word [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} will arrive at exciting
questions and success. Rather than summarizing the lessons of this
section with numbered points, as with many other sections in the book, I
end with [Table
5.1](#part0013_split_002.html_TN-tblw-9){#part0013_split_002.html_TN-tblw-9-back-2
.calibre1}. The table outlines the examples of my [ASK]{.italic}
framework (which is not meant to be comprehensive) for arriving at
questions for experimentalists.

::::: {#part0013_split_002.html_TN-tblw-9 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[Table 5.1.]{.label2} [ASK[: Examples of how to ask research
questions]{.italic}]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-354
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-678
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-800
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-20
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-798
.calibre1}![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00030.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

[]{#part0013_split_003.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0013_split_003.html_A-sec-19 .sec}
# []{#part0013_split_003.html_page_126 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-347 .calibre1}Formalizing the Research Process {#part0013_split_003.html_calibre_pb_3 .a}

[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-269
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1025
.calibre1}Implementation of a sound experiment requires meticulous
attention to detail when it comes to identifying the best stimuli,
measures, sample, and context. Yet, despite how much time researchers
spend thinking through each piece of an experiment, they inevitably will
forget the details, even if it is hard to imagine in the moment. For
this reason, researchers need to keep careful records of all decisions
(Geddes
[2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-204){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-204-back-3
.calibre1}: 43[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}88). As an example, I
will discuss Druckman et al.
([2018b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-156){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-156-back
.calibre1}) in detail. The experiment tests whether partisan media
stories can influence people who do not
[]{#part0013_split_003.html_page_127 .calibre1}watch them. This can
occur via a two-step communication flow: (1) a story (e.g., about the
environmental risks posed by drilling for oil) affects those who watch
it (e.g., making them more opposed to drilling); (2) those who watched
and had their opinions changed talk about the issue with others who did
not watch (e.g., discuss drilling); and (3) those conversations
influence the people who did not watch and their opinions end up
resembling the opinions of those who did watch (e.g., those who did not
watch are then indirectly shaped by the media, becoming opposed to
drilling) (Lazarsfeld et al.
[1948](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-338){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-338-back
.calibre1}). In the experiment, the authors randomly assigned people to
a control group (no watching or discussing the news/issue); a group that
watched partisan news but did not subsequently discuss it; a group that
watched the news and then discussed it (with others who did not watch);
or a group that did not watch the news but discussed the topic with
those who did watch it. They demonstrate that individuals who did not
watch the news but discussed it had opinions that matched those of
people who just watched it (and differed from those in the control who
neither watched nor discussed). Put another way, partisan media can
influence the opinions of people who do not watch it via a two-step
communication flow.

A sampling of the design decisions included (1) choosing the news
networks (e.g., MSNBC and Fox News); (2) deciding whether participants
should be forced to watch one network or be able to choose which
networks to watch (e.g., MSNBC, Fox News, PBS); (3) deciding on the
medium of exposure (e.g., videos); (4) identifying the issue (e.g.,
drilling); (5) crafting the precise messages (e.g., focusing on
environmental risks or economic gains from job creation); (6)
determining the size of the discussion group (e.g., four people) and the
composition of the groups (e.g., all from one party, mix from different
parties); and (7) deciding on the precise outcome measures (e.g.,
support for drilling, partisan identity strength). The authors also made
decisions regarding whether the networks should show incongruent stories
(e.g., MSNBC focusing on economic gains from drilling); whether there
should be conditions with news exposure and no discussion; whether there
should be conditions with discussion but no news exposure among anyone;
and whether attitudes on the outcome variables should be measured pre-
and post-treatment. The authors made other choices about the recruitment
plan (e.g., from campus, community organizations); the random assignment
approach (e.g., what if the plan is to have a mixed partisan group but
only those from the same party arrive at the session?); and the analysis
plan (e.g., which groups to compare to test the
[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-270
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1026
.calibre1}hypotheses).

[]{#part0013_split_003.html_page_128
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-2
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-59
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-215 .calibre1}While
this admittedly constitutes a particularly complex experiment, the
reality holds that even for the simplest of experiments, a large number
of choices need to be made. It is crucial, at each stage, to explicate
and write down the rationale. For example, in the case of this
experiment, the authors created a document to justify the choice of
drilling as the issue to study. The authors selected drilling in order
to build on prior work on partisan reasoning (Levendusky
[2010](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-345){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-345-back
.calibre1}; Druckman et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-161){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-161-back
.calibre1}), and to use real television segments to increase
experimental realism (since they were more captivating than false
stories likely could be). Drilling was also an issue on which partisans
did not hold such strong priors so as to be unmoveable from media
exposure.[^9^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-144){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-144-back
.calibre1} While these types of details demand substantial attention in
the moment, they are easily forgotten without documentation. In the case
of this experiment, the
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}design["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
document filled twenty-eight single-spaced pages, beginning with the
motivation and including details about the stimuli, outcome measures,
predictions, analysis plans, and
more.[^10^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-145){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-145-back
.calibre1}

This goal of the two-step communication flow experiment was to test a
theory and identify patterns, rather than inform policy. If one is
implementing an experiment with the goal to inform those who make policy
directly, then a design document might also include a discussion of
Al-Ubaydli et al.[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2017, 2020, 2021)
aforementioned scalability considerations (see [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0013_split_003.html_CN-bp-3-back-14
.calibre1}). For example, be clear to articulate the target
policy-relevant environment such as where an intervention would be
implemented (and what intervention); what actors would be involved and
what are their incentives; and, as a general matter,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}backwards induct to address the
potential threats to scaling with the experimental
design["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Al-Ubaydli et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-4){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-4-back-2
.calibre1}: 38). That is, think through how the results would translate
to a precise policy situation and what challenges would arise in the
process.

[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-259
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-339
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-739
.calibre1}Documentation continues once the data are collected.
Experimentalists must temper their excitement of finally analyzing the
data and make sure to maintain documentation of how they conduct
analyses. Specifically, []{#part0013_split_003.html_page_129
.calibre1}this involves keeping careful notes on data collection (e.g.,
were the sessions run without incident?); analytical decisions such as
the creation of variables (e.g., in the aforementioned experiment, it
involved aggregating three items based on a factor analysis); the
selection of cases (e.g., excluding pure Independents); and analysis
decisions (e.g., using inverse probability weighting based on group
assignment). Without documentation, by the time one decides upon the
most accurate and informative result presentation, he or she may have
scant memory of how to recreate it out of a mess of poorly named
variables and many analysis files.

I advocate for writing these planning and analysis documents because
they facilitate multiple goals. For one, they make it much easier to
write the []{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-203
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-295
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-359
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-459
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-685
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1007 .calibre1}actual
paper: The documents form part of the paper. The documents also
facilitate satisfying transparency requirements that come with the
publishing process. Open science[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
transparency expectations involve experimentalists posting their
stimuli, outcome measures (e.g., surveys), data collection details,
data, and analysis code in independent public registries (Miguel et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-378){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-378-back
.calibre1}; Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-412-back-3
.calibre1}: 1424; Chistensen and Miguel
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-97-back
.calibre1}; Aczel et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-1){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-1-back
.calibre1}; Boudreau
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-62){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-62-back
.calibre1}). Transparency ensures an understanding of the scientific
processes underlying empirical claims (Lupia and Elman
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-354){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-354-back-2
.calibre1}; Elman et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-177){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-177-back
.calibre1}). The principles can be found, for example, in political
science[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s Data Access & Research
Transparency (DA-RT) initiative (Lupia and Elman
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-354){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-354-back-3
.calibre1}) that stimulated revision to the American Political Science
Association[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (APSA) [Guide to
Professional Ethics in Political Science]{.italic}. That guide (2012:
9[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}10) states that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}researchers have an ethical obligation
to facilitate the evaluation of their evidence-based knowledge claims
through data access, production transparency, and analytic transparency
so that their work can be tested or
replicated.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} A large number of social
science journals, including most that publish experimental research,
endorse these guidelines by requiring authors to post material and data
(Boudreau
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-62){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-62-back-2
.calibre1}). While scholars using other methods (e.g., qualitative
approaches) debate the application of these principles to their work
(e.g., Monroe
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-386){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-386-back
.calibre1}), experimentalists generally endorse the basic ideas.
Moreover, experimentalists have gone further by generating a list of
reporting expectations in papers, including participant eligibility and
recruitment, number of participants, descriptive statistics, evidence of
treatment delivery, and weighting procedures if used, among other
considerations (Gerber et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-209){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-209-back
.calibre1}).[^11^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-146){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-146-back
.calibre1} The life of an experimentalist
[]{#part0013_split_003.html_page_130 .calibre1}will be much easier if he
or she diligently keeps notes on all of these aspects through the
process rather than having to go back and recreate it later. While this
may sound easy and obvious, it again
[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-260
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-340
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-740 .calibre1}requires
self-discipline to slow down and document.

[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-49
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-60
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-190
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-238
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-294
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-161 .calibre1}These
transparency expectations allow other researchers to verify the results
and confirm the robustness of the findings. Transparency also helps
other researchers replicate and extend the experiment (e.g., using the
materials), and use it for large-scale reanalyses and
meta-analyses.[^12^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-147){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-147-back
.calibre1} Indeed, Mullinix et al.
([2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-393-back-5
.calibre1}), Coppock et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-112-back-8
.calibre1}), and Coppock
([2019b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-109){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-109-back-5
.calibre1}), all discussed in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0013_split_003.html_CN-bp-3-back-15
.calibre1}, reanalyzed and replicated large sets of experiments because
they had access to those data and materials via the TESS program (which
posts all material and data). Zigerell
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-521){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-521-back
.calibre1}) accesses seventeen previously conducted (TESS) survey
experiments to perform a new analysis of racial prejudice; he reports a
distinct and provocative finding that for
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}White participants
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} pooled results did not detect a net
discrimination for or against White targets, but, for Black participants
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} pooled results indicated the presence
of a small-to-moderate net discrimination in favor of Black
targets["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (1). These efforts and
concomitant knowledge gains are only possible through transparency. That
other scholars build on and refine prior experiments also speaks to the
importance of documenting the design decisions. That is, another
researcher building on one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s work may
inquire about an aspect of the experiment and it is much better to
access an old document that detailed the thinking at the time than to
rely on one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s memory. (Indeed, I had to
access the aforementioned twenty-eight-page document for virtually all
the details described earlier concerning the two-step communication flow
experiment.) In essence, this helps ensure your work has more impact
since helping other scholars build on work perpetuates its influence. As
mentioned earlier, one way to stimulate a new experimental idea is to
build on and extend prior work, to learn about the details from others,
and to proactively provide details to others to ensure collective
intellectual progress.

As data transparency becomes the norm, experimentalists should attend to
how that could affect results. Specifically, Connors et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-104){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-104-back
.calibre1}) point out that even small contextual changes can alter
participant []{#part0013_split_003.html_page_131
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-70
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDXloc-tgt-149
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-162
.calibre1}behavior; with transparency rules, many Institutional Review
Boards now require that participants be informed of a statement such as:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}if this research is published, your
response will be made publicly available to other researchers. You will
never be asked for your name. Only the responses you give to the
questions that follow will be available for
download["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Connors et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-104){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-104-back-2
.calibre1}: 191). Connors et al. conduct an experiment comparing
responses of individuals who receive this de-identified posting
notification against those who do
not.[^13^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-148){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-148-back
.calibre1} They find the disclosure significantly alters responses. For
example, those notified report lower levels of political knowledge
(e.g., they are more likely to report that they do not know answers to
questions); more support for abortion (i.e., more pro-choice attitudes);
and increased levels of self-reported vote
turnout.[^14^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-149){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-149-back
.calibre1} Thus, informing respondents of data transparency can have
positive effects on data quality (e.g., more accurate knowledge reports)
but also exacerbate social desirability reporting (e.g., overreporting
turnout). Scholars conducing experimental work that involve measures
where respondents may think about how responses reflect on them should
thus account for such possible effects, perhaps by considering
measurement methods that reduce social desirability bias (see [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_003.html_CN-bp-2-back-26
.calibre1}[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s discussion, Rosenfeld et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-454){#part0013_split_003.html_REFe-r-454-back-2
.calibre1}).[^15^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-150){#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-150-back
.calibre1} This is not to say scholars should forgo
[]{#part0013_split_003.html_page_132 .calibre1}transparency with
research participants, but rather that they should be cognizant of its
possible []{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-204
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-296
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-360
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-460
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-686
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_003.html_IDClocr-tgt-1008
.calibre1}effects.

In sum, conducting an experiment involves a myriad of decisions;
formalizing a process to keep track of choices ensures clear
documentation; lessens the extent to which one has to rely on his or her
memory; provides text for papers that eventually may be written;
facilitates compliance with transparency publication guidelines; and
helps in conversations with others who share similar interests. It also
can provide some perspective by forcing an experimentalist to think
through each choice and recollect the rationale as he or she reviews
prior decisions at each stage. All of that said, this entails a
substantial time investment [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the
reality is that quality experiments typically do.

Given the current extent to which transparency expectations are
formalized in the publication process, academic norms need to change.
Tenure standards must evolve to recognize that quantitative productivity
may be vitiated due to the increased time commitments needed to ensure
transparent research. Indeed, a generation ago, one would not have had
to spend time creating detailed appendices about the implementation of
an experiment, or make user-friendly versions of the experimental
material and data. These are nontrivial tasks for which institutions
must account when assessing an
individual[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s research. In essence,
providing these materials helps provide a public good since other
researches can build on them and that needs to be recognized.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0013_split_004.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0013_split_004.html_A-sec-19 .sec}
::: {#part0013_split_004.html_B-sec-25 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-291 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-301 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-335 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-683 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-763 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-773 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-833 .calibre1}Preregistration and Pre-analysis Plans {#part0013_split_004.html_calibre_pb_4 .b}

Another item one should consider before implementing an experiment is to
preregister it. Preregistration entails registering a study in an
independent repository prior to data collection (Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back
.calibre1}; Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-96-back-3
.calibre1}: 99[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}119). Authors provide
enough details so that other scholars know what hypotheses have been
tested on what populations (Malhotra
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-362-back-2
.calibre1}). Prominent registries include the Open Science
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_133 .calibre1}Framework (OSF), Evidence
in Governance and Politics (EGAP), and AsPredicted (Boudreau
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-62){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-62-back-3
.calibre1}). These repositories provide the scientific community with
access to the universe of studies on a given topic.

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-397
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-447
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-559
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-785 .calibre1}If
practiced by all or most scholars working on a given topic,
preregistration can help vitiate publication bias, which occurs when
publication decisions are based on criteria unrelated to research
quality.[^16^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-151){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-151-back
.calibre1} The most notable type of publication bias, the file drawer
bias, occurs if a positive test result (i.e., a statistical test that
rejects the null hypothesis of no effect) is more likely to be published
than a negative test result (i.e., a test that does not reject the null
hypothesis) (Rosenthal
[1979](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-455){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-455-back
.calibre1}).[^17^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-152){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-152-back
.calibre1} The published record of research skews from the true
distribution of results, overstating the collective strength of positive
or statistically significant findings. For example, if one out of ten
studies shows that sending varying types of health-related text messages
leads people to eat less fatty food, and only that one study is
published, the result is a mis-portrayal of the effect of text messages.

A large literature documenting publication bias in the social sciences
exists (e.g., Gerber et al.
[2000](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-210){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-210-back-3
.calibre1}; Gerber and Malhotra
[2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-215){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-215-back
.calibre1}; Gerber et al.
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-216){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-216-back-2
.calibre1}; Franco et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-190){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-190-back
.calibre1}; Brown et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-65){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-65-back-2
.calibre1}; Fanelli et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-182){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-182-back
.calibre1}; Christensen and Miguel
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-97-back-2
.calibre1}: 924[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}931; Andrews and Kasy
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-10){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-10-back
.calibre1}; Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-96-back-4
.calibre1}: 31[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}55; Berinsky et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-40){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-40-back
.calibre1}; Malhotra
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-362-back-3
.calibre1}). Preregistration of studies serves as an antidote to the
file drawer bias
problem.[^18^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-153){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-153-back
.calibre1} Scholars who conduct meta-analyses to aggregate literatures
can include unpublished and published experiments on a topic to assess
the robustness of an effect (assuming data from unpublished studies are
also made
available).[^19^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-154){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-154-back
.calibre1} For example, even if the aforementioned nine text
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_134 .calibre1}messaging studies with
null effects were not published, preregistration along with data
availability would ensure a more accurate synthesis of the literature.
Preregistration in that sense serves as a public good, which should not
add much additional work if one writes the planning documents
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-398
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-448
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-560
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-786
.calibre1}discussed.[^20^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-155){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-155-back
.calibre1}

Many preregistrations, including the aforementioned registries, also ask
researchers to report the hypotheses, outcome measures, experimental
conditions, sample size, plans to exclude outlier data, and a detailed
[pre-analysis plan]{.italic} of how they will test their hypotheses once
they finish data collection. Such an analysis plan requires specific
descriptions of the statistical tests that will be employed and what
evidence would constitute support for a hypothesis. Pre-analysis plans
supersede basic registration with the intent to vitiate data mining
(p-hacking), and/or push researchers to justify why they deviate from
the plan. Some argue that pre-analysis plans enhance the credibility of
research (Humphreys et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-276){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-276-back
.calibre1}; Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-2
.calibre1}).

The planning documents discussed envelope a pre-analysis plan, and serve
a crucial function by forcing the experimentalist to visualize the data
before collecting it. This can prevent a design error where the data
collected do not allow for the testing of the hypotheses. For example,
in the two-step communication flow experiment, so many decisions had to
be made concerning the treatment conditions that it may have been easy
to forget which condition comparisons constitute the key test. In that
case, the main comparison is between those who watch the news and do not
discuss it against those who do not watch but discuss it (i.e., do the
conversations in essence ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}stand
in["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for the watching?). The pure control
group (where individuals neither watch nor discuss) is not central. A
pre-analysis plan ensured attention to identifying the appropriate
analyses and the need for particular experimental conditions. Echoing
[Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_004.html_CN-bp-2-back-27
.calibre1}, it forces experimentalists to carefully assess the relevant
counterfactuals being tested, collect the appropriate data, and
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_135 .calibre1}have a plan on what they
will do with the data upon receiving it (also see Humphreys et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-276){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-276-back-2
.calibre1}: 10)

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-143
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-365
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-481
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-667
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-769 .calibre1}That
said, developing a pre-analysis plan and even formally registering
should not hamstring discovery. This can occur when scholars insist on a
hierarchical distinction between confirmatory/predictive and
exploratory/postdictive hypotheses where the former appear in the
pre-analysis plan and the latter do not (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-412-back-4
.calibre1}: 1423). While not always explicit, it seems that many voices
in the field value a priori hypotheses over exploratory
ones.[^21^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-156){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-156-back
.calibre1} This preference follows for three reasons. First, a
confirmatory hypothesis appears to be further along in the scientific
process [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} it means a question has been
asked, a theory developed, a hypothesis generated, and data collected.
In contrast, exploratory work could be construed as more
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}theory
generation,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and thus earlier along in
the progress of science. Second, some construe exploratory findings as
requiring subsequent research (Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-3
.calibre1}: 2604[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2605), with more
experiments/data collection needed before publication. Third,
exploratory findings that are not preregistered could be viewed as post
hoc and any explanations to explain them could be construed as
rationalizations with less credibility (Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-4
.calibre1}: 2600[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}2601). The concern is
that analysis not specified in advance could be inauthentic, with
authors engaging in either questionable research practices,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}p-hacking,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}fishing["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
to find significant results (Simmons et al.
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-469){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-469-back
.calibre1}; Humphreys et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-276){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-276-back-3
.calibre1}).[^22^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-157){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-157-back
.calibre1} These behaviors refer to the inappropriate analysis and
reanalysis of data in order to find statistically significant results
(e.g., excluding relevant conditions, outcome measures, or data points
until significant results are found) (see Franco et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-191){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-191-back
.calibre1},
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-192){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-192-back
.calibre1}; Zigerell
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-520){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-520-back
.calibre1}).[^23^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-158){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-158-back
.calibre1}

These are reasonable concerns that formal pre-analysis plans address by
forcing researchers to make non-registered choices explicit, since
hypotheses would be clearly delineated as confirmatory or exploratory
(Coffman and Niederle
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-147-back
.calibre1}: 82). Yet, in assessing the value of
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_136 .calibre1}pre-analysis plans, one
must draw a distinction between the research process and the publication
process. As explained, pre-analysis plans constitute a vital part of the
research process, but its role in the publication process comes with
pros (e.g., addressing p-hacking) and cons. The cons occur if authors,
reviewers, and editors strongly, and sometimes blindly, privilege
confirmatory over exploratory results even with the just discussed
arguments for why one may do so (e.g., Olken
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-417){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-417-back
.calibre1}; Christensen and Miguel
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-97-back-3
.calibre1}: []{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-144
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-366
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-482
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-668
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-770
.calibre1}958[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}959).[^24^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-159){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-159-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-115
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-209
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-787
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1051 .calibre1}To see
why, consider three downsides of strict adherence to pre-analysis plans
in the publication
process.[^25^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-160){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-160-back
.calibre1} First, even when hypotheses are innovative and high-quality,
inattention to careful data collection can lead to null results.
Overemphasis on pre-analysis plans shifts the basis of publication
decisions toward the existence of a priori hypotheses and away from
using statistical significance. If science operated ideally and research
quality did not vary, this change would be an obvious improvement. Alas,
as has hopefully been made clear in the prior chapters, conducting an
experiment and collecting quality data involve difficult processes. A
sampling of considerations includes ensuring meeting the assumptions for
causal inference (e.g., excludability, SUTVA); the use of validated
treatments (e.g., via manipulation checks); high levels of experimental
realism; accurate and valid outcome measures; sufficient subsample sizes
if moderators are to be tested, etc. Many of these considerations
concern issues [during data
collection]{.italic}.[^26^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-161){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-161-back
.calibre1} Even the most clever and []{#part0013_split_004.html_page_137
.calibre1}well-developed hypothesis does nothing to ensure the proper
implementation of a quality experimental data collection. In contrast,
the existence of a statistically significant result often indicates not
only a strong theory or intuitive expectations but also sufficient
statistical power, well-designed treatments and outcome variables, and
carefully executed data collection (Malhotra
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-362-back-4
.calibre1}) (e.g., it is difficult to obtain significance in light of
all the errors that could occur during data
collection).[^27^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-162){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-162-back
.calibre1} This is not to dismiss the concern of data drudging/p-hacking
that sometimes leads to statistical significance. Instead, the point is
that, in the extreme, if one puts emphasis on pre-analysis plans, the
result could be the publication of a lot of null results due to poor
theory, designs, and/or []{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-116
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-210
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-788
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-1052
.calibre1}implementation.

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-33
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-102
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-194
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-208
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-232
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-246
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-248 .calibre1}The
answer of which publication decision criterion is preferable depends on
how one defines quality. For example, Nosek et al.
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-5
.calibre1}: 2602) focus on reproducibility, whereas others might focus
on the aforementioned criteria (i.e., that define quality data
collection, such as experimental realism, valid measures, piloted
stimuli, etc.). Determining the best approach requires an empirical
assessment that, to date, has not been conducted (Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-6
.calibre1}: 2602). Related to this, Coffman and Niederle
([2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-147-back-2
.calibre1}: 84[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}88) suggest that the
benefits of pre-analysis plans in terms of limiting false positives
(e.g., due to p-hacking), accrue much more in research domains where
there will only be a single test of a hypothesis (e.g., an expensive,
resource intensive field experiment). In such cases, there are not
follow-up studies that could correct for false-positives or positive
results from data drudging. These types of studies also tend to be ones
that have policy goals and as such benefit from a careful consideration
of the aforementioned scalability issues in the process of thinking
about the analyses. In contrast, in research areas where we might expect
several experimental tests that can identify the strongest theory,
protection against false positives or data drudging may be less crucial.
Hence, pre-analysis plans should perhaps receive less weight than
statistical significance. In short, the extent of the advantage of
pre-analysis plans in terms of the aggregate scientific record depends
on the area of
research.[^28^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-163){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-163-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_138
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-251
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-271
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-367
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-913
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-955
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-995 .calibre1}A second
downside with strict adherence to pre-analysis is that it ostensibly
