assumes that any exploratory data analyses reflect post hoc theorizing,
therefore requiring further data collection. Yet, the social sciences
include many approaches and theoretical frameworks, some of which may
not have been considered prior to data collection. For example, Druckman
and Shafranek
([2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-162){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-162-back
.calibre1}) present an audit experiment where they sent requests to
undergraduate admissions offices to explore biases in responsiveness.
They varied the race of the sender (i.e., Black/White) as well as
political referents in the requests (i.e., references to a civic
organization, a political organization, the Young Democrats, or the
Young Republicans). The authors had designed the study to assess the
independent effects of racial and political bias. They find that
discrimination occurs only toward Blacks who mention politics in any
way; that is, an intersectional bias, not something the authors had
previously considered (i.e., they had planned to look at racial and
political bias independently). The intersectional finding, though,
coheres with the theory of racial threat, where members of a majority
group act aversely to minorities who may threaten their political,
economic, or cultural standing (e.g., Blalock
[1967](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-53){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-53-back
.calibre1}; Craig et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-116){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-116-back
.calibre1}). That is, the authors[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
results connect with a theory that they had not initially considered.
After the fact, it seems problematic if the publication process was to
penalize the authors, given the existence of a clear theoretical
explanation that they had previously failed to
recognize.[^29^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-164){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-164-back
.calibre1} One might respond by claiming the process can adapt to such
situations (e.g., Humphreys et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-276){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-276-back-4
.calibre1}: 10[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}13, 18), but that would
introduce a large gray area with no clear guidelines on evaluating what
constitutes more
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}acceptable["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
exploratory theorizing. In short, the complexity of social science
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} accounting for context and
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_139 .calibre1}time
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} has resulted in disciplines with
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-131
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-657
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-733
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-867 .calibre1}multiple
approaches and theories. It is not clear that identifying applicable
theories after data collection should, in turn, require the collection
of more data (which, as mentioned, is a common refrain on exploratory
findings). Well-grounded theorizing can take place following data
collection.[^30^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-165){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-165-back
.calibre1} Coffman and Niederle
([2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-147-back-3
.calibre1}: 88) make a similar argument and point out that we can rely
on the review process and robustness tests to evaluate the extent of
p-hacking rather than
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}handcuffing["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
the researcher, or prioritizing research based on it being
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}confirmatory["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
or
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}exploratory["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(also see Laitin
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-330){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-330-back
.calibre1}). Scholars should distinguish p-hacking from post
hoc/exploratory theorizing, with the former being a questionable
research practice and the latter, as explained, being a part of the
research process in fields with many theories (Devezer et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-130-back
.calibre1}).[^31^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-166){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-166-back
.calibre1}

The point at which theorizing occurs connects to
Roth[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([1995](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-456){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-456-back-2
.calibre1}) goals of experiments: as discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_004.html_CN-bp-2-back-28
.calibre1}, searching facts, speaking to theorists, whispering in the
ears of princes. Pre-analysis plans in the
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_140
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-163
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-184
.calibre1}publication process may work best in testing well-developed
theory or when assessing a policy intervention (especially when it is
expensive to implement, as noted). Yet, much
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and in some areas the clear majority
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} of social science experimentation
involves searching for facts. In other words, data can help researchers
formulate specific relationships beyond their prior intuitions and
expectations. For example, Miller and
Krosnick[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2000](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-382){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-382-back
.calibre1}) classic article on political media effects speculated that
effects ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}[may]{.italic} be apparent only
among the relatively unusual group of citizens who are both highly
knowledgeable and highly trusting of the
media["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (304; emphasis added). This
statement represents an important finding from an incredibly influential
experiment, but it is not clear that the authors expected to find this
relationship. Had they presented the result as exploratory, it could
have hampered the study[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s publication and
[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-132
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-658
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-734
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-868
.calibre1}influence.

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-112
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-148
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-203
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-219
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-272 .calibre1}As a
third downside of strict adherence to pre-analysis plans, the process
may stunt innovation since scholars become incentivized to only test
well-developed hypotheses. Pre-analysis plans aim to generate greater
reproducibility (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-412-back-5
.calibre1}: 1423; Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-413-back-7
.calibre1}: 2602), but reproducibility does not always stimulate
innovation (Coffman and Nierdele
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-147-back-4
.calibre1}: 89). Relatedly, scholars may be less inclined to add
downstream outcome measures to their experiments
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is, measures that may not be the
subject of the main hypotheses but rather concern second-order
consequences (Sondheimer
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-489){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-489-back
.calibre1}). For instance, Druckman et al.
([2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-142){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-142-back-2
.calibre1}) show that framing universal healthcare in terms of lessening
inequality or raising costs not only alters healthcare opinions but also
leads to changes in opinions about immigration and taxes; the healthcare
frames change the way individuals think about these other issues (c.f.,
Hopkins and Mummolo
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-270){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-270-back
.calibre1}). The concern is that scholars may limit the number of these
types of post-treatment measures for fear of being accused of data
mining. Alternatively, scholars may not even fully exploit the data they
collect, restricting themselves to the pre-analysis plan, thereby
missing potentially important dynamics that could constitute crucial
innovations (Anderson
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-9){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-9-back
.calibre1}; Gelman
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-206){#part0013_split_004.html_REFe-r-206-back
.calibre1}). In fact, it could even preclude the use of alternative
statistical analyses of the same hypothesis that are essential to ensure
robustness, an essential aspect of
Popper[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s idea of consistently testing
relationships in []{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-252
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-368
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-914
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-956
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-996 .calibre1}various
ways.[^32^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-167){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-167-back
.calibre1}

[]{#part0013_split_004.html_page_141
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDXloc-tgt-229 .calibre1}In sum,
preregistration of an experiment prior to collecting data constitutes a
public good that can address publication biases, as these biases
generate misleading depictions of accumulated knowledge. The inclusion
of a detailed pre-analysis plan also serves a central purpose for the
researcher, ensuring that the experiment will produce the data that
he/she needs to test the key hypotheses. It may be that certain journals
also require preregistration and/or pre-analysis plans and, thus,
researchers should stay abreast of such developments prior to data
collection. In some cases, such as an experiment testing a clear theory
or an expensive one-shot experiment, the pros of adherence to a
pre-analysis plan may outweigh the cons.

Yet, in many cases, researchers, reviewers, and editors may do more harm
than good by strictly following pre-analysis plans. They need to be
conscious that doing so replaces one common criteria for publication
(statistical significance) for another (clear a priori hypotheses).
Whether reliance on a priori hypotheses as a criterion undermines
research quality and innovation remains unclear. At the very least, when
deciding whether to fully endorse these publishing institutions, one
should recognize the potential downsides (e.g., publishing poorly
implemented experiments, precluding the identification of relevant
theories after data collection and analysis, and undermining innovation)
against the possible upsides (e.g., vitiating bias against high quality
null results, possibly reducing
p-hacking).[^33^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-168){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-168-back
.calibre1} To be clear, those who advocate for widespread and strict use
of pre-analysis plans in the publication process do so with good
intention. Yet, such an approach should be done with extreme caution, if
at all, due to the complexity of the social sciences. The reality is
that most fields consist of a multitude of theories, and that the data
often clarify what []{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-292
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-302
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-336
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-684
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-764
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-774
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_004.html_IDClocr-tgt-834 .calibre1}theories
apply.[^34^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-169){#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-169-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0013_split_005.html}

::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::: {#part0013_split_005.html_A-sec-19 .sec}
:::::::::::::::: {#part0013_split_005.html_B-sec-26 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_005.html_page_142 .calibre1}Summary {#part0013_split_005.html_calibre_pb_5 .b}

Experiments play a role in the scientific process; and, in many ways,
not the most essential role. One should always connect experiments to
the reasoning that led one to use the method in the first place. This
entails documenting the rationale for the approach and then every
decision along the way. By so doing, one contributes to transparency and
the construction of a research program where
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-96 .calibre1}experiments build
upon each another. This is a tedious but necessary part of doing
experiments. The process itself makes it easy to then preregister and
submit a pre-analysis plan in a public registry. These issues
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} transparency, preregistration plans,
and []{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-222 .calibre1}pre-analysis
plans [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} echo the institutional reforms
put forth by the open science movement. These initiatives, if followed
to the letter, have the potential to fundamentally alter how experiments
are conducted, presented, and disseminated. Many of the initiatives seem
enticing on their face, but there are also multiple considerations and
potential
downsides.[^35^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-170){#part0013_split_005.html_FN-fn-170-back
.calibre1}

Before summarizing the argument from the previous section, I will make
two additional points. First, I have advocated for
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-72 .calibre1}documentation as a
way to improve the care put into experiments and the insights coming out
of them. The []{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-201 .calibre1}open
science motivations are broader, hoping to improve the practice of
science writ large. Whether it does so remains an open question.
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-27 .calibre1}Christensen and
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-183 .calibre1}Miguel
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_005.html_REFe-r-97-back-4
.calibre1}: 970) explain, there

::: disp-quote
is also the question about the impact that the adoption of these new
practices will ultimately have on the reliability of empirical research.
Will the use of study registries, PAPs \[pre-analysis plans\],
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDXloc-tgt-71 .calibre1}disclosure
statements, and open data and materials lead to improved research
quality in a way that can be credibly measured and assessed? To this
point, the presumption among advocates
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is that these changes will indeed
lead to improvements, but rigorous evidence on these effects
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} will be important in determining
which practices are in fact most
effective.[^36^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-171){#part0013_split_005.html_FN-fn-171-back
.calibre1}
:::

As mentioned, open science initiatives strongly endorse the use of
formal pre-analysis plans based on the belief that it generates better
science, but ultimately it remains unclear that the practice in fact
does so.

[]{#part0013_split_005.html_page_143
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-765
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-771
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-795
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-853 .calibre1}This
leads to my second point: Whether one fully endorses some of the reforms
depends on how one evaluates research quality. One metric that receives
considerable attention is replicability, the topic to which I turn next.
As I will discuss, whether replicability should be a central metric
remains unclear. The following summarizes the main points of the
previous section:

:::::::::::::: {#part0013_split_005.html_NL-lst-18 .list3}
::::::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Experimentalists benefit from documenting every design
decision in detail, reviewing the rationale and the implications. They
also should develop a plan for how the data will be analyzed.

:::::: {#part0013_split_005.html_NL-lst-19 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} This ensures a long-standing record, facilitates writing a
paper later, and helps to satisfy transparency and publication
expectations such as providing access to stimuli and outcome measures.
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} This type of record is important for future work that may
expand on a given design.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} A data analysis plan pushes the researcher to confirm that
data collected will provide a suitable test of the hypotheses of
interest.
:::
::::::
:::::::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} Preregistration refers to authors, prior to data
collection, formally registering their study in an independent registry.
In the aggregate, preregistration of studies on a given topic serves as
an antidote to the file drawer publication bias problem where
statistically significant results are, all else constant, favored in the
publication process.
:::

::::::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} Formal pre-analysis plans refer to a part of a
preregistration that details the exact plan for data analysis. Many draw
a distinction between confirmatory hypotheses that are part of a
pre-analysis plan and exploratory hypotheses that are not.

:::::: {#part0013_split_005.html_NL-lst-20 .list4}
::: list-item
[a]{.label2} Advocates argue that following pre-analysis plans helps
minimize the inappropriate analysis of data and thus puts scientific
discovery on firmer footing.
:::

::: list-item
[b]{.label2} Yet, many downsides should give one pause when it comes to
strict adherence to a formal pre-analysis plan: It privileges a priori
theory as a basis for publication over statistical significance (which
may correlate with quality given the number of mistakes one can make in
an experiment that would prevent finding significant results); ignores
that many theories exist including theories an experimentalist may not
have initially considered; and constrains innovation and the study of
downstream consequences.
:::

::: list-item
[c]{.label2} Pre-analysis plans when testing clear and well-developed
theory and/or practical interventions that involve large investments are
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_page_144 .calibre1}sensible. In the latter
case, the expense of the study may preclude further work that can build
on unexpected findings. In other situations, however, the downsides of
strict pre-analysis plans may outweigh their
[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-348
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-766
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-772
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-796
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_005.html_IDClocr-tgt-854
.calibre1}benefits.
:::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0013_split_006.html}

::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0013_split_006.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
# []{#part0013_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-355 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_006.html_IDClocr-tgt-851 .calibre1}Doing It Again? Replication {#part0013_split_006.html_calibre_pb_6 .a}

Once one finishes an experiment, the next step is to analyze the data
and, typically, to write a paper. The documentation process just
discussed provides the author with part of the paper in advance;
otherwise, it often helps to emulate the writing from papers that the
experimentalist admires (also see Gerber and Green
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-212){#part0013_split_006.html_REFe-r-212-back-19
.calibre1}: 424[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}446). Beyond writing,
another issue that invariably arises is whether to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replicate["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
the study. Even more, some suggest research agendas focused on the
replication of a prior experiment or experiments (e.g., OSC 2015: 943,
aac4716[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}1; Nosek et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_006.html_REFe-r-413-back-8
.calibre1}: 2602; c.f.; Devezer et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_006.html_REFe-r-130-back-2
.calibre1}). Most would agree that replication plays a meaningful role
in science by
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}correcting["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
extant findings that do not hold or by tempering overgeneralizations
(e.g., Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_006.html_REFe-r-412-back-6
.calibre1}:
1423).[^37^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-172){#part0013_split_006.html_FN-fn-172-back
.calibre1} That said, in thinking about whether replication should be a
part of a research agenda [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} either as a
starting point or as a follow-up [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one
must consider what replications entail exactly; how they should be
interpreted; why they can be ambiguous; and the opportunity cost of
investing in replications.
:::
::::
:::::

[]{#part0013_split_007.html}

::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::: front-matter-part
::::::: {#part0013_split_007.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
:::::: {#part0013_split_007.html_B-sec-27 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_007.html_page_145 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-413 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-423 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-705 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-729 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-841 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-845 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1061 .calibre1}Defining Replication {#part0013_split_007.html_calibre_pb_7 .b}

The first question concerns how to define replication. Freese and
Peterson
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-195){#part0013_split_007.html_REFe-r-195-back
.calibre1}) offer a useful approach by differentiating
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}new["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
studies along two dimensions: (1) are the data new? (yes or no) and (2)
is the analysis the same as in the original study? (yes or no). They
characterize the old data/same analysis cell as being
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}verifiability,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
since in essence one seeks to verify what was done (i.e., rerun the same
analysis on the same data). The old data/different analysis cell refers
to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}robustness,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
as that means checking that the same results hold (e.g., using a
different but still appropriate statistical/analytical technique).
Freese and Peterson call the new data/similar analysis category
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}repeatability,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
since, for many, the expectation is that when one collects data with a
new sample using a similar design/procedure/analysis the results should
repeat. The final cell involves
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}generalization["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} when a researcher collects new data
using distinct methods or [in different settings]{.italic}. [Table
5.2](#part0013_split_007.html_TN-tblw-10){#part0013_split_007.html_TN-tblw-10-back-2
.calibre1} provides a summary of this framework.

::::: {#part0013_split_007.html_TN-tblw-10 .table-wrap}
::: caption
[[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDXloc-tgt-245 .calibre1}Table
5.2.]{.label2} [Freese and Peterson[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-195){#part0013_split_007.html_REFe-r-195-back-2
.calibre1}) forms of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replication["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.title1}
:::

::: front-matter-part
![](knowledge_base/md/general/druckman-2022-experimental-thinking_images/images/00031.gif){.graphic1}
:::
:::::

I focus here, following many recent discussions, on Freese and
Peterson[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}repeatability.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Should an experimentalist devote time to repeating an experiment? Many
refer to
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}repeatability["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replication,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
as will I (e.g., Bollen et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-57){#part0013_split_007.html_REFe-r-57-back
.calibre1}; National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
2019).[^38^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-173){#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-173-back
.calibre1}

In thinking about replication, recall that experiments use or exploit an
intervention to address the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference.
That problem, as explained in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_007.html_CN-bp-2-back-29
.calibre1}, involves comparing [Outcome(treatment, unit) versus
Outcome(control, unit)]{.italic}, or determining the difference, D~O~,
between: ([Outcome(treatment, unit) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Outcome(control, unit]{.italic}))~O~. Is there a significant D~O~? The
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}O["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
subscript indicates an [original]{.italic} data collection. One cannot
perfectly compute D~O,~ and thus, experimentalists address the problem
by employing a design using the statistical
[]{#part0013_split_007.html_page_146 .calibre1}(random assignment) or
scientific (control) approach. This includes all of the underlying
assumptions discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_007.html_CN-bp-2-back-30
.calibre1}, as well as dealing with the potential problems that could
arise in conducting an experiment. Put another way, one must meet the
relevant assumptions required for strong causal inference.

Replication, in essence, involves addressing the same Fundamental
Problem of Causal Inference but doing it another time with a new data
source. Here
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}R["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} refers
to [replication]{.italic} and the goal is to estimate: D~R~ =
([Outcome(treatment, unit) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
Outcome(control, unit]{.italic}))~R~. For many, replication succeeds if
D~O~ [≈]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} D~R~ (i.e., if the process is
replicated, the outcome should replicate as
well).[^39^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-174){#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-174-back
.calibre1} Consequently, a compounded problem of causal inference exists
since it entails addressing the problem twice, thereby making and
hopefully ensuring the satisfaction of the underlying assumptions of the
design. However, it becomes even more difficult as the original
experimental result also is a function of the elements of external
validity [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the sample (S), the treatment
(T), the outcome measure (M), and the context (C). O is a [f]{.italic}
(S, T, O, C)~O~. R also is a function of its sample, treatment, measure,
and context [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} R is a [f]{.italic} (S, T,
O, C)~R~. For a replication to be
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}successful,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
then, one not only has to address the Fundamental Problem of Causal
Inference twice, but also assume/ensure fairly invariant samples,
treatments, outcome measures, and contexts, or at least ones that do not
moderate the experimental effect. That is, [f]{.italic} (S, T, O, C)~O~
[≈]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [f]{.italic} (S, T, O, C)~R~. Clearly,
these are difficult conditions to meet, and often very challenging even
to assess. I refer to this as the fundamental problem of replication:
addressing the Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference twice
[and]{.italic} addressing approximate invariance across the original and
replication studies in samples, treatments, measures, and
contexts.[^40^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-175){#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-175-back
.calibre1} Experimentalists considering replication must assess not only
(the statistical or scientific) solutions to the Fundamental Problem of
Causal Inference but also systematically the dimensions of external
validity. Failure to do so could result in a very misleading set of
results. If one pursues a follow-up data collection, then one must think
through the elements of the fundamental problem of
[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-414
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-424
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-706
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-730
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-842
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-846
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_007.html_IDClocr-tgt-1062
.calibre1}replication.
::::::
:::::::
::::::::
:::::::::

[]{#part0013_split_008.html}

::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: front-matter-part
::::: {#part0013_split_008.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
:::: {#part0013_split_008.html_B-sec-28 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_008.html_page_147 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-673 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-847 .calibre1}Interpreting Replications {#part0013_split_008.html_calibre_pb_8 .b}

How does one assess if something replicated (D~O~
[≈]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} D~R~) (e.g., National Academies of
Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2019)? There exist a host of
distinct metrics that scholars use to assess whether single or multiple
replication attempts repeat prior work, and it remains unclear how to
identify the most relevant metric (Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_008.html_REFe-r-85-back
.calibre1}:
1434).[^41^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-176){#part0013_split_008.html_FN-fn-176-back
.calibre1} An even more perplexing methodological issue concerns the
presumed null hypothesis with a replication: studies replicate (e.g.,
D~O~ = D~R~). The question then typically is whether we should reject
the null hypothesis, which would suggest a failed replication. This
approach means documenting replication entails [accepting]{.italic} a
null hypothesis (Hedges
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-259){#part0013_split_008.html_REFe-r-259-back
.calibre1}). As explained in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_008.html_CN-bp-2-back-31
.calibre1}, experimental analyses generally aim to [reject]{.italic} a
null of no difference (e.g., treatment mean = control mean), with the
goal to offer evidence of a causal effect. That is, one wants to
ultimately reject, not accept the null hypothesis. Recognizing this
contrary logic between experimental tests and replication approaches,
Hedges and Schauer
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-260){#part0013_split_008.html_REFe-r-260-back
.calibre1}: 11[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}12) suggest that

::: disp-quote
if the goal of conducting a replication is to determine that study
results are similar, then this \[i.e., H~o~: D~O~ = D~R~\] is the wrong
inferential structure. Instead, the burden of proof should be on
replication, rather than non-replication. In this setup, the null
hypothesis is that the studies failed to replicate
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and rejecting the null hypothesis
would mean that we conclude that the studies replicate.
:::

We would be looking to see if we can reject the null that D~O~
[≠]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} D~R~. This changes the way to think
about replication as it requires conclusive
[]{#part0013_split_008.html_page_148 .calibre1}evidence of replication
rather than evidence of non-replication (to reject the null). It shifts
the burden of proof to replication (also see Hedges
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-259){#part0013_split_008.html_REFe-r-259-back-2
.calibre1}).[^42^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-177){#part0013_split_008.html_FN-fn-177-back
.calibre1} Replication efforts, with this logic, should not assume that
the original H~0~ (from the study being replicated) has been irrevocably
rejected.

[]{#part0013_split_008.html_IDXloc-tgt-103 .calibre1}This highlights why
many tests are needed to determine whether a causal proposition has or
has not withstood falsification. Practically, this means a research
agenda focused on replication should consider a distinct inferential
structure. If instead one pursues replication to assess if a theoretical
proposition holds in multiple tests [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
that is, to conduct follow-up tests after an initial experiment
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one should discuss the follow-up
(replication) results in terms of finding or not finding evidence
consistent with the initial hypothesis, rather than in terms of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replication.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
This highlights the problem of comparing effect sizes between an
original study and a replication since particular effect sizes likely
vary due to unidentified sources. This segues into my next point, which
concerns the ambiguity of []{#part0013_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-674
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_008.html_IDClocr-tgt-848
.calibre1}replications.
::::
:::::
::::::
:::::::

[]{#part0013_split_009.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0013_split_009.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
::: {#part0013_split_009.html_B-sec-29 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-377 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-385 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-421 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-501 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-843 .calibre1}The Ambiguity of Replication {#part0013_split_009.html_calibre_pb_9 .b}

The fundamental problem of replication is inherently difficult (also see
Wong and Steiner
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-516){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-516-back
.calibre1}). On the external validity dimensions, using identical
treatments and outcome measures does not ensure the replication of the
context (or timing). Moreover, even a similarly drawn sample from the
same population can introduce uncertainty as many sample pools evolve
over time due to a changing world (see, e.g., Aronow et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-19){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-19-back
.calibre1}; Arechar and Rand
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-18){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-18-back
.calibre1}).[^43^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-178){#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-178-back
.calibre1} When one ostensibly fails to find results consistent with a
prior experiment (i.e., D~O~ [≠]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} D~R~ in
the typical treatment), it provides an opportunity for learning à la my
previous discussion of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}kaput.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

A failure to find the same results again could stem from an inability to
satisfy internal validity assumptions in addressing the Fundamental
Problem of Causal Inference (e.g., low experimental realism). Or, it may
reflect external validity issues such that generalization may be
constrained []{#part0013_split_009.html_page_149 .calibre1}to
[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-327
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-433
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-485
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-755
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-997
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-999
.calibre1}particular
conditions.[^44^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-179){#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-179-back
.calibre1} In political science [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a
discipline focused on context and time (e.g., Druckman and Lupia
[2006](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-158){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-158-back-3
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a vast number of findings
undoubtedly apply only to particular contexts and times. The same surely
holds in other fields where social and economic settings alter
behaviors. Indeed, as discussed in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0013_split_009.html_CN-bp-3-back-16
.calibre1}, studies independently deemed less sensitive to contextual
considerations (e.g., time, rural/urban) were more likely to replicate
in a massive replication effort of 100 experiments (Van Bavel et al.
[2016a](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-504){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-504-back-2
.calibre1}).

To take this argument a step further, consider replication from a
Popperian perspective. One attempts to test a hypothesis that already
has evidence in its favor. However, if the replication does not produce
consistent results, then one either abandons the theory, or, assuming no
methodological errors, amends the theory based on its conditionality (on
replication and theory development, see Klein
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-317){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-317-back-2
.calibre1}).[^45^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-180){#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-180-back
.calibre1} This process then can lead to a more focused test to try to
directly isolate conditions/moderators. Put another way, it often is
difficult to know if an attempted replication constitutes a replication
study or a generalization study, due to the challenge of discerning what
qualifies as ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}different
settings["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Bollen et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-57){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-57-back-2
.calibre1}: 7). Freese and Peterson
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-195){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-195-back-3
.calibre1}: 158) acknowledge this slippery slope in pointing out that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}what empirical work tests is not so much
propositions as such but ideas about the scope in which the propositions
apply [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} The chronic ambiguity of
generalizability is whether explanations revised to accommodate new
results constitute a legitimate advance or are simply ad hoc
explanations.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This strikes me as the
vital question with any experiment that aims to repeat what had
previously been done. In fact, in some cases it would be odd for an
experiment implemented at a later time to find a result consistent with
prior studies, such as studies on topics where societal attitudes (e.g.,
gender, race) have clearly changed over time (e.g., Burden et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-74){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-74-back
.calibre1}; Krupnikov et al. 2021b; Valenzuela and Reny
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-503){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-503-back
.calibre1}). Devezer et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-130-back-3
.calibre1}) make the point that replication need not and perhaps should
not be seen as cornerstone of science, as it depends on the field/area
of study. Among other dynamics, context and time dependency means that
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}science does
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} rather often, in fact
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} make claims about non-reproducible
phenomena and deems such claims to be
[]{#part0013_split_009.html_page_150 .calibre1}true in spite of the
non-reproducibility. In these instances what scientists do is to define
and implement appropriate criteria for assessing the rigor and the
validity of the results [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} without
making a reference to replication or reproduction of an experimental
result["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
([]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-328
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-434
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-486
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1000 .calibre1}Devezer
et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-130-back-4
.calibre1}: 2[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}3).

[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-34
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-259 .calibre1}Given the
fundamental problem of replication, it seems in many, if not, most
cases, it would be most productive for researchers to treat inconsistent
results as a generalization issue. The challenge, then, for the
researcher, is to identify the scope conditions (also see Redish et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-451){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-451-back
.calibre1}:
5043).[^46^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-181){#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-181-back
.calibre1} In short, I recommend a forward-looking view of
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replication["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} if one finds inconsistent results,
they might consider it an opportunity to build in a new direction rather
than a time to look back dismissively at prior experiments (Anderson et
al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-8){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-8-back
.calibre1}).

I am far from the first to point out the challenges and ambiguity of
replication. Most notably, Collins
([1985](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-102){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-102-back
.calibre1},
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-349){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-349-back
.calibre1}) accentuates the interpretative nature of experiments and
replications in the sciences. In essence, every time an experiment is
done again, it involves new explanatory factors
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} even if unmeasured
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} due to unavoidable changes between
experiments (e.g., in timing, context, sample). One should think about
such changes rather than assuming invariance. The end goal should be to
accumulate knowledge on a given topic. Testing a proposition with
repeated experiments and/or expanding on prior designs is a route to
knowledge, but only when experimentalists carefully consider potential
differences and use inconsistent results (e.g., kaput) as a way to
generate new work.

[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-557
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-717
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1085 .calibre1}One
example of carefully considering differences comes from Westwood et al.
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-512){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-512-back
.calibre1}). In prior work, Lelkes and Westwood
([2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-341){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-341-back
.calibre1}) revealed limits to the extent partisans discriminate against
each another in []{#part0013_split_009.html_page_151 .calibre1}the
[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-17
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-113
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-159
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-263
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDXloc-tgt-278
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-273 .calibre1}United
States (e.g., they do not endorse direct harm to their political
opponents). Those data came from 2014 and the authors realized that the
partisan context had substantially changed by 2017, with the election of
President Trump. They reran the experiment in 2017, speculating but not
finding an increase in prejudice. Here the authors sought to assess
contextual change and replicated as a way to test for the effects
(although they find scant evidence of change). Busby and Druckman
([2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-76){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-76-back
.calibre1}) do the same thing in their replication study of how
irrelevant effects can impact political opinion, replicating their prior
experiment from a distinct context. Both these examples highlight the
usefulness of what Janz and Freese (2021) call
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}replicating
yourself.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

Another example is Stark et al.
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-490){#part0013_split_009.html_REFe-r-490-back
.calibre1}), who replicate survey question order effects across
contexts; they show, for example, that priming evenhandedness via
question order occurs more strongly in countries with more
individualistic as opposed to collectivistic cultures (priming is not
needed in collectivist cultures). They conclude that the norm of
evenhandedness does not depend on individual attributes as much as the
variations in culture. Here the contextual effects were theorized in
advance and replications tested them. These examples reveal that it can
be beneficial to think about context before conducting a replication.

Practically, experimentalists should neither assume a prior result will
replicate nor launch a research agenda for the sole purpose of
replication. Rather, use prior designs and systematically extend them to
explore new explanatory factors, particularly those related to context,
and seek to []{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-378
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-422
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-502
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-844
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-558
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-718
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_009.html_IDClocr-tgt-1086
.calibre1}generalize results.
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0013_split_010.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0013_split_010.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
::: {#part0013_split_010.html_B-sec-30 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-689 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-849 .calibre1}Opportunity Costs to Replication {#part0013_split_010.html_calibre_pb_10 .b}

I just argued that a replication ultimately should be done in the
service of testing an extant theory, empirical relationship, or policy
intervention. This understanding appears often lost on those doing
replications and creates a bit of a dilemma. On the one hand, a good
replication tries to emulate all that was done in the original study. On
the other hand, a good experiment uses the most valid constructs, etc.
For me, the latter goal should take precedence. If that means the
replication becomes less of a match to the prior study, the trade-off is
acceptable if it makes for a stronger test of the proposition under
study.[^47^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-182){#part0013_split_010.html_FN-fn-182-back
.calibre1} The ultimate end goal is []{#part0013_split_010.html_page_152
.calibre1}not replication but assessing the causal relationship under
consideration so as to inform a theory (Stroebe and Strack
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-492){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-492-back
.calibre1}; Nosek and Errington
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-414){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-414-back
.calibre1}). If a replication fails, a Popperian perspective should lead
a researcher to ask what to do with the underlying theory, not what it
means for the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}rate of
replication["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [per se]{.italic}. As
Smaldino
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-476){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-476-back
.calibre1}: 9) states, ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}we
don[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}t just want science to be
reproducibility. We want it to help us to make better sense of the world
[...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} we need good
theory.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-37
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-244 .calibre1}The
implication is that a research agenda that replicates with the focus on
mechanically repeating what was done in the past comes with an
opportunity cost of doing something that can more directly advance
theory. At times, doing the same exact thing makes sense, to be sure,
but even then experimentalists need to carefully consider the
aforementioned inevitable differences over time and context. This also
applies to operationalizations [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} recall
the project described in [Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0013_split_010.html_CN-bp-3-back-17
.calibre1} where different teams of researchers sought to test the same
hypotheses from the areas of moral judgment, negotiation, and implicit
cognition. In so doing, they opted for different stimuli that, in turn,
led to very distinct results (Landy et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-332){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-332-back
.calibre1}). This project of ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}conceptual
replication["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} provided crucial insight
into the robustness of different theoretical constructs concerning their
operationalizations, and would have been missed if the teams instead all
strictly replicated one approach. The point is that resources and times
are best spent in the service of addressing the questions that motivate
our research agendas and not strictly replicating past work for the sake
of doing so. This perspective [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that
replications inherently will vary [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
questions the worth of programs that purportedly aim to serve as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}checks on
science["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} via replication.

[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-235
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-409
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-651 .calibre1}That
said, this is not meant as an argument against repeating past study
designs. As discussed earlier, a fruitful way to design an experiment
entails building on past efforts. Given the challenges of creating sound
stimuli, valid measures, and useful designs, researchers can benefit
from extending designs that have already overcome such challenges. This
often involves replicating parts of the design and building in
additional conditions/stimuli. For example, recall the Druckman and
Nelson
([2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-160){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-160-back-3
.calibre1}) campaign finance framing experiment detailed in prior
chapters. Thus far, it has been described as a study that randomly
exposed some respondents to a special interests frame (for campaign
finance regulation) and others to a free speech frame (against campaign
finance regulation) or a control. This experiment revealed a framing
effect (e.g., the special interests frame led to more support for
regulation), which confirms much prior work on the topic in line with
the classic Nelson et al.
([1997](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-407){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-407-back
.calibre1}) study. However, []{#part0013_split_010.html_page_153
.calibre1}Druckman and Nelson (2003) also
[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-305
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDXloc-tgt-309 .calibre1}sought to
identify conditions under which framing effects occur, theorizing that
they may not when exposure is followed by interpersonal discussions
among people with varying opinions. They thus added experimental
conditions that included conversations and show that these interactions
condition framing effects. In essence, they conceptually replicated
canonical framing studies en route to adding to them and identifying
generalizability conditions. They sought to generalize and innovate via
replication rather than to replicate for
replication[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sake. The very factors that
make experiments so difficult to conduct and interpret apply even more
so to replications. This is why a fruitful strategy is to build on
well-established experimental protocols when designing new studies.
Validation and verification have a role to play (Dunning
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-167){#part0013_split_010.html_REFe-r-167-back
.calibre1}), but achieving those goals need not prevent simultaneous
innovation. Time is better spent using replication as a route to
novelty, as, in essence that is being done whether the experimentalist
knows it or not (i.e., every experiment is a new test under new
conditions and thus providing
[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-386
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-690
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-756
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-850
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-998
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-236
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-274
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-410
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_010.html_IDClocr-tgt-652
.calibre1}something novel).
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0013_split_011.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0013_split_011.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
::: {#part0013_split_011.html_B-sec-31 .sec}
## []{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-61 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-201 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-223 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-285 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-341 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-719 .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-897 .calibre1}Accumulating Studies to Identify Effect Sizes {#part0013_split_011.html_calibre_pb_11 .b}

This line of argument leads me back to a point brought up in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_011.html_CN-bp-2-back-32
.calibre1} about effect sizes. There I suggested that unless a policy
goal drives an experimental agenda (see the scalability discussion in
[Chapter
3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0013_split_011.html_CN-bp-3-back-18
.calibre1}), the effect size of a treatment from a given experiment
pales in significance to whether one falsified the hypothesis. Here I
offer a caveat, arguing that the importance of effect sizes lies in the
accumulation of evidence, via replications of some sort. It is through
this process that one might isolate an effect size, which I acknowledge,
even for non[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} policy-oriented
experiments, can be a quantity of interest for substantive reasons that
do and, as I have argued, should drive any experimental design. For
example, whether the effect is small or large informs debates such as:
how much do negative advertisements influence voting turnout; how much
does being a minority undermine legislative responsiveness; and how much
does being a female candidate affect electability.

However, to see why identifying an effect size is best done (or can only
be reliably done) via an accumulation of evidence, consider experiments
on party cue effects. One could argue the effect size from a given
experiment on party cues (e.g., where the experimenter asks respondents
about a policy after randomly receiving an endorsement from a party or
no endorsement) matters by providing knowledge about the extent to which
[]{#part0013_split_011.html_page_154 .calibre1}parties shape opinions.
The impact of party endorsements informs what we know about political
attitude formation and normative theories of
[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-289 .calibre1}democratic
representation (e.g., Druckman
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-140){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-140-back
.calibre1}). Yet, studies suggest the effect of party cues on policy
opinions ranges from 3[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}43 percent of the
policy opinion scale (Bullock
[2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-145){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-145-back
.calibre1}; also see Bullock
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-69){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-69-back
.calibre1}). Tappin
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-495){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-495-back
.calibre1}) explains that such variations reflect huge differences
across designs, including the type of baseline policy information
provided; the source of the cue (e.g., a party leader, the party in
general, etc.); the format of the cue (e.g., varying percentages of
partisans support a given policy); whether cues come from one or more
parties; distinct outcome measures (e.g., binary versus scaled outcome
and in the case of the latter the extremity of the scale endpoints); and
more (also see Slothuus
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-474){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-474-back-2
.calibre1}). Tappin further points out [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and then focuses upon [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} how party cue
experiments differ dramatically in the policy issues used
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example, there is no sampling from
a population of policy issues (Druckman and Leeper
[2012a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-153){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-153-back-2
.calibre1}; Clifford et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-99){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-99-back
.calibre1}; also see [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_011.html_CN-bp-2-back-33
.calibre1} discussion). This astute observation makes clear that
inferring and generalizing about a specific effect size from one or a
few experiments is challenging, if not misleading, due to invariable
design differences that one may not even imagine until a mature
literature develops.

[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-1
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-73
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-187
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-193
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-551
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-579
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-615
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-643
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-693
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-715
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-899
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-915
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-1037
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-1069 .calibre1}This is
why I have minimized the relevance of effect sizes from single
experiments. That said, experimentalists can pursue a few approaches to
identify the size of a given treatment. First, naturally occurring data,
particularly when an intervention occurs either randomly or in a way
where one can be confident about unit homogeneity (see [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_011.html_CN-bp-2-back-34
.calibre1}) can offer insight. For instance, Slothuus and Bisgaard
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-475){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-475-back
.calibre1}) track Danish citizens[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} policy
opinions when their political parties, without warning, reversed their
positions on two salient welfare issues (i.e., satisfying the unit
homogeneity assumption). They find that partisans changed their opinions
in line with their parties by roughly 15 percentage points. Their study
provides a naturally occurring baseline of party cue effects against
which experiments can compare themselves and isolate design features to
explain deviations from the baseline. Second, insight can be garnered by
comparing effect sizes across types of experiments so as to see if there
is a consistency in effect sizes (e.g., fields versus laboratory;
Coppock and Green
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-110){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-110-back
.calibre1}). Strong correspondence leads to confidence in the size of a
treatment.

Third, the ideal approach entails aggregating multiple experiments
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} including if not especially
replications [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} since doing so envelopes
unknowable dimensions that vary across experiments (e.g., timing,
context, []{#part0013_split_011.html_page_155 .calibre1}sample, etc.).
This ostensibly contradicts my prior hesitation about replications. To
clarify, my point is that any replication unavoidably differs from prior
work and this is exactly why aggregating similar studies provides
progress by, in essence, controlling for unidentified confounds. This
reality accentuates the advantages of replicating and also extending
prior experiments, or as stated,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}innovate via
replication.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}

Meta-analyses synthesize treatment effect sizes to arrive at a single
effect size estimate. For example, one may be interested in the effect
of negative political advertising on voting turnout: How much does it
change turnout? As discussed in [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_011.html_CN-bp-2-back-35
.calibre1}, an initial experiment reported that those exposed to
negative advertisements are 2.5 percent less likely to vote than those
exposed to no political advertisement (Ansolabehere et al.
[1994](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-13){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-13-back
.calibre1}:
833).[^48^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-183){#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-183-back
.calibre1} But that single study had idiosyncratic features of focusing
on particular campaigns (in California) at a particular time (1990s)
with particular types of negative ads (e.g., focusing on salient
campaign themes). Once many more studies were conducted across various
samples, contexts, times, and stimuli, Lau et al.
([2007](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-336){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-336-back-2
.calibre1}) meta-analyzed them (111 studies) to isolate the likely
effect size (also see Lau et al.
[1999](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-335){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-335-back-2
.calibre1}). They find an aggregate effect size of a fairly meaningless
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}.07. Here then the accumulation of
studies led to a conclusion of no significant effect size, but one can
easily imagine a situation where an aggregation of results leads to a
positive or negative and substantively meaningful effect. For instance,
Costa
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-113){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-113-back-4
.calibre1}) meta-analyzes forty-one experiments on legislative
responsiveness [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} à la Butler and
Broockman
([2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-79){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-79-back-3
.calibre1}) [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} isolating an effect size
such that requests from minority constituents are almost 10 percent less
likely to receive a response than nonminority constituents (249).
Oschatz and Marker
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-421){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-421-back
.calibre1}) meta-analyze fourteen experiments on the persuasiveness of
narrative messages (i.e., those with a beginning, middle, and end that
provide information about the scene, characters, and conflict), finding
that it changes attitudes by about .14 standard deviations more than
analogues nonnarrative messages.

Schwarz and Coppock
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-460){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-460-back
.calibre1}) meta-analyze sixty-seven candidate choice experiments on
gender; these studies had produced wide-ranging effects, from women
candidates receiving nearly 11 percent fewer votes than comparable men
candidates, to women garnering greater than 9 percent more votes. The
authors aggregate the studies to identify that an
[]{#part0013_split_011.html_page_156 .calibre1}effect estimate of being
a woman candidate (relative to a male candidate) leads to a roughly 2
percentage point gain in vote margin. This example highlights not only
the danger of overinterpreting a single treatment effect, given the 20
percent range in effect sizes in the literature, but also how taking
stock of a variation in treatments can advance knowledge by pushing
researchers to consider the source of the variation (Borenstein et al.
[2009](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-61){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-61-back
.calibre1}: part 4). Schwarz and Coppock
([2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-460){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-460-back-2
.calibre1}) exploit the fact that the studies explored a host of other
factors aside from gender, finding, for example, that the gender bonus
is marginally larger for White candidates than Black candidates
(although short of
significance).[^49^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-184){#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-184-back
.calibre1}

Indeed, meta-analyses provide the most insight when they identify
relationships between []{#part0013_split_011.html_IDXloc-tgt-153
.calibre1}effects sizes and aspects of the intervention, context,
sample, etc. (e.g., Lepper et al.
[1999](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-343){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-343-back
.calibre1}). In that vein, it is essential that any aggregation endeavor
includes only studies that employ commensurate treatments and outcome
measures. As meta-analyses proliferate, ensuring comparability and
inclusion of high-quality studies becomes vital; ultimately, a
meta-analysis constitutes an observational study that combines data
based on the quantity and not the quality of observations and thus the
author(s) must serve as quality gatekeepers (Packer
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-422){#part0013_split_011.html_REFe-r-422-back
.calibre1}).

In sum, a given experimental treatment effect is limited by the
conditions of the test, conditions that one typically cannot fully
appreciate until considering a range of studies that introduce plausible
variations in the tests (à la the party cue examples). Thus, a single
effect should be cautiously interpreted but can become essential,
informing substantive debates when one synthesizes a literature.
Replications play a vital role in that process, particularly when they
explicitly extend upon what is
[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-62
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-202
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-224
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-286
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-342
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-720
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-898
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-2
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-74
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-188
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-194
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-552
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-580
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-616
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-644
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-694
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-716
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-900
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-916
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-1038
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_011.html_IDClocr-tgt-1070 .calibre1}already
known.[^50^](#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-185){#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-185-back
.calibre1}
:::
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0013_split_012.html}

:::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::::::: {#part0013_split_012.html_A-sec-20 .sec}
::::::::: {#part0013_split_012.html_B-sec-32 .sec}
## Summary {#part0013_split_012.html_calibre_pb_12 .b}

When one completes an experiment, an obvious question concerns what to
do next (beyond writing a paper). Whether one should invest in
conducting another experiment on the same topic
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is replicate prior
[]{#part0013_split_012.html_page_157 .calibre1}work
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is not an easy question to answer,
particularly in light of its emphasis via the open science movement.
Replications play a vital role in the scientific progress, but often
less so for replication[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sake, and more
as a route to generalization, innovation, and aggregation. A summary of
my main points concerning how to think about and pursue replications is
as follows:

:::::::: {#part0013_split_012.html_NL-lst-21 .list3}
::: list-item
[(1)]{.label2} Replication typically refers to repeating a prior
experimental design with new data. Doing so entails addressing
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the fundamental problem of
replication.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} This refers to the reality
that any replication compounds the Fundamental Problem of Causal
Inference since it must be done twice, [and]{.italic} one must attend to
the dimensions of external validity in the process (e.g., are the
samples, treatments, measures, and contexts comparable?).
Experimentalists interested in repeating a study need to systematically
assess these issues (instead of assuming they are met).
:::

::: list-item
[(2)]{.label2} If an experimentalist[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
research agenda is to replicate prior studies, the inferential structure
needs to treat the null hypothesis as a failed replication. When the
goal is to conduct another study as a follow-up, an experimentalist
should focus on finding evidence consistent with the initial hypothesis
(rather than in terms of replication of the effect found in the earlier
experiment).
:::

::: list-item
[(3)]{.label2} Replication efforts are inherently ambiguous, given
variations in the dimensions of external validity. It can be more
productive to think of repeated studies (replications) as attempts to
generalize. This can be done by systematically assessing changes prior
to the replication or doing it afterward. Regardless, the goal should be
to consider how another (replication) experiment informs the original
hypothesis or hypotheses.
:::

::: list-item
[(4)]{.label2} Replication for
replication[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s sake comes with an
opportunity cost. Instead, a repeated study should be done to advance
theory. This often means repeating a prior design, but extending it in
innovative ways. In that sense, replication is a crucial route to
progress in experimental social science because it provides the basis
for innovation in theory.
:::

::: list-item
[(5)]{.label2} While the effect sizes from a single experiment and/or a
replication should be interpreted with great caution, a series of
similar experiments, including replications, can be aggregated via a
meta-analysis to isolate an average effect size and to explore sources
of []{#part0013_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-356
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_012.html_IDClocr-tgt-852
.calibre1}variations in effects.
:::
::::::::
:::::::::
::::::::::
:::::::::::
::::::::::::

[]{#part0013_split_013.html}

::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: front-matter-part
::::: {#part0013_split_013.html_A-sec-21 .sec}
# []{#part0013_split_013.html_page_158 .calibre1}Conclusion {#part0013_split_013.html_calibre_pb_13 .a}

The []{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-21
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-261
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-357
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-497
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-855
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-923 .calibre1}goal of
experimentation is to arrive at generalizable causal inferences. This is
done in the context of the larger scientific process that,
realistically, does not operate mechanically from question to theory to
hypothesis to test to result. There exists an inevitable back and forth
between data and theory in much of the social sciences, where multiple
theories explain behavior in varying contexts. That reality should be a
meaningful consideration as people think about experiments. It also
affects how to approach steps prior, during, and after an experiment.
Questions emerge from various sources, including failures from other
experiments. Conducting an experiment is much more complex than it
appears at first glance, which is why it is important to maintain clear
records throughout the process. That said, experimentalists should not
be constrained by formal pre-analysis plans, or mechanical efforts to
replicate prior work. As Sniderman
([1995](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-482){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-482-back
.calibre1}: 465) states,

::: disp-quote
[to replicate an existing finding [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to
follow the precise path taken by a previous researcher]{.italic}, and
then improve on the data or methodology in one way or another. Believe
that, and you miss what science [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
including social science [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} is really
about. Accuracy, meticulousness, exactness
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} all are virtues
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} but they are minor virtues and should
not be worshiped as the soul of science. Imagination, originality,
creativity, seeing what others not only failed to see but did not even
suspect [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is the heart of science
of the first order. (Italics in original)
:::

To be clear, replication and innovation are not incompatible. In fact,
replication, when thought of as a way to generalize and aggregate,
offers a springboard to innovation. The key is to maintain curiosity, do
so rigorously, and remember that the ultimate goal is to answer
questions about the world. In that spirit, the highlights of this
chapter are as follows:

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   The questions that can lead to an experiment come from a host of
    sources as captured by [ASK: assess]{.italic} the world and the
    field; [socialize]{.italic} with colleagues, teachers/students, and
    nonacademics; and build on experiments that failed or were
    [kaput]{.italic}.

-   Document every design decision in detail, starting with the
    rationale for the study through the plan to analyze the data. This
    ensures a careful record and makes writing a paper and meeting
    transparency requirements easier.

-   Experimentalists should preregister the existence of their study as
    one approach for helping with publication bias.

-   []{#part0013_split_013.html_page_159 .calibre1}Part of the
    documentation should include a careful plan on how to analyze the
    data. However, if an experimentalist formally adds that plan to the
    preregistration, do not be hamstrung by it
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as an author or a reviewer
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as that privileges certain types
    of work; ignores the reality of there being many theories in the
    social sciences; and can limit innovation. (The exception is in
    cases of very well-developed theory and/or large-scale policy
    experimentation.)

-   Once an experiment is done, it is worth considering doing it again.
    But this involves addressing ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}the
    fundamental problem of replication["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    by accounting for common, if not inevitable, variability in
    dimensions of external validity.

-   Repeat experiments or replications should typically be used to test
    the same hypotheses as the original (rather than to assess the
    replication rate per se), and as a way to generalize, innovate, and
    aggregate in a []{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-22
    .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-262
    .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-358
    .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-498
    .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-856
    .calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-924
    .calibre1}given area.
:::
:::::
::::::

:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: front-matter-part
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fng-5 .fn-group}
::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-136 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0013_split_000.html_FN-fn-136-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-199
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-297 .calibre1}The idea
of open science has a long history stemming back to when scientists
first began sharing resources and communicating findings in journals.
More recently, the term has been used to refer to proponents of an open
research culture as encapsulated by the Transparency and Openness
Promotion (TOP) Committee guidelines, spearheaded by the Center for Open
Science ([https://cos.io](https://cos.io){.calibre1}). The TOP
guidelines focus on journal procedures and policies for publication, but
in so doing, they also highlight basic principles that purportedly
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}translate scientific norms and values
into concrete actions["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-412-back-7
.calibre1}: 1423; also see Dunning
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-167){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-167-back-2
.calibre1}; Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-96-back-5
.calibre1}). The guidelines include standards that can be distinguished
into three areas: those that reward researchers for engaging in open
practices (i.e., citing data and materials, replication); those that
allow for reproducibility and evaluation (i.e., posting data, code, and
materials in a trusted repository; having independent verification of
analyses; having design transparency standards for review); and those
that address values resulting from preregistration (i.e.,
preregistration of the existence of the study, preregistration of
analysis plans). I discuss several of these ideas in what follows.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-137 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-137-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-99 .calibre1}Another way to
assess is to engage in exploratory research
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} that is,
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}an attempt to discover something new and
interesting, by working your way through a research
topic["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Swedberg
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-493){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-493-back
.calibre1}: 17). Here, empirical inquiry can help identify questions to
pursue further.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-138 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-138-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} The
importance of socialization as a way to generate research questions
accentuates the role of equitable professional opportunities. If
particular groups or individuals do not have access, they are
essentially disenfranchised from a crucial way in which scholars
identify questions to explore.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-139 .fn}
[[^4^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-139-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-54
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-110
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-123
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-161 .calibre1}A
remarkable example of the former is
Kahneman[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
([2002](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-302){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-302-back
.calibre1}) autobiographical note about teaching early in his career:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}To teach effectively I did a lot of
serious thinking about valid intuitions on which I could draw and
erroneous intuitions that I should teach students to overcome. I had no
idea, of course, but I was laying the foundation for a program of
research on judgment under uncertainty.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
This program of research came to be one of the most impactful in the
social sciences (e.g., Kahneman
[2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-303){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-303-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-140 .fn}
[[^5^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-140-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Selected examples from my experience include a framing experiment on
campaign finance (Druckman and Nelson
