[2003](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-160){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-160-back-4
.calibre1}); an experiment on framing versus cue taking (Druckman et al.
[2010](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-144){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-144-back
.calibre1}); an experiment on information search (Druckman et al.
[2012](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-142){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-142-back-3
.calibre1}); an experiment on the impact of elite polarization (Druckman
et al.
[2013](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-161){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-161-back-2
.calibre1}); an experiment on irrelevant even effects (Busby et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-77){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-77-back
.calibre1}); an experiment on pain perceptions (Druckman et al.
[2018c](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-163){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-163-back-2
.calibre1}); an experiment on incivility (Druckman et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-257){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-257-back
.calibre1}); an experiment on climate change communication (Bayes et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-31){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-31-back-3
.calibre1}); and an experiment on disability services (Druckman et al.
[2021b](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-157){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-157-back
.calibre1}). In each of the cases, the student generated the area of
interest and general questions and then we collaborated, sometimes along
with a graduate student, to read the literature, derive hypotheses,
design an experiment, etc.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-141 .fn}
[[^6^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-141-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They specifically used block random assignment to ensure balance on
environmental views, prior cosponsorship of the bill in a previous
Congress, years served, ideology, geography of the district office, and
prior presidential vote share in the district.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-142 .fn}
[[^7^](#part0013_split_001.html_FN-fn-142-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
That said, in all fairness, it is easier to be bold and endure failure
for established researchers with sizeable budgets. For early career
scholars, an experimental failure may be more problematic due to a lack
of resources to pursue another direction. It is here where mentoring
plays a vital role to guiding earlier career scholars to make research
investments in anticipation of what they would be able to do should the
results not fulfill expectations (and how to assess risk in light of
budgetary realities). It also accentuates the need for research funding
programs targeted to early career scholars.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-143 .fn}
[[^8^](#part0013_split_002.html_FN-fn-143-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-189 .calibre1}While not related
to experiments, one telling (and also remarkable) story comes from Oskar
Morgenstern, who wandered into a library and arbitrarily perused a book
that provided a crucial insight for his and von Neumann construction of
game theory (Morgenstern
[1976](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-389){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-389-back
.calibre1}). He states (1976: 811), ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}I
note a curious incident that shows how chance can influence the
direction of scientific work.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-144 .fn}
[[^9^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-144-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This point may seem, at first glance, to be a bias in favor of finding
an effect, but the rationale is that the
study[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s goals concerned whether a
two-step communication can occur and this requires initial media
influence (or else there is no possible
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}two
step["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-145 .fn}
[[^10^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-145-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-224 .calibre1}The document also
included pretest analyses to ensure that the segments on oil from the
different networks (i.e., MSNBC and Fox) were perceived as being in the
anticipated direction (i.e., against and for drilling), and were equally
persuasive in terms of logic (so that one segment did not exhibit a
strong effect for reasons of argument strength, which would have been a
confound).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-146 .fn}
[[^11^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-146-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Some of the reporting recommendations have generated debate,
particularly regarding whether one should report balance tests by
comparing measured covariates across experimental conditions to assess
the success of randomization (c.f., Gerber et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-208){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-208-back
.calibre1}; Mutz and Pemantle
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-400){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-400-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-147 .fn}
[[^12^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-147-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
the most extreme cases, it could allow for the identification of
fabrication (Christensen and Miguel
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-97-back-5
.calibre1}: 945).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-148 .fn}
[[^13^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-148-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-4
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-57
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-225
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-262 .calibre1}They also
explore the effects of, in addition to the posting notification,
explaining that the data will be made available to reduce the risk of
scientific fraud. However, the added statement has no additional effect
beyond the basic disclosure.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-149 .fn}
[[^14^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-149-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
The results are conditioned by self-monitoring scores; for example, in
the treatment condition, low self-monitors (i.e., those less worried
about presenting themselves in a way to impress an audience) are more
likely to answer
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}don[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}t
know["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to the knowledge questions (Connors
et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-104){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-104-back-3
.calibre1}: 196[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}197), and more likely to
express pro-choice attitudes (Connors et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-104){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-104-back-4
.calibre1}: 199). Further, high self-monitors overreport their income
when told of the disclosure (Connors et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-104){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-104-back-5
.calibre1}: 200[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}201).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-150 .fn}
[[^15^](#part0013_split_003.html_FN-fn-150-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
discussing transparency, two other points are worth keeping in mind.
First, there are the well-acknowledged privacy concerns. Some data
cannot be fully posted without comprising the anonymity of the
respondents. For example, data from an audit study of state legislators
that include detailed information about each legislator would make it
easy to identify whether particular legislators responded. This, in
turn, becomes problematic when anonymity is essential to conduct a
study. Even de-identified data sometimes allow motivated third parties
to identify particular respondents (Christensen and Miguel
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-97){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-97-back-6
.calibre1}: 969). In these scenarios, the solution involves not fully
posting data and making it available via request, and assurance from the
requestor to protect anonymity (i.e., an application for restricted data
access). This is the approach, for example, taken by the American
National Election Studies; see
[https://electionstudies.org/restricted-data-access](https://electionstudies.org/restricted-data-access){.calibre1}.
Second, some have politicized data transparency to undermine the
application of credible scientific research. For example, in 2019, the
Environmental Protection Agency appealed to the need for data
transparency as an explanation to ignore widely agreed upon credible
scientific data that include personal health information (thereby making
it un-releasable) (Friedman
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-197){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-197-back
.calibre1}). This action increased the difficulty of maintaining and
enacting clean air and water rules that reference evidence on the
effects of pollution from the confidential data. Here a sound scientific
principle that has some caveats concerning data privacy is being used to
promote an ostensible political agenda (de-identifying the data would be
possible but would cost hundreds of millions of dollars) (Frideman
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-197){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-197-back-2
.calibre1}). Scientists themselves need to communicate about
transparency in effective ways to prevent undermining the application of
science (e.g., Ioannidis
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-289){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-289-back
.calibre1}: 107).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-151 .fn}
[[^16^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-151-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} My
definition of publication bias is broader than others, which focus on a
bias that occurs when the publication decision depends on the result
(e.g., Brown et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-65){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-65-back-3
.calibre1}: 94). Implicit in these definitions, however, is the idea
that the result drives decisions, all else constant. As Malhotra
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-362-back-5
.calibre1}) clarifies, publication bias occurs
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}when the statistical significance of
findings influences publication probability [conditional on the quality
of the study design]{.italic}["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (italics
in the original). A broader definition allows for inclusion of other
types of biases such as decisions based on the identity of the author or
sponsor of the study. For an assessment of various types of biases, see
Fanelli et al.
([2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-182){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-182-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-152 .fn}
[[^17^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-152-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-127 .calibre1}The focus is
typically on the peer-reviewed scientific literature (Brown et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-65){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-65-back-4
.calibre1}: 93), rather than the so-called gray literature that includes
conference papers, dissertations, etc. Rosenthal
([1979](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-455){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-455-back-2
.calibre1}: 638) states ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}studies
published in the behavioral sciences are a biased sample of the studies
that are actually carried out.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} The
metaphor is that many studies end up in the file drawer rather than
academic journals.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-153 .fn}
[[^18^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-153-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
See Malhotra et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-362-back-6
.calibre1}) for a discussion of various other solutions (also see
Andrews and Kasy
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-10){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-10-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-154 .fn}
[[^19^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-154-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Preregistration is a norm/requirement in other fields, most notably when
it comes to medical clinical trials (for discussion, see Humphreys et
al.
[2013](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-276){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-276-back-5
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-155 .fn}
[[^20^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-155-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-32
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-193
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-221 .calibre1}Coffman
and Niederle
([2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-147-back-5
.calibre1}: 90[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}91) suggest that
downsides of preregistration include researchers revealing their plans
before the project is completely finished
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} this requires consideration of the
trade-off of maintaining an up-to-date registry against ensuring privacy
for a particular amount of time. They also mention that sometimes the
lack of publication from a registered study may be due to budget
challenges rather than null results, and more generally, a registry
needs to be organized in a useful and searchable manner. These are all
important practical points, as is the reality that some scholars may be
unaware that a given journal requires preregistration; that could be
addressed by allowing scholars to preregister upon submission.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-156 .fn}
[[^21^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-156-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-115
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-298 .calibre1}It is
explicit in the aforementioned TOP guidelines, which state that the
ideal journal ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}requires pre-registration
of studies with analysis plans and provides link and badge in article to
meeting requirements["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Nosek et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-412){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-412-back-8
.calibre1}: 1424). It is worth noting that what constitutes confirmatory
as opposed to exploratory hypotheses, is not always clear (Devezer et
al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-130-back-5
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-157 .fn}
[[^22^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-157-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Similar terms include data snooping and data butchery.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-158 .fn}
[[^23^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-158-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} A
related concept is the ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}garden of forking
paths["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (Gelman and Loken
[2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-207){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-207-back
.calibre1}) where researchers, often unconsciously, make particular
analytic decisions that lead to an outcome that may not occur if other
reasonable decisions had been made (and could have been made to ensure
robustness).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-159 .fn}
[[^24^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-159-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Another institution makes this norm explicit by instituting
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-230 .calibre1}results-blind
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-250 .calibre1}review where
journals conditionally accept articles prior to data collection or
without disclosure of the data collection, based on the theory and
design along with the pre-analysis plan. The journal publishes the
article, assuming the author(s) follows the data collection plan,
regardless of the statistical significance of the results (e.g., Nyhan
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-415){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-415-back
.calibre1}; Findley et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-185){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-185-back
.calibre1}). The [Journal of Experimental Political Science]{.italic}
offers this option. They have an initial review stage prior to the
presentation of results. Then at the second review stage, the article is
accepted if: (1) the research question and rationale for hypotheses did
not change; (2) the experimental procedures were followed closely and
any departures are noted and justified; (3) unregistered post hoc
analyses are clearly labeled, justified, methodologically sound, and
informative; and (4) conclusions are justified by the data (including
considerations of data quality).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-160 .fn}
[[^25^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-160-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Devezer et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-130){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-130-back-6
.calibre1}) also point out that nothing prevents a pre-analysis plan
from including problematic statistical procedures while giving a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}veneer of
rigor["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (14).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-161 .fn}
[[^26^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-161-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Another institution is a []{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-276
.calibre1}standard operating procedure: a statement from a lab of
default practices for handling certain types of issues, such as how to
handle attrition; how to define noncompliance; and whether to exclude
subjects who state that they discerned the purpose of the experiment
(Lin and Green
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-349){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-349-back-2
.calibre1}). This practice could, in theory, address some of these
concerns if it is faithfully followed, but it is not clear it can cover
all the issues that could arise.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-162 .fn}
[[^27^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-162-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-215
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-943 .calibre1}That
said, there are aspects of experiments that could enhance the likelihood
of statistical significance, particularly demand effects. I suspect
though that these are dwarfed by the various elements that make it
difficult to achieve significance; moreover, the extent of demand
effects remains unclear in many areas (e.g., Mummolo and Peterson
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-394){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-394-back-3
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-163 .fn}
[[^28^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-163-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Further, the extent of p-hacking appears to vary across disciplines
(Coffman and Niederle
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-147){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-147-back-6
.calibre1}: 83[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}84) and method (Brodeur
et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-64){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-64-back
.calibre1}). Thus, the usefulness of pre-analysis plans as a way to
limit p-hacking may be contingent on discipline (e.g., it appears to
occur much more often in psychology than economics) and method (e.g., it
appears to be less common with random assignment experiments). Indeed,
for particular types of experiments, p-hacking may be extremely unlikely
due to sheer expense. Consider a survey experiment on a probability
sample with an N of 2,000 and twelve items (i.e., treatments and
measures) costs roughly \$18,000
([https://tessexperiments.org/html/limits.html](https://tessexperiments.org/html/limits.html){.calibre1}).
And twelve items do not provide a lot of leeway for data drudging.
Finally, there is nothing inherently problematic about publishing null
results sans a formal preregistration or pre-analysis plan based on an
assessment of the quality of the experiment; that is, one can address
publication bias by altering norms around publishing null results
without requiring a pre-analysis plan for publication.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-164 .fn}
[[^29^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-164-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
the paper itself, the authors clarify that this was an exploratory
hypothesis but attempt to argue it stands on a strong theoretical basis.
How such an approach generally works in the publication process
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-216
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDClocr-tgt-944 .calibre1}is
unclear.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-165 .fn}
[[^30^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-165-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-56
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-68
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-160
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-196
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-287 .calibre1}Nosek et
al.
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-413){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-413-back-9
.calibre1}) address the reality that data collections do not always go
as planned, and suggest solutions including being transparent about
deviations, having sequential preregistrations, or preregistering a
decision tree depending on steps in data analyses. The [Journal of
Experimental Political Science]{.italic} addresses deviations by noting
that at step 2 in the review process, reviewers will assess if the
conclusions are justified, which includes considerations of data
quality. These are reasonable points but also lead to at least two
possible problems. First, if the motivation for these institutions is to
prevent researchers from engaging in problematic behaviors, what would
prevent them from doing so in reporting data quality issues (e.g., not
exploring SUTVA violations, engagement on the part of subjects, or all
manipulation checks)? Second, a null result may stem from problems in
implementation that the researcher does not recognize (or does not want
to recognize). For instance, as mentioned earlier, I was in the midst of
conducting an experiment on nuclear energy attitudes when the Fukushima
Daiichi nuclear disaster occurred. This event invalidated the entire
experiment as would have been obvious at the time. However, less
dramatic events in the world [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} or the
experimental context [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} can undermine
quality and thus null results may stem from contextual changes or poor
implementation rather than reflecting quality null results. Pre-analysis
plans also do not directly address a host of other questionable research
practices, such as running multiple experiments and only reporting one
with the predicted results, or collecting more data than planned but
only including a subset (Malhotra
[2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-362){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-362-back-7
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-166 .fn}
[[^31^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-166-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
a review of survey experiments, Sniderman
([2018](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-483){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-483-back-5
.calibre1}: 274) makes a similar point while expressing concern about
precommitment to hypotheses: ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}What has
become controversial is the persistence of uncertainty after
presentation of results. The fashion now is to conceive of hypothesis
testing as a one-off [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a decisive
demonstration that a claim is or is not valid. Perhaps this is useful.
But a different conception of research underpins this review: learning
as you go, each advance pointing to a possible new advance. If one can
see ahead, one can rarely see far ahead. It is learning what you had not
known that allows you to learn (something of) what you still do not
know. The research process is a process
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} a progression of
trials.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-167 .fn}
[[^32^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-167-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} In
my own experience, I once reported the results of an experiment using an
aggregated outcome measured that took the average of three items. As
this was preregistered, the reviewers found it fine, but one suggested I
move analyses of each separate individual item
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} the results of which suggested less
robust relationships [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} to an appendix,
since it was not in the pre-analysis plan. This was reasonable (in the
context of that paper), but the concern is that insistence of strict
adherence to a pre-analysis plan could prevent rather than encourage
more robust analyses.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-168 .fn}
[[^33^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-168-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-209
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-282 .calibre1}It may be
that statistical significance correlates with higher quality
implementation, while pre-analysis correlates with less p-hacking
(although this is speculative); which should be privileged depends on
one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s perspective. Further, empirically,
it remains unclear just how problematic the publication bias problem is
(due to few meta-analyses) and how often inauthentic p-hacking occurs
among experimentalists.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-169 .fn}
[[^34^](#part0013_split_004.html_FN-fn-169-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Some may say that adherence to a strict pre-analysis plan can adapt to
these issues as long as researchers explicitly explain themselves;
however, as noted, this introduces a large gray area and the fear is
scholars end up relying on the easy heuristic of whether the
pre-analysis plan was strictly followed (and hence, the issues I have
raised).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-170 .fn}
[[^35^](#part0013_split_005.html_FN-fn-170-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} A
tangentially related point is that open science practices present a
social dilemma since they are individually costly, but (advocates say)
they would benefit the collectivity (see Kraft-Todd and Rand 2021).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-171 .fn}
[[^36^](#part0013_split_005.html_FN-fn-171-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Disclosure statements refer to reporting standards as well as funding
and conflict of interest statements.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-172 .fn}
[[^37^](#part0013_split_006.html_FN-fn-172-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-200
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-271 .calibre1}In recent
years, few issues have garnered as much attention as replication. Many
believe that social science finds itself in a midst of a replication
crisis (e.g., Nature
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-404){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-404-back
.calibre1}; Finkel et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-187){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-187-back
.calibre1}; Baker
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-24){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-24-back
.calibre1}; Smaldino and McElreath
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-477){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-477-back
.calibre1}; Motyl et al.
[2017](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-392){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-392-back
.calibre1}; National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine
2019). This stems partially from the widely discussed Open Science
Collaboration[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s (2015) project that
involved more than 250 scholars attempting to replicate 100 experiments
in 3 highly ranked psychology journals (from 2008). They (2015: 943)
report that ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}39% of effects were
subjectively rated to have replicated the original
result["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (also see Doyen et. al. 2012;
Lynott et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-357){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-357-back
.calibre1}; Hagger et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-243){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-243-back
.calibre1}; Wagenmakers et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-509){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-509-back
.calibre1}). That said, other replication efforts have been more
successful (e.g., Klein et al.
[2014](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-317){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-317-back-3
.calibre1}; Mullinix et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-393-back-6
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-2
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-2
.calibre1}; Coppock et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-112-back-9
.calibre1}; Stark et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-490){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-490-back-2
.calibre1}; Yeager et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-519){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-519-back-3
.calibre1}; Ruggeri et al.
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-458){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-458-back
.calibre1}). The extent of the crisis is thus debatable (see Fanelli
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-181){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-181-back
.calibre1}: 2628), and, for the reasons I discuss next, interpreting the
results of any failed replication is tricky. Finally, one rarely
discussed point is that an individual replication study can only serve
as a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}correction["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
to the extent that its findings enter the literature (often the
published literature) and there may be publication biases unique to
replication studies (see Berinsky et al.
[2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-40){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-40-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-173 .fn}
[[^38^](#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-173-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
The term
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}reproducibility["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
is often synonymous with Freese and
Peterson[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}verifiability["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
(National Academies of Sciences, Engineering, and Medicine 2019).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-174 .fn}
[[^39^](#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-174-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Or
put another way, this compares: ([Outcome(treatment, unit)]{.italic}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [Outcome(control, unit)]{.italic})~O~
versus ([Outcome(treatment, unit)]{.italic}
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} [Outcome(control, unit)]{.italic})~R,~
with the expectation that a successful replication will mean the near
equivalence of the functions being compared.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-175 .fn}
[[^40^](#part0013_split_007.html_FN-fn-175-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Brandt et al.
([2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-63){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-63-back
.calibre1}) provide a useful guide to replication, revealing the large
number of considerations in play.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-176 .fn}
[[^41^](#part0013_split_008.html_FN-fn-176-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-81
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-223
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-226
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-247
.calibre1}[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-249 .calibre1}Examples
include replication/reproducibility rates, which are the proportion of
studies that produce significant effects in the same direction (e.g.,
Mullinix et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-393){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-393-back-7
.calibre1}; OSC 2015; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-3
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-3
.calibre1}; Coppock et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-112){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-112-back-10
.calibre1}), prediction intervals where one can estimate, say, the 95
percent prediction interval for the original estimate and test how many
replications fall in that interval (or alternatively, confidence
intervals) (e.g., Cumming
[2008](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-120){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-120-back
.calibre1}; OSC 2015; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-4
.calibre1}; Patil et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-427){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-427-back
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-4
.calibre1}); peer beliefs about replication such as prediction markets
(e.g., Dreber et al.
[2015](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-136){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-136-back
.calibre1}; OSC 2015; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-5
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-5
.calibre1}); effect size differences between original and replication
studies (e.g., OSC 2015; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-6
.calibre1}); meta-analytic approaches (e.g., OSC 2015; Camerer et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-85){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-85-back-7
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-6
.calibre1}); the small telescopes approach that estimates whether a
replication effect size is significantly smaller than a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}small
effect["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} in the original study with a
one-sided test at the 5 percent level (e.g., Simonsohn
[2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-472){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-472-back
.calibre1}; Camerer et al.
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-86){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-86-back-7
.calibre1}); a Bayesian approach that assigns a probability to a truth
claim and then views replications as a way to increase evidence for or
against the original claim (e.g., Goodman et al.
[2016](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-223){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-223-back
.calibre1}); and the positive predicted value, which is the proportion
of positive results in statistics (e.g., Goodman and Greenland
[2007](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-224){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-224-back
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-177 .fn}
[[^42^](#part0013_split_008.html_FN-fn-177-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Hedges
([2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-259){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-259-back-3
.calibre1}) and Hedges and Schauer
([2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-260){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-260-back-2
.calibre1}) further explain that due to statistical uncertainty in the
original and replication studies, it is often impossible to adequately
assess the success of a replication with a single replication attempt.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-178 .fn}
[[^43^](#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-178-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Peyton et al.
([2021](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-434){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-434-back
.calibre1}) explore this issue with regard to respondent attention
levels and suggest approaches to correct for shifting levels of
attention (to address what they refer to as temporal validity).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-179 .fn}
[[^44^](#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-179-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Another possibility, when replicating the work of others, is that it
stems from questionable research practices in the original experiment.
This explanation seems to be a common implicit presumption in the
aforementioned replication crisis narrative, yet there is little
evidence along these lines (see Fanelli
[2018](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-181){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-181-back-2
.calibre1}: 2628).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-180 .fn}
[[^45^](#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-180-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Realistically, one should not rely on a single replication (Cook and
Campbell
[1979](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-106){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-106-back-7
.calibre1}: 31).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-181 .fn}
[[^46^](#part0013_split_009.html_FN-fn-181-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
[]{#part0013_split_013.html_IDXloc-tgt-45 .calibre1}In particular, there
needs to be considerably more attention paid to theorizing about
context. This point is unfortunately often missed. Take, for instance,
this statement about replications:
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}studies differ at minimum in their
method factors [...]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} \[including\]
seemingly minor factors such as the social context, the subject pool,
and the time of day["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} (McShane et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-372){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-372-back
.calibre1}: 99). Social context, samples, and timing are far from
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}minor
factors.["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Similarly, I had an experience
of being part of a large scale replication project where my task was to
replicate an experiment that looked at the role of opinions about
government services (e.g., education, health care) on candidate choice
in the United States (between a clear versus ambiguous candidate). The
original data were collected in 2007 whereas the replication took place
in 2020. Much time and expense were put into ensuring the same stimuli
and type of sample were used in the replication, but at no point in the
process was there discussion about the changed context, which included
the 2020 data being collected in the midst of a global pandemic and a
contentious presidential campaign. It is possible that changed context
would not affect the relationship under study, but it certainly should
have been carefully considered.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-182 .fn}
[[^47^](#part0013_split_010.html_FN-fn-182-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} Of
course if the aim of a replication is to explicitly assess over-time
changes, then one should keep elements the same; see [Chapter
2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0013_split_013.html_CN-bp-2-back-36
.calibre1}[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s discussion of measurement
trade-offs.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-183 .fn}
[[^48^](#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-183-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They find that positive advertisements mobilize turnout by about 2.5
percent; thus, exposure to a negative as opposed to a positive
advertisement reduces vote intention by 5 percent.
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-184 .fn}
[[^49^](#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-184-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
They also look at respondent characteristics such as gender (women
respondents give a larger bonus) and partisanship (Republican
respondents give a smaller bonus).
:::

::: {#part0013_split_013.html_FN-fn-185 .fn}
[[^50^](#part0013_split_011.html_FN-fn-185-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This suggests that replication research programs are better off
attempting to replicate fewer phenomena many times than replicating many
phenomena a few times (McShane et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-372){#part0013_split_013.html_REFe-r-372-back-2
.calibre1}).
:::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::
:::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::::

[]{#part0014_split_000.html}

::: {#part0014_split_000.html_CN-bp-6 style="height:0pt"}
:::

:::::: front-matter-part
::: {.pcalibre .header1}
# [[]{#part0014_split_000.html_page_160 .calibre1}6]{.label1} Designing ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Experiments {#part0014_split_000.html_calibre_pb_0 .cn}
:::

:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0014_split_000.html_A-sec-06 .sec}
Most texts on experiments focus on design specifics, types of
experiments (e.g., laboratory, survey, field), how to conduct analyses,
and/or particular applications. I have taken a distinct approach by
focusing on how to think about experiments. I have done so in response
to the massive changes that have occurred, over the past decades, with
regards to social science experimentation. It was just twenty years ago
when I received journal reviews on multiple occasions dismissing
experimental work as
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}inappropriate["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
and thus rejecting the submissions on their face. Now, it is not
atypical to receive reviews questioning whether nonexperimental work can
be useful if one wants to make a causal claim. Much has changed and this
evolution has occurred concomitant with new technological opportunities
and altered sociological norms about science (e.g., open science).

One might think that during this transformative time, the fundamentals
of experimentation have changed as well. Yet, they have not; if
anything, scholars may be less apt to attend to some important details,
since data collection costs have become so much lower and thus less is
at stake with each data collection. With that in mind, in this chapter I
highlight the steps involved in designing a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiment. Consistent with the theme of the book, these steps place
experiments in the larger social scientific process in which experiments
constitute one step. The idea is not to offer a checklist; rather, it
involves a research process in which one situates the
study.[^1^](#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-186){#part0014_split_000.html_FN-fn-186-back
.calibre1} I now turn to these steps, after which I offer brief
concluding thoughts.
:::
::::
::::::

[]{#part0014_split_001.html}

:::::: front-matter-part
::::: front-matter-part
:::: {#part0014_split_001.html_A-sec-22 .sec}
# []{#part0014_split_001.html_page_161 .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-227 .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-351 .calibre1}Steps to Designing ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}Good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} Experiments {#part0014_split_001.html_calibre_pb_1 .a}

What it means to be a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiment is unclear [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} and increasingly
so, given recognition that null results can be of interest. I have
obviously touched on a host of criteria, including satisfying causal
inference assumptions; experimental realism; attending to multiple
dimensions of external validity; carefully articulating the relevant
points of comparison; and so on. Ultimately, a
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}good["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiment is one that offers a contribution to extant knowledge. Here I
provide a list of steps that, all else constant, should lead to improved
experiments.[^2^](#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-187){#part0014_split_001.html_FN-fn-187-back
.calibre1} In some sense, they are straightforward steps that echo the
scientific process and [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} perhaps
ironically given my critiques [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
formalize the open science practices for the sake of producing better
quality research (aside from ensuring it is transparent, etc.). To be
clear, following these steps involves the investment of substantial time
to maximize the worth of experimental data collection. For me, the
process takes on average about nine months, although it varies depending
on the complexity and novelty of the experiment (i.e., novelty in the
sense of my own prior knowledge on the topic). The time also will of
course differ among individuals depending on their work styles, career
stages, and life circumstances. I list out the steps in broad strokes
(for more details, including examples, see Druckman et al.
[2018a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-149){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-149-back-2
.calibre1}). With each step, I provide a brief example, but ones that
barely touch on the relevant issues.

::: {.section .front-matter-part}
-   []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-15
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-63
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-234 .calibre1}[Big
    picture idea]{.italic}: a short (i.e., few pages) document on the
    general topic and why it is relevant to understanding social,
    political, and/or economic phenomena. This document ideally iterates
    multiple times for feedback. It serves as an essential starting
    point by including a ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}big
    think["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} question and explaining why it
    is worth addressing. Even when one builds on a well-established
    experimental paradigm, this step serves as a check to ensure
    existing work has developed in a way that has not lost sight of what
    scholars ultimately want to understand.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, instead
        of immediately designing an audit study of democratic
        responsiveness, a scholar should step back and consider why one
        would want to study responsiveness (e.g., relative to other
        aspects of representation or democratic processes).
    :::

-   [Detailed []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-43
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-233
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-561
    .calibre1}literature review]{.italic}: an exhaustive search of
    research on the topic and detailed descriptions of specific studies.
    Ideally, it leads to []{#part0014_split_001.html_page_162
    .calibre1}the identification of multiple potential research
    directions, some of which are tabled for the future. It is crucial
    here to look for literature across fields and methods to assess the
    state of knowledge and the strengths and weaknesses of different
    approaches. It is at this stage that the researcher should identify
    specific gaps in existing knowledge that he/she hopes to address.
    The literature covered should be determined by substance and not
    method.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, one
        should not only review audit studies on democratic
        responsiveness; also included should be work on what we know
        about democratic responsiveness more generally, including
        theoretical work on representation (e.g., Mansbridge
        [2003](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-366){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-366-back
        .calibre1}; Disch
        [2011](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-132){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-132-back
        .calibre1}); qualitative work on elected officials (e.g., Fenno
        [1978](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-184){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-184-back
        .calibre1}); quantitative work on the public
        opinion[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}policy connection (e.g.,
        Erikson et al.
        [2002](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-179){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-179-back
        .calibre1}); and so on. Most of this document will not
        ultimately become part of a publication but it serves as a
        foundation for the project and its potential
        []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-44
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-234
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-562
        .calibre1}contribution.
    :::

-   [Research []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-66
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-235
    .calibre1}question(s) and outcomes]{.italic}: Given the
    identification of a gap in existing work, the next step is to put
    forth a specific question (or questions) to be addressed. This
    includes identifying the precise outcome variable(s) of interest.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, say one
        wants to study bias in responsiveness
        [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} one specific gap might be
        whether there is discrimination in response to requests based on
        one[']{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}s
        ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}personal
        position["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} relative to the elected
        officials. Fenno
        ([1978](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-184){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-184-back-2
        .calibre1}) identifies four
        ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}constituents,["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
        including the geographic (i.e., those in the district),
        reelection (i.e., supporters), primary (i.e., strong
        supporters), and personal (i.e. intimates). The question of
        interest may be how representatives respond to members of these
        distinct constituents [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} do they
        favor intimates over those in the
        district?[^3^](#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-188){#part0014_split_001.html_FN-fn-188-back
        .calibre1} The outcome may be whether representatives respond to
        a constituency service request, and, if so, the content of the
        responses. One may further want to compare levels of
        responsiveness to different constituencies against levels of
        responsiveness to requests from minority constituents (given it
        is a focus in the literature).
    :::

-   [Theory []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-239
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-483
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-991 .calibre1}and
    hypotheses]{.italic}: development of a theory and hypotheses to be
    tested. This often involves accessing distinct literatures,
    sometimes from other disciplines. Researchers should take their time
    to derive []{#part0014_split_001.html_page_163 .calibre1}concrete
    and specific predictions. As part of this step, potential mediators
    and/or moderators should be specified. Also, in putting forth
    predictions, one must be careful to isolate the comparisons to be
    used (see [Chapters
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-2-back-37
    .calibre1} and
    [4](#part0012_split_000.html_CN-bp-4){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-4-back-9
    .calibre1}).

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, in
        developing a theory about whether an elected representative may
        be more or less likely to respond to inquiries from distinct
        constituencies, one may turn to work on campaign communications,
        fundraising, interest groups, voting, corporate strategy (in
        economics/business), interpersonal interactions and trust (in
        psychology), etc. Further, one may posit a mechanism such as
        trust [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example, does the
        elected official trust that a response to a given inquiry will
        generate meaningful support? [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
        and moderators such as the electoral competitiveness of a given
        district. The researcher must think through the relevant
        comparisons. It might be most interesting to compare all other
        types of responsiveness relative to the geographic constituency
        condition, as normatively the official represents those
        individuals. Alternatively, perhaps comparing supporters to
        intimates is more meaningful to see the impact of resources on
        responsiveness. Or, comparisons against racial bias may be most
        intriguing to assess the relative sizes of inequities in
        []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-240
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-484
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-992
        .calibre1}representation.
    :::

-   [Research []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-231
    .calibre1}design]{.italic}: The scholar puts forth a design (see
    Leeper
    [2011](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-339){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-339-back
    .calibre1}). This includes at least six components, as follows:

    ::::::::::::: {#part0014_split_001.html_NL-lst-26 .list4}
    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [(1)]{.label3} Discussion of the designs used by others who have
    addressed similar questions, and how the proposed design connects
    with previous work. As emphasized in [Chapter
    5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-5-back-11
    .calibre1}, the ideal strategy is to utilize and extend prior
    designs.
    :::

    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [(2)]{.label3} Discussion of how such a design will provide data
    relevant to the larger question(s).
    :::

    :::::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [(3)]{.label3} Identifying where the data will come from, which
    includes:

    ::::: list4
    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [a]{.label3} Consideration of the sample and any potential biases.
    Importantly, as highlighted in [Chapter
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-2-back-38
    .calibre1}, it is essential to explicitly state the target
    populations to which one hopes to make an inference. This includes
    the units, contexts, measures, and outcomes.
    :::

    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [b]{.label3} Detailed measures and where the measures were obtained
    [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for example, were they used in
    prior studies? The measures need to clearly connect to the
    hypotheses, including the mediators/moderators. One should discuss
    the validity and accuracy of the measures (see [Chapter
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-2-back-39
    .calibre1}).
    :::
    :::::
    ::::::

    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [[]{#part0014_split_001.html_page_164 .calibre1}(4)]{.label3} In
    many cases, the design may be too practically complex (e.g., number
    of experimental conditions relative to a realistic sample size), and
    decisions must be made on what can be trimmed without interfering
    with the goal of the study. This process will typically require a
    power analysis (see [Chapter
    2](#part0010_split_000.html_CN-bp-2){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-2-back-40
    .calibre1}).
    :::

    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [(5)]{.label3} A discussion of necessary pretests of stimuli,
    question wordings, etc.
    :::

    :::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [(6)]{.label3} Issues related to internal and external validity
    should be discussed (see [Chapter
    3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-3-back-19
    .calibre1}).

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, one
        should consider distinct approaches and designs used to address
        questions about legislative responsiveness. If an audit approach
        is chosen, past work should be reviewed with reference to the
        initial ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}big
        picture["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} question. One might use
        a sample of state legislators, as is common, to conduct an audit
        (assuming the level of constituency theory put forth applies).
        It would be important to justify operationalization decisions
        [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} for instance, the geographic
        constituency could be operationalized with a zip code reference,
        while the strong supporter condition could be operationalized
        with reference to donations. It is important to justify that
        those operationalizations would be perceived as such by
        officials. This requires piloting and consideration of the
        information equivalence problem discussed in [Chapter
        3](#part0011_split_000.html_CN-bp-3){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-3-back-20
        .calibre1} (e.g., do certain zip codes also signal wealth?).
        Then one must turn to the content of the request and how
        responses might be coded, etc. Finally, as mentioned, if the
        projected sample size is too small, some experimental conditions
        may have to be cut. If the aforementioned design planned to
        cross the four types of constituents with their race, then the
        number of conditions may be too many and difficult decisions
        []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-232 .calibre1}must be
        made.
    :::
    ::::
    :::::::::::::

-   [Data []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-205
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-211
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-255
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-767
    .calibre1}collection document]{.italic}: a step-by-step plan of how
    data will be collected so as not to later forget such details as
    recruitment, implementation, etc.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, how
        will one obtain contact information for elected officials; what
        e-mail accounts will be used to send inquiries on what dates;
        how are bounce back e-mails treated, etc.?
    :::

-   [Data analysis plan]{.italic}: There needs to be a clear data
    analysis plan [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} how
    [exactly]{.italic} will the data be used to test hypotheses? The
    researcher should directly connect the design and measures to the
    hypotheses. This often []{#part0014_split_001.html_page_165
    .calibre1}involves making a table with each measure and how it maps
    onto specific hypotheses. What techniques will be used if the data
    collection is imperfect (e.g., smaller sample size than expected)?
    The plan ensures that the right data will be collected, and it
    provides a blueprint of what to do once one receives the data.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, à la
        the prior discussion, what conditions will be compared to what
        and how might outcomes be coded? One might initially compare
        percentages of responses; however, analyses might also include
        coding the content, in which case precise coding rules need to
        be laid out, as do approaches to analysis.

    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} This step is akin to
        an internal pre-analysis plan [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
        it is why in [Chapter
        5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0014_split_001.html_CN-bp-5-back-12
        .calibre1} I emphasized the importance of such a plan for
        research quality. The point is to ensure that the researcher
        collects the right data and knows what to do with the data once
        it is collected [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} rather than to
        differentiate confirmatory and exploratory hypotheses. It is
        common to deviate from this plan, but having a plan is essential
        to avoid []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-206
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-212
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-256
        .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-768
        .calibre1}mis-collecting data.
    :::

-   [Institutional []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-150
    .calibre1}Review Board]{.italic}: The researcher should complete the
    Institutional Review Board (IRB) process. If the project involves
    nontrivial deception or other aspects that could prevent/delay IRB
    approval, the researcher should directly contact an IRB
    representative early in the development of the project. In many
    cases, the researcher will work with the IRB to figure out what is
    feasible. The process can take weeks or months and so starting early
    is important (perhaps even before the prior steps). Researchers
    should attend to ethical, legal, and political considerations where
    applicable (King and Sands
    [2015](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-315){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-315-back
    .calibre1}). An additional benefit of following the steps outlined
    is that the researcher will have already prepared nearly all
    information and documents required for an IRB application.

    ::: {.section .front-matter-part}
    -   [[◦]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}]{.label3} For example, for an
        audit study with no consent or debriefing, an IRB that is not
        familiar with such studies may generate many iterations that
        could slow down implementation if not addressed sufficiently
        early.
    :::

-   [Merging []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-207
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-257 .calibre1}the
    pieces]{.italic}: At this point, the researcher merges the
    aforementioned pieces into a single document and, ideally, others
    review it. This step serves as a check that, in the process of
    moving from the larger abstract research question to the specific
    analysis plan, a disconnect did not emerge. In some instances, the
    particular design no longer speaks to the initial motivating
    question or no longer clearly fills a gap in the literature.

    :::: {#part0014_split_001.html_NL-lst-31 .list4}
    ::: {.pcalibre1 .list-item}
    [[]{#part0014_split_001.html_page_166 .calibre1}o]{.label3} For
    example, one may have started out by asking if elected officials
    equally represent their constituents, but ended with a design that
    had to be cut down due to power considerations. The new design may
    be focused on the relative bias in responsiveness toward
    ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}intimates["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
    and ["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}strong
    supporters["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} as conditioned by race
    (e.g., varied race in the design). This remains an interesting
    question but it is no longer about the treatment of the geographic
    constituency []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-208
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-258 .calibre1}writ
    large.
    :::
    ::::

-   []{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-95
    .calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDXloc-tgt-142
    .calibre1}[Implementation]{.italic}: From here, data are collected
    and analyzed, and the planning document serves as a guide to writing
    up the results and, potentially, identifying reasons why one may not
    have found what was expected. A part of this implementation involves
    careful record-keeping of data analytic choices. This also requires
    careful attention and documentation of challenges that arise during
    data collection such as participants not arriving or complying,
    programming errors, naturally occurring events during data
    collection that could alter the data, and other unanticipated
    problems (see Karlan and Appel
    [2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-308){#part0014_split_001.html_REFe-r-308-back-5
    .calibre1}).
:::

The entire approach means that writing much of a paper entails cutting
out superfluous parts, as one has an exhaustive experimental design
document before data are even collected. There is nothing magical about
these steps. The framework simply breaks down the research process into
manageable pieces and forces researchers to think through each specific
decision, which reduces the likelihood of a design error. In an age
where data are so easily obtainable, the risk is that researchers jump
right to the design stage (the fifth of ten steps), or the data analysis
plan (the seventh of ten steps), without considering the construction of
a theory. Open science initiatives that focus on transparency,
preregistration, and replication rarely mention the first four steps
(prior to the research design). Yet, one can only arrive at a meaningful
pre-analysis plan by spending time thinking about the question being
asked; reviewing the relevant literature; specifying a specific question
and outcome; and developing a theory and hypotheses. These early steps
are as vital to conducting meaningful experiments as are ensuring
transparency or conducting replications. Put another way, much of what
makes a good experiment occurs well before one even starts to think
about the experimental particulars. When one gets to the step of
experimental specifics, the theory should guide the choice of
conditions, comparisons, and analytic approaches. The experimentalist
should be able to easily explain what the test will add to extant
knowledge and how the data will be analyzed to
[]{#part0014_split_001.html_page_167 .calibre1}do so. Only then can we
start to worry about many of the topics covered in this book and other
discussions about the experimental
method.[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-228
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_001.html_IDClocr-tgt-352 .calibre1}
::::
:::::
::::::

[]{#part0014_split_002.html}

:::::::::: front-matter-part
:::: front-matter-part
::: {#part0014_split_002.html_A-sec-23 .sec}
# Conclusion {#part0014_split_002.html_calibre_pb_2 .a}

[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-317
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-349
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-935 .calibre1}I began
this book with quotes from presidents of the American Political Science
Association. The first quote, from more than 100 years ago, suggested
that experiments were an impossibility in political science (Lowell
[1910](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-351){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-351-back-2
.calibre1}). Methodological and technological developments, along with
scholarly ingenuity, have shown this is not the case
[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans} experiments play a crucial role not
just in political science but the social sciences more generally. The
second quote, from 2020, expressed concern that an overreliance on
experiments would constrict the questions social scientists address
(Smith
[2020](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-479){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-479-back-7
.calibre1}). This concern is reasonable, given the remarkable rise of
experiments and their central place in recent epistemological debates
about open science. I share the concern and my hope is that I have
addressed it with a two-pronged answer. First, thinking experimentally
introduces a host of considerations that are difficult to address.
Consequently, in many cases the hurdles to experimentation will
hopefully cause scholars to recognize the relevance of other methods to
answering particular questions. Second, the steps that make for a good
experiment are exactly those which hopefully prevent experiments from
constricting the questions asked [--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
experiments are worthwhile when they constitute an appropriate method to
address the question embedded in a larger research enterprise. Put
another way, if the constriction concern proves accurate, not only might
social scientists narrow the questions they address, but they may do so
in less than ideal ways (e.g., the experiments will not themselves be
compelling).

Alas, I think there is reason for hope that experiments have not yet
constricted questions generally or in a way that has substantially
undermined quality. The last two decades have seen a host of
foundational contributions from experiments and most are used in sound,
thoughtful ways that advance knowledge. Social science experimentation
can evolve in even more exciting ways, given the new opportunities that
come with widespread acceptance of the method and technological and
sociological advances. But, this will only happen if scholars
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}think["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
about experiments carefully, recognizing not only their possibilities
but also their limitations: Any experiment requires making a host of
assumptions, inferential leaps, and much tedious work. As I stated at
the outset, experiments seem often to be designed and implemented
quickly and not []{#part0014_split_002.html_page_168 .calibre1}connected
to the full scientific process. This is a problem; experiments need to
be thought of as one part of a scientific process and not the first
part. They need to be used when appropriate and build on/have an
interplay with questions, observations, and theory. Moreover, conducting
a quality experiment requires thinking through a litany of decisions
discussed throughout the book. A good experiment is slow moving (given
the host of considerations), which is counter to the current fast-moving
temptations available in the social sciences.

[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-18
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDXloc-tgt-277 .calibre1}Campbell
and Stanley
([1963](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-90){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-90-back-3
.calibre1}: 3) worried about a poverty of experiments stemming from a
lack of data and null results. Those concerns increasingly have become
less relevant; however, a new poverty of poor designs, inappropriate
analyses, limited use of data, and/or flawed interpretation is always a
possibility. Preventing that poverty requires engaging in careful
experimental thinking that will ensure the sustained contributions of
experiments to the accumulation of social science
[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-318
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-350
.calibre1}[]{#part0014_split_002.html_IDClocr-tgt-936
.calibre1}knowledge.
:::
::::

::::::: front-matter-part
:::::: {#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fng-6 .fn-group}
::: {#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-186 .fn}
[[^1^](#part0014_split_000.html_FN-fn-186-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
Gerber et al.
([2014](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-209){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-209-back-2
.calibre1}) and Boudreau
([2021](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-62){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-62-back-4
.calibre1}) offer superb
["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}checklists["]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}
for specific aspects of experiments (also see Christensen et al.
[2019](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-96){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-96-back-6
.calibre1}: 143[--]{.unicode-altfont-dejavusans}157).
:::

::: {#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-187 .fn}
[[^2^](#part0014_split_001.html_FN-fn-187-back){.calibre1}]{.label2}
This list originally appeared in Druckman et al.
([2018a](#part0015_split_000.html_REFe-r-149){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-149-back-3
.calibre1}).
:::

::: {#part0014_split_002.html_FN-fn-188 .fn}
[[^3^](#part0014_split_001.html_FN-fn-188-back){.calibre1}]{.label2} As
discussed in [Chapter
5](#part0013_split_000.html_CN-bp-5){#part0014_split_002.html_CN-bp-5-back-13
.calibre1}, Kalla and Broockman
([2016](#part0015_split_001.html_REFe-r-304){#part0014_split_002.html_REFe-r-304-back-2
.calibre1}) present a related experiment on the impact of donor versus
non-donor requests for access.
:::
::::::
:::::::
::::::::::

[]{#part0015_split_000.html}
